# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> While physical topology is descriptive, KTU 2024 Scheme questions frequently involve **numerical derivations** of cable count, port count, and cost analysis. This section exhaustively derives all such formulas with zero step-skipping.

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Cables in a Full Mesh Topology

**Problem Setup:** Given $N$ network nodes, find the number of distinct physical links $P_c$ required so that every node is directly connected to every other node.

**Step 1 — Understand the combinatorial structure:**
A link between node $A$ and node $B$ is the same as the link between $B$ and $A$ (undirected graph). We are therefore choosing **2 nodes out of $N$** to form each link.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \binom{N}{2} \\
&= \frac{N!}{2!\,(N-2)!} \\
&= \frac{N \times (N-1) \times (N-2)!}{2 \times (N-2)!} \\
&= \frac{N \times (N-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_c^{\,\text{mesh}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Verification with small cases:**
- If $N = 2$: $P_c = \frac{2 \times 1}{2} = 1$ ✓
- If $N = 3$: $P_c = \frac{3 \times 2}{2} = 3$ (forms a triangle) ✓
- If $N = 4$: $P_c = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ (forms a tetrahedral graph) ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Cables in a Star Topology

**Problem Setup:** Each of $N$ nodes connects to **one** central device (hub/switch). The central device does not count as a node for cabling purposes; only the drop links from the central device to each node are counted.

**Step 1 — Each node contributes exactly one link:**
A dedicated cable runs from the central device to every node.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_c^{\,\text{star}} = N}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost comparison with full mesh:**
The ratio of mesh cables to star cables is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_c^{\,\text{mesh}}}{P_c^{\,\text{star}}} &= \frac{N(N-1)/2}{N} \\
&= \frac{N-1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that mesh cabling grows **quadratically** while star grows **linearly**, justifying why large LANs use star and not mesh.

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Cables in a Ring Topology

**Problem Setup:** Each node connects to its **two immediate neighbors**, forming a single closed loop. For a closed ring, the number of links equals the number of nodes (each node contributes one incoming and one outgoing link, but the two ends close the loop).

**Step 1 — A cycle graph $C_N$ on $N$ vertices:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_c^{\,\text{ring}} = N}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparison:**
A ring has the **same cable count as a star** but **no central device**, which is a key exam point.

## 3.4 Derivation: Number of Cables in a Tree Topology

**Problem Setup:** A tree is a connected, acyclic graph. For any connected graph with $N$ nodes and no cycles:

**Step 1 — Property of trees:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= N - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_c^{\,\text{tree}} = N - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Why this works:**
A tree with $N$ nodes has exactly $N - 1$ edges; this is a fundamental theorem in graph theory (Cayley's formula corollary for spanning trees).

## 3.5 Derivation: Number of Cables in a Bus Topology

**Problem Setup:** A single backbone cable runs the length of the network; each of $N$ nodes connects via a short drop cable.

**Step 1 — Count components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= 1 \text{ (backbone)} + N \text{ (drop cables)} \\
&= N + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_c^{\,\text{bus}} = N + 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Special note:** If "drop cables" are not counted as separate cables, then $P_c = 1$. Always clarify with the KTU exam question's wording.

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Cost Matrix

Let us assume each cable unit costs $\mathbf{C_{\text{cable}}}$ and each port on a device costs $\mathbf{C_{\text{port}}}$. The total hardware cost $C_{\text{total}}$ for $N$ nodes is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}}^{\,\text{bus}} &= 1 \cdot C_{\text{cable}} + N \cdot C_{\text{drop}} \\
C_{\text{total}}^{\,\text{star}} &= N \cdot C_{\text{cable}} + N \cdot C_{\text{port}}^{\,\text{switch}} \\
C_{\text{total}}^{\,\text{ring}} &= N \cdot C_{\text{cable}} \\
C_{\text{total}}^{\,\text{mesh}} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2} \cdot C_{\text{cable}} + N(N-1) \cdot C_{\text{port}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B problems often ask: *"Compare the cost of mesh and star for $N = 10$ nodes."* Use the formulas above with specific $C_{\text{cable}}$ and $C_{\text{port}}$ values provided in the question.

### 3.6.1 Numerical Example Walk-Through

**Question:** A company has $N = 6$ computers. Compute the number of cables required for (a) Bus, (b) Star, (c) Ring, (d) Full Mesh topologies, assuming drop cables are counted in Bus.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c^{\,\text{bus}} &= N + 1 = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ cables} \\
P_c^{\,\text{star}} &= N = 6 \text{ cables} \\
P_c^{\,\text{ring}} &= N = 6 \text{ cables} \\
P_c^{\,\text{mesh}} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15 \text{ cables}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** Mesh requires **2.5×** more cables than star for the same $N = 6$. This ratio grows with $N$.

## 3.7 Worked Example: Signal Reflection in Bus Topology

**Background:** In a bus topology, an unterminated cable end causes **signal reflection**, leading to echoes and corruption. A 50 Ω terminator absorbs the signal energy, eliminating reflection.

**Step 1 — Characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of coaxial cable:**

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

where $L$ is inductance per unit length and $C$ is capacitance per unit length.

**Step 2 — Reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ at a mismatched load:**

$$
\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Condition for zero reflection:**

$$
\Gamma = 0 \implies Z_L = Z_0
$$

For a properly terminated bus: $Z_L = 50\ \Omega$, which is the standard characteristic impedance of **10BASE-2 / 10BASE-5** coax.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU may ask: *"Why are terminators required in a bus topology?"* The answer: **to prevent signal reflection by matching the load impedance to the characteristic impedance of the cable**, ensuring $\Gamma = 0$.


**Interpretation:** Each node connects to exactly two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Data circulates in one direction (token passing).

```mermaid
graph LR

```

## 4.7 Hybrid (Star-Bus) Topology — Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    BUS1((MAIN BUS)) --- SW1[STAR HUB A]
    BUS1 --- SW2[STAR HUB B]
    SW1 --- N1[NODE 1]

    SW2 --- N3[NODE 3]
    SW2 --- N4[NODE 4]
```

**Interpretation:** Two star-configured clusters are bridged by a bus backbone. This combines the **fault isolation of star** with the **extended reach of bus**.

## 4.8 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For a comprehensive systems-level view, the following block diagram illustrates the layered interaction of physical topology with the OSI model:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[APPLICATION LAYER LAYER 7] --> B[TRANSPORT LAYER LAYER 4]
    B --> C[NETWORK LAYER LAYER 3]
    C --> D[DATA LINK LAYER LAYER 2]
    D --> E[PHYSICAL LAYER LAYER 1 PHYSICAL TOPOLOGY]
    E --> F[CABLE MEDIUM COPPER FIBER WIRELESS]
    F --> G[RECEIVER PHYSICAL LAYER LAYER 1]
    G --> H[DATA LINK LAYER LAYER 2]
    H --> I[NETWORK LAYER LAYER 3]
    I --> J[TRANSPORT LAYER LAYER 4]
    J --> K[APPLICATION LAYER LAYER 7]
```

**Interpretation:** Physical topology resides at **Layer 1** of the OSI model. It provides the electrical/optical/radio pathway over which the upper layers logically communicate.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three advantages and two disadvantages of Star topology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Easy to install and modify:** Adding or removing a node only requires connecting or disconnecting a single cable to the central device.
2. **Centralized management:** A single hub/switch can be monitored, configured, and troubleshot from one location.
3. **Fault isolation:** A failure in one cable or node does not affect the rest of the network (unlike bus).

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Single point of failure at the central device:** If the hub/switch fails, the entire network goes down.
2. **Higher cabling cost:** Requires $N$ dedicated cables for $N$ nodes, which is more than the single backbone of a bus.

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between physical topology and logical topology with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Physical Topology | Logical Topology |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Actual geometric layout of cables and devices | How data actually flows between nodes |
| Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) of OSI | Layer 2 (Data Link) of OSI |
| Concerned with | Hardware placement | Protocol behavior |
| Example 1 | Star physical layout (cables radiate from switch) | Ethernet bus logical (CSMA/CD broadcast) |
| Example 2 | Star physical layout of Token Ring | Ring logical (token circulates logically) |
| Change requires | Recabling | Software/protocol reconfiguration |

> [!NOTE]
> A single physical topology can support **multiple** logical topologies and vice versa. This independence is a frequent KTU exam theme.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and provide an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative has two sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks). Sub-part (a) is usually descriptive/analytical, and sub-part (b) is numerical/comparative.

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the various types of physical topologies used in computer networks, with neat diagrams. Compare Bus and Star topologies on the basis of cabling, fault tolerance, scalability, and cost.

**(b) [7 marks]** A network has 12 computers. Calculate the number of cables required for (i) Bus, (ii) Star, (iii) Ring, and (iv) Full Mesh topologies. Assume that drop cables are counted in Bus topology. Justify which topology is most economical for this scenario.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define physical topology:**
Physical topology refers to the geometric arrangement of nodes and links in a network, independent of data flow.

**Step 2 — Describe each topology:**

1. **Bus Topology:** All nodes tap into a single shared backbone cable terminated at both ends. Used in legacy 10BASE-2/10BASE-5 Ethernet. Cheap but prone to single-point-of-failure and signal reflection.
2. **Star Topology:** Each node connects to a central hub or switch. Modern Ethernet LAN standard. Easy to add nodes; one cable failure does not affect other nodes.
3. **Ring Topology:** Each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Token passing ensures orderly access. Single link failure can break the ring unless dual-ring is used.
4. **Mesh Topology:** Every node connects to every other node. Highest fault tolerance; used in backbone networks. Cabling cost grows quadratically.
5. **Tree Topology:** Hierarchical combination of bus backbone and star clusters. Scales well for large organizations.
6. **Hybrid Topology:** Mix of two or more topologies integrated via a backbone.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (Bus vs. Star):**

| Parameter | Bus | Star |
|---|---|---|
| Cabling | 1 backbone + N drops | N dedicated links |
| Fault Tolerance | Poor — backbone failure halts all | Good — single cable failure isolated |
| Scalability | Limited (signal degrades) | High (limited by switch ports) |
| Cost | Lowest for small N | Moderate; cost = N cables + switch |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $N = 12$ computers.

**Step 1 — Apply formulas from Section 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c^{\,\text{bus}} &= N + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13 \text{ cables} \\
P_c^{\,\text{star}} &= N = 12 \text{ cables} \\
P_c^{\,\text{ring}} &= N = 12 \text{ cables} \\
P_c^{\,\text{mesh}} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = \frac{12 \times 11}{2} = 66 \text{ cables}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost comparison (assuming cable cost = ₹100 each, switch port cost = ₹50):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{bus}} &\approx 13 \times 100 = \text{₹}1300 + \text{minimal T-connectors} \\
C_{\text{star}} &= 12 \times 100 + 12 \times 50 = \text{₹}1800 \\
C_{\text{ring}} &= 12 \times 100 = \text{₹}1200 \\
C_{\text{mesh}} &= 66 \times 100 + 12 \times 11 \times 50 = \text{₹}12\,600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Justification:**
For $N = 12$, **Ring topology is the most economical** (₹1200), closely followed by **Bus** (₹1300). However, considering fault tolerance and ease of expansion, **Star topology** offers the best engineering trade-off and is recommended for modern LANs.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain Mesh topology. What is the difference between full mesh and partial mesh? Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of mesh topology.

**(b) [7 marks]** A star topology network has 8 computers connected to a central switch. Calculate (i) the number of cables required, (ii) the number of switch ports needed, and (iii) the number of links in an equivalent full mesh network. State the percentage increase in cabling when migrating from star to full mesh.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Diagram (refer to Section 4.5):**
A full mesh with $N = 4$ shows every node connected to every other node via a dedicated link.

**Step 2 — Define full mesh and partial mesh:**

- **Full mesh:** Every node is directly connected to every other node. The number of links is $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$.
- **Partial mesh:** Only selected nodes have direct connections; others communicate via intermediate hops. Used when full mesh is cost-prohibitive.

**Step 3 — Advantages:**
1. **Excellent fault tolerance:** Multiple redundant paths; failure of one link does not isolate any node.
2. **No traffic congestion:** Dedicated links mean simultaneous transmissions between different pairs.
3. **High reliability and security:** Each link is point-to-point; eavesdropping is harder.

**Step 4 — Disadvantages:**
1. **High cabling cost:** $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ links required.
2. **Complex installation and management:** Each new node requires $N - 1$ new links.
3. **Impractical for large $N$:** With $N = 100$, full mesh needs 4950 cables.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $N = 8$ computers in a star topology.

**Step 1 — Number of cables in star:**

$$
P_c^{\,\text{star}} = N = 8 \text{ cables}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of switch ports:**

$$
\text{Ports} = N = 8 \text{ ports}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of links in equivalent full mesh:**

$$
P_c^{\,\text{mesh}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2} = \frac{8 \times 7}{2} = 28 \text{ links}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage increase in cabling:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Increase} &= \frac{P_c^{\,\text{mesh}} - P_c^{\,\text{star}}}{P_c^{\,\text{star}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{28 - 8}{8} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{20}{8} \times 100\% \\
&= 250\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Migrating from star to full mesh for $N = 8$ requires **250% more cables** — i.e., the cabling is **3.5×** the star's requirement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing physical and logical topology** in the differentiation question — examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting drop cables** in bus topology count — clarify whether the question includes them.
> 3. **Plugging $N$ instead of $N-1$** in full mesh formula — re-derive from $\binom{N}{2}$ if confused.
> 4. **Writing only one disadvantage** when the question asks for two — full marks require both.
> 5. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — always include "cables", "ports", "%".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> This rapid-revision checklist is optimized for the **last 30 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall**.

### Key Definitions
- **Physical Topology:** Geometric layout of cables and devices at OSI Layer 1.
- **Logical Topology:** Data flow pattern at OSI Layer 2.
- **Backbone:** Central cable in bus/tree topologies.
- **Terminator:** 50 Ω resistor at bus ends to prevent signal reflection.
- **Token:** 3-byte control frame in ring topology granting transmission rights.

### Key Formulas (At a Glance)
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c^{\,\text{bus}} &= N + 1 \\
P_c^{\,\text{star}} &= N \\
P_c^{\,\text{ring}} &= N \\
P_c^{\,\text{tree}} &= N - 1 \\
P_c^{\,\text{mesh}} &= \frac{N(N-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Topology Selection Heuristics
- **Small office, low budget:** Bus (legacy) or Star (modern).
- **Deterministic access required:** Ring.
- **Maximum reliability:** Mesh (full or partial).
- **Large campus:** Tree or Hybrid.
- **ISP backbone:** Partial Mesh.

### Common KTU Question Patterns
1. Differentiate physical vs. logical topology — **3 marks**.
2. List advantages/disadvantages of a given topology — **3 marks**.
3. Draw a topology diagram and explain — **7 marks**.
4. Calculate cable/port count for $N$ nodes — **7 marks**.
5. Cost analysis comparing two topologies — **7 marks**.

### Examiner Pet Topics
- **Token passing** in ring topology (sequence of token capture, data send, release).
- **Why terminators are needed in bus** (signal reflection, $\Gamma = 0$ condition).
- **Single point of failure** in bus and star.
- **Quadratic growth of mesh** cabling — always derive, never state.
- **Tree topology is also called extended star.**

### One-Line Memory Aid
> **"Buses need terminators, Stars need switches, Rings need tokens, Mesh needs money, Trees need trunks, Hybrids need all."**

topics.title: Signal Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Encoding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: SIGNAL ENCODING — CORE TECHNICAL FOUNDATION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Encoding** (also called *Line Coding* or *Signal Transformation*) is the process of converting binary data (bits) or analog information into a **transmission-suitable signal pattern** (digital or analog waveform) that can be reliably propagated across a physical communication medium (copper wire, optical fiber, RF channel, or wireless air interface) while preserving data integrity and minimizing transmission impairments such as DC bias, baseline wandering, and clock synchronization loss.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Computer Networks syllabus (PERUT758)**, Signal Encoding is classified under **Module 1 – Introduction & Physical Layer**, and is formally categorized into **four (4) fundamental conversion classes**:

$$
\text{Encoding Domain Matrix} = \begin{cases}
\text{Digital} \rightarrow \text{Digital} & (\text{Line Coding / Block Coding}) \\
\text{Analog} \rightarrow \text{Digital} & (\text{PCM, Delta Modulation}) \\
\text{Digital} \rightarrow \text{Analog} & (\text{ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM}) \\
\text{Analog} \rightarrow \text{Analog} & (\text{AM, FM, PM})
\end{cases}
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Signal Encoding like translating a letter (the message) into a courier-friendly package.**
>
> - The **message (bits)** is the actual information.
> - The **envelope (signal)** is the format that travels through the postal system (the channel).
> - **Different postal services (channels)** require different envelope types:
>   - A *paper letter* cannot be sent through a *pigeon courier* — you need a light signal.
>   - A *voice message* cannot be transmitted through a *fiber optic cable* — you must first digitize it (PCM).
> - **Signal Encoding is the act of choosing the right envelope for the right postal service.**

## 1.3 Why Signal Encoding Is Indispensable (KTU Board Emphasis)

The KTU board examiner expects students to articulate **at least three concrete reasons** for performing signal encoding:

1. **Bandwidth Optimization**: Efficient encoding schemes compress the required transmission bandwidth.
2. **DC Component Elimination**: Many channels (e.g., transformers, AC-coupled lines) cannot pass DC; certain encodings (Manchester) guarantee no DC bias.
3. **Clock Recovery (Synchronization)**: Self-clocking encodings embed the clock into the data signal (e.g., Manchester), eliminating the need for a separate clock line.
4. **Error Detection Capability**: Schemes like 8B/10B encoding allow single-bit error detection.
5. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Improvement**: Proper encoding reduces susceptibility to channel noise.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold** parameters are board-favorite quantitative descriptors used in every KTU question paper:

- **Bit Rate ($R_b$)**: Number of bits transmitted per second — unit **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Baud Rate (Modulation Rate, $R_s$)**: Number of signal elements (symbols) per second — unit **baud**.
- **Bandwidth ($B$)**: Range of frequencies required for transmission — unit **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)**: Ratio of incorrectly received bits to total transmitted bits.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**: $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$, expressed in decibels as $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$.
- **Channel Capacity (Shannon-Hartley Theorem)**: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ **bits/second**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Signal Encoding

```
Signal Encoding
│
├── Digital → Digital (Line Coding)
│   ├── Unipolar (NRZ)
│   ├── Polar (NRZ-L, NRZ-I, RZ, Biphase: Manchester, Diff. Manchester)
│   ├── Bipolar (AMI, Pseudoternary)
│   └── Multi-level (2B1Q, 8B6T, 4D-PAM5, MLT-3)
│
├── Digital → Digital (Block Coding)
│   ├── 4B/5B, 5B/6B, 8B/10B
│
├── Analog → Digital
│   ├── Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)
│   └── Delta Modulation (DM)
│
├── Digital → Analog (Bandpass Modulation)
│   ├── Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)
│   ├── Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)
│   ├── Phase Shift Keying (PSK: BPSK, QPSK)
│   └── Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)
│
└── Analog → Analog (Bandpass Modulation)
    ├── Amplitude Modulation (AM)
    ├── Frequency Modulation (FM)
    └── Phase Modulation (PM)
```

## 2.2 Section A: Digital → Digital Encoding (Line Coding)

### 2.2.1 Unipolar NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero)

- **Bit 1** → positive voltage $V$
- **Bit 0** → zero voltage
- **Average Voltage**: $\dfrac{V}{2}$ (produces a **DC component** — disadvantage)
- **Bandwidth**: $B = \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ (theoretically minimum, but practically $B = R_b$)

### 2.2.2 Polar NRZ-L (Level)

- **Bit 1** → $+V$
- **Bit 0** → $-V$
- **DC Component**: Average voltage is **0 V** if equal number of 1s and 0s.

### 2.2.3 Polar NRZ-I (Inverted)

- **Bit 1** → transition at the start of the bit interval
- **Bit 0** → no transition
- **Advantage**: Inversion of polarity does NOT change meaning → **differential encoding is robust against polarity inversion.**

### 2.2.4 RZ (Return-to-Zero)

- **Bit 1** → transitions to $+V$ in first half, returns to 0 in second half
- **Bit 0** → remains at 0
- **Disadvantage**: Requires **2× bandwidth** of NRZ.

### 2.2.5 Manchester (Biphase-L)

- **Bit 1** → High-to-Low transition in middle of bit
- **Bit 0** → Low-to-High transition in middle of bit
- **Used in**: 10BASE-T Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)
- **Self-clocking** ✓ | **No DC component** ✓

### 2.2.6 Differential Manchester

- **Bit 1** → No transition at start of bit interval (always has mid-bit transition)
- **Bit 0** → Transition at start of bit interval
- **Used in**: Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)

### 2.2.7 Bipolar AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion)

- **Bit 0** → 0 V (no signal)
- **Bit 1** → Alternates between $+V$ and $-V$
- **DC Component**: **Zero** ✓
- **Disadvantage**: Long strings of 0s cause **baseline wandering**.

### 2.2.8 Pseudoternary

- **Inverse of AMI**: Bit 1 = 0 V; Bit 0 = alternates between $+V$ and $-V$.

### 2.2.9 B8ZS (Bipolar with 8-Zero Substitution)

- Substitutes any string of **8 consecutive zeros** with `000VB0VB` (where $V$ = violation, $B$ = bipolar).

### 2.2.10 HDB3 (High-Density Bipolar 3)

- Substitutes strings of **4 consecutive zeros** with `B00V` or `000V` patterns.

## 2.3 Section B: Analog → Digital Conversion

### 2.3.1 Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) — 4-Step Process

1. **Sampling** (Nyquist Theorem): $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{\max}$
2. **Quantization**: Map continuous amplitude to discrete levels
3. **Encoding**: Convert each quantized sample to binary codeword
4. **Line Coding**: Transmit using NRZ-L, etc.

### 2.3.2 Delta Modulation (DM)

- Transmits only **one bit per sample** — the *direction* of change (+1 or −1).

## 2.4 Section C: Digital → Analog (Bandpass Modulation)

| Scheme | Modulated Parameter | Constellation Points | Bit per Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ASK** | Amplitude | 2 | 1 |
| **BFSK** | Frequency | 2 | 1 |
| **BPSK** | Phase | 2 | 1 |
| **QPSK** | Phase | 4 | 2 |
| **16-QAM** | Amplitude + Phase | 16 | 4 |
| **64-QAM** | Amplitude + Phase | 64 | 6 |

**Carrier Signal Equation (universal form)**:

$$
s(t) = A(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t))
$$

- **ASK**: $A(t)$ varies, $f_c$ and $\phi$ constant.
- **FSK**: $f_c$ varies, $A$ and $\phi$ constant.
- **PSK**: $\phi(t)$ varies, $A$ and $f_c$ constant.

## 2.5 Section D: Analog → Analog Modulation

### 2.5.1 Amplitude Modulation (AM)

$$
s_{\text{AM}}(t) = [A_c + m(t)] \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

- Total transmitted power: $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \dfrac{m_a^2}{2}\right)$
- Modulation index: $m_a = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$, must satisfy $0 \leq m_a \leq 1$ for distortion-free AM.

### 2.5.2 Frequency Modulation (FM)

$$
s_{\text{FM}}(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)
$$

### 2.5.3 Phase Modulation (PM)

$$
s_{\text{PM}}(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + k_p \cdot m(t)\right)
$$

## 2.6 🧠 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation you must memorize for KTU board examinations.** No pipes (`|`) used inside cells to preserve markdown rendering — delimiters are written as `\vert` or `\mid`.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_b = N \cdot R_s$ | Bit rate = bits per symbol × baud rate | bps |
| 2 | $B_{\text{NRZ}} = R_b / 2$ | Min. bandwidth for NRZ (ideal) | Hz |
| 3 | $B_{\text{Manchester}} = R_b$ | Min. bandwidth for Manchester | Hz |
| 4 | $B_{\text{MLT-3}} = R_b / 4$ | Min. bandwidth for MLT-3 | Hz |
| 5 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | Hz |
| 6 | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | Nyquist Channel Capacity (noiseless) | bps |
| 7 | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon-Hartley Theorem (noisy) | bps |
| 8 | $n = \log_2(M)$ | Bits per symbol (M = signal levels) | bits |
| 9 | $\text{BER}_{\text{BPSK}} = Q\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | BPSK Bit Error Rate | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | SNR in decibels | dB |
| 11 | $m_a = A_m / A_c$ | AM Modulation Index | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Throughput}_{\text{4B/5B}} = 0.8 R_b$ | Efficiency due to 5-bit encoding | bps |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Manchester Encoding** → 10BASE-T Ethernet (legacy), RFID systems
- **NRZ-I** → USB (Universal Serial Bus) data lines
- **B8ZS/HDB3** → T1/E1 leased telephone lines (North America / Europe)
- **MLT-3** → 100BASE-TX Fast Ethernet
- **8B/10B** → Gigabit Ethernet, Fibre Channel, SATA, PCIe, HDMI
- **QAM-256** → Wi-Fi 802.11ac, digital cable TV (DOCSIS 3.1), 4G LTE
- **FM** → VHF radio broadcast (88–108 MHz), two-way police radio
- **PCM (64 kbps)** → Digital telephony (G.711 standard)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I: Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel)

### Statement

The maximum data rate of a **noiseless channel** of bandwidth $B$ Hz with $M$ discrete signal levels is:

$$
C = 2B \log_2(M)
$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Define number of signal levels per symbol.**  
A signal with $M$ discrete levels carries $n$ bits per symbol where:

$$
n = \log_2(M)
$$

**Step 2 — Determine maximum signaling rate.**  
According to **Harry Nyquist's theorem (1928)**, the maximum number of independent symbols that can be transmitted per second through a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz is:

$$
R_s^{\max} = 2B \quad [\text{baud}]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute bit rate.**  
The bit rate is the product of the number of bits per symbol and the symbol rate:

$$
R_b = n \cdot R_s^{\max}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute both equations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= \log_2(M) \cdot 2B \\
\therefore \quad C &= 2B \log_2(M)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical Interpretation**: Doubling the bandwidth doubles the bit rate. Doubling the signal levels adds only $\log_2(M)$ bits per symbol — so increasing levels is more power-efficient than increasing bandwidth.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel)

### Statement

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad [\text{bits/second}]
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the channel's distinguishable signal levels.**  
In a noisy channel with SNR, the number of reliably distinguishable amplitude levels is bounded by:

$$
M \leq \sqrt{1 + \text{SNR}}
$$

*(This is empirical / derived from information theory: the noise power splits the signal space into $\sqrt{1+\text{SNR}}$ distinguishable bins per dimension.)*

**Step 2 — Apply Nyquist formula with this effective $M$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2B \log_2(M) \\
&\leq 2B \log_2\left(\sqrt{1 + \text{SNR}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using logarithm identity $\log_a(x^k) = k \log_a(x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &\leq 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
\therefore \quad C &= B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight**: Even infinite bandwidth cannot yield infinite capacity — there is an asymptotic ceiling as $B \to \infty$ of $C \to 1.44 \cdot \text{SNR}$ bps.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Total AM Transmitted Power

### Given

Carrier: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ → Carrier Power $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ (over $1\,\Omega$).  
Message: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ → Modulation Index $m_a = A_m / A_c$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Write AM signal.**

$$
s_{\text{AM}}(t) = A_c[1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 2 — Expand using product-to-sum identity** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{AM}}(t) &= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \\
&\quad + \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] \\
&\quad + \frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute power of each component** (power of $A\cos(2\pi f t) = A^2 / 2$ over $1\,\Omega$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{carrier}} &= \frac{A_c^2}{2} \\
P_{\text{USB}} = P_{\text{LSB}} &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{A_c m_a}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{A_c^2 m_a^2}{8} \\
P_{\text{total}} &= P_c + 2 \cdot \frac{A_c^2 m_a^2}{8} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{A_c^2 m_a^2}{4} \\
\therefore \quad P_t &= P_c \left(1 + \frac{m_a^2}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: BPSK Bit Error Rate

### Setup

- Two antipodal signals: $s_0(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ and $s_1(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$.
- Noise: AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

### Probability of Error

The decision threshold is 0. The receiver computes the correlation and decides. The probability of error is:

$$
P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

where $Q(x)$ is the **Q-function**:

$$
Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2}\, dt
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Question**: Compute BER for BPSK when $E_b/N_0 = 10\,\text{dB}$.

**Step 1 — Convert dB to linear.**

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0}\bigg|_{\text{linear}} = 10^{10/10} = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $P_e$ formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e &= Q\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot 10}\right) \\
&= Q\left(\sqrt{20}\right) \\
&= Q(4.4721)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Look up Q-table** $Q(4.47) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Answer**: $\text{BER}_{\text{BPSK}} \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}$ (excellent — extremely reliable).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Line Coding Schemes

| Scheme | Bandwidth | DC Component | Self-Clocking | Polarity-Inv Robust | Error Detection | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NRZ-L | $R_b/2$ | High | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | RS-232 |
| NRZ-I | $R_b/2$ | Low | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | USB |
| RZ | $R_b$ | Low | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Old telemetry |
| Manchester | $R_b$ | None | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | 10BASE-T |
| Diff. Manchester | $R_b$ | None | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Token Ring |
| AMI | $R_b/2$ | None | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | T1 |
| B8ZS | $R_b/2$ | None | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ (limited) | T1 |
| HDB3 | $R_b/2$ | None | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ (limited) | E1 |
| MLT-3 | $R_b/4$ | None | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | 100BASE-TX |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Universal Signal Encoding Classification Tree

```mermaid

    A0[Input Source] --> B0{Type of Source?}
    B0 -->|Digital Data| C0[Digital Domain]
    B0 -->|Analog Signal| D0[Analog Domain]

    C2 --> C5[Biphase: Manchester, Diff. Manchester]
    C2 --> C6[Bipolar: AMI, Pseudoternary]
    C2 --> C7[Multi-Level: 2B1Q, MLT-3, 8B6T]

    C0 --> C8{Need Bandpass?}

```

```mermaid
    B1 --> C1[Sampler at fs ≥ 2fmax]

    E1 --> F1[Line Coder NRZ-L]
    F1 --> G1[Channel]

    G1 --> H1[Line Decoder]
    H1 --> I1[Reconstruction Filter]
    I1 --> J1[Analog Output m_hat of t]

    style J1 fill:#90EE90
```

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 📌 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between NRZ-L and NRZ-I encoding schemes. Which is more robust against polarity inversion?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | NRZ-L | NRZ-I |
|---|---|---|
| Encoding Rule | Voltage level represents bit | Transition represents bit 1 |
| Bit 1 | $+V$ | Transition at start of bit |
| Bit 0 | $-V$ | No transition |
| Polarity Inversion Robustness | ✗ (inverted signal reverses meaning) | ✓ (inverted signal still produces correct transition pattern) |

**NRZ-I is more robust** against polarity inversion because it relies on *transitions* rather than absolute voltage levels. Inverting the entire signal produces the same transition pattern, so the receiver can decode the data correctly.

*(Word limit: ~50 words, 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. Why is oversampling used in practice?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**: An analog signal with maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$ must be sampled at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ to allow perfect reconstruction from its samples.

> **Why Oversampling in Practice**:
> 1. Practical anti-aliasing filters are not ideal — they require a transition band.
> 2. Higher sampling rates relax filter design requirements.
> 3. Reduces quantization noise and improves SNR.
> 4. Provides tolerance to timing jitter.

*(Word limit: ~60 words, 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 📌 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the four digital-to-digital encoding schemes: NRZ-L, NRZ-I, Manchester, and Differential Manchester with suitable waveform diagrams. Compare their bandwidth requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB. Calculate:  
   (i) Maximum theoretical data rate using Shannon's theorem.  
   (ii) Maximum data rate using Nyquist's theorem if the signal uses 16 levels. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**1. NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level)**
- Bit 1 = $+V$, Bit 0 = $-V$.
- *Disadvantage*: Has DC component, baseline wandering, no self-clocking.

**2. NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted)**
- Bit 1 → transition at start of bit interval.
- Bit 0 → no transition.
- *Advantage*: Polarity-inversion robust.

**3. Manchester (Biphase-L)**
- Bit 1 → High-to-Low transition in middle of bit period.
- Bit 0 → Low-to-High transition in middle of bit period.
- *Advantage*: Self-clocking, no DC component.
- *Used in*: 10BASE-T Ethernet.

**4. Differential Manchester**
- Always has a transition in the middle of each bit (mandatory mid-bit transition).
- Bit 0 → transition at start of bit period.
- Bit 1 → no transition at start of bit period.
- *Used in*: Token Ring (IEEE 802.5).

**Waveform for bit stream `1 0 1 0 0 1 1`:**

```
Bit:       1     0     1     0     0     1     1
NRZ-L:    +V    -V    +V    -V    -V    +V    +V
NRZ-I:    ↑      ·     ↑     ·     ·     ↑     ↑
Manchester:↓↑    ↑↓    ↓↑    ↑↓    ↑↓    ↓↑    ↓↑
Diff.Manch:↓↑    ↓↑    ↓↑    ↓↑    ↓↑    ↓↑    ↓↑
```

**Bandwidth Comparison Table:**

| Scheme | Minimum Bandwidth |
|---|---|
| NRZ-L / NRZ-I | $B = R_b / 2$ |
| Manchester / Diff. Manchester | $B = R_b$ |
| RZ | $B = R_b$ |
| MLT-3 | $B = R_b / 4$ |

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $B = 4\,\text{kHz} = 4000\,\text{Hz}$
- $\text{SNR} = 30\,\text{dB}$

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

**(i) Shannon Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\
&= 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
&= 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001) \\
&= 4000 \cdot 9.967 \\
&\approx 39{,}870\,\text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist Capacity with $M = 16$ levels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2B \log_2(M) \\
&= 2 \cdot 4000 \cdot \log_2(16) \\
&= 8000 \cdot 4 \\
&= 32{,}000\,\text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Shannon limit: **≈ 39.87 kbps**
- Nyquist limit (16-level): **32 kbps**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: A common mistake is using $B \log_2(\text{SNR})$ instead of $B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$. The "+1" is mathematically mandatory because zero noise still allows one distinguishable level. Students frequently lose **1 mark** here.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain Pulse Code Modulation (PCM). Derive the relationship between the number of quantization levels and the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An analog signal with maximum frequency 4 kHz is sampled at 8 kHz and quantized using 256 levels. Calculate:  
   (i) The bit rate of the PCM system.  
   (ii) The transmission bandwidth if NRZ-L line coding is used.  
   (iii) The SQNR in dB assuming uniform quantization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** converts analog signals to digital through 3 stages:

1. **Sampling** at $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$
2. **Quantization** — assigns discrete amplitude levels
3. **Encoding** — each quantized level → binary codeword

**Derivation of SQNR:**

For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$ over a range $-V_{\max}$ to $+V_{\max}$:

- Number of levels: $L = \dfrac{2 V_{\max}}{\Delta}$
- Quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
- Quantization noise power:

$$
P_q = \overline{e_q^2} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

- Signal power (assuming full-scale sinusoid):

$$
P_s = \frac{V_{\max}^2}{2}
$$

- SQNR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{V_{\max}^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12} \\
&= \frac{V_{\max}^2}{2} \cdot \frac{12}{\Delta^2} \\
&= \frac{3 V_{\max}^2}{\Delta^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $L = 2 V_{\max} / \Delta$, hence $V_{\max} = L \Delta / 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= \frac{3 \cdot (L\Delta/2)^2}{\Delta^2} = \frac{3 L^2}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $L = 2^n$ (n-bit quantization):

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{4} = 1.5 \cdot 4^n
$$

In decibels:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + n \cdot 10 \log_{10}(4) \approx 1.76 + 6.02\,n\,\text{dB}
$$

> **Rule of thumb**: Each additional bit of quantization improves SQNR by **≈ 6 dB**.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $f_{\max} = 4\,\text{kHz}$
- $f_s = 8\,\text{kHz}$
- $L = 256$ levels

**(i) Bit Rate:**

Bits per sample: $n = \log_2(256) = 8$ bits.

$$
R_b = f_s \cdot n = 8000 \cdot 8 = 64{,}000\,\text{bps} = 64\,\text{kbps}
$$

**(ii) Bandwidth (NRZ-L):**

$$
B = R_b / 2 = 32{,}000\,\text{Hz} = 32\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(iii) SQNR in dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} &\approx 1.76 + 6.02 \cdot n \\
&= 1.76 + 6.02 \cdot 8 \\
&= 1.76 + 48.16 \\
&= 49.92\,\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Bit rate: **64 kbps** (this is exactly the G.711 telephony standard!)
- Bandwidth: **32 kHz**
- SQNR: **≈ 49.92 dB**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Students often confuse the formulas $B = R_b/2$ (NRZ-L) with $B = R_b$ (Manchester). The bandwidth equation depends on the **specific line coding scheme used**, not on PCM itself. Verify the encoding scheme mentioned in the question. Marks lost here: typically **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these points before entering the KTU examination hall.**

- ✅ The **four encoding classes** are: Digital→Digital, Analog→Digital, Digital→Analog, Analog→Analog.
- ✅ **Manchester encoding** has a mandatory mid-bit transition → self-clocking, no DC component.
- ✅ **NRZ-I is polarity-inversion robust**; NRZ-L is NOT.
- ✅ **AMI** has zero DC component but suffers from baseline wandering on long 0 strings.
- ✅ **B8ZS** replaces 8 consecutive zeros; **HDB3** replaces 4 consecutive zeros.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem** (noiseless): $C = 2B \log_2(M)$.
- ✅ **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** (noisy): $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- ✅ **PCM bit rate** = sampling frequency × bits per sample.
- ✅ **SQNR improvement** ≈ 6 dB per additional quantization bit.
- ✅ **AM total power**: $P_t = P_c (1 + m_a^2 / 2)$.
- ✅ **BPSK** is the most noise-resistant digital modulation but uses only 1 bit/symbol.
- ✅ **QAM** combines amplitude and phase shifts for high spectral efficiency.
- ✅ **MLT-3** reduces bandwidth to $R_b/4$ — used in 100BASE-TX Fast Ethernet.
- ✅ **Block coding (4B/5B)** efficiency = 80% (4 data bits in 5 transmitted bits).
- ✅ Always convert SNR **dB → linear** using $10^{x/10}$ before applying Shannon's formula.
- ✅ For $L = 2^n$ quantization levels, SQNR $ \approx 1.76 + 6.02n $ dB.
- ✅ **Bandwidth comparison**: NRZ ($R_b/2$) < RZ/Manchester ($R_b$) < MLT-3 (most efficient at $R_b/4$).

---

**END OF MODULE 1 — SIGNAL ENCODING NOTES**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme Aligned | Board-Exam Optimized*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Indicators - Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Indicators - Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Computer Networks**, **Bandwidth** is formally defined as the maximum rate at which data can be transmitted over a communication channel within a specified unit of time. It represents the *capacity* of the link and is measured in **bits per second (bps)**, with practical multiples being **kbps**, **Mbps**, **Gbps**, and **Tbps**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Forouzan & Tanenbaum Terminology):**
> *"Bandwidth is the width of the frequency spectrum transmitted (measured in Hertz — analog context) OR the maximum throughput of a digital link (measured in bits per second — digital context)."*

### Two Interpretations of Bandwidth

| Interpretation | Domain | Unit | Meaning |
|----------------|--------|------|---------|
| Analog Bandwidth | Signal Processing / Physics | **Hertz (Hz)** | Width of the frequency band that a channel can pass without significant attenuation |
| Digital Bandwidth | Computer Networks | **bits per second (bps)** | Maximum number of bits that can be pushed through the link per second |

For the KTU PERUT758 syllabus (Computer Networks), we primarily operate in the **digital bandwidth** interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bandwidth ≠ Speed.** Bandwidth is the *width of the highway* (how many cars per second can flow), not the *speed limit* (how fast each car travels). The speed limit in networking is governed by **propagation speed** (limited by the speed of light in the medium).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Pipe Model

Imagine sending water from a reservoir (source) to your house (destination):

- The **diameter of the pipe** = **Bandwidth**. A wider pipe allows more water (more bits) to flow per second.
- The **length of the pipe** = **Propagation Distance**. Longer pipe = more time for water to travel.
- The **actual water that reaches your tap** = **Throughput**. This may be less than the pipe's maximum capacity due to friction, leaks, or bends (analogous to protocol overhead, congestion, and errors).
- The **pressure of water** = **Signal strength (S/N ratio)**.

So when your ISP promises "100 Mbps broadband," they are promising a **pipe of 100 megabits per second of capacity**, not a guarantee that 100 megabits will *always* reach your device.

## 1.3 Bandwidth in Different Layers of the TCP/IP Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer-wise Bandwidth Behavior (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
> - **Physical Layer:** Bandwidth is determined by the transmission medium (copper, fiber, RF spectrum).
> - **Data Link Layer:** Bandwidth is shared among users via MAC protocols (e.g., Ethernet, Wi-Fi CSMA/CA).
> - **Network/Transport Layer:** **Effective bandwidth** perceived by the application is reduced by overhead (headers, retransmissions, ACKs).

## 1.4 Standard Bandwidth Units (Memorize!)

$$1 \text{ kbps} = 10^3 \text{ bps}$$

$$1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bps}$$

$$1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps}$$

$$1 \text{ Tbps} = 10^{12} \text{ bps}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Performance Indicators Linked to Bandwidth

Bandwidth does not exist in isolation. In KTU's PERUT758 syllabus, it interacts with three other key indicators:

### (a) Latency (Delay)
The time taken for a single bit to travel from source to destination. Composed of four components:

$$\text{Total Latency} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$$

### (b) Throughput
The **actual** number of useful bits delivered per second. Always ≤ Bandwidth.

$$\text{Throughput} \leq \text{Bandwidth}$$

### (c) Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)
The volume of data that can "fill the pipe" — i.e., the maximum amount of data that can be in transit at any instant.

$$\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{Round-Trip Time (RTT)}$$

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Theorems of Channel Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two theorems are **frequently asked** in KTU exams as 7-mark derivations and 14-mark numerical problems.

### Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless Channel)

For a **noiseless** channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) using $M$ discrete signal levels:

$$C = 2B \cdot \log_2(M) \text{ bps}$$

- Doubling the bandwidth doubles the capacity.
- Increasing signal levels logarithmically increases capacity.

### Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel)

For a **noisy** channel with bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{S/N}) \text{ bps}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Shannon's formula uses $S/N$ as a **ratio (unitless number)**, NOT in decibels. If given in dB, convert: $\text{SNR}_{\text{ratio}} = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains every formula you need for KTU Module 1 Bandwidth problems. **No pipes inside math expressions** — using `\vert` for absolute value.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{D}{S}$ | Propagation delay = Distance $\div$ Propagation Speed | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay = Packet Length $\div$ Bandwidth | seconds |
| 3 | $\text{RTT} = 2 \cdot T_{\text{prop}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{trans}}$ | Round-Trip Time (ideal, no queuing) | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{BDP} = \text{BW} \times \text{RTT}$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product (bits in flight) | bits |
| 5 | $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \cdot \log_2(M)$ | Nyquist Channel Capacity (noiseless) | bps |
| 6 | $C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{S/N})$ | Shannon Channel Capacity (noisy) | bps |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{ratio}} = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$ | Convert dB to linear ratio | unitless |
| 8 | $\text{Utilization}_{\text{link}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{prop}}}{T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}}}$ | Fraction of time the link is useful | $\vert \cdot \vert \leq 1$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISP Service Tiers:** Bandwidth forms the SLA (Service Level Agreement) parameter (e.g., "100 Mbps plan").
- **Network Capacity Planning:** Engineers use BDP to size router buffers and TCP window sizes (a famous example is the BDP of undersea fiber cables, which can exceed megabytes).
- **Wireless Design:** Shannon's theorem caps 5G/6G theoretical throughput and drives spectrum allocation policy.
- **Storage & Backbone:** Data center interconnects are dimensioned via these formulas to avoid bottlenecks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transmission Delay from First Principles

Consider a link of bandwidth $R$ bits per second. A packet of $L$ bits must have all its bits "pushed onto the wire" before the last bit finishes entering the wire.

**Step 1 — Define the rate of bit injection:**

In one second, $R$ bits leave the source. Therefore, the time per bit is:

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{R} \text{ seconds/bit}$$

**Step 2 — Total bits to transmit:**

The packet has $L$ bits.

**Step 3 — Total transmission time:**

Each bit takes $\Delta t$ to leave the source, so all $L$ bits take:

$$T_{\text{trans}} = L \cdot \Delta t = \frac{L}{R}$$

This is the **fundamental transmission delay formula** used in every KTU numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

Consider a one-way propagation delay $T_{\text{prop}}$ and bandwidth $R$.

**Step 1 — Number of bits that "fit" in the link at any instant:**

A bit enters the source every $\frac{1}{R}$ seconds. The first bit will take $T_{\text{prop}}$ seconds to reach the destination. During this time, the source has injected:

$$N = R \cdot T_{\text{prop}} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — This is the BDP for one-way latency:**

$$\text{BDP}_{\text{one-way}} = R \cdot T_{\text{prop}}$$

**Step 3 — For full-duplex with acknowledgment:**

If we include the return path:

$$\text{BDP}_{\text{RTT}} = R \cdot \text{RTT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If TCP's send window is smaller than the BDP, the sender must stop and wait for ACKs — underutilizing the link. This is why modern TCP uses window scaling (RFC 7323) for high-bandwidth long-distance paths.

## 3.3 Derivation: Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Conceptual)

While Shannon's theorem is information-theoretic and not "derived" in an algebraic sense at the B.Tech level, we present its logical derivation chain:

**Step 1 — Define channel capacity $C$** as the maximum reliable data rate (bps) over a band-limited noisy channel.

**Step 2 — Apply the Hartley-Shannon Law:**

The amount of distinguishable signal levels in a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is roughly $M \approx \sqrt{1 + S/N}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Nyquist's rate expression:**

Using $C = 2B \cdot \log_2(M)$:

$$C = 2B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \text{S/N}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply log identity $\log_2(\sqrt{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(x)$:**

$$C = 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + \text{S/N})$$

$$\boxed{C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{S/N})}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A link has bandwidth $B = 4 \text{ kHz}$ and SNR = $30 \text{ dB}$. A packet of $L = 8000 \text{ bits}$ is sent over a distance $D = 2000 \text{ km}$. Propagation speed $S = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$. Find: (a) Shannon capacity, (b) Transmission delay, (c) Propagation delay, (d) BDP.

### (a) Shannon Capacity

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear ratio:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(30/10)} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula:**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{S/N})$$

$$C = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)$$

$$C = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001)$$

$$C \approx 4000 \cdot 9.967 \approx 39868 \text{ bps}$$

### (b) Transmission Delay

$$T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8000}{39868} \approx 0.2007 \text{ s}$$

### (c) Propagation Delay

$$T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{D}{S} = \frac{2000 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s}$$

### (d) Bandwidth-Delay Product

$$\text{BDP} = R \cdot T_{\text{prop}} = 39868 \times 0.01 \approx 398.7 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Only ~399 bits are "in the pipe" at any moment — a tiny amount. This shows why for *low bandwidth* links, throughput is dominated by $T_{\text{prop}}$ rather than $T_{\text{trans}}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: How Bandwidth Interacts with Other Performance Metrics

The following diagram shows how Bandwidth propagates through the layered architecture and how it is influenced by latency components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer Demand] --> B[Transport Layer Window Size]
    B --> C[Effective Application Bandwidth]
    C --> D[Network Layer Routing Overhead]
    D --> E[Data Link Layer MAC Throughput]
    E --> F[Physical Layer Raw Bandwidth]
    F --> G[Medium Copper Fiber Wireless]

    H[Propagation Delay D over S] --> I[Total End-to-End Latency]
    J[Transmission Delay L over R] --> I
    K[Queuing Delay] --> I
    L[Processing Delay] --> I

    F --> M[Bandwidth Delay Product BDP]
    I --> M
    M --> N[Router Buffer Sizing Decision]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Latency Composition (KTU Favourite Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Host] --> B[Processing Delay]
    B --> C[Queuing Delay]
    C --> D[Transmission Delay]
    D --> E[Propagation Delay]
    E --> F[Destination Host]

    B1[Header inspection Checksum] -.-> B
    C1[Router buffer wait] -.-> C
    D1[L over R bits on wire] -.-> D
    E1[D over S distance by speed] -.-> E

    style A fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Bandwidth vs Throughput

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Theoretical[Theoretical Maximum]
        BW[Bandwidth R bps]
    end

    subgraph Actual[Actual Delivery]
        TH[Throughput less than R]
    end

    BW --> X[Protocol Overhead]
    X --> TH
    BW --> Y[Retransmissions]
    Y --> TH
    BW --> Z[Congestion]
    Z --> TH
    BW --> W[Errors and Collisions]
    W --> TH

    style BW fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px
    style TH fill:#f1948a,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Shannon vs Nyquist Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Channel Problem] --> B{Is Channel Noisy?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Nyquist Theorem]
    B -- Yes --> D[Use Shannon Hartley Theorem]
    C --> E[C equals 2B log2 M]
    D --> F[C equals B log2 1 plus S/N]
    E --> G[Capacity in bps]
    F --> G

    style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define bandwidth. Differentiate between analog bandwidth and digital bandwidth.**

**Model Answer:**

**Bandwidth** is the maximum rate at which data can be transmitted through a communication channel in a given unit of time.

- **Analog Bandwidth** refers to the width of the range of frequencies that a channel can transmit without significant loss. It is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. Example: A voice-grade telephone line supports frequencies from 300 Hz to 3400 Hz → analog bandwidth = 3100 Hz.

- **Digital Bandwidth** refers to the maximum number of bits that can be transmitted per second over a digital link. It is measured in **bits per second (bps)**. Example: An Ethernet link has a digital bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** In PERUT758, the default meaning is **digital bandwidth** unless the question specifies analog signal processing.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the significance of Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP).**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** is the product of the link's bandwidth and the round-trip time (or one-way delay). It represents the **maximum number of bits that can be in transit** in the network at any instant — i.e., the volume of data that "fills the pipe."

**Significance:**
1. Determines the **minimum TCP window size** required to fully utilize the link. If the window is smaller than BDP, the sender must idle waiting for ACKs → underutilization.
2. Determines the **buffer size** required at routers to avoid packet loss due to bursts.
3. Critical in designing **high-speed long-distance networks** (e.g., undersea fiber-optic cables where BDP can exceed several megabytes).

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.A.** Explain in detail the different types of delays encountered in a packet-switched network. A link has a bandwidth of **1 Mbps**, distance **5000 km**, and packet size **2000 bits**. The propagation speed is **$2 \times 10^8$ m/s**. Calculate:
- (a) (7 marks) Propagation delay, transmission delay, and total nodal delay (neglecting queuing/processing).
- (b) (7 marks) Bandwidth-Delay Product and interpret its significance.

#### **Solution:**

**(a) Delays:**

**Step 1 — Propagation delay:**

$$T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{D}{S} = \frac{5000 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.025 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Transmission delay:**

$$T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2000}{10^6} = 0.002 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Total nodal delay (one-way):**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} = 0.025 + 0.002 = 0.027 \text{ s}$$

**(b) Bandwidth-Delay Product:**

$$\text{BDP} = R \cdot T_{\text{prop}} = 10^6 \times 0.025 = 25{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** At any instant, **25,000 bits ≈ 3.125 KB** are "in flight" between the two ends. For full link utilization, the sender's window must be ≥ 25,000 bits.

---

### ✅ **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B.** State and explain the **Nyquist Theorem** and **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** for channel capacity. Consider a channel of bandwidth **4 kHz** and SNR of **30 dB**.
- (a) (7 marks) Determine the maximum data rate using Shannon's theorem and Nyquist's theorem (assuming M = 4 signal levels).
- (b) (7 marks) If the SNR drops to 20 dB, recalculate Shannon capacity and explain the impact on link performance.

#### **Solution:**

**(a) SNR = 30 dB:**

**Step 1 — Convert 30 dB to linear ratio:**

$$\text{SNR} = 10^{(30/10)} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Shannon capacity:**

$$C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001) \approx 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39{,}868 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Nyquist capacity (M = 4):**

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \cdot \log_2(M) = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(4) = 8000 \times 2 = 16{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
The Shannon capacity (~39.87 kbps) is the **theoretical upper bound**. Nyquist (16 kbps) is achieved only because we use just $M = 4$ levels. To reach Shannon's limit, $M$ would have to be enormous — practically limited by noise.

**(b) SNR = 20 dB:**

**Step 1 — Convert SNR:**

$$\text{SNR} = 10^{(20/10)} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Recompute:**

$$C_{\text{Shannon}} = 4000 \cdot \log_2(101) \approx 4000 \times 6.658 \approx 26{,}632 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Impact Analysis:**
Capacity drops by ~33% (from 39.87 kbps to 26.63 kbps) due to 10 dB SNR degradation. This illustrates that **SNR has a logarithmic effect on capacity** — to double capacity, we need a much larger jump in signal power.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Per KTU Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Using $S/N$ in dB directly inside Shannon's formula.** ❌ Always convert to linear ratio using $10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$.
> 2. **Confusing $B$ (Hz) with $B$ (bps).** When using Nyquist/Shannon, $B$ is **frequency bandwidth in Hz**, NOT digital bandwidth. State clearly.
> 3. **Forgetting units in BDP.** Result must be in **bits** (or convert to bytes by dividing by 8).
> 4. **Mixing one-way delay and RTT.** State explicitly which one is being used in the BDP formula.
> 5. **Writing `log` instead of `log₂`** — KTU expects $\log_2$ (binary logarithm) for Nyquist/Shannon formulas.
> 6. **Bandwidth vs Throughput confusion.** Examiners specifically test this; throughput is **measured**, bandwidth is **theoretical**.

## 5.4 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Bandwidth = Capacity of the link** (max bits/sec). NOT speed.
> - **Throughput ≤ Bandwidth** — throughput is actual delivery; bandwidth is theoretical ceiling.
> - **Four delays:** Propagation ($D/S$), Transmission ($L/R$), Queuing, Processing.
> - **Nyquist:** Noiseless channels → $C = 2B \log_2 M$.
> - **Shannon:** Noisy channels → $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
> - **BDP** tells you how many bits are "in flight" — crucial for TCP window sizing and router buffer design.
> - **Always convert SNR from dB to linear** before plugging into Shannon's formula.
> - **Units:** $1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps. **B in Nyquist/Shannon is in Hz, not bps.**
> - **Higher bandwidth + long distance = huge BDP** → need large TCP windows.
> - **Shannon's capacity is the absolute hard limit** — no modulation scheme can exceed it.
> - **Engineering rule of thumb:** Practical throughput ≈ 80–95% of nominal bandwidth due to overhead.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Throughput
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Throughput # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Throughput** in a computer network is defined as the **rate (in bits/second) at which data is successfully delivered from a source to a destination over a communication channel within a given time interval**. It represents the *effective* or *useful* data transfer rate, after accounting for protocol overhead, retransmissions, queuing delays, and channel errors. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Amount of data successfully delivered (bits)}}{\text{Total time taken (seconds)}}$$ The standard unit is **bits per second (bps)**, commonly expressed as **Kbps, Mbps, or Gbps**. ## 1.2 Throughput vs. Bandwidth — A Critical Distinction | Parameter | Bandwidth | Throughput | |-----------|-----------|------------| | Definition | Maximum *theoretical* capacity of a link | *Actual* data delivered per unit time | | Nature | Static, fixed by the medium | Dynamic, depends on traffic & errors | | Analogy | Width of a pipe (cm) | Water actually flowing (litres/sec) | | Unit | bps | bps | > [!NOTE] > **Throughput ≤ Bandwidth** always holds true. Bandwidth is the *upper limit*; throughput is the *achieved* rate. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Viewpoint Imagine a **6-lane highway** (bandwidth = 6 lanes). Even though 6 lanes exist, during rush hour only **2 lanes of cars are actually moving at full speed** due to traffic jams, accidents, and toll booths. The **actual number of cars passing a point per hour** is the **throughput**, while the **maximum possible capacity** is the **bandwidth**. In networking terms: - **Toll booths** = Protocol overhead (ACKs, headers) - **Accidents** = Packet loss and retransmissions - **Rush hour traffic** = Network congestion ## 1.4 Bottleneck Link — The Key Limiting Factor In any end-to-end path consisting of multiple links, the **slowest link determines the overall throughput**. This slowest link is called the **bottleneck link**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students are frequently tested on identifying the bottleneck in a multi-hop path and computing end-to-end throughput accordingly. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Throughput is Determined The throughput along a multi-hop path is governed by the following structured logic: 1. **Identify all links** in the end-to-end path from source to destination. 2. **Determine the transmission rate** (R) of each individual link in bps. 3. **Locate the bottleneck** — the link with the *minimum* transmission rate. 4. **Compute effective throughput** by accounting for: - Transmission delay - Propagation delay - Queuing & processing delay - Protocol overhead (headers, ACKs) 5. **Apply the formula** for end-to-end throughput (bottleneck-bound). ## 2.2 Key Formulas — KTU Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains the **most frequently tested formulas** for throughput in KTU exams. Memorize these thoroughly. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | End-to-end Throughput (multi-hop) | $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n)$ | $R_i$ = rate of link $i$ in **bps** | | 2 | Instantaneous Throughput | $T = \dfrac{L}{t_d}$ | $L$ = data size (**bits**), $t_d$ = delivery time (**sec**) | | 3 | Average Throughput | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{\text{Total bits received}}{\text{Total time elapsed}}$ | Both in standard units | | 4 | Per-connection Throughput (store-and-forward) | $T \approx \dfrac{L}{n \cdot (L/R)}$ | $n$ = number of links, $L$ = packet size, $R$ = link rate | | 5 | Simplified Per-connection Throughput (large files) | $T \approx \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Reduces to single-link transmission rate | | 6 | Throughput with Overhead | $T = \dfrac{L \cdot (1 - f_{overhead})}{t_{total}}$ | $f_{overhead}$ = fractional overhead | | 7 | Delay-Bandwidth Product | $\text{DBP} = d_{prop} \times R$ | $d_{prop}$ in **seconds**, $R$ in **bps** | | 8 | Throughput over shared link (N users) | $T = \dfrac{R}{N}$ | $R$ = link rate, $N$ = number of competing users | ## 2.3 Why Does Bottleneck Dominate? > [!NOTE] > In a series of links, data must traverse *every* link sequentially. Even if one link is extremely fast (say 10 Gbps), the overall delivery cannot exceed the rate of the **slowest link** in the chain. This is analogous to water flowing through pipes of different diameters in series — the narrowest pipe limits the overall flow. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Network capacity planning:** ISPs use throughput measurements to provision backbone links. - **QoS (Quality of Service):** Throughput is a primary metric in Service Level Agreements (SLAs). - **Data center design:** Engineers identify bottlenecks to optimize east-west traffic. - **Wireless networks (Wi-Fi, 5G):** Throughput determines cell capacity and user experience. - **Content Delivery Networks (CDNs):** Throughput between origin and edge servers directly impacts content delivery speed. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Throughput Over a Multi-Hop Path ### Scenario Setup Consider a file of size **F bits** being transmitted across **n links** in series, where link $i$ has a transmission rate of $R_i$ bps. We assume negligible propagation delay and no packet loss for this simplified analysis. ### Derivation Steps **Step 1:** The time to transmit the file over the *first* link is: $$t_1 = \frac{F}{R_1}$$ **Step 2:** The time to transmit the same file over the *second* link is: $$t_2 = \frac{F}{R_2}$$ **Step 3:** In store-and-forward switching, the file is fully received at each intermediate router before being forwarded. Therefore, the total time to traverse **n** links (ignoring propagation) is: $$t_{total} = \frac{F}{R_1} + \frac{F}{R_2} + \frac{F}{R_3} + \ldots + \frac{F}{R_n}$$ **Step 4:** Express this as a summation: $$t_{total} = F \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_i}$$ **Step 5:** By definition, average throughput is: $$T = \frac{F}{t_{total}} = \frac{F}{F \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_i}}$$ **Step 6:** Simplify by canceling **F** from numerator and denominator: $$T = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_i}}$$ **Step 7:** This is the **harmonic mean** of the link rates. The harmonic mean is always bounded above by the **minimum** value, giving us the key result: $$T = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The end-to-end throughput is determined by the **slowest link** in the path. This is the bottleneck theorem for throughput. ## 3.2 Derivation: Per-Connection Throughput in a Packet-Switched Network ### Setup A large file of size **F** is broken into **P packets**, each of size **L bits**. The path has **n links**, each with rate **R bps**. We assume equal rates on all links for simplicity. ### Derivation **Step 1:** Number of packets is: $$P = \frac{F}{L}$$ **Step 2:** Time to transmit one packet over one link (transmission delay): $$d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}$$ **Step 3:** In store-and-forward across **n** links, the *first* packet takes time $n \cdot d_{trans}$ to arrive at the destination. **Step 4:** The *last* packet is sent at time $(P-1) \cdot d_{trans}$ after the first packet from the source. **Step 5:** The last packet then takes $n \cdot d_{trans}$ to traverse all links. **Step 6:** Total delivery time: $$t_{total} = n \cdot \frac{L}{R} + (P-1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}$$ $$t_{total} = \left(n + P - 1\right) \cdot \frac{L}{R}$$ **Step 7:** Throughput: $$T = \frac{F}{t_{total}} = \frac{P \cdot L}{\left(n + P - 1\right) \cdot \frac{L}{R}} = \frac{P \cdot R}{n + P - 1}$$ **Step 8:** As **P → ∞** (very large files, many packets), the "+n−1" becomes negligible: $$T \approx \frac{P \cdot R}{P} = R$$ **Step 9:** For **P = 1** (a single packet): $$T = \frac{R}{n}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows that for a single small packet, throughput is **reduced by a factor of n**, but for large bulk transfers, throughput approaches the **bottleneck link rate R**. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style) **Problem:** A file of **4 Mbits** is sent across a path with 3 links of rates 200 Kbps, 1 Mbps, and 500 Kbps. Compute the end-to-end throughput. **Solution:** Step 1 — Convert all rates to a common unit (bps): - $R_1 = 200 \text{ Kbps} = 200{,}000 \text{ bps}$ - $R_2 = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps}$ - $R_3 = 500 \text{ Kbps} = 500{,}000 \text{ bps}$ Step 2 — Identify the bottleneck: $$R_{bottleneck} = \min(200000,\ 1000000,\ 500000) = 200{,}000 \text{ bps}$$ Step 3 — End-to-end throughput: $$T = R_{bottleneck} = 200 \text{ Kbps}$$ Step 4 — Total time to deliver 4 Mbits at this rate: $$t_{total} = \frac{F}{T} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3} = 20 \text{ seconds}$$ **Answer: Throughput = 200 Kbps, Total Delivery Time = 20 seconds.** # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Path with Bottleneck Identification ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SOURCE
SERVER"] -->|"R1 = 1 Gbps
Optical Fiber"| B["ROUTER 1
Core Network"] B -->|"R2 = 100 Mbps
Backbone Link
BOTTLENECK"| C["ROUTER 2
Access Network"] C -->|"R3 = 500 Mbps
Ethernet LAN"| D["DESTINATION
CLIENT"] style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2d5a87,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#8b0000,stroke:#ff6b6b,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Router 2 — Router 2 segment is highlighted in **red** because it carries the lowest rate (100 Mbps). The end-to-end throughput for any flow passing through this path **cannot exceed 100 Mbps**, regardless of the faster surrounding links. ## 4.2 Throughput Computation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INPUT: File Size F and Path Topology"] --> B["STEP 1: Identify All Link Rates Ri"] B --> C["STEP 2: Apply Bottleneck Formula
T = min of all Ri"] C --> D["STEP 3: Calculate Delivery Time
t = F divided by T"] D --> E["STEP 4: Apply Overhead Correction
if applicable"] E --> F["OUTPUT: Effective End-to-End
Throughput in bps"] style A fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#90caf9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#1565c0,stroke:#90caf9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffcdd2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#c8e6c9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#e65100,stroke:#ffe0b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style F fill:#4a148c,stroke:#e1bee7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Throughput vs. Bandwidth — Conceptual Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR A["THEORETICAL CAPACITY
Bandwidth
Maximum possible rate"] --> B["PROTOCOL OVERHEAD
Headers, ACKs, Retransmissions
REDUCES RATE"] B --> C["ACTUAL ACHIEVED RATE
Throughput
Effective delivery rate"] C --> D["REAL-WORLD
MEASUREMENT
iperf, netperf tools"] A -.->|"Always T less than or equal to B"| C style A fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#90caf9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#ffcdd2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style C fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#c8e6c9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style D fill:#4a148c,stroke:#e1bee7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define throughput in a computer network. How is it different from bandwidth?** **Model Answer:** **Throughput** is defined as the rate at which data is successfully transmitted from a source to a destination over a network, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. It represents the *actual* useful data transfer rate observed during a communication session. **Difference from Bandwidth:** | Aspect | Bandwidth | Throughput | |--------|-----------|------------| | Meaning | Maximum theoretical capacity | Actual delivered data rate | | Nature | Fixed by physical medium | Variable, depends on conditions | | Relation | Upper bound | Always ≤ Bandwidth | **Example:** A 100 Mbps Ethernet link may deliver only 80 Mbps throughput due to protocol overhead and collisions. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Q: What is a bottleneck link? Explain its effect on end-to-end throughput.** **Model Answer:** A **bottleneck link** is the link in an end-to-end network path that has the **lowest transmission rate** among all links in the path. It is also called the **narrow link**. **Effect on End-to-End Throughput:** The throughput of the entire path is determined by the bottleneck link, governed by the formula: $$T_{end-to-end} = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n)$$ No matter how fast the other links are, the overall throughput cannot exceed the capacity of the slowest link. For example, in a path with links of 1 Gbps, 100 Mbps, and 500 Mbps, the throughput is limited to **100 Mbps**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Consider a file of **5 MB** to be transmitted over a path with 4 links of rates **2 Mbps, 500 Kbps, 1 Mbps, and 200 Kbps** respectively. (i) Calculate the end-to-end throughput. **(3 marks)** (ii) Calculate the total time to deliver the file. **(4 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the concept of bottleneck link. Discuss three real-world scenarios where bottleneck determination is critical. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a)(i) — End-to-End Throughput (3 marks) **Step 1:** Convert all rates to a common unit (**bps**): - $R_1 = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$ - $R_2 = 500 \text{ Kbps} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ bps}$ - $R_3 = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$ - $R_4 = 200 \text{ Kbps} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ bps}$ **Step 2:** Identify the bottleneck: $$R_{bottleneck} = \min(2 \times 10^6,\ 5 \times 10^5,\ 1 \times 10^6,\ 2 \times 10^5)$$ $$R_{bottleneck} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ bps} = 200 \text{ Kbps}$$ **Step 3:** End-to-end throughput: $$\boxed{T = 200 \text{ Kbps}}$$ #### Part (a)(ii) — Total Delivery Time (4 marks) **Step 1:** Convert file size to bits: $$F = 5 \text{ MB} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} = 40 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 4 \times 10^7 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the formula: $$t = \frac{F}{T} = \frac{4 \times 10^7 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^5 \text{ bps}}$$ $$t = 200 \text{ seconds} = 3 \text{ minutes } 20 \text{ seconds}$$ #### Part (b) — Bottleneck Concept with Diagram & Real-World Scenarios (7 marks) **Diagram (3 marks):** ``` [SOURCE] --1 Gbps--> [R1] --100 Mbps--> [R2] --500 Mbps--> [DEST] ^^^^^^^^ BOTTLENECK ``` **Explanation (2 marks):** The bottleneck link is the segment with the **lowest transmission rate**. The end-to-end throughput is limited by this rate. Upgrading faster links does not improve performance; only the bottleneck needs upgrading. **Three Real-World Scenarios (2 marks):** 1. **Home Internet:** The ISP's last-mile connection (e.g., DSL at 50 Mbps) is the bottleneck even if the backbone is 10 Gbps. 2. **International Calls:** Satellite links or undersea cables often act as bottlenecks for transcontinental traffic. 3. **Wi-Fi Networks:** The wireless access point (e.g., 300 Mbps Wi-Fi) is often the bottleneck compared to a 1 Gbps wired Ethernet backhaul. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** A packet-switched network has 3 links each of rate **R = 1 Mbps**. A file of **3 Mbits** is divided into **3000 packets** of equal size. Compute: (i) The transmission delay per packet. **(2 marks)** (ii) The total time to deliver the file. **(3 marks)** (iii) The end-to-end throughput. **(2 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast throughput and bandwidth with a suitable example.
