Because Selective Repeat retransmits *only* the lost frame and the receiver *buffers* the rest, the analytical efficiency is identical to Go-Back-N. The difference lies in **bandwidth wasted on retransmissions**, which is reflected by a separate *bandwidth-efficiency* factor (often called $\eta$):

$$
\eta_{\text{SR}} = 1 - P \qquad \text{(probability of no loss)}
$$

where $P$ is the bit error probability. For KTU exams, the formula $U = W / (1 + 2a)$ is the canonical answer.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]** Consider a channel of bandwidth $R = 4$ kbps and propagation speed $V = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The distance between sender and receiver is $d = 2000$ km. Frame size is $L = 1000$ bits. Compute: (a) the value of $a$, (b) Stop-and-Wait utilization, (c) minimum window size $W$ for Go-Back-N to achieve $U = 1$, and (d) the number of sequence-number bits needed.

**Given:** $R = 4 \times 10^3$ bps, $V = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, $d = 2 \times 10^6$ m, $L = 1000$ bits.

**Part (a) — Compute $a$:**

$$
t_p = \frac{d}{V} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{4 \times 10^3} = 0.25 \text{ s} = 250 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{t_p}{t_f} = \frac{10}{250} = 0.04
$$

**Part (b) — Stop-and-Wait Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.04)} = \frac{1}{1.08} \approx 0.9259 = 92.59\%
$$

**Part (c) — Minimum $W$ for Go-Back-N to saturate:**

$$
W \geq 1 + 2a = 1 + 0.08 = 1.08 \implies W_{\min} = 2 \text{ frames}
$$

(Window size must be an integer, so $W = 2$ already saturates this very "fast" channel.)

**Part (d) — Sequence-number bits for GBN:**

$$
2^k \geq W + 1 = 3 \implies k \geq \lceil \log_2 3 \rceil = 2 \text{ bits}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Protocol When?

| Scenario | Best Protocol | Justification |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| $a \ll 1$ (short, fast link — e.g., LAN) | **Stop-and-Wait** | Already gives $U \approx 1$; simpler to implement |
| $a \approx 1$ (moderate) | **Go-Back-N** | Modest window suffices; cumulative ACK simplifies receiver |
| $a \gg 1$ (long, slow — e.g., satellite) | **Selective Repeat** | Avoids wasteful retransmission of many correct frames |
| Receiver has **scarce memory** | **Go-Back-N** | Receiver window = 1 |
| Receiver has **plentiful memory** | **Selective Repeat** | Buffering is feasible; efficiency maximized |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sender/Receiver Window State Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    senderIdle[Sender Idle Window Closed] -->|Start Transmission| senderOpen[Sender Window Opens to Size W]
    senderOpen -->|Transmit Frame i| channelBus[Channel: Frame i in Transit]
    channelBus -->|Bit Error or Loss| timeoutEvent[Timeout for Frame i]
    timeoutEvent --> gbnAction{Protocol Type}
    gbnAction -- Go-Back-N --> gbnRetrans[Retransmit Frame i AND all subsequent]
    gbnAction -- Selective Repeat --> srRetrans[Retransmit ONLY Frame i]
    channelBus -->|Frame i OK| receiverCheck{Receiver Check}
    receiverCheck -- Go-Back-N --> gbnRecv[Accept i, Discard i+1 to W-1 if out of order]
    receiverCheck -- Selective Repeat --> srRecv[Buffer i, Deliver in-order to Network Layer]
    gbnRecv --> ackSend[Send Cumulative ACK n]
    srRecv --> sackSend[Send Selective ACK n]
    ackSend --> senderOpen
    sackSend --> senderOpen
    gbnRetrans --> channelBus
    srRetrans --> channelBus
```

## 4.2 Protocol Comparison — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph senderSide[SENDER SIDE]
        sApp[Application Layer Data] --> sFrag[Frame Assembly and Sequencing]
        sFrag --> sWindow[Sliding Window Controller]
        sWindow --> sTimer[Per-Frame Timer]
        sWindow --> sTx[Physical Transmitter]
    end

    subgraph channelBlock[TRANSMISSION CHANNEL]
        sTx --> medium[(Propagation Medium d, V)]
        medium --> rRx[Physical Receiver]
    end

    subgraph receiverSide[RECEIVER SIDE]
        rRx --> rWindow[Sliding Window Controller]
        rWindow --> rCheck[CRC and Error Check]
        rCheck -- GBN mode --> rDiscard[Discard Out of Order Frames]
        rCheck -- SR mode --> rBuffer[Buffer Out of Order Frames]
        rDiscard --> rAckGen[Generate Cumulative ACK]
        rBuffer --> rAckGen
        rAckGen --> sTimer
    end
```

## 4.3 Sequence-Number Wraparound (Why $2^k$ matters for Selective Repeat)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Window 0: Send Frames 0 to W-1] --> P2[All ACKed, Window Slides]
    P2 --> P3[Window 1: Send Frames W to 2W-1]
    P3 --> P4[After 2W Frames, Sequence Numbers Wrap to 0]
    P4 --> P5{Receiver Ambiguity Test}
    P5 -- If 2^k less than 2W --> P6[ERROR: Receiver cannot tell old from new]
    P5 -- If 2^k greater or equal 2W --> P7[OK: Sequence Numbers Unique]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Go-Back-N only needs $2^k \geq W + 1$:** The receiver never accepts a frame outside its window of size 1. By the time sequence numbers wrap back to 0, the receiver has already advanced past those old numbers — so only $W + 1$ distinct values are needed to disambiguate. Selective Repeat must keep all $W$ frames of the *previous* window distinguishable from the *current* window, hence the factor of 2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` 
**Differentiate between Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ with respect to retransmission strategy, receiver window size, and minimum sequence number bits required.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Go-Back-N ARQ | Selective Repeat ARQ |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Retransmission Strategy** | On error, retransmits the erroneous frame *and all subsequent* frames already sent | Retransmits *only* the specific lost or erroneous frame |
| **Receiver Window Size** | Always **1** (only next-in-sequence frame accepted) | Equal to sender window size $W$ (out-of-order frames buffered) |
| **Sequence Number Bits ($k$)** | Must satisfy $2^k \geq W + 1$ | Must satisfy $2^k \geq 2W$ |
| **Receiver Complexity** | Lower — no buffering needed | Higher — must buffer and reorder |
| **Bandwidth Wastage on Error** | Higher — wastes bandwidth on already-corrected frames | Lower — only the damaged frame is resent |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` 
**Define the parameter $a$ used in sliding window analysis. A link has bandwidth $1$ Mbps, frame length $1000$ bits, distance $100$ m, and propagation speed $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Compute $a$.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The parameter $a$ is the ratio of propagation time to transmission time, representing how many "frame times" fit inside one propagation delay.

$$
a = \frac{t_p}{t_f} = \frac{d \cdot R}{V \cdot L}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
t_p = \frac{d}{V} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

$$
a = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0005
$$

Since $a \ll 1$, this is a short, fast link — Stop-and-Wait ARQ would already be highly efficient.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper]`
**(a)** Explain the Sliding Window Protocol with a neat sender/receiver window diagram. Discuss how the window size affects channel utilization, with the formula $U = W / (1 + 2a)$ for Go-Back-N ARQ. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A satellite link operates at $R = 1$ Mbps with a one-way propagation delay of $270$ ms. Frame size is $L = 1000$ bits. ACK transmission time is negligible. Compute: (i) the parameter $a$, (ii) Stop-and-Wait utilization, (iii) minimum window size $W$ for Go-Back-N ARQ to achieve $100\%$ utilization, and (iv) the minimum number of sequence-number bits required. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Sliding Window Protocol — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

The Sliding Window Protocol allows the sender to transmit **multiple frames** before receiving acknowledgments, controlled by a logical "window" of size $W$. Both sender and receiver maintain this window.

**Sender Window Mechanics:**
- Holds $W$ sequence numbers of frames that may be sent.
- On receiving ACK $n$, the window slides forward by one (or more) position, freeing the right-edge slot.
- Permits pipelining — the channel is kept continuously busy.

**Receiver Window Mechanics:**
- **Go-Back-N:** Window size = 1. Accepts only the next-in-sequence frame. Out-of-order frames are dropped.
- **Selective Repeat:** Window size = $W$. Accepts and buffers frames within the window, delivers to network layer in order.

**Effect on Utilization:**
With $W$ frames pipelined, total useful transmission time per cycle is $W \cdot t_f$. If the ACK for the first frame returns only after $t_f + 2 t_p$, then:

$$
U_{\text{GBN}} = \frac{W}{1 + 2a}
$$

If $W \geq 1 + 2a$, the pipeline never drains and $U = 1$ (100%).

**Diagram (describe in words for the exam):** Draw two horizontal strips representing the sender window and receiver window, both labeled with sequence numbers $0, 1, 2, \dots, W-1$. Show an arrow indicating the window sliding forward after an ACK arrives. Mark sent frames inside the sender window and "expected frame" at the receiver.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps, $t_p = 270$ ms $= 0.27$ s, $L = 1000$ bits.

**Step (i) — Compute $a$:**

$$
t_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{t_p}{t_f} = \frac{270 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 270
$$

**Step (ii) — Stop-and-Wait Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 540} = \frac{1}{541} \approx 1.85 \times 10^{-3} = 0.185\%
$$

(The channel is utterly wasted — only $0.185\%$ of the bandwidth carries user data!)

**Step (iii) — Minimum $W$ for $U = 1$ in Go-Back-N:**

$$
W \geq 1 + 2a = 1 + 540 = 541
$$

So $W_{\min} = 541$ frames.

**Step (iv) — Sequence number bits for GBN:**

$$
2^k \geq W + 1 = 542 \implies k \geq \lceil \log_2 542 \rceil = \lceil 9.082 \rceil = 10 \text{ bits}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $a = 270$
- $U_{\text{S\&W}} \approx 0.185\%$
- $W_{\min} = 541$
- $k_{\min} = 10$ bits

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]`
**(a)** With a clear timeline diagram, derive the channel utilization formula for Stop-and-Wait ARQ. Explain why this protocol is unsuitable for high-latency links. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a Selective Repeat ARQ system with a window size of $W = 4$ frames, sequence numbers $0$ to $7$, and link parameters $R = 50$ kbps, $L = 500$ bits, $d = 5000$ km, $V = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate the maximum achievable throughput and the protocol efficiency. If frame 2 is lost, explain the receiver's recovery procedure. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Stop-and-Wait Derivation (7 marks):**

**Timeline:**
- $t = 0$: Sender begins transmitting Frame 0.
- $t = t_f$: Frame 0 fully transmitted; last bit begins propagation.
- $t = t_f + t_p$: Frame 0 fully received; ACK transmitted instantly.
- $t = t_f + 2 t_p$: ACK arrives at sender; sender begins Frame 1.

**Derivation:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = t_f + 2 t_p
$$

$$
U = \frac{t_f}{t_f + 2 t_p} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

**Why unsuitable for high-latency links:**
When $a$ is large (e.g., $a = 270$ for geostationary satellite), $U \to \frac{1}{2a} \to 0$. The sender waits idle for $2 t_p$ between every frame — most of the expensive bandwidth is **wasted silence**. For example, at $a = 270$, $U \approx 0.18\%$, meaning the user pays for a $1$ Mbps link but receives only $\approx 1.85$ kbps of throughput.

**Solution:** Use Sliding Window Protocols with $W \geq 1 + 2a$, so the sender always has frames in flight — channel utilization approaches $100\%$.

**(b) Selective Repeat Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W = 4$, $R = 50 \times 10^3$ bps, $L = 500$ bits, $d = 5 \times 10^6$ m, $V = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute $t_p$ and $t_f$:**

$$
t_p = \frac{d}{V} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{500}{50 \times 10^3} = 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a$:**

$$
a = \frac{t_p}{t_f} = \frac{25}{10} = 2.5
$$

**Step 3 — Check pipeline condition:**

$$
1 + 2a = 1 + 5 = 6
$$

Since $W = 4 < 6$, the pipeline is **not saturated**. We use the unsaturated formula.

**Step 4 — Protocol Efficiency:**

$$
U = \frac{W}{1 + 2a} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 66.67\%
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Throughput:**

$$
\text{Throughput} = U \times R = \frac{2}{3} \times 50 \text{ kbps} = 33.33 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 6 — Recovery procedure when Frame 2 is lost:**

1. Receiver receives Frames 0, 1, 3, 4 successfully (sequence numbers within its window 0–3).
2. Frame 3 and 4 are **out-of-order** but valid — receiver **buffers them** (this is the key SR feature).
3. Receiver sends SACK for Frame 1 (acknowledging it as the last in-order frame received).
4. Receiver **does not send a Negative ACK** — it simply does not advance its left edge past the missing Frame 2.
5. Sender's timer for Frame 2 expires after timeout; only Frame 2 is retransmitted.
6. Receiver accepts Frame 2, then delivers Frames 2, 3, 4 in correct order to the Network Layer, and sends SACK for Frame 4.

**Final Answers:**
- Efficiency $U = 66.67\%$
- Throughput $= 33.33$ kbps
- Recovery: Only Frame 2 is retransmitted; Frames 3 and 4 are buffered at receiver.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the integer constraint on $W$:** If you compute $W_{\min} = 541.08$, the answer is $W_{\min} = 542$, not $541$.
> 2. **Confusing $W + 1$ vs $2W$ rule:** Many students write $2^k \geq W$ for Selective Repeat. The correct inequality is $2^k \geq 2W$ — failing this causes **ambiguity errors** at the receiver and is heavily penalized.
> 3. **Using bits vs bytes for $L$:** A common trap. Frame size in KTU problems is always in **bits** when plugged into $L / R$. If the problem says "1000 bytes," convert to $8000$ bits first.
> 4. **Ignoring ACK transmission time:** If the problem does **not** explicitly say "ACK transmission time is negligible," include it in your formula: $T_{\text{cycle}} = t_f + 2 t_p + t_{\text{ACK}}$.
> 5. **Mixing up pipeline saturation:** Students often write $U = W / (1 + 2a)$ even when $W \geq 1 + 2a$. Always state the regime you are operating in.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sliding Window Protocols**

- **Three protocols:** Stop-and-Wait ($W = 1$), Go-Back-N (sender $W$, receiver $1$), Selective Repeat (sender $W$, receiver $W$).
- **Parameter $a$:** $a = \dfrac{d \cdot R}{V \cdot L}$ — dimensionless, the master variable of every numerical.
- **Stop-and-Wait efficiency:** $\quad U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ — always $\leq 50\%$ in practice; near-zero for satellites.
- **Pipeline-saturated condition:** $\quad W \geq 1 + 2a \implies U = 1$.
- **Unsaturated Go-Back-N / Selective Repeat efficiency:** $\quad U = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$.
- **Throughput:** $\quad \text{Throughput} = U \times R$ in bits/second.
- **Sequence number bits — Go-Back-N:** $\quad 2^k \geq W + 1$.
- **Sequence number bits — Selective Repeat:** $\quad 2^k \geq 2W$.
- **Receiver window of GBN = 1:** Out-of-order frames are **dropped**, not buffered.
- **Receiver window of SR = $W$:** Out-of-order frames are **buffered** for in-order delivery.
- **Cumulative ACK** (GBN) acknowledges all frames up to $n - 1$.
- **Selective ACK / SACK** (SR) acknowledges a specific frame $n$.
- **Why SR needs $2^k \geq 2W$:** Prevents the receiver from confusing a fresh wrap-around frame with an old retransmission still pending.
- **Real-world relevance:** TCP uses a sliding window combining flow + congestion control; satellite modems always use Selective Repeat because $a$ is huge.
- **Memory vs Bandwidth trade-off:** SR maximizes bandwidth but consumes receiver memory proportional to $W$.
- **KTU favorite trap:** When $W$ and $a$ are given, ALWAYS check the regime $W \lessgtr 1 + 2a$ **before** writing the efficiency formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer - Channel Allocation Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer - Channel Allocation Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer** is the lower half of the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** in the OSI reference model. Its fundamental responsibility is to arbitrate and regulate access to a **shared broadcast communication channel** among multiple competing stations (nodes), ensuring orderly, efficient, and fair utilization of the transmission medium.

The **Channel Allocation Problem** refers to the core engineering challenge in the MAC sublayer: *How should a single, shared broadcast channel of capacity C bps be allocated among N competing users who wish to transmit data, when (a) the number of users varies dynamically, (b) traffic is typically bursty, and (c) only a subset of users wish to transmit at any given instant?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The channel allocation problem is the gateway to studying *Multiple Access Protocols*. The goal is **maximizing channel utilization (throughput)** while **minimizing frame delay**, subject to the constraint of **collision-free or collision-managed** access.

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Assumptions of the Channel Allocation Model

1. **Station Model:** N independent stations generate frames at an aggregate rate λ. Once a frame is generated, the station is *blocked* until the frame is successfully transmitted.
2. **Single Channel Assumption:** A single broadcast channel of capacity **C bps** is shared. All stations transmit and receive on this same channel, and collisions occur if two frames overlap in time at the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Assumptions (Tanenbaum/Kurose Standard Model):**
> - **Continuous Time vs. Slotted Time:** Slotted systems divide time into discrete slots (with collision detection possible).
> - **Carrier Sense vs. No Carrier Sense:** Some stations can detect channel activity; others transmit blindly.
> - **Collision Detection vs. No Collision Detection:** Stations may or may not detect collisions after they occur.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Telephone Party-Line"

Imagine a small village where **50 families** share a **single telephone party-line** (everyone hears every conversation). The **Channel Allocation Problem** is precisely the challenge of deciding:
- When can you speak?
- What if two people talk at once? (a **collision**)
- What if no one talks? (channel is **idle**, hence wasted)

The MAC sublayer is essentially the **"etiquette rulebook"** that the village must collectively obey so that the single line is used efficiently, fairly, and with minimal garbled overlap. The protocols we study (ALOHA, CSMA, Token Passing, etc.) are simply different etiquette rulebooks with different performance trade-offs.

### 1.4 Classification of Channel Allocation Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Top-Level Strategies for Channel Allocation:**
>
> 1. **Static / Fixed Channelization** (FDM, TDM, CDM, WDM) — divides the channel rigidly in advance.
> 2. **Dynamic / Random Access** (ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD) — stations contend for the channel; collisions are accepted and resolved.
> 3. **Collision-Free / Controlled Access** (Bit-Map, Token Ring, Binary Countdown, FDDI) — collisions are prevented entirely by a deterministic arbitration rule.

| Strategy Type | Channel Utilization @ Light Load | Channel Utilization @ Heavy Load | Typical Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static (FDM/TDM) | Poor (wasted slots) | Excellent (no contention) | TDM, FDM |
| Random Access | Excellent (immediate access) | Poor (many collisions) | ALOHA, CSMA |
| Controlled / Collision-Free | Good (some delay) | Excellent (no collisions) | Token, Bit-Map |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static Channel Allocation (Fails at Bursty Traffic)

In **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** or **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)**, the channel capacity C is divided into N fixed sub-channels. Each user gets an exclusive slice.

**The Key Theorem of Static Allocation:**
For N users, each generating traffic with mean rate λ (Poisson arrivals), the probability that a user's offered load is > C/N causes queueing delay that grows **without bound** as N increases. The expected delay T is:

$$T = \frac{1}{\mu C - \lambda}$$

where μ is the per-user service rate. As N → ∞, T → ∞. This proves that **static allocation is fundamentally inefficient for bursty traffic**, which is the universal characteristic of computer network traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Punchline:** "Static channel allocation assumes the worst-case (that all N users transmit simultaneously), and so it wastes capacity in the typical case where only a small subset is active. Dynamic allocation exploits this burstiness."

### 2.2 Dynamic Channel Allocation — Five Assumptions

1. **Independent Traffic Model:** Each station generates frames independently.
2. **Single Channel:** One broadcast channel, all stations share it.
3. **Collisions:** A collision occurs when ≥ 2 frames overlap in time; all colliding frames are destroyed and must be retransmitted.
4. **Continuous Time / Slotted Time:** Defines the granularity of transmission attempts.
5. **Carrier Sense / No Carrier Sense:** Defines whether stations listen before transmitting.

### 2.3 PURE (UNSLOTTED) ALOHA — Operation

- Invented at the **University of Hawaii (Norman Abramson, 1970)** for radio packet networks.
- **Rule:** A station transmits a frame as soon as it has one ready. There is no listen-before-talk and no time synchronization.
- **Collision rule:** If two stations transmit in a window of **2 × frame_time** centered on the start of a frame, both frames are destroyed (the "vulnerable period" is 2T).
- After collision, the station waits a **random backoff time** and retransmits.

### 2.4 SLOTTED ALOHA — Operation

- **Rule:** Time is divided into discrete slots, each equal to one frame transmission time T.
- A station may **only begin transmission at the start of a slot**.
- The vulnerable period shrinks from **2T to T** (collision only if two stations pick the same slot).
- This **doubles** the maximum throughput of pure ALOHA.

### 2.5 CARRIER SENSE MULTIPLE ACCESS (CSMA) — The Listening Family

- **1-Persistent CSMA:** Sense channel; if idle, transmit immediately; if busy, keep sensing until idle, then transmit (with probability 1). High collision risk under high load.
- **Non-Persistent CSMA:** Sense channel; if idle, transmit; if busy, wait a **random time** before sensing again. Reduces collisions at the cost of more idle time.
- **p-Persistent CSMA** (slotted only): Sense channel; if idle, transmit with probability *p*; with probability *1-p*, defer to next slot. Compromise between throughput and delay.
- **CSMA/CD** (Ethernet): Adds **Collision Detection** — station listens to its own transmission, aborts immediately upon detecting interference, and jams the channel to alert others.

### 2.6 COLLISION-FREE PROTOCOLS

- **Bit-Map Protocol:** N stations, each bit in a "contention period" represents a station that wants to transmit. Throughput at low load: **d/(N+d)**; at high load: **d/(1+d)**.
- **Binary Countdown:** Stations OR their binary addresses onto a shared bus; the highest-indexed bit wins arbitration. **O(log N) resolution time**.
- **Token Passing / Token Ring (IEEE 802.5):** A small 3-byte **token** circulates; only the holder may transmit. **Bounded delay**, naturally collision-free.
- **FDDI (Fiber Distributed Data Interface):** Dual counter-rotating fiber rings; uses **Timed Token Rotation** to provide real-time guarantees.

### 2.7 LIMITED-CONTENTION PROTOCOLS

The **Adaptive Tree Walk Protocol** dynamically estimates the current load (k active stations) and selects an optimal depth *q* in a binary tree to split contenders, achieving the best of both pure ALOHA (low delay at light load) and collision-free (high throughput at heavy load). Optimal *q* satisfies: $$2^q = k$$ (each slot is shared by ≤ 1 expected station).

### 2.8 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | Throughput S, Load G | **Pure ALOHA**; max S = **1/(2e) ≈ 0.184** at G = 0.5 | Computing pure ALOHA throughput |
| 2 | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | Throughput S, Load G | **Slotted ALOHA**; max S = **1/e ≈ 0.368** at G = 1.0 | Computing slotted ALOHA throughput |
| 3 | $E = \frac{1}{1+2a}$ | 1-Persistent CSMA efficiency | $a = t_{prop} / t_{trans}$ | 1-persistent CSMA max efficiency |
| 4 | $E = \frac{1}{1+a}$ | Non-Persistent CSMA efficiency | $a = t_{prop} / t_{trans}$ | Non-persistent CSMA max efficiency |
| 5 | $T = \frac{1}{\mu C - \lambda}$ | Mean delay under static FDM/TDM | N users, rate λ each | Showing static allocation diverges |
| 6 | $a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{trans}} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}$ | Dimensionless parameter | d=distance, R=data rate, v=propagation speed, L=frame length | Relating distance to collision window |
| 7 | $S_{low} = \frac{d}{N+d}$ | Bit-Map low-load throughput | d frames, N stations | Bit-Map protocol |
| 8 | $S_{high} = \frac{d}{1+d}$ | Bit-Map high-load throughput | d frames, N stations | Bit-Map protocol |
| 9 | $E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44a}$ | Ethernet (CSMA/CD) approx. | $a = t_{prop}/t_{trans}$ | Classical Ethernet efficiency |
| 10 | $2^q \approx k$ | Adaptive tree depth | k active stations | Limited-contention optimal split |

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Rule of Thumb:** *Slotted ALOHA is exactly twice as efficient as Pure ALOHA under optimal load* — this 0.368 vs. 0.184 ratio is a **favorite KTU exam question**.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Classical Ethernet (IEEE 802.3):** Uses **1-persistent CSMA/CD** with binary exponential backoff. The transition to switched Ethernet (full-duplex) effectively eliminates collisions.
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** Uses **CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance) because radios cannot detect collisions reliably. RTS/CTS handshake mitigates hidden terminal problem.
- **Satellite / 4G / 5G Random Access Channels:** Slotted ALOHA variants underpin the **RACH (Random Access Channel)** in LTE/5G.
- **Industrial Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) & Token Ring (802.5):** Used in factory automation and IBM legacy networks for deterministic real-time delivery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION: PURE ALOHA THROUGHPUT EQUATION

**Step 1: Define the offered load G.**
Let G be the mean number of transmission attempts (new + retransmitted frames) per **frame time T**. The probability of k transmission attempts in any frame time, by Poisson statistics, is:

$$P[k] = \frac{G^k \cdot e^{-G}}{k!}$$

**Step 2: Define the vulnerable window.**
A frame just transmitted will be destroyed if **any other station** transmits a frame starting anywhere in the window **[t_0 − T, t_0 + T]**, where t_0 is the start of our frame. This window has duration **2T**.

**Step 3: Compute the average number of attempts in the vulnerable window.**
Because the mean is G per frame time, the mean number of attempts in a window of length **2T** is **2G**.

**Step 4: Compute the probability of zero other transmissions (i.e., successful transmission).**
By Poisson, the probability that the mean 2G yields zero actual events is:

$$P_{success} = e^{-2G}$$

**Step 5: Compute throughput S.**
Throughput S is the fraction of frame times that carry a *successful* frame. The offered load is G attempts per frame time; the fraction that succeed is $e^{-2G}$:

$$\boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-2G}}$$

**Step 6: Maximize S to find peak throughput.**
Differentiate w.r.t. G and set to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dG} &= e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} \\
&= e^{-2G} \cdot (1 - 2G) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

This yields **G = 0.5**. Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= 0.5 \cdot e^{-2 \times 0.5} \\
&= 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2e} \\
&\approx 0.1839
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Result:** Maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA = **18.4%** of the raw channel capacity. The remaining **81.6%** is wasted in idle time and collisions.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION: SLOTTED ALOHA THROUGHPUT EQUATION

**Step 1: Define slotted time.**
Time is divided into slots, each of duration T (one frame). A station may only start transmission at a slot boundary.

**Step 2: Vulnerability shrinks to a single slot.**
A collision occurs **only if** one or more other stations also pick the same slot. The vulnerable window is now exactly **T**, not 2T.

**Step 3: Mean attempts in one slot.**
Mean offered load per slot = G (attempts per frame time).

**Step 4: Probability of success.**
By Poisson, the probability that the slot has no other transmission (i.e., exactly our one frame in the slot):

$$P_{success} = e^{-G}$$

**Step 5: Throughput equation.**

$$\boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-G}}$$

**Step 6: Maximize S.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dG} &= e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} \\
&= e^{-G} \cdot (1 - G) \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Yields **G = 1.0**. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= 1 \cdot e^{-1} \\
&= \frac{1}{e} \\
&\approx 0.3679
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Result:** Maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA = **36.8%** of raw capacity. **Exactly double** the pure ALOHA peak (since the vulnerable period is halved, the exponent's coefficient halves, and the optimal G doubles — net effect: $2 \times \frac{1}{2e} = \frac{1}{e}$).

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION: CSMA EFFICIENCY (NON-PERSISTENT)

**Step 1: Define the dimensionless parameter a.**

$$a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{trans}} = \frac{d / v}{L / R} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}$$

where d = cable length, v = propagation velocity (≈ 2×10⁸ m/s in cable), L = frame length in bits, R = data rate in bps.

**Step 2: Model the propagation and collision behavior.**
For non-persistent CSMA, assume that a collision can only occur during a small window around the start of a transmission. The time wasted per transmission attempt is the time it takes for the colliding station to either (a) detect idle and start, or (b) detect collision and abort.

**Step 3: Compute efficiency.**
For non-persistent CSMA, the **fraction of channel capacity used for successful transmission** is approximated as:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{1 + a}}$$

**Step 4: For 1-persistent CSMA**, the efficiency is slightly worse because a station transmits *immediately* upon sensing idle, causing more collisions:

$$\boxed{E_{1-pers} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** As $a \to 0$ (either very short cables, very high data rates, or very long frames), efficiency → 1. This is exactly why modern gigabit Ethernet uses *switches* — they make a, the per-segment parameter, effectively 0, eliminating collisions.

---

### 3.4 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — PEAK PERFORMANCE COMPARISON

| Protocol | Max Throughput | Optimal Load G* | Vulnerable Window | CSMA/CD Required? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure ALOHA** | $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ | 0.5 | 2T | No |
| **Slotted ALOHA** | $1/e \approx 0.368$ | 1.0 | T | No |
| **1-Persistent CSMA** | $1/(1+2a)$ | — | ~ 2t_prop | No |
| **Non-Persistent CSMA** | $1/(1+a)$ | — | ~ t_prop | No |
| **CSMA/CD (Ethernet)** | $1/(1+6.44a)$ | — | $2t_{prop}$ | **YES** |
| **Token Ring** | ~ 1.0 (delay-bound) | N/A | N/A | No |
| **Bit-Map (high load)** | $d/(1+d) \to 1$ as d→∞ | N/A | N/A | No |

---

### 3.5 WORKED NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Consider a Pure ALOHA system. Each station transmits at an average rate of **12 frames per minute**, and the channel capacity supports **60 frames per minute** (frame time T = 1 sec). Find the throughput and the probability that a given transmission is successful.

**Step 1: Compute G.**
G = (total offered traffic in frames per frame time) = $\frac{\text{attempts per frame time}}{\text{}} = 12/60 = 0.2$

**Step 2: Compute throughput S.**

$$S = G \cdot e^{-2G} = 0.2 \cdot e^{-0.4} = 0.2 \cdot 0.6703 = 0.1341$$

**Step 3: Successful frames per minute.**
Successful frames/sec $= S \times 60 = 0.1341 \times 60 = 8.046$ frames/min.

**Step 4: Probability a given transmission is successful.**
$P_{success} = e^{-2G} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703 = 67.03\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification at G = 0.5 (optimal):** S = 0.184, so ~ 11 successful frames/min. At G = 0.5, success probability = e⁻¹ = 36.8%, but the offered load is higher.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete Taxonomy of Channel Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Channel Allocation Problem] --> B[Static Channelization]
    A --> C[Dynamic Channel Allocation]
    A --> D[Hybrid Limited Contention]

    B --> B1[FDM - Frequency Division]
    B --> B2[TDM - Time Division]
    B --> B3[CDM - Code Division]
    B --> B4[WDM - Wavelength Division]

    C --> C1[Random Access Contention]
    C --> C2[Collision Free Protocols]
    C --> C3[Limited Contention Adaptive]

    C1 --> C11[Pure ALOHA]
    C1 --> C12[Slotted ALOHA]
    C1 --> C13[CSMA Family]
    C13 --> C131[1 Persistent CSMA]
    C13 --> C132[Non Persistent CSMA]
    C13 --> C133[p Persistent CSMA]
    C13 --> C134[CSMA CD Ethernet]
    C13 --> C135[CSMA CA WiFi]

    C2 --> C21[Bit Map Protocol]
    C2 --> C22[Binary Countdown]
    C2 --> C23[Token Ring 802.5]
    C2 --> C24[Token Bus 802.4]
    C2 --> C25[FDDI Dual Ring]

    C3 --> C31[Adaptive Tree Walk]

    A1[Throughput vs Load] -.-> C11
    A1 -.-> C12
    A1 -.-> C131
    A1 -.-> C132
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Pure ALOHA Collision & Recovery

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S1 as Station A
    participant S2 as Station B
    participant S3 as Station C
    participant CH as Shared Channel

    Note over S1,S3: Time t0 - Both stations have a frame ready
    S1->>CH: Transmits Frame 1 (starts at t0)
    S2->>CH: Transmits Frame 2 (starts at t0 + delta)
    Note over CH: COLLISION in window of 2T centered at t0
    CH-->>S1: Garbled signal received
    CH-->>S2: Garbled signal received

    Note over S1,S3: Each waits a RANDOM BACKOFF time
    S1->>S1: Backoff random R1
    S2->>S2: Backoff random R2

    S1->>CH: Retransmits Frame 1 at t0 + R1
    CH-->>S1: ACK success
    S2->>CH: Retransmits Frame 2 at t0 + R2
    CH-->>S2: ACK success

    Note over S3: Heard nothing, can transmit freely
    S3->>CH: Transmits Frame 3 (no collision)
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Plot: Throughput vs Load (Pure ALOHA vs Slotted ALOHA)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Throughput S vs Offered Load G"
    x-axis "Offered Load G (attempts per frame time)" [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5]
    y-axis "Throughput S" 0 --> 0.4
    line [0, 0.194, 0.184, 0.158, 0.368, 0.358, 0.334]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the plot:** Pure ALOHA peaks at G = 0.5 with S ≈ 0.184. Slotted ALOHA peaks at G = 1.0 with S ≈ 0.368. Beyond the peak, throughput *decreases* as more collisions destroy more frames than can be successfully delivered — a classic **instability** of random access protocols.

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: CSMA/CD Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sensing: Frame Ready
    Sensing --> Transmitting: Channel Idle
    Sensing --> Backoff: Channel Busy
    Backoff --> Sensing: Random Wait Elapsed
    Transmitting --> CollisionDetected: Energy Spike Detected
    Transmitting --> Success: Transmission Complete
    CollisionDetected --> Jam: Send Jam Signal
    Jam --> Backoff: Wait Binary Exp Backoff
    Success --> [*]
    Backoff --> Backoff: 16 Failed Attempts Give Up
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Channel Allocation Problem. Why is static channel allocation (TDM/FDM) inefficient for computer network traffic?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Channel Allocation Problem is the task of dynamically sharing a single broadcast channel of capacity *C* bps among *N* competing, independent stations whose traffic is typically **bursty** — meaning most stations are silent most of the time.

Static allocation (TDM/FDM) is inefficient because:
1. It **wastes capacity** when fewer than N stations are active (the typical case), since the unused slots/frequencies are reserved but unutilized.
2. It **scales poorly** with N — mean delay diverges as $T = 1/(\mu C - \lambda)$ grows without bound for large N.
3. It cannot adapt to **bursty** traffic patterns characteristic of LAN/internet applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often say "TDM is bad" without stating the *reason* — that traffic is bursty. Always explicitly mention the **bursty nature** of computer traffic for full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. Compare their maximum throughputs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|---|---|---|
| Time Model | Continuous | Discrete slots of size T |
| Transmission Rule | Anytime | Only at slot boundary |
| Vulnerable Window | **2T** | **T** |
| Max Throughput | **1/(2e) ≈ 0.184** (18.4%) | **1/e ≈ 0.368** (36.8%) |
| Optimal Load G | 0.5 | 1.0 |
| Synchronization | Not required | Required |

Slotted ALOHA achieves **exactly double** the throughput of Pure ALOHA by halving the vulnerable period.

---

### PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CSMA/CD** protocol used in classical Ethernet. Clearly describe the roles of carrier sensing, collision detection, the **binary exponential backoff algorithm**, and the **jam signal**. Use a neat timing diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1-km long, 10-Mbps CSMA/CD LAN uses a propagation speed of 2×10⁸ m/s. Frames are 256 bits long. Compute:
(i) The parameter $a = t_{prop}/t_{trans}$.
(ii) The maximum efficiency of 1-persistent CSMA.
(iii) The maximum efficiency of non-persistent CSMA.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Carrier Sensing:**
Before transmitting, the station senses the medium. If idle, the station begins transmission immediately. This avoids most "obvious" collisions.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Collision Detection (CD):**
Because a frame takes time to propagate, collisions can still occur if two stations both sensed an idle channel simultaneously. CD allows a station to **listen to its own transmission** and compare with the signal on the medium. If energy differs, a collision is detected.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Jam Signal:**
Upon detecting a collision, the station transmits a 32-bit **jam signal** to ensure that all other transmitting stations are also aware of the collision. The jam signal is at least as long as the time to detect the collision (≥ 2 × t_prop), guaranteeing propagation to all corners of the network.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Binary Exponential Backoff:**
After the jam, the station waits a random time:
- After the *i*-th collision, choose *k* uniformly from {0, 1, ..., 2^i − 1}.
- Wait $k \times 51.2 \mu s$ (slot time for 10-Mbps Ethernet).
- After 16 collisions, the controller gives up and reports an error.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Timing Diagram Reference:**
| Station A | senses idle | → | transmits | → | detects collision | → | sends jam | → | waits k×slot | → | retransmits |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Station B | transmits in parallel | | | | detects collision | | sends jam | | waits k′×slot | | retransmits |

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute t_trans and t_prop.**

$$t_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{256 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 25.6 \mu s$$

$$t_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 5 \mu s$$

**Step 2: Compute a.**

$$a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{trans}} = \frac{5 \mu s}{25.6 \mu s} = 0.1953$$

**Step 3: 1-persistent CSMA efficiency.**

$$E_{1-pers} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.1953)} = \frac{1}{1.3906} = 0.7191 \approx 71.9\%$$

**Step 4: Non-persistent CSMA efficiency.**

$$E_{non-pers} = \frac{1}{1 + a} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.1953} = \frac{1}{1.1953} = 0.8366 \approx 83.7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse the **frame transmission time** ($L/R$) with the **propagation time** ($d/v$). Always write both explicitly.
> 2. The **2a** in the 1-persistent formula comes from the fact that the *combined* vulnerable period can extend up to 2 propagation times; do not write it as $1+2a^2$ or similar.
> 3. Forgetting to convert km to m (×1000) and Mbps to bps (×10⁶) is the most common numerical error.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat diagrams, describe the operation of **Pure ALOHA** and **Slotted ALOHA** protocols. Derive expressions for the throughput of both and find their maximum throughputs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Slotted ALOHA network operates at 1 Gbps. Each frame is 1250 bytes. A station attempts to transmit one new frame per millisecond (Poisson arrivals). Compute:
(i) The frame transmission time.
(ii) The offered load *G*.
(iii) The probability of successful transmission per slot.
(iv) The expected number of successful transmissions per second.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Pure ALOHA Operation:**
- Continuous time, no carrier sense.
- Station transmits as soon as a frame is ready.
- Vulnerable period = 2T (where T = frame transmission time).
- Collisions resolved by **random backoff and retransmission**.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Slotted ALOHA Operation:**
- Time is divided into discrete slots, each of length T.
- Stations may **only** begin transmission at the start of a slot boundary (requires synchronization, e.g., a special beacon station).
- Vulnerable period reduces to T (only same-slot collisions).

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Diagram (textual):**
```
Pure ALOHA:
       [Frame A]  (collides with B if B starts within 2T)
          [Frame B]
          <--vulnerable period = 2T-->

Slotted ALOHA:
  ----| slot k |----| slot k+1 |----| slot k+2 |----
       [F1][F2]      (collision if both in same slot)
        ^vulnerable = T^
```

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Derivation recap:**
- Pure ALOHA: $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, max at G=0.5, $S_{max} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$.
- Slotted ALOHA: $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$, max at G=1.0, $S_{max} = 1/e \approx 0.368$.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
Slotted ALOHA doubles the peak throughput of Pure ALOHA.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Frame transmission time.**

$$t_{trans} = \frac{1250 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{1 \times 10^9 \text{ bps}} = \frac{10000 \text{ bits}}{10^9 \text{ bps}} = 10 \mu s$$

**Step 2: Offered load G.**
The station attempts 1 frame per millisecond = 1000 frames/sec. Each slot = 10 μs = $10^{-5}$ sec. So:

$$G = 1000 \text{ frames/s} \times 10^{-5} \text{ s/frame} = 0.01$$

**Step 3: Probability of success per slot.**

$$P_{success} = e^{-G} = e^{-0.01} \approx 0.9900$$

**Step 4: Expected successful transmissions per second.**

Throughput $S = G \cdot e^{-G} = 0.01 \cdot 0.9900 = 0.0099$ successful frames per frame time.

Slots per second = $1 / 10 \mu s = 10^5$ slots/s.

Successful frames per second = $S \times \text{slots/s} = 0.0099 \times 10^5 = 990$ frames/sec.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Converting **bytes → bits** (multiply by 8) is often forgotten.
> 2. *G* must be expressed in **attempts per slot** (or per frame time), not attempts per second.
> 3. The success probability $P = e^{-G}$ (slotted) and $P = e^{-2G}$ (pure) are different — read carefully which protocol the question asks for.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Numbers & Facts (KTU Hot List):**
>
> - Pure ALOHA max throughput = **0.184 (1/2e)** at G = **0.5**.
> - Slotted ALOHA max throughput = **0.368 (1/e)** at G = **1.0**.
> - Vulnerable window: Pure = **2T**, Slotted = **T**.
> - 1-Persistent CSMA: $E = 1/(1+2a)$; Non-Persistent: $E = 1/(1+a)$.
> - $a = (d \cdot R) / (v \cdot L)$ is dimensionless — *the most tested CSMA parameter*.
> - Bit-Map low-load throughput = $d/(N+d)$; high-load = $d/(1+d)$.
> - Ethernet uses **1-persistent CSMA/CD + Binary Exponential Backoff + 32-bit jam signal**.
> - Token Ring throughput approaches **1.0** at high load with **bounded delay**.
> - Limited-contention (Adaptive Tree) splits at depth $q$ where $2^q \approx k$ active stations.
> - CSMA/CA (WiFi) cannot detect collisions → uses **RTS/CTS** to avoid them.
>
> **Key Insight to Carry Forward:**
> "The evolution of MAC protocols is a story of trading **collision risk** for **coordination overhead** — from no-sensing ALOHA, to listen-before-talk CSMA, to listen-while-talking CSMA/CD, to switched full-duplex where the problem vanishes entirely."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Multiple Access Protocols # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 (Data Link Layer)** > Multiple Access Protocols sit at the heart of the **Data Link Layer sublayer (Logical Link Control / MAC Sublayer)**. They dictate how numerous independent stations share a single, shared broadcast medium without catastrophic collision destruction. ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Multiple Access Protocol (MAP)** is a deterministic or probabilistic set of rules that govern how multiple transmitting and receiving stations, connected to a common shared transmission medium (coaxial cable, optical fiber bus, wireless radio spectrum, satellite transponder), arbitrate access to that medium. The objective is to maximize channel utilization (throughput) while minimizing frame collision, average frame delay, and ensuring fairness among contending nodes. In the KTU 2024 scheme, MAPs are officially classified under the **IEEE 802 reference model**, specifically under the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Builder > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Conference Room Debate** Imagine a large round table with **8 people** in a conference room. Only **one microphone** is at the center, and only the person holding it can speak. If everyone grabs at once, **noise (collision)** occurs and nobody is understood. - **Random Access (ALOHA/CSMA):** Everyone just shouts whenever they have something to say. If two people shout at the exact same time, they both stop, wait a random duration, and try again. *(Simple, but inefficient when many users exist).* - **Controlled Access (Token Passing/Polling):** A wooden gavel (the *token*) is passed around. Only the person holding the gavel may speak. *(Efficient, ordered, but adds latency for token circulation).* - **Channelization (TDMA/FDMA/CDMA):** Each person gets a reserved time slot, a reserved pitch (frequency), or a unique code language to speak simultaneously. *(Like giving each speaker their own private invisible channel).* ## 1.3 Why Are Multiple Access Protocols Needed? The fundamental problem is the **Broadcast Channel Bottleneck**: | Property | Specification | |---|---| | **Bandwidth** | Shared, finite resource (e.g., **10 Mbps Ethernet**, **54 Mbps Wi-Fi**) | | **Collision Domain** | All connected stations hear each other | | **Cost** | Laying duplicate physical media is prohibitively expensive | | **Goal** | One shared medium must serve N users simultaneously with minimum collisions | > [!TIP] > **Exam Mnemonic — "RCC"** for the three KTU-syllabus families of MAPs: > **R**andom Access | **C**ontrolled Access | **C**hannelization # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 KTU Taxonomy of Multiple Access Protocols Multiple Access Protocols are mathematically and architecturally divided into three primary families: ### 2.1.1 Random Access Protocols (Contention-Based) **Philosophy:** "Transmit first, apologize later." Any station may transmit at any time without prior coordination. Collisions are an accepted statistical reality; recovery mechanisms repair the damage. **Operational Steps (Universal Logic):** 1. A station with a ready frame senses or transmits based on protocol rules. 2. If collision occurs, the station detects it (collision detection) or assumes it. 3. The station backs off for a **random retransmission delay**. 4. Retransmission is attempted after the timeout. **Sub-Protocols (KTU Sub-Syllabus):** - **Pure ALOHA** - **Slotted ALOHA** - **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)** — variants: 1-persistent, Non-Persistent, p-persistent - **CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)** — used in classic Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) - **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** — used in Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) ### 2.1.2 Controlled Access Protocols (Coordination-Based) **Philosophy:** "No one speaks without permission." A central controller or distributed token grants explicit transmission rights. **Sub-Protocols:** - **Reservation** — stations reserve slots in advance (e.g., IEEE 802.11 RTS/CTS) - **Polling** — central controller (master) polls each slave (used in mainframe terminals, 802.11 PCF) - **Token Passing** — logical ring circulates a 3-byte token frame (used in Token Ring IEEE 802.5, FDDI) ### 2.1.3 Channelization Protocols (Dedicated Allocation) **Philosophy:** "Divide the medium; each user gets a private slice." No contention; orthogonal allocation guarantees collision-free transmission. **Sub-Protocols:** - **FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)** — analog cellular 1G, satellite - **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)** — GSM 2G cellular - **CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access)** — 3G cellular (IS-95, UMTS, CDMA2000) ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Formulas for KTU ESE — Module 2** > The following table consolidates every numerical formula needed for board exam problems on Multiple Access Protocols. | Protocol | Key Formula | Variable Definitions | Maximum Throughput | |---|---|---|---| | **Pure ALOHA** | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S$ = Throughput (frames/frame-time), $G$ = Offered Load | $S_{max} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ i.e. **18.4%** | | **Slotted ALOHA** | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $S$ = Throughput, $G$ = Offered Load | $S_{max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ i.e. **36.8%** | | **CSMA/CD Efficiency** | $\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$ | $a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}$, $T_p$ = propagation time, $T_t$ = transmission time | Approaches **100%** as $a \to 0$ | | **Pure ALOHA Vulnerable Period** | $V_p = 2 \cdot T_{frame}$ | $T_{frame}$ = duration of one frame | **2 frame-times** | | **Slotted ALOHA Vulnerable Period** | $V_p = T_{frame}$ | — | **1 frame-time** | | **FDMA Channel Count** | $N = \lfloor B_{total} / B_{channel} \rfloor$ | $B_{total}$ = total bandwidth, $B_{channel}$ = per-user bandwidth | Integer bound | | **TDMA Frame Duration** | $T_{frame} = N \cdot T_{slot} + G_{guard}$ | $N$ = users, $T_{slot}$ = slot time, $G_{guard}$ = guard band time | — | | **CDMA Processing Gain** | $GP = \frac{W}{R}$ | $W$ = chip rate (chips/s), $R$ = data rate (bps) | — | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pure/Slotted ALOHA:** Historically used in the **ALOHAnet** (University of Hawaii, 1970s) — the conceptual grandparent of Ethernet. - **CSMA/CD:** The dominant wired LAN protocol for IEEE 802.3 Ethernet up to **1 Gbps**. Superseded by full-duplex switches (collision-free). - **CSMA/CA:** Mandatory for wireless (Wi-Fi 802.11) because radios cannot listen while transmitting (no collision detection possible). - **TDMA:** Used in **GSM** cellular systems — each mobile gets 1/8th of a 4.615 ms frame. - **CDMA:** Used in **3G cellular**, GPS, and some satellite systems — provides soft capacity, frequency reuse, and anti-jamming. - **FDMA:** Used in **analog 1G AMPS**, traditional radio broadcast, and cable TV upstream/downstream channelization. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** Modern networks increasingly use **switched full-duplex topologies**, making the contention-based protocols semi-obsolete for wired LANs. However, they remain critically relevant in **wireless networks (Wi-Fi, satellite, IoT)** and in **legacy industrial control systems** that still deploy bus architectures (CAN, Modbus). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pure ALOHA Maximum Throughput ### 3.1.1 System Model and Assumptions - All frames have **identical fixed length** $L$ bits. - Frame transmission time = 1 unit (frame-time). - Infinite population of independent stations generate new frames according to a **Poisson distribution** with mean $G$ frames per frame-time (offered load). - Vulnerable period = **2 × frame-time** (any frame whose leading edge arrives within ±1 frame-time of the start of our transmission collides with us). ### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation **Step 1:** Define throughput $S$ as the average number of successful transmissions per frame-time: $$S = G \cdot P(\text{success})$$ **Step 2:** Probability that no other station generates a frame during the vulnerable period. Since arrivals are Poisson with rate $G$ per frame-time, for time interval $t$: $$P(\text{0 arrivals in time } t) = e^{-G \cdot t}$$ **Step 3:** For Pure ALOHA, the vulnerable period is $t = 2$ frame-times. Substituting: $$P(\text{success}) = e^{-2G}$$ **Step 4:** Therefore, throughput becomes: $$S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$$ **Step 5:** To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $G$ and set to zero: $$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0$$ **Step 6:** Solving yields: $$1 - 2G = 0 \implies G_{opt} = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 7:** Substitute $G_{opt} = 0.5$ back into the throughput equation: $$S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-2 \cdot 0.5} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e}$$ **Step 8:** Numerical evaluation: $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 2.71828} \approx \frac{1}{5.43656} \approx 0.18394$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Pure ALOHA can deliver a maximum channel utilization of approximately **18.4%** — meaning **81.6%** of all transmitted frames are lost to collisions or remain backlogged. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slotted ALOHA Maximum Throughput ### 3.2.1 Modification of System Model - Time is divided into discrete **slots of duration = 1 frame-time**. - Stations may **only transmit at the beginning of a slot** (synchronized global clock required). ### 3.2.2 Mathematical Derivation **Step 1:** With synchronization, the vulnerable period shrinks from 2 frame-times to **1 frame-time** because transmissions can only begin at slot boundaries (a collision can only occur with a frame starting in the same slot). **Step 2:** Probability of success becomes: $$P(\text{success}) = e^{-G}$$ **Step 3:** Throughput equation: $$S = G \cdot e^{-G}$$ **Step 4:** Differentiation for maximum: $$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0$$ **Step 5:** Solving: $$G_{opt} = 1$$ **Step 6:** Substituting back: $$S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e}$$ **Step 7:** Numerical evaluation: $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{2.71828} \approx 0.36788$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Slotted ALOHA **doubles** Pure ALOHA's efficiency to **36.8%**, achieved at the cost of mandatory network-wide time synchronization. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CSMA/CD Efficiency (Ethernet) ### 3.3.1 Parameter Definition Let: - $T_t$ = transmission time of one frame = $\frac{L}{R}$ (Frame length / Bandwidth) - $T_p$ = one-way propagation delay = $\frac{d}{v}$ (Distance / Signal propagation speed) - $a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}$ = the ratio of propagation time to transmission time ### 3.3.2 Mathematical Derivation **Step 1:** Worst-case collision detection time for a frame at the most distant station = $2 \cdot T_p$ (signal must travel to the far end, collision propagates back). **Step 2:** Total time the channel is "wasted" per collision cycle (detection + jamming) $\approx 2 \cdot T_p + \text{jam signal}$. **Step 3:** Classical result derived by Metcalfe and Boggs (1976) for IEEE 802.3 with truncated binary exponential backoff: $$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful time}}{\text{Total time}} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2 \cdot T_p \cdot (\text{backoff factor})}$$ **Step 4:** Empirically validated expression (accounting for the worst-case slotting of backoff): $$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$$ **Step 5:** Limiting case analysis: $$\lim_{a \to 0} \eta = \frac{1}{1 + 0} = 1 \quad \text{(100% efficiency)}$$ **Step 6:** For typical Ethernet parameters: $T_p \approx 25.6 \, \mu s$ for 5 km coax, $T_t \approx 51.2 \, \mu s$ for 512-bit minimum frame at 10 Mbps, giving $a = 0.5$: $$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{1}{4.22} \approx 23.7\%$$ ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | 1-persistent CSMA | CSMA/CD | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Carrier Sensing** | No | No | Yes | Yes | | **Collision Detection** | No | No | No | Yes | | **Synchronization Required** | No | Yes | No | No | | **Max Throughput ($S_{max}$)** | **18.4%** | **36.8%** | **52.8%** | Up to **~95%** | | **Vulnerable Period** | $2 \cdot T_t$ | $T_t$ | $2 \cdot T_p$ | $2 \cdot T_p$ | | **Vulnerability Type** | Continuous | Slot boundary | Propagation | Propagation + jam | | **Practical Use** | ALOHAnet (legacy) | Legacy slotted systems | Theoretical baseline | IEEE 802.3 Ethernet | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Note on $S_{max} \approx 52.8\%$ for 1-persistent CSMA:** This theoretical maximum assumes **infinite propagation speed** ($T_p = 0$). In real networks with $T_p > 0$, throughput drops sharply as the propagation delay grows relative to frame transmission time. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Multiple Access Protocol Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Multiple Access Protocols"]:::root --> B["Random Access"]:::branch A --> C["Controlled Access"]:::branch A --> D["Channelization"]:::branch B --> B1["Pure ALOHA"]:::leaf B --> B2["Slotted ALOHA"]:::leaf B --> B3["CSMA"]:::leaf B --> B4["CSMA CD"]:::leaf B --> B5["CSMA CA"]:::leaf C --> C1["Reservation"]:::leaf C --> C2["Polling"]:::leaf C --> C3["Token Passing"]:::leaf D --> D1["FDMA"]:::leaf D --> D2["TDMA"]:::leaf D --> D3["CDMA"]:::leaf B1 -.Uses.-> X1["Transmit freely, vulnerable period = 2T"]:::note B2 -.Uses.-> X2["Transmit at slot start, vulnerable period = T"]:::note B3 -.Uses.-> X3["Sense carrier before transmit"]:::note B4 -.Uses.-> X4["Sense and detect collisions"]:::note B5 -.Uses.-> X5["Sense and avoid via backoff"]:::note classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#111827,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef branch fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef leaf fill:#10b981,stroke:#065f46,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1.5px classDef note fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 4 4 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — CSMA/CD Operational State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Sensing: Frame ready Sensing --> Transmitting: Channel idle Sensing --> Waiting: Channel busy Waiting --> Sensing: Backoff expired Transmitting --> Monitoring: Signal sent Monitoring --> Success: No collision detected Monitoring --> Collision: Collision detected Collision --> Jam: Send jam signal Jam --> Backoff: Increment attempt counter Backoff --> Sensing: After random delay Success --> Idle: Frame delivered Success --> [*]: Complete Backoff --> Failure: Max retries exceeded Failure --> [*]: Frame dropped ``` ## 4.3 TDMA Channelization — Time-Frequency-Code Slot Grid ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Frame["TDMA Superframe Structure"] direction LR slot1["Slot 1 — User A"]:::slotA slot2["Slot 2 — User B"]:::slotB slot3["Slot 3 — User C"]:::slotC slot4["Slot 4 — User D"]:::slotD slot5["Slot 5 — User E"]:::slotA slot6["Slot 6 — User F"]:::slotB guard["Guard Time"]:::guard end slot1 --> slot2 --> slot3 --> slot4 --> slot5 --> slot6 --> guard note1["Each user gets exclusive
time slice per cycle.
Guard time prevents
synchronization drift."]:::annotation -.- slot4 classDef slotA fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af classDef slotB fill:#8b5cf6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5b21b6 classDef slotC fill:#ec4899,color:#ffffff,stroke:#831843 classDef slotD fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#92400e classDef guard fill:#6b7280,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f2937 classDef annotation fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 4 4 ``` ## 4.4 CDMA Spread Spectrum — Code Channel Separation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["User Data Streams"] u1["User 1 — Voice 9.6 kbps"]:::data1 u2["User 2 — Voice 9.6 kbps"]:::data2 u3["User 3 — Voice 9.6 kbps"]:::data3 end subgraph Spread["Spread Spectrum Stage"] s1["Multiply by Code C1
Chips at 1.228 Mcps"]:::spread1 s2["Multiply by Code C2
Chips at 1.228 Mcps"]:::spread2 s3["Multiply by Code C3
Chips at 1.228 Mcps"]:::spread3 end u1 --> s1 u2 --> s2 u3 --> s3 subgraph Channel["Shared Radio Channel 25 MHz"] sum["SUMMED SPREAD SIGNAL
All codes overlap in
frequency and time"]:::channel end s1 --> sum s2 --> sum s3 --> sum subgraph Rx["Receiver Bank"] r1["Correlate with C1
Recover User 1"]:::rx1 r2["Correlate with C2
Recover User 2"]:::rx2 r3["Correlate with C3
Recover User 3"]:::rx3 end sum --> r1 sum --> r2 sum --> r3 classDef data1 fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a classDef data2 fill:#fce7f3,color:#831843 classDef data3 fill:#d1fae5,color:#065f46 classDef spread1 fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff classDef spread2 fill:#ec4899,color:#ffffff classDef spread3 fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff classDef channel fill:#1f2937,color:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px classDef rx1 fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a classDef rx2 fill:#fce7f3,color:#831843 classDef rx3 fill:#d1fae5,color:#065f46 ``` ## 4.5 Throughput Comparison Curve (Conceptual Plot) ```mermaid flowchart LR axis["X-axis: Offered Load G
Y-axis: Throughput S"]:::axis pa["Pure ALOHA
Peak S = 18.4% at G = 0.5"]:::curveA sa["Slotted ALOHA
Peak S = 36.8% at G = 1.0"]:::curveB csmacd["CSMA/CD
Peak S approaches 90%+"]:::curveC axis --- pa axis --- sa axis --- csmacd pa ~~~ sa ~~~ csmacd classDef axis fill:#f9fafb,stroke:#374151,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px classDef curveA fill:#fee2e2,color:#7f1d1d classDef curveB fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f classDef curveC fill:#d1fae5,color:#064e3b ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Compare Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA with respect to vulnerable period and maximum throughput.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Criterion | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | |---|---|---| | **Time Synchronization** | Not required | Required (global clock) | | **Vulnerable Period** | $2 \cdot T_{frame}$ | $T_{frame}$ | | **Throughput Equation** | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | | **Maximum Throughput** | $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ at $G = 0.5$ | $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ at $G = 1$ | The key advantage of Slotted ALOHA is the **halving of the vulnerable period** by restricting transmission to slot boundaries, doubling efficiency. The drawback is the requirement of a global clock synchronization mechanism. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Why is CSMA/CD unsuitable for wireless networks? How does CSMA/CA overcome this limitation?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** CSMA/CD is unsuitable for wireless networks because of two fundamental reasons: 1. **Collision Detection Impossible:** A radio station cannot simultaneously transmit and listen to the medium. The transmitted signal (often at **100 mW** or more) completely overwhelms any incoming received signal (micro-watts), making detection of a remote collision impossible during active transmission. 2. **Hidden Node Problem:** Station A may hear B, and B may hear C, but A may not hear C. A collision at B from A and C is undetectable at A. **CSMA/CA Solution:** CSMA/CA uses **collision avoidance via RTS/CTS handshake** (Request to Send / Clear to Send). A node reserves the channel by exchanging short control frames with the Access Point before transmitting data, plus uses **ACK-based** positive acknowledgment for reliability, and **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** for virtual carrier sensing. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module 2 question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are two alternative full-14-mark questions (Question A OR Question B style internal choice). --- ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024] **Q(a) [7 Marks]: Explain the working of Slotted ALOHA. Derive its maximum throughput.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. Working Principle of Slotted ALOHA (3 Marks):** - Time on the channel is divided into **discrete time slots** of duration $T$, where $T$ equals the transmission time of one fixed-length frame. - All stations are **synchronized** to a common clock (typically via a central station transmitting a periodic beacon). - A station wishing to transmit a frame **must wait until the beginning of the next time slot**. - If **no collision** occurs (only one frame was transmitted in the slot), the transmission succeeds. - If **two or more stations transmit in the same slot**, a collision occurs, all frames are lost, and the stations schedule retransmission at the end of a **random number of slots** (backoff). **2. Throughput Derivation (4 Marks):** Let $G$ = average number of transmission attempts per slot (Poisson distributed). Probability of exactly $k$ attempts in a slot: $$P(k) = \frac{G^k \cdot e^{-G}}{k!}$$ Successful transmission occurs only if **exactly one** station attempts ($k=1$): $$P(\text{success}) = P(1) = G \cdot e^{-G}$$ Throughput $S$ = average successful frames per slot: $$S = G \cdot e^{-G}$$ **3. Maximum Throughput Calculation:** $$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G_{opt} = 1$$ $$S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368 \text{ or } 36.8\%$$ **Q(b) [7 Marks]: With a neat diagram, explain CSMA/CD. Discuss how collisions are detected and the binary exponential backoff algorithm works.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. CSMA/CD Operation (3 Marks):** CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) extends CSMA by enabling stations to **monitor the channel during transmission** and immediately detect collisions, aborting transmission to save bandwidth. Used in IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. **Operational Steps:** 1. Station senses the carrier. If idle → transmit immediately. If busy → wait. 2. During transmission, station continues monitoring signal on the cable. 3. If collision detected → transmit a **32-bit jam sequence** to ensure all stations recognize the collision. 4. Terminate transmission, wait a **random backoff time**, then retry. 5. After **16 consecutive failures**, frame is dropped with an error report to upper layers. **2. Collision Detection Mechanism (2 Marks):** In Ethernet, collision detection uses DC voltage level sensing. A normal signal swings between $-2.5$V and $+2.5$V. When two stations transmit simultaneously, the superimposed voltage exceeds the threshold (around $\pm 1.5$V differential), triggering the collision detection circuitry. The minimum frame size of **64 bytes (512 bits)** at **10 Mbps** ensures that a station transmits for at least $51.2 \, \mu s$, long enough for the signal to propagate to the farthest station (max 2500 m) and for any collision to be detected before frame transmission completes — guaranteeing collision detection reliability. **3. Binary Exponential Backoff Algorithm (2 Marks):** After the $n$-th collision for a given frame, the station chooses a random integer $k$ uniformly from the range: $$k \in \left\{ 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{n} - 1 \right\} \quad \text{where } n \leq 10$$ Wait time = $k \times 51.2 \, \mu s$ (one slot time for 10 Mbps Ethernet). After 10 collisions, the range freezes at $\left\{ 0, \ldots, 1023 \right\}$. After 16 collisions, the attempt is aborted. This **exponentially increasing randomness** ensures the network adapts to congestion dynamically. --- ### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q(a) [7 Marks]: Describe the three broad categories of Multiple Access Protocols with at least one example for each. Compare their relative advantages and disadvantages.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. Random Access Protocols (2.5 Marks):** No station has superior priority; any station may transmit at any time. Collisions are accepted and recovered from via retransmission. **Examples:** Pure ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA. **Advantages:** Simple to implement, no central controller needed, low delay at low load. **Disadvantages:** Throughput collapses at high load due to collisions (e.g., Pure ALOHA max 18.4%). **2. Controlled Access Protocols (2.5 Marks):** A station may transmit only when explicitly authorized by a controller or when it holds a logical token. No collisions by design. **Examples:** - **Reservation** — stations send reservation requests, transmit in reserved slots (e.g., satellite systems). - **Polling** — central controller polls each station (e.g., 802.11 PCF, IBM SNA). - **Token Passing** — circulating 3-byte token grants right to transmit (e.g., Token Ring IEEE 802.5, FDDI). **Advantages:** Deterministic, predictable maximum delay, collision-free. **Disadvantages:** Single point of failure (controller/token loss), overhead for token circulation, complex coordination. **3. Channelization Protocols (2 Marks):** Channel bandwidth is divided into orthogonal sub-channels assigned to users. No contention; users transmit simultaneously without interference. **Examples:** - **FDMA** — divides bandwidth into frequency bands (analog 1G cellular). - **TDMA** — divides time into slots per user (GSM 2G). - **CDMA** — assigns unique orthogonal codes; users share full bandwidth (3G cellular, GPS). **Advantages:** Guaranteed bandwidth, no collisions, supports real-time traffic well. **Disadvantages:** Wasted idle allocation (TDMA), inflexible frequency planning (FDMA), complex power control required (CDMA). **Q(b) [7 Marks]: Explain FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA with appropriate diagrams. State one real-world application of each.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. FDMA — Frequency Division Multiple Access (2 Marks):** The total bandwidth $B$ is split into $N$ non-overlapping frequency bands, each assigned to one user permanently or on demand. Each user transmits simultaneously using a unique carrier frequency. Guard bands separate adjacent channels to prevent inter-channel interference. **Real-world Application:** Analog AMPS cellular (1G), cable TV downstream channel allocation. **2. TDMA — Time Division Multiple Access (2.5 Marks):** Users share the entire bandwidth but transmit in **discrete, non-overlapping time slots** within a repeating frame structure. Each user gets $T/N$ time per frame (where $N$ = users). Requires precise synchronization; uses **guard times** between slots to absorb timing jitter. **Example:** GSM (2G) — 8 users share a 200 kHz channel via 8 time slots per 4.615 ms TDMA frame. **3. CDMA — Code Division Multiple Access (2.5 Marks):** All users transmit simultaneously across the **entire bandwidth** using the **entire time**, distinguished only by unique pseudo-random spreading codes. Each data bit is XORed with a high-rate **chip sequence** (e.g., 1.228 Mchips/s for 9.6 kbps voice, yielding a processing gain of **128**). At the receiver, correlation with the correct code extracts that user's signal; others appear as low-level noise. **Example:** IS-95 (CDMA One) 2G/3G cellular, UMTS W-CDMA 3G, GPS satellite positioning (Gold codes). **Comparison of Capacity:** $$N_{FDMA} = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{total}}{B_{channel}} \right\rfloor \quad , \quad N_{TDMA} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{frame}}{T_{slot}} \right\rfloor \quad , \quad N_{CDMA} \approx \frac{GP}{E_b/N_0 \cdot \text{factor}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > - **Pitfall 1:** Students frequently write "$S = G \times e^{-2G}$" for Slotted ALOHA. The exponent MUST be $-G$, not $-2G$. Lose **2 marks** instantly. > - **Pitfall 2:** For CSMA/CD, students often forget to mention the **minimum frame size (64 bytes)** constraint and its link to maximum network diameter. The IEEE 802.3 standard requires both to be related via the relationship $d_{max} = v \times T_{frame}/2$. > - **Pitfall 3:** Confusing **CSMA/CA** with **CSMA/CD**. CSMA/CA is for **wireless**, CSMA/CD is for **wired**. Mixing up is an automatic **1-mark deduction**. > - **Pitfall 4:** For CDMA derivations, students sometimes forget to define **chips, chip rate, processing gain**, and **orthogonality of codes** — all KTU-mandated terminology. > - **Pitfall 5:** Writing through derivation for Slotted ALOHA without differentiating $S$ w.r.t. $G$ and showing $\frac{dS}{dG} = 0$ loses **2 marks** for skipping mathematical rigor. > - **Pitfall 6:** Omitting the **Poisson assumption** when deriving ALOHA throughput equations — examiners deduct **1 mark** for not justifying the model. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Multiple Access Protocols** **A. Classification (Must Memorize):** - Three families: **Random Access**, **Controlled Access**, **Channelization** - Random Access sub-types: ALOHA family, CSMA family, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA **B. Numerical Formulas (Board Exam Hot List):** - Pure ALOHA: $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, max = $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ - Slotted ALOHA: $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$, max = $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ - CSMA/CD efficiency: $\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a}$ where $a = T_p / T_t$ - FDMA: bandwidth division; TDMA: time division; CDMA: code division **C. Key Conceptual Differentiations:** - **Pure vs Slotted ALOHA:** Synchronization requirement halves vulnerable period, doubling efficiency - **CSMA vs CSMA/CD:** CSMA only senses before transmit; CSMA/CD also senses during transmit - **CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA:** Wired (collision detection) vs Wireless (collision avoidance) - **TDMA vs FDMA:** Time orthogonal vs Frequency orthogonal division - **CDMA vs TDMA/FDMA:** Users share full bandwidth and time; distinguished by code **D. Real-World Application Anchors:** - **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** → CSMA/CD - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** → CSMA/CA - **GSM (2G)** → TDMA with FDMA - **UMTS / 3G / GPS** → CDMA - **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)** → Token Passing - **ALOHAnet / Satellite Random Access** → ALOHA family **E. Vulnerable Period Quick Reference:** - Pure ALOHA: $2 \cdot T_{frame}$ - Slotted ALOHA: $T_{frame}$ - CSMA variants: $2 \cdot T_{propagation}$ **F. Mandatory KTU Terminology for Full Marks:** - Poisson arrival model - Binary Exponential Backoff - Jam sequence - Processing Gain (CDMA) - Guard band / Guard time - NAV (Network Allocation Vector) - Truncated binary exponential backoff - Maximum propagation delay **G. Examiner's Pet Questions (Predict for KTU 2024 ESE):** 1. Derive the throughput of Slotted ALOHA and show that it doubles Pure ALOHA. 2. Compare CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA in the context of wired vs wireless. 3. Explain the binary exponential backoff algorithm of Ethernet. 4. Distinguish between FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA with diagrams. 5. Why is minimum frame size (64 bytes) mandated in 802.3 Ethernet?

topics.title: Ethernet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ethernet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — ETHERNET: CORE DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethernet** is the dominant **Local Area Network (LAN)** technology standardized under the **IEEE 802.3** family. It defines the physical (PHY) layer signaling, the **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sub-layer protocol — primarily **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** — and the **frame format** for transmitting variable-size data packets over a shared medium (originally coaxial cable, later twisted-pair copper, and now fiber optics).

From the KTU 2024 syllabus standpoint, Ethernet belongs to the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** of the OSI model and is the most widely deployed wired LAN technology in the world. It operates at speeds ranging from **10 Mbps** (classical Ethernet) to **400 Gbps** (modern variants) while maintaining **backward compatibility** at the frame format level.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polite Conversation Highway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Multilingual Café Hallway**
> Imagine a narrow **one-way hallway** where many people want to walk through simultaneously.
> 1. Before stepping in, each person **listens** (Carrier Sense).
> 2. If someone is talking, you **wait** quietly (deferral).
> 3. If two people happen to start at the same instant, they bump — a **collision** occurs.
> 4. Both stop, say "Sorry!" (jam signal), wait a random **pause** (backoff), then try again.
> 5. If they keep colliding, each time the random pause gets **progressively wider** (binary exponential backoff), so they are less likely to clash again.

Ethernet's *MAC layer* runs exactly this protocol — making chaotic shared-media access orderly and statistically fair.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Data Rate:** **10 Mbps** (10BASE-T), **100 Mbps** (Fast Ethernet), **1 Gbps**, **10 Gbps**, up to **400 Gbps**.
> - **Maximum Cable Segment Length (10BASE5):** **500 m**.
> - **Maximum Cable Length (10BASE-T with 4 repeaters):** **2.5 km**.
> - **Minimum Frame Size:** **512 bits = 64 bytes** (mandatory for collision detection).
> - **Maximum Frame Size:** **1518 bytes** (without preamble/SFD).
> - **Slot Time (10 Mbps):** **51.2 μs**.
> - **MAC Address Size:** **48 bits (6 bytes)**.
> - **Repeaters Allowed:** Up to **4** between any two stations.

## 1.4 Ethernet Family Tree (Naming Convention)

The IEEE naming convention follows **`<Speed in Mbps><Signaling><Segment Type>`**:

| Standard | Speed | Signaling | Medium | Max Segment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **10BASE-5** | 10 Mbps | Baseband | Thick Coax | 500 m |
| **10BASE-2** | 10 Mbps | Baseband | Thin Coax | 185 m |
| **10BASE-T** | 10 Mbps | Baseband | Twisted Pair | 100 m |
| **100BASE-TX** | 100 Mbps | Baseband | Cat-5 TP | 100 m |
| **1000BASE-T** | 1 Gbps | Baseband | Cat-5e/6 | 100 m |
| **10GBASE-T** | 10 Gbps | Baseband | Cat-6a/7 | 100 m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The IEEE 802.3 Standard Architecture

Ethernet operates across **two sub-layers** of the OSI Data Link Layer:

1. **LLC (Logical Link Control) — IEEE 802.2:** Provides service access points to the Network Layer.
