| Duration/ID (2 B) | — | **Dropped** | — |
| Address 1 = RA = BSSID (6 B) | — | **Dropped** (this is the AP's MAC) | — |
| Address 2 = TA = STA MAC (6 B) | — | **Dropped** (redundant with SA) | — |
| Address 3 = SA = STA MAC (6 B) | SA | **Remapped to SA** (6 B) | SA (6 B) |
| Sequence Control (2 B) | — | **Dropped** | — |
| Body (Data) | Preserved | — | Data (variable) |
| FCS (4 B) | — | **Recomputed** | FCS (4 B) |
| — | — | **Inserted** | Preamble (7 B) + SFD (1 B) |
| DA (Server MAC) | Kept as DA | — | DA (6 B) |

**Role of BSSID and 4-Address Format (4 marks):**
- The **BSSID (Basic Service Set Identifier)** is the MAC address of the AP and acts as the *transit hub* for all wireless traffic in the BSS.
- The 4-address format (To DS / From DS bits) allows the AP to *tunnel* the original Source and Destination MAC addresses through the wireless header even though the immediate sender/receiver on the air interface is the AP itself.
- Specifically: **Address 1 (RA) = BSSID**, **Address 2 (TA) = STA**, **Address 3 = Original SA or DA** (depending on direction), and **Address 4** is reserved for WDS/mesh repeater chains.
- The AP must perform the 3-address → 4-address → 3-address mapping at the bridge boundary, which is functionally identical to the 802.3 ↔ 802.5 translation logic studied above.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "translation" with "encapsulation."** Many students write "the bridge just adds a header" — that is *encapsulation*, not *translation*. Translation *rebuilds* the frame.
> 2. **Always recompute the FCS** — never assume the original FCS is valid for the destination LAN. A 3-mark question can fail if you say "the FCS is preserved."
> 3. **Bit-order reversal is mandatory for non-canonical addressing.** Forgetting this is a guaranteed mark loss in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **MTU mismatch is asymmetric.** Students frequently say "the bridge drops oversized frames in both directions," but 802.3 → 802.5 is always safe.
> 5. **Mention both DA and SA handling, not just DA.** Examiners look for complete address-mapping coverage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **translational bridge** connects two LANs with **different IEEE 802 standards** and **rebuilds the frame** at the MAC layer.
- An **encapsulation bridge** preserves the **original frame** and wraps it in a backbone frame.
- The **seven incompatibilities** to resolve: frame format, addressing length, bit-order, MTU, data rate, priority, FCS polynomial.
- **802.3 fields** = Preamble + SFD + DA + SA + Length + Data + FCS.
- **802.5 fields** = SD + AC + FC + DA + SA + Data + FCS + ED + FS.
- **802.11 fields** = Frame Control + Duration/ID + Address 1–3 (or 4) + Seq Control + Data + FCS.
- **FCS must always be recomputed** using the same CRC-32 polynomial.
- **MTU problem** is asymmetric: 802.3 → 802.5 is safe; 802.5 → 802.3 is not.
- **Bit-order reversal** is needed when bridging canonical (802.3) to non-canonical (legacy 802.5) addresses.
- **Modern Wi-Fi APs are translational bridges** between 802.11 and 802.3.
- The **BSSID** is the AP's MAC address and serves as Address 1 (RA) in the 802.11 header.
- Translational bridging is a **Layer 2** function; fragmentation is a **Layer 3** concern (IP).
- Today, translational bridging is largely subsumed by **routers and Layer 3 switches**, but the *principle* lives on in Wi-Fi APs, IoT gateways, and VLAN translation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeaters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Repeaters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Repeater** is an electronic device that operates exclusively at the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) reference model**. Its sole function is to **regenerate, retime, and amplify** weakened or distorted signals received from one network segment and retransmit them onto another segment, thereby extending the physical reach of a Local Area Network (LAN) beyond the natural attenuation limits of the transmission medium.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a repeater is classified as a **Layer 1 (Physical Layer) connectivity device** — it is transparent to data, meaning it does not interpret MAC addresses, IP addresses, frames, or packets. It simply deals with raw **bits** in the form of electrical, optical, or radio signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Repeaters operate purely on the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)**. They do **NOT** understand frames, packets, MAC addresses, or IP addresses. They regenerate bit-level signals only.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are whispering a message to a friend seated at the very back of a long, noisy auditorium. By the time your voice reaches them, the words have become jumbled and faint because the sound weakened (attenuated) and mixed with background noise.

A **Repeater** is like a person standing in the **middle of the auditorium** who carefully **listens to your whisper, cleans up the noise, and re-shouts** the message with fresh energy to the next section of people. This "re-shouter" doesn't know what the message means — they just faithfully reproduce the pattern of sound (analogous to electrical pulses) so that the person at the back hears it just as clearly as the person in the front row.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standards

- **Standard Ethernet Cable Maximum Segment Length (10BASE-T / 10BASE-5):** **100 meters** (for twisted pair), **500 meters** (for thick coax)
- **Attenuation:** Signal power loss measured in **decibels (dB)**
- **Propagation Delay:** Time taken for a bit to travel end-to-end, measured in **microseconds (µs)**
- **5-4-3 Rule:** Maximum of **5** segments, **4** repeaters, and **3** populated segments in classic 10BASE5 Ethernet
- **Latency introduced by a repeater:** Typically less than **1 microsecond (1 µs)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** A Repeater is a **Layer 1 Physical Layer device** whose sole purpose is to **reconstruct (regenerate) attenuated or noisy signals** to their original strength and shape, allowing network segments to be joined and extended beyond the natural cable-length limitations of the transmission medium.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Repeaters Needed? — The Problem of Signal Degradation

As a signal travels through any physical medium (copper wire, optical fiber, or air), it suffers from three primary impairments:

1. **Attenuation:** The progressive loss of signal amplitude (power) as distance increases.
2. **Noise Interference:** Random unwanted electrical energy that distorts the original signal.
3. **Distortion:** Change in signal shape due to the differing propagation speeds of different frequency components.

When attenuation is severe enough, the receiving node cannot reliably distinguish between a logical '0' and a logical '1' — this leads to a high **Bit Error Rate (BER)**.

### 2.1.1 Signal Regeneration vs. Simple Amplification

A true repeater does **not** merely amplify the signal. There is a critical difference:

| Aspect | Simple Amplifier | True Repeater (Regenerator) |
|---|---|---|
| Action on noise | Amplifies noise **along with** the signal | **Reconstructs** the signal, discards noise |
| Output quality | Degraded (noise + signal amplified together) | Near-original (clean bit pattern restored) |
| Use case | Audio systems, RF boosters | Digital networks (Ethernet, fiber optics) |
| OSI Layer | Not a network device | Layer 1 (Physical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** A repeater is a **regenerator**, not just an amplifier. It decodes the incoming bits, removes noise, and re-creates a clean, fresh copy of the original bit stream. This is why it is mandatory to mention "regeneration" in KTU theory answers.

## 2.2 Operational Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

A repeater operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Signal Reception:** The repeater receives a weakened electrical/optical signal from the incoming segment.
2. **Sampling & Decision:** The repeater samples the incoming waveform at the **bit period** boundaries and makes a binary decision (threshold detection) — interpreting each sample as either a '0' or '1'.
3. **Clock Recovery:** The repeater extracts the timing clock from the incoming bit stream (retiming) to maintain synchronization.
4. **Signal Reconstruction:** A clean, fresh pulse representing each bit is generated internally.
5. **Retransmission:** The freshly constructed bit pulse is transmitted onto the outgoing segment at full original strength.

## 2.3 Types of Repeaters

### 2.3.1 Based on Function
- **Analog Repeater:** Amplifies the entire incoming waveform (signal + noise). Used in radio/TV broadcasting.
- **Digital Repeater (True Regenerator):** Recovers bits, removes noise, and retransmits. Used in Ethernet and digital telecommunications.

### 2.3.2 Based on Transmission Medium
- **Electrical Repeater:** For copper cables (10BASE-T, 10BASE-2).
- **Optical Repeater (Optical-Electrical-Optical Converter):** For fiber optic cables. Converts optical signal → electrical → re-transmits as optical.
- **Radio Repeater (Wireless):** Receives radio waves, amplifies/regenerates, and re-broadcasts. Used in cellular networks and Wi-Fi extenders.

### 2.3.3 Based on Physical Placement
- **Local Repeater:** Placed in-line between two cable segments within the same building.
- **Remote Repeater:** Connects two physically distant network segments (e.g., between two buildings) using a separate "drop" cable.

## 2.4 The 5-4-3 Rule (Classic Ethernet)

In legacy 10BASE5 (Thick Ethernet) and 10BASE2 (Thin Ethernet) networks, the IEEE 802.3 standard defined a strict design rule to prevent excessive propagation delay and collision-domain problems:

- **5:** Maximum of **5** network segments in series.
- **4:** Maximum of **4** repeaters connecting these segments.
- **3:** Maximum of **3** segments may have **attached stations (hosts)**; the remaining 2 are "inter-repeater links" used solely to connect repeaters.

This rule ensures the round-trip propagation delay stays within the **51.2 µs** slot time required for proper CSMA/CD collision detection.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise LANs:** Extending Ethernet across large office floors or warehouses.
- **Telecommunications:** Regenerating optical signals in long-haul submarine fiber cables (e.g., transoceanic cables use dozens of in-line regenerators).
- **Cellular Networks:** Cellular base stations use radio repeaters to extend coverage in tunnels, valleys, and rural areas.
- **Industrial Automation:** Extending fieldbus networks (Profibus, Modbus) across factory floors.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signal Attenuation (Power Loss) | $L = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | **Decibels (dB)** | Computing signal power loss over distance |
| Received Signal Power | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} - L_{total}$ | **dBm** | Estimating signal at receiver |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $\text{SNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | **dB** | Determining link quality |
| Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **bits/second (bps)** | Maximum error-free data rate |
| Bit Error Rate (BER) | $\text{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total bits transmitted}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Quantifying link reliability |
| 5-4-3 Rule Limits | Max 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated | Discrete integers | Legacy Ethernet design constraint |
| Maximum Segment Length (10BASE-T) | $D_{max} = 100$ m | **meters** | Twisted-pair copper segment limit |
| Propagation Delay per Meter (typical) | $t_{prop} \approx 5$ ns/m | **ns/m** | Estimating round-trip time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The **Shannon Capacity** formula $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ is the most commonly tested formula in Part A (3-mark) questions. Always state it in words: "Channel capacity in bits per second equals bandwidth in Hertz times log base 2 of one plus signal-to-noise ratio."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Repeaters in a Bus Network

Consider a classic 10BASE5 Ethernet network with the following IEEE 802.3 constraints:

- Maximum end-to-end propagation delay allowed: $T_{max} = 51.2\ \mu s$ (slot time)
- Signal propagation velocity in coax: $v \approx 2 \times 10^8\ m/s$ (≈ 200 m/µs, i.e., 5 ns/m)
- Maximum single-segment cable length: $L_{seg} = 500\ m$

**Step 1 — Compute one-way propagation delay for a single segment:**

$$t_{one\text{-}way} = \frac{L_{seg}}{v} = \frac{500\ m}{2 \times 10^8\ m/s} = 2.5\ \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Convert to round-trip delay (signal must travel to the far end and the collision jam signal must return):**

$$t_{round\text{-}trip} = 2 \times 2.5\ \mu s = 5.0\ \mu s$$

**Step 3 — For the 5-4-3 configuration, the worst case is signal traversing the full 5 segments:**

$$t_{total\text{-}one\text{-}way} = 5 \times 2.5\ \mu s = 12.5\ \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Round-trip for the entire network:**

$$t_{round\text{-}trip\text{-}total} = 2 \times 12.5\ \mu s = 25.0\ \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Verify against slot time:**

$$25.0\ \mu s < 51.2\ \mu s \quad \checkmark$$

The design satisfies the collision-detection requirement. Adding a 5th repeater is permissible only because the accumulated round-trip delay still leaves a safety margin of $51.2 - 25.0 = 26.2\ \mu s$ to account for repeater latency and inter-repeater cable delays.

## 3.2 Derivation: Attenuation and Required Repeater Spacing

Given a copper cable with attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 10\ dB/km$ and a minimum receivable power threshold of $P_{rx,\min} = -30\ dBm$, with transmit power $P_{tx} = 0\ dBm$:

**Step 1 — Express received power in dB:**

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} - \alpha \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$-30 = 0 - (10 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot d \cdot 1000$$

$$-30 = -10 \cdot d \quad \text{where } d \text{ is in km}$$

$$d = 3\ km$$

**Step 3 — Conclude maximum cable length without a repeater is 3 km. A repeater must be inserted before this distance to restore the signal.**

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Repeaters vs Other Devices)

| Feature | Repeater | Hub | Bridge | Switch | Router |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) |
| **Reads MAC Address?** | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Reads IP Address?** | No | No | No | No | Yes |
| **Collision Domains** | Same (one big domain) | Same | Separates domains | Separates per port | Separates per interface |
| **Broadcast Domains** | Same | Same | Same | Same | Separates |
| **Primary Function** | Regenerate bits | Multiport repeater | Filter frames by MAC | Forward frames intelligently | Route packets between networks |
| **Number of Ports** | Typically 2 | Multiple (4/8/16/24) | Typically 2 | Many | Many |
| **Intelligent?** | No (transparent) | No | Partially | Yes | Yes (highly) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A 10BASE5 Ethernet LAN has a total end-to-end cable length of **2400 m**. The signal propagation speed is **200 m/µs** and each repeater introduces a latency of **0.5 µs**. The network uses **4 repeaters** in series. Calculate:

(a) The one-way propagation delay of the cable alone.
(b) The total one-way delay including repeaters.
(c) Whether the network can correctly perform CSMA/CD collision detection. (Slot time = 51.2 µs)

### Solution

**(a) One-way propagation delay of cable:**

$$t_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2400\ m}{200\ m/\mu s} = 12\ \mu s$$

**(b) Total one-way delay including 4 repeaters:**

$$t_{total} = t_{prop} + (N_{repeaters} \times t_{repeater})$$

$$t_{total} = 12\ \mu s + (4 \times 0.5\ \mu s) = 12 + 2 = 14\ \mu s$$

**(c) Round-trip delay check (CSMA/CD requires the collision signal to return within slot time):**

$$t_{round\text{-}trip} = 2 \times t_{total} = 2 \times 14 = 28\ \mu s$$

$$28\ \mu s < 51.2\ \mu s \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The network can successfully perform CSMA/CD collision detection because the round-trip delay (28 µs) is well within the 51.2 µs slot time limit, leaving a **23.2 µs safety margin**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Repeater

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    segA[Network Segment A] -->|Attenuated Signal| rxBlock[Receiver Front End]
    rxBlock --> ampBlock[Amplifier Stage]
    ampBlock --> clkBlock[Clock Recovery Circuit]
    clkBlock --> detectBlock[Threshold Decision Circuit]
    detectBlock --> reshapeBlock[Signal Reconstruction Block]
    reshapeBlock --> txBlock[Transmitter Driver]
    txBlock -->|Regenerated Signal| segB[Network Segment B]
    
    style segA fill:#e1f5ff
    style segB fill:#e1f5ff
    style rxBlock fill:#fff4e1
    style ampBlock fill:#fff4e1
    style clkBlock fill:#fff4e1
    style detectBlock fill:#fff4e1
    style reshapeBlock fill:#fff4e1
    style txBlock fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.2 Repeater Placement in Extended LAN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hostA1[Host A1] --> segA1[Segment 1]
    hostA2[Host A2] --> segA1
    segA1 --> rep1[Repeater 1]
    rep1 --> segA2[Segment 2 Inter Link]
    segA2 --> rep2[Repeater 2]
    rep2 --> segA3[Segment 3]
    hostA3[Host A3] --> segA3
    hostA4[Host A4] --> segA3
    segA3 --> rep3[Repeater 3]
    rep3 --> segA4[Segment 4 Inter Link]
    segA4 --> rep4[Repeater 4]
    rep4 --> segA5[Segment 5]
    hostA5[Host A5] --> segA5
    hostA6[Host A6] --> segA5
    
    style rep1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style rep2 fill:#ffe1e1
    style rep3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style rep4 fill:#ffe1e1
    style segA1 fill:#d4f1d4
    style segA3 fill:#d4f1d4
    style segA5 fill:#d4f1d4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In the 5-4-3 rule illustration above, **5 segments** are connected by **4 repeaters**, and only **3 segments** (1, 3, and 5) have attached hosts. Segments 2 and 4 are pure inter-repeater links with no stations.

## 4.3 Signal Regeneration Concept Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    txSig[Original Transmitted Signal Clean Pulse] --> dist1[Travels Through Copper Cable]
    dist1 --> attn[Attenuated Noisy Signal At Receiver]
    attn --> regenBlock[Repeater Regenerates Discards Noise]
    regenBlock --> cleanSig[Restored Clean Signal Full Strength]
    cleanSig --> dist2[Continues Down Next Cable Segment]
    
    style txSig fill:#c8e6c9
    style attn fill:#ffcdd2
    style cleanSig fill:#c8e6c9
    style regenBlock fill:#fff9c4
```

## 4.4 OSI Layer Placement of Network Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    osi7[Application Layer Layer 7 Gateway Proxy]
    osi6[Presentation Layer Layer 6 Gateway Proxy]
    osi5[Session Layer Layer 5 Gateway Proxy]
    osi4[Transport Layer Layer 4 Gateway Proxy]
    osi3[Network Layer Layer 3 Router]
    osi2[Data Link Layer Layer 2 Bridge Switch]
    osi1[Physical Layer Layer 1 Repeater Hub]
    
    osi1 --> osi2
    osi2 --> osi3
    osi3 --> osi4
    osi4 --> osi5
    osi5 --> osi6
    osi6 --> osi7
    
    style osi1 fill:#ffcccb
    style osi2 fill:#ffe4b5
    style osi3 fill:#fffacd
    style osi4 fill:#e0e0e0
    style osi5 fill:#e0e0e0
    style osi6 fill:#e0e0e0
    style osi7 fill:#e0e0e0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Cue:** In the OSI layer diagram, the **Repeater** is anchored at the very bottom — **Layer 1 (Physical)** — confirming it has **zero awareness of any higher-layer constructs** like MAC addresses, IP addresses, or ports.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Repeater. At which layer of the OSI model does it operate?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** A Repeater is a physical device that receives, regenerates, and retransmits signals from one network segment to another to extend the physical range of a LAN.
- **(1 Mark)** It operates at the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the OSI model.
- **(1 Mark)** It is transparent to data — it does not interpret MAC addresses, IP addresses, or frame contents. Its only function is to **regenerate (not merely amplify)** the bit stream, removing accumulated noise in the process.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Repeater and an Amplifier.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** An **amplifier** boosts the entire incoming signal (signal + noise) and is typically used in analog systems.
- **(1 Mark)** A **repeater** regenerates the digital bit pattern by sampling the input, deciding whether each bit is '0' or '1', and retransmitting a fresh, clean copy.
- **(1 Mark)** Therefore, a repeater **removes noise** during regeneration, while an amplifier **amplifies the noise along with the signal**, leading to progressive signal degradation if used in long digital links.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **5-4-3 rule** used in classical Ethernet networks. Why is this rule important, and how does it relate to the **51.2 µs slot time**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture and working of a digital repeater**. Discuss the **role of clock recovery and threshold detection** in the regeneration process. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) The 5-4-3 Rule (7 Marks):**

The 5-4-3 rule is a design guideline defined in the IEEE 802.3 standard for classical Ethernet (10BASE5 / 10BASE2) bus networks to ensure proper **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** operation.

- **(2 Marks)** The rule states that a single Ethernet collision domain may consist of a maximum of:
  - **5** network segments connected in series,
  - **4** repeaters connecting these segments,
  - **3** segments that have **attached end stations** (the other 2 are inter-repeater links).

- **(2 Marks)** The rule is necessary because of **signal propagation delay**. For CSMA/CD to work correctly, a station must be able to detect any collision that occurs anywhere in the network before it finishes transmitting the minimum frame (which is **64 bytes = 512 bits** at 10 Mbps = 51.2 µs).

- **(2 Marks)** If the round-trip propagation delay exceeds 51.2 µs, a station may finish transmitting a short frame **before** the collision signal returns from the far end, causing the collision to go undetected and the frame to be lost. The 5-4-3 rule guarantees that the total round-trip delay (5 segments × 500 m × 2 × 5 ns/m = 25 µs) remains comfortably below 51.2 µs.

- **(1 Mark)** Conclusion: The 5-4-3 rule ensures collision detection integrity and bounds the maximum network diameter in classical Ethernet.

**(b) Internal Architecture of a Digital Repeater (7 Marks):**

- **(2 Marks)** A digital repeater consists of the following functional blocks:
  1. **Receiver Front-End** – Captures the incoming attenuated signal from the cable.
  2. **Amplifier Stage** – Boosts the weak signal to a processable level.
  3. **Clock Recovery Circuit (PLL)** – Extracts the bit timing from the incoming data stream using a Phase-Locked Loop.
  4. **Threshold Detector / Decision Circuit** – Samples the waveform at the center of each bit cell and decides whether the bit is '0' or '1' by comparing against a voltage threshold.
  5. **Signal Reconstructor** – Generates a fresh, clean pulse representing the decided bit.
  6. **Transmitter Driver** – Sends the reconstructed bit onto the outgoing segment at full power.

- **(2 Marks)** **Role of Clock Recovery:** Because the incoming signal is noisy and possibly distorted, the repeater needs an accurate timing reference to know *when* to sample each bit. The PLL locks onto the transitions in the incoming bit stream and provides a clean sampling clock. Without clock recovery, the threshold detector would sample at the wrong instants and make incorrect bit decisions.

- **(2 Marks)** **Role of Threshold Detection:** The threshold detector converts the analog waveform into a clean digital logic level. Any noise present in the input signal that falls below the threshold is effectively rejected. This is the *key step* that makes a repeater a regenerator rather than a mere amplifier — noise is **discarded**, not amplified.

- **(1 Mark)** Block diagram: (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic flow).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** A 10BASE5 Ethernet network spans **2500 m** end-to-end. Signal propagation speed is **200 m/µs**, and each repeater adds **0.6 µs** of latency. The network uses **3 repeaters**. Compute the **one-way cable delay**, **total one-way delay**, and **round-trip delay**. Will CSMA/CD work correctly? Justify with reference to the **51.2 µs slot time**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of repeaters**. Explain why a repeater is **not a security device** and why it **cannot filter traffic** like a bridge or switch. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

- **(1 Mark)** One-way cable propagation delay:

$$t_{cable} = \frac{2500\ m}{200\ m/\mu s} = 12.5\ \mu s$$

- **(2 Marks)** Total one-way delay (cable + 3 repeaters):

$$t_{total} = t_{cable} + (N_{rep} \times t_{rep}) = 12.5 + (3 \times 0.6) = 12.5 + 1.8 = 14.3\ \mu s$$

- **(2 Marks)** Round-trip delay:

$$t_{RTD} = 2 \times 14.3 = 28.6\ \mu s$$

- **(2 Marks)** **Verdict for CSMA/CD:**

$$28.6\ \mu s < 51.2\ \mu s \quad \checkmark$$

**CSMA/CD will work correctly** because the round-trip delay (28.6 µs) is well within the IEEE 802.3 slot time of 51.2 µs, leaving a safety margin of 22.6 µs for jitter and inter-frame gaps.

**(b) Repeater Advantages, Limitations, and Security (7 Marks):**

**Advantages (3 Marks):**
1. **Simple and inexpensive** — repeaters are among the cheapest networking devices.
2. **Extends network reach** — overcomes the 100 m / 500 m cable-length limits of Ethernet.
3. **Transparent operation** — no software/configuration needed; it works at the bit level.
4. **Improves signal quality** — by regenerating (not amplifying), it actively removes accumulated noise.

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
1. **Cannot filter traffic** — every bit is retransmitted in both directions; no segmentation of collision domains.
2. **Does not reduce congestion** — in fact, it **increases** the size of the collision domain, worsening collision probability.
3. **No intelligence** — cannot differentiate between valid frames, corrupted frames, or noise bursts.

**Why a Repeater is NOT a Security Device and Cannot Filter Traffic (2 Marks):**
A repeater has **no awareness of MAC addresses, IP addresses, or frame boundaries**. It simply reproduces the electrical/optical bit pattern. Consequently:
- It **cannot drop frames** based on source/destination MAC (a bridge can).
- It **cannot segment broadcast domains** (a router can).
- It **cannot enforce access control lists** (a firewall can).
- Any malicious traffic is faithfully forwarded in both directions — repeaters contribute **zero security**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write that a repeater "amplifies" the signal. Use the precise term **"regenerates"** to score full marks.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the 5-4-3 rule with the modern switched Ethernet — it applies only to legacy 10BASE5/10BASE2 shared-media Ethernet with CSMA/CD.
> - **Do NOT** attribute MAC-address filtering or routing capability to a repeater — these are Layer 2 / Layer 3 functions.
> - Always mention the **51.2 µs slot time** when justifying the 5-4-3 rule — examiners explicitly look for this number.
> - In numerical problems, **carry the units** (µs, m) throughout the calculation. Skipping units is a common 0.5-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Repeater = Layer 1 (Physical Layer) device** — operates on raw bits, not frames.
- **Function:** Regenerate (not merely amplify) the signal to remove noise and restore amplitude.
- **Does not** interpret MAC addresses, IP addresses, or frame structure.
- **Cannot** filter traffic, segment broadcast domains, or provide security.
- **Increases collision domain size** (all segments connected by repeaters share one collision domain).
- **5-4-3 Rule:** Max 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments (legacy 10BASE5 Ethernet).
- **51.2 µs slot time** governs the maximum network diameter in CSMA/CD Ethernet.
- **Types:** Electrical, optical, and radio (wireless) repeaters; analog vs digital (regenerative).
- **Modern relevance:** Largely replaced by switches, bridges, and optical amplifiers (EDFAs). Still used in long-haul fiber, cellular networks, and industrial buses.
- **Shannon Capacity Formula** (high-yield for KTU Part A): $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- **Key exam keywords to use in answers:** "regenerate," "Physical Layer," "bit-level," "transparent," "collision domain," "5-4-3 rule," "51.2 µs slot time."
- **Distinguish clearly:** Repeater (Layer 1) vs Hub (Layer 1 multiport) vs Bridge (Layer 2) vs Switch (Layer 2) vs Router (Layer 3).
- **Always** justify numerical answers by comparing the computed round-trip delay against the **51.2 µs** threshold to conclude CSMA/CD viability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hubs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hubs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Hub** is a physical layer (Layer 1 of the OSI Reference Model) networking device used to connect multiple Ethernet devices, making them act as a single network segment. Functionally, a hub is a **multiport repeater** — it receives an incoming electrical signal on one port and regenerates (repeats) and broadcasts that identical signal out through every other active port simultaneously, without any intelligent frame inspection, MAC-address learning, or filtering logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, a hub is strictly classified as a **Layer 1 (Physical Layer) device**, not a Data Link Layer device. Students frequently lose marks by incorrectly labeling it as a Layer 2 device — always state "Physical Layer / Layer 1."

Key operating characteristics:
- Operates on **electrical signals / bits** (does not interpret frames).
- Number of ports typically ranges from **4, 8, 12, 16, or 24 ports**.
- Standard port speed: **10 Mbps** (legacy), **100 Mbps** (Fast Ethernet hub), rarely 1 Gbps.
- Forms a **shared media broadcast domain**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **public-address (PA) speaker system in a classroom**:
- One student (device A) speaks into a microphone (sends data).
- The amplifier (hub) does not understand what is being said — it just amplifies the voice and pipes it out through **every speaker in the room**, including the speaker right next to the speaker (back to the same person).
- Every other listener (devices B, C, D, ...) hears the message and decides whether it is meant for them.

The hub behaves exactly like this amplifier:
1. It blindly amplifies whatever electrical signal arrives at one port.
2. It **floods** the signal out of all other ports.
3. The receiving devices themselves decide to accept or drop the frame based on the destination MAC address.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Insight:** Because the hub cannot differentiate ports, every connected device sees *everyone else's traffic*. This is why hubs are inherently insecure and inefficient compared to switches.

## 1.3 Physical vs Logical Layer Placement

| Property | Hub | Switch |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| Intelligence | None (no decisions) | Learns MAC addresses |
| Traffic handling | Broadcasts to all | Forwards selectively |
| Collision Domain | **Single** (one big collision domain) | **Separate** per port |
| Duplex | Half-duplex only | Full-duplex supported |

## 1.4 Classification of Hubs

KTU frequently tests the three-tier classification of hubs. Memorize this hierarchy:

1. **Passive Hub** — Acts purely as a physical connector (signal splitter). It does **not regenerate** the signal. Acts like a simple junction box. Maximum cable distance is severely limited.
2. **Active Hub** — Functions as a **multiport repeater**. It **regenerates and amplifies** the incoming signal before retransmission, extending the effective cable distance. Most common type.
3. **Intelligent Hub** (sometimes called "Smart Hub") — An active hub with embedded **management/monitoring features** (SNMP, port status, traffic statistics). It is essentially the precursor to modern managed switches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step

When a frame's electrical signal arrives at a hub port, the following deterministic sequence occurs:

- **Step 1 — Signal Reception:** The hub's physical interface receives the encoded bits (e.g., Manchester or NRZ encoding) on the incoming pair of the twisted pair cable.
- **Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:** The hub's repeater circuitry cleans, amplifies, and retimes the signal to restore its original amplitude. This is the **repeater function**.
- **Step 3 — Collision Detection (CSMA/CD):** The hub senses if the voltage on the segment exceeds the collision threshold. If so, it generates a **32-bit jam signal** to all ports, ensuring every device recognizes the collision.
- **Step 4 — Broadcasting:** The conditioned signal is simultaneously transmitted out of **every other port** (never back to the source port, theoretically).
- **Step 5 — Reception Decision:** Each connected NIC (Network Interface Card) reads the frame's destination MAC address and either accepts (if matched) or discards the frame in hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why broadcast?** Because the hub has no MAC-address table. It cannot know which port leads to which destination. Hence it must flood. This is the root cause of hub inefficiency.

## 2.2 Bandwidth and Throughput Mathematics

Because every port shares the same physical medium and every transmission is broadcast to all ports, the **effective bandwidth per node degrades linearly** with the number of active transmitting devices.

### 2.2.1 Effective Bandwidth per Node (Theoretical Worst Case)

Let:
- $B$ = Aggregate link bandwidth of the hub (in Mbps).
- $N$ = Number of actively transmitting nodes.
- $T_{slot}$ = Time slot for one frame transmission.

The aggregate throughput $R_{agg}$ and average per-node throughput $R_{node}$ under simultaneous transmission are:

$$
R_{agg} = B
$$

$$
R_{node} = \frac{B}{N}
$$

### 2.2.2 Maximum Cable Length and 5-4-3 Rule

Ethernet hubs operating at 10BASE-T must obey the **5-4-3 Repeater Rule**:

> In a shared Ethernet segment using repeaters/hubs:
> - There can be a maximum of **5** segments of cable.
> - Connected by a maximum of **4** repeaters/hubs.
> - Only **3** of those segments may have end devices (nodes) attached.
> - The other 2 segments are used purely as inter-hub links.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per-node bandwidth | $R_{node} = B / N$ | Mbps | $N$ = active transmitters |
| 5-4-3 Rule | 5 segments, 4 hubs, 3 populated | count | Applies to 10BASE-T shared Ethernet |
| Max segment length (10BASE-T) | 100 | metres | Twisted-pair copper |
| Max segment length (10BASE-FL fiber) | 2000 | metres | Fiber-optic variant |
| Collision domain size | All ports = 1 domain | logical | Hub = single collision domain |
| Broadcast domain size | All ports = 1 domain | logical | Hub = single broadcast domain |
| Jam signal length | 32 | bits | Standard CSMA/CD jam sequence |
| Slot time (10 Mbps Ethernet) | 51.2 | microseconds (µs) | 512 bit-times |
| Slot time (100 Mbps Ethernet) | 5.12 | microseconds (µs) | 512 bit-times |
| Round-trip delay budget | $\le$ 575 | bit-times | Worst-case to detect collision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The 5-4-3 rule is critical when designing legacy 10BASE-T hub-based networks. Modern star-topology networks with switches do **not** require this rule because each switch port is its own collision domain.

## 2.4 Limitations of Hubs (Why They Are Obsolete)

1. **Single Collision Domain:** Every device competes for the same medium → throughput collapses under heavy load.
2. **No Security:** Any device with a packet sniffer (e.g., Wireshark) can capture all traffic passing through the hub — classic **promiscuous mode attack**.
3. **Half-Duplex Only:** Devices cannot send and receive simultaneously; they must use CSMA/CD.
4. **Bandwidth Waste:** Even unicast traffic is flooded everywhere.
5. **Broadcast Storms:** Malfunctioning devices can flood the network with broadcast frames.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not write "hubs create separate collision domains per port" — this describes a **switch**, not a hub. Hubs create a **single shared collision domain** across all ports.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Problem: Computing Per-Node Effective Throughput

### Problem Statement

A **10 Mbps** Ethernet hub has **8 devices** connected to its ports. Assume all 8 devices are actively transmitting simultaneously under ideal CSMA/CD conditions (no collisions, no retransmissions). Compute the effective per-node throughput. Repeat the calculation when only **2 devices** are transmitting.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $B = 10 \text{ Mbps}$
- Aggregate link capacity of the hub.

**Case 1: 8 active transmitters**

Apply the formula:

$$
R_{node} = \frac{B}{N}
$$

Substitute $B = 10$ Mbps and $N = 8$:

$$
R_{node}^{(8)} = \frac{10 \text{ Mbps}}{8}
$$

$$
R_{node}^{(8)} = 1.25 \text{ Mbps per node}
$$

**Case 2: 2 active transmitters**

$$
R_{node}^{(2)} = \frac{10 \text{ Mbps}}{2}
$$

$$
R_{node}^{(2)} = 5 \text{ Mbps per node}
$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the active transmitters from 2 to 8 quadruples the contention, reducing per-node throughput by the same factor (5 → 1.25 Mbps).

> [!NOTE]
> This linear-degradation model is **idealized**. In reality, CSMA/CD collisions and backoff algorithms make the effective throughput significantly lower than $B/N$, especially when $N > 4$ for 10 Mbps Ethernet.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem: Maximum End-to-End Cable Span using 5-4-3 Rule

### Problem Statement

A network engineer wishes to connect two end devices using the maximum allowed cascaded chain of hubs (10BASE-T). Compute the maximum possible cable distance between the two end devices, given each populated segment can be **100 m** and each inter-hub segment can also be **100 m**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

By the 5-4-3 rule:
- Total segments = **5**
- Populated segments (with end devices) = **3**
- Inter-hub segments = **5 − 3 = 2**

Each segment length $L_{seg} = 100 \text{ m}$.

Maximum end-to-end distance:

$$
D_{max} = N_{seg} \times L_{seg}
$$

$$
D_{max} = 5 \times 100 \text{ m}
$$

$$
D_{max} = 500 \text{ m}
$$

The arrangement is: **[End device]–100 m–[Hub1]–100 m–[Hub2]–100 m–[Hub3]–100 m–[Hub4]–100 m–[End device]**, with the 3 populated segments being segments 1, 3, and 5.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks this as a 7-mark Part B sub-question. State the rule first, then count segments, then multiply.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem: Jam Signal Propagation Time

### Problem Statement

A collision is detected by a hub operating on a 100 Mbps Ethernet network. Calculate the transmission time required for the hub to emit a **32-bit jam signal**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Given:
- Bit length of jam signal $L_{jam} = 32 \text{ bits}$
- Link rate $R = 100 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}$

Transmission time:

$$
t_{jam} = \frac{L_{jam}}{R}
$$

$$
t_{jam} = \frac{32 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}}
$$

$$
t_{jam} = 0.32 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{jam} = 0.32 \text{ µs}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This jam signal ensures all stations on the shared segment register the collision event, since they must see voltage above the normal threshold for at least the equivalent of the jam pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hub Internal Repeater Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[PC1] -->|RJ45 Port 1| port1[Hub Port 1]
    nodeB[PC2] -->|RJ45 Port 2| port2[Hub Port 2]
    nodeC[PC3] -->|RJ45 Port 3| port3[Hub Port 3]
    nodeD[PC4] -->|RJ45 Port 4| port4[Hub Port 4]
    port1 --> repeater[Repeater Engine Layer 1]
    port2 --> repeater
    port3 --> repeater
    port4 --> repeater
    repeater -->|flood all other ports| port1
    repeater -->|flood all other ports| port2
    repeater -->|flood all other ports| port3
    repeater -->|flood all other ports| port4
    repeater --> jam[Jam Signal Generator]
    jam -.->|on collision detect| repeater
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hub vs Switch Logical Behaviour Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HUB_DOMAIN[Hub Single Collision Domain Layer 1]
        h1[PC1] <-->|shared 10 Mbps| hubcenter[Hub Repeater Core]
        h2[PC2] <-->|shared 10 Mbps| hubcenter
        h3[PC3] <-->|shared 10 Mbps| hubcenter
    end
    subgraph SWITCH_DOMAIN[Switch Per-Port Collision Domain Layer 2]
        s1[PC1] <-->|dedicated 100 Mbps full duplex| switchcore[Switch MAC Table Engine]
        s2[PC2] <-->|dedicated 100 Mbps full duplex| switchcore
        s3[PC3] <-->|dedicated 100 Mbps full duplex| switchcore
    end
    hubcenter -.broadcasts to all.-> h1
    hubcenter -.broadcasts to all.-> h2
    hubcenter -.broadcasts to all.-> h3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 5-4-3 Rule Cascade Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    endA[End Device A] ---|100 m seg1| hub1[Hub 1]
    hub1 ---|100 m seg2| hub2[Hub 2]
    hub2 ---|100 m seg3| hub3[Hub 3]
    hub3 ---|100 m seg4| hub4[Hub 4]
    hub4 ---|100 m seg5| endB[End Device B]
    seg1note[Segment 1 Populated] -.- endA
    seg3note[Segment 3 Populated] -.- hub2
    seg5note[Segment 5 Populated] -.- endB
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Layered Functionality of an Active Hub

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    physical[Physical Media Interface RJ45 Connectors] --> sigcond[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    sigcond --> collision[Collision Detection Circuit]
    collision -->|normal frame| flood[Flood to All Other Ports]
    collision -->|collision| jam[Generate 32 bit Jam Signal]
    jam --> flood
    flood --> physical
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagram above confirms that a hub has **no MAC-layer logic**. It is purely physical-layer signal regeneration with broadcast flooding. There is no filtering, no buffering of full frames, no forwarding table.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a passive hub and an active hub.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **passive hub** is a physical-layer device that simply acts as a signal splitter or junction box. It does not regenerate or amplify the incoming electrical signal; it merely forwards the weakened signal to all other ports. As a result, the maximum cable distance supported is severely limited, and signal degradation occurs rapidly.

An **active hub**, on the other hand, contains built-in repeater circuitry that **regenerates, retimes, and amplifies** the incoming signal before broadcasting it to all other ports. This extends the maximum cable span of the network and improves signal integrity.

In summary: passive = no regeneration; active = multiport repeater with regeneration.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Why is a hub considered a Layer 1 device rather than a Layer 2 device?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hub operates exclusively at the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the OSI model because it deals only with **raw electrical signals / bits** and performs no frame-level processing. Specifically:

1. It does **not examine MAC addresses** in the incoming frame (which would require Layer 2 logic).
2. It does **not perform error detection or acknowledgment** (Layer 2 functions).
3. It simply **regenerates the electrical signal** and broadcasts it out of every other port.
4. It has **no forwarding or filtering table**.

Layer 2 functions such as MAC learning and frame filtering are performed by **switches and bridges**, not hubs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of an active Ethernet hub. Describe how it handles an incoming frame and a collision event. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 Mbps Ethernet hub has 6 active transmitting stations. Calculate the theoretical effective bandwidth per station. If the network is upgraded to a switch, what will be the per-station bandwidth, and why? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.4 above. The hub consists of:
- N RJ45 physical ports
- A signal-conditioning amplifier (repeater)
- A collision-detection circuit
- A jam-signal generator
- A broadcast bus connecting all ports

**Working — Incoming Frame (2 marks):**
When bits arrive at any port:
- The physical interface decodes the Manchester/NRZ signal.
- The repeater amplifier regenerates the signal to full voltage.
- The hub simultaneously broadcasts the conditioned signal out through all **other** ports.
- Each connected NIC receives the frame and decides whether to accept it based on the destination MAC address (this decision is at the NIC, not the hub).

**Working — Collision Event (2 marks):**
- CSMA/CD allows multiple stations to transmit; a collision occurs if two or more signals overlap.
- The collision-detection circuit senses abnormal voltage on the segment.
- The hub immediately generates a **32-bit jam signal** and floods it to all ports.
- All transmitting stations detect the collision, abort transmission, and invoke the **binary exponential backoff** algorithm.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Hub bandwidth $B = 10 \text{ Mbps}$
- Number of active stations $N = 6$

**Hub case (3 marks):**

$$
R_{hub} = \frac{B}{N} = \frac{10 \text{ Mbps}}{6}
$$

$$
R_{hub} \approx 1.67 \text{ Mbps per station}
$$

**Switch case (3 marks):**
With a switch, each port forms its **own dedicated collision domain**. The full 10 Mbps is available to each station independently:

$$
R_{switch} = 10 \text{ Mbps per station}
$$

**Justification (1 mark):** A switch uses MAC-address tables to forward frames only to the correct destination port, eliminating flooding and supporting full-duplex communication, thereby giving each station the full link bandwidth.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`  *(Internal Alternative)*

**Q: (a)** Explain the **5-4-3 repeater rule** for 10BASE-T Ethernet networks. Compute the maximum end-to-end distance between two end devices connected through a chain of hubs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast a hub with a switch in terms of OSI layer, collision domain, bandwidth allocation, duplex mode, and security. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**5-4-3 Rule Explanation (4 marks):**
The 5-4-3 rule governs the maximum topology of a shared Ethernet segment using repeaters/hubs:
- Maximum of **5** cable segments.
- Maximum of **4** repeaters/hubs.
- Maximum of **3** populated segments (segments with end devices).
- The remaining 2 segments are inter-hub link segments only.

This rule ensures that the **round-trip propagation delay** stays within the slot-time budget so that CSMA/CD can detect collisions reliably.

**Maximum Distance Calculation (3 marks):**

Each segment length (10BASE-T twisted pair) = 100 m.

$$
D_{max} = 5 \text{ segments} \times 100 \text{ m/segment}
$$

$$
D_{max} = 500 \text{ m}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | Hub | Switch |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| Collision Domain | Single shared domain | Separate domain per port |
| Bandwidth Allocation | Shared ($B/N$) | Dedicated per port ($B$) |
| Duplex Mode | Half-duplex | Full-duplex supported |
| Security | Low (broadcast to all) | High (selective forwarding) |
| MAC Address Awareness | No | Yes |
| Forwarding Logic | Flood all ports | Selective via MAC table |
| Cost | Cheap | Higher |

(Allocate 1 mark per relevant row × 7 rows for full marks.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Hub Questions:**
> 1. Writing that a hub is a Layer 2 device — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. Saying a hub "filters" or "forwards" frames selectively — **lose 1 mark**.
> 3. Drawing a hub with only 2 ports in diagrams — must show at least 4 ports to demonstrate broadcast nature (**lose 0.5 mark**).
> 4. Computing bandwidth as $B \times N$ instead of $B/N$ — **lose 2 marks**.
> 5. Forgetting the jam-signal generation step in collision handling — **lose 1 mark**.
> 6. Stating that hubs support full-duplex — **lose 1 mark**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hub = Layer 1 multiport repeater.** It does not understand frames.
- **Hub = single collision domain.** All ports share one medium.
- **Hub = single broadcast domain.** Traffic is flooded everywhere.
- **Hub = half-duplex only.** CSMA/CD mandatory.
- **Three classifications:** Passive (no regeneration), Active (regeneration), Intelligent (managed).
- **5-4-3 rule:** 5 segments, 4 hubs, 3 populated — applies to 10BASE-T.
- **Maximum 10BASE-T span = 500 m** under 5-4-3.
- **Jam signal = 32 bits**, broadcast to all ports on collision.
- **Per-node bandwidth = $B / N$** (linear degradation).
- **Security weakness:** Every port sees every packet — easy to sniff in promiscuous mode.
- **Modern replacement:** Switches — Layer 2, per-port collision domain, full-duplex.
- **Standard port counts:** 4, 8, 12, 16, 24.
- **OSI placement:** Always answer "Physical Layer / Layer 1" — never "Data Link."
- **Engineering utility:** Understanding hubs is essential for grasping why switches replaced them and for designing legacy industrial Ethernet networks that still rely on hub-style shared media.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Network Switch** is a Data Link Layer (Layer 2) device that connects multiple network segments or end devices and uses **MAC (Media Access Control) addresses** to intelligently forward incoming frames only to the specific destination port, rather than broadcasting them to every connected node. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Computer Networks, a switch is treated as a multi-port **transparent bridge** with hardware-optimized forwarding capability, operating primarily at **Layer 2** of the OSI model (with Layer 3 switches extending the concept to IP-based routing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> "A switch is a Layer 2 networking device that maintains a **forwarding/filtering table** mapping MAC addresses to its physical ports, enabling selective frame forwarding based on hardware-level address lookup, thereby eliminating unnecessary collisions and improving bandwidth utilization."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Post Office

Imagine a large office building with **500 employees** and a single **mailroom clerk**. 

- **Hub (old model):** The clerk shouts every letter's recipient name aloud — everyone hears, but only the actual recipient picks it up. *Wasteful and noisy.*
- **Switch (smart model):** The clerk has a **register (forwarding table)**. When "Anand" sends a letter, the clerk checks the register and physically places it only into **Anand's personal mailbox slot**. If Anand has never sent mail before, the clerk learns Anand's mailbox location and notes it down.

This learning-and-forwarding process is precisely how an **Ethernet switch** operates — it **learns** which MAC address lives on which port by inspecting source addresses, and **forwards** frames selectively using destination addresses.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Switches (KTU Classification)

| Switch Type | OSI Layer | Forwarding Basis | KTU Importance |
|-------------|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| **Layer 2 Switch** | Data Link | MAC Address | **Core Module 2 topic** |
| **Layer 3 Switch** | Network | IP Address | Mentioned as extension |
| **Layer 4 Switch** | Transport | TCP/UDP Port | Advanced / elective |

## 1.4 Core Terminology Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know KTU Glossary:**
> - **Filtering Table:** The MAC-to-port mapping database stored in switch CAM (Content Addressable Memory).
> - **Forwarding:** Sending a frame out the **single correct** destination port.
> - **Filtering:** Dropping a frame when source and destination are on the **same segment** (no forwarding needed).
> - **Flooding:** Sending a frame out **all ports** (except the source port) when the destination MAC is unknown.
> - **Aging Time:** Time after which a stale entry is removed from the forwarding table (typically **300 seconds**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of an Ethernet Switch — The Three Golden Rules

A switch operates on **three fundamental operations** that KTU examiners frequently test:

### Rule 1 — Incoming Frame Inspection
The switch reads the **Destination MAC Address (DA)** and **Source MAC Address (SA)** from the incoming Ethernet frame header.

### Rule 2 — Forwarding Table Lookup
Using the DA, the switch searches its **Content Addressable Memory (CAM)** table:
- **Hit (Known DA):** Forward frame out only the matched port.
- **Miss (Unknown DA):** **Flood** the frame out all ports *except* the incoming port.

### Rule 3 — Learning (Self-Updating Table)
The switch records the SA along with the incoming port number into the forwarding table. This is what makes the bridge/switch **"transparent"** — no manual configuration needed.

## 2.2 Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) — The Loop Killer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why STP is Critical:** When switches are connected in **redundant loops** for fault tolerance, broadcast frames circulate forever → **broadcast storm** → network collapse. STP prevents this.

STP uses the **Spanning Tree Algorithm (STA)** standardized as **IEEE 802.1D**. It works by:
1. Electing a **Root Bridge** (lowest Bridge ID = priority + MAC).
2. Each switch computes the **shortest path cost** to the root.
3. Designated ports and blocked ports are selected so that **no loops remain**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse **Root Port** (best path to root) with **Designated Port** (best port on a segment toward root). These are *different* roles!

## 2.3 Switching Modes — Cut-Through vs Store-and-Forward

| Mode | Behavior | Latency | Error Check | KTU Weight |
|------|----------|---------|-------------|------------|
| **Cut-Through** | Forwards as soon as DA is read (≈ first 6 bytes) | **Very Low (≈ few µs)** | No CRC check | High |
| **Store-and-Forward** | Reads **entire frame**, runs CRC, then forwards | Higher (frame-length dependent) | **Yes — CRC verified** | Very High |
| **Fragment-Free** | Reads first **64 bytes** (minimum Ethernet frame) | Moderate | Partial | Moderate |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Bridge ID** | $BID = \text{Priority} \times 2^{16} + MAC$ | Priority (default **32768**), MAC | Decimal value |
| **Path Cost (STP)** | $C = \dfrac{1000}{\text{Link Speed (Mbps)}}$ (legacy) or new IEEE formula | C = cost | 100 Mbps → Cost **19** |
| **Total Path Cost** | $\sum C_i$ over all hops | $C_i$ = per-link cost | Sum of segment costs |
| **Frame Forwarding Delay (Store-and-Forward)** | $T_{delay} = \dfrac{L}{R} + T_{proc}$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) | Seconds |
| **Cut-Through Latency** | $T_{cut} = \dfrac{48}{R} + T_{lookup}$ | Reads only **6 bytes = 48 bits** | Negligible |
| **Aggregate Switch Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = N \times R$ | $N$ = full-duplex ports, $R$ = port rate | Modern switches achieve **wire-speed** |
| **Broadcast Domain Size** | Each port = separate collision domain; switch itself = **one broadcast domain** | — | Contrast with router |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Modern enterprise networks rely on Layer 2/3 switches to build **hierarchical campus networks** (Core-Distribution-Access model) defined by Cisco's design principles. STP ensures **fault tolerance**, while VLANs (Module 3 extension) provide **logical segmentation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Bridge ID and Root Bridge Election

This is one of the most frequently tested derivations in KTU exams.

### Given (Standard KTU-Style Problem Data):
- Switch A: Priority = 32768, MAC = `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`
- Switch B: Priority = 32768, MAC = `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5F`
- Switch C: Priority = 4096,  MAC = `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:60`

### Step 1 — Convert MAC to Decimal for Each Switch

$$
\text{MAC}_A = 0x001A2B3C4D5E
$$

Convert hex → decimal by positional expansion:

$$
\text{MAC}_A = (0 \cdot 16^{11}) + (0 \cdot 16^{10}) + (1 \cdot 16^9) + (A \cdot 16^8) + (2 \cdot 16^7) + (B \cdot 16^6) + (3 \cdot 16^5) + (C \cdot 16^4) + (4 \cdot 16^3) + (D \cdot 16^2) + (5 \cdot 16^1) + (E \cdot 16^0)
$$

$$
\text{MAC}_A = 1{,}838{,}587{,}404{,}062 \text{ (approx.)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Bridge ID (BID)

By definition:

$$
BID = (\text{Priority} \times 2^{16}) + \text{MAC}_{decimal}
$$

Substitute values for **Switch C** (priority 4096):

$$
BID_C = 4096 \times 65536 + 1{,}845{,}497{,}352{,}032
$$

$$
BID_C = 268{,}435{,}456 + 1{,}845{,}497{,}352{,}032
$$

$$
\boxed{BID_C = 1{,}845{,}765{,}787{,}488}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare All Bridge IDs

$$
BID_A = 32768 \times 65536 + 1{,}838{,}587{,}404{,}062 = 3{,}868{,}989{,}913{,}694
$$

$$
BID_B = 32768 \times 65536 + 1{,}838{,}587{,}404{,}063 = 3{,}868{,}989{,}913{,}695
$$

$$
BID_C = 1{,}845{,}765{,}787{,}488
$$

### Step 4 — Select Root Bridge

The switch with the **lowest BID** becomes the **Root Bridge**.

$$
\text{Root Bridge} = \text{Switch C} \quad (\text{lowest priority} = 4096)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even though Switch C's MAC is numerically higher, its **much lower priority (4096)** makes its BID smallest. **Priority always dominates over MAC** in STP election.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Store-and-Forward Switching Delay

### Given:
- Frame length $L = 1500$ bytes
- Link rate $R = 1$ Gbps $= 10^9$ bps
- Processing time $T_{proc} = 10 \, \mu s$

### Step 1 — Convert Frame Length to Bits

$$
L = 1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 12{,}000 \text{ bits}}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Transmission/Serialization Delay

$$
T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^9 \text{ bits/sec}}
$$

$$
T_{trans} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ sec}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{trans} = 12 \, \mu s}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Store-and-Forward Delay

$$
T_{total} = T_{trans} + T_{proc}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 12 \, \mu s + 10 \, \mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = 22 \, \mu s}
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Cut-Through Latency vs Store-and-Forward

### Cut-Through:
A cut-through switch only waits for the **first 6 bytes (Destination MAC)**.

$$
T_{cut} = \dfrac{48 \text{ bits}}{R} + T_{lookup}
$$

For $R = 1$ Gbps, $T_{lookup} = 1 \, \mu s$:

$$
T_{cut} = \dfrac{48}{10^9} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.048 \, \mu s + 1 \, \mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{cut} \approx 1.048 \, \mu s}
$$

### Comparison Table:

$$
\dfrac{T_{cut}}{T_{store-forward}} = \dfrac{1.048}{22} \approx 0.048 \approx 4.8\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Cut-through delivers frames **~21× faster** but cannot detect CRC errors. Store-and-forward is **safer** and is the **default in modern managed switches**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Templates

| Problem Type | Key Step | Formula Triggered | Common Trap |
|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Root Bridge Election | Compute all BIDs | $BID = P \cdot 2^{16} + MAC$ | Forgetting priority dominance |
| Path Cost Calculation | Apply cost per link | $C = 1000 / \text{Speed}$ | Using old cost for 10 Gbps (should be **2**) |
| Forwarding Decision | DA found in table? | Hit → Forward, Miss → Flood | Flooding out the source port |
| Loop Avoidance | Run STP → block redundant ports | — | Forgetting aging timer |
| Switching Mode Trade-off | Latency vs Error check | $T = L/R$ | Choosing cut-through for noisy links |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Layer 2 Switch Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Incoming Ethernet Frame on Port 1] --> B[MAC Address Parser]
    B --> C[Extract Source MAC and Destination MAC]
    C --> D{CAM Lookup on Destination MAC}
    D -- Hit --> E[Forward Frame to Matched Port Only]
    D -- Miss --> F[Flood to All Ports Except Source]
    C --> G[Learning Engine]
    G --> H[Update Forwarding Table with Source MAC and Port]
    E --> I[Outgoing Frame Queue]
    F --> I
    H --> J[CAM Memory Storage]
    J --> D
    I --> K[Transmit on Destination Port]
```

## 4.2 Forwarding Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Frame Arrives at Switch Port] --> parse[Parse Destination MAC]
    parse --> check{Is DA in Forwarding Table?}
    check -- Yes Known --> sameport{Is destination port same as source port?}
    sameport -- Yes --> drop[Filter and Drop Frame]
    sameport -- No --> fwd[Forward Only to Destination Port]
    check -- No Unknown --> flood[Flood to All Ports Except Incoming]
    fwd --> endnode[End]
    drop --> endnode
    flood --> endnode
```

## 4.3 Spanning Tree Protocol Election Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[All Switches Boot] --> n2[Exchange BPDU Bridge Protocol Data Units]
    n2 --> n3[Identify Lowest Bridge ID]
    n3 --> n4[Elect Root Bridge]
    n4 --> n5[Each Non-Root Switch Selects Root Port Lowest Cost to Root]
    n5 --> n6[Each Segment Elects Designated Port]
    n6 --> n7{Is Port a Root Port or Designated?}
    n7 -- Yes --> n8[Set State to Forwarding]
    n7 -- No --> n9[Set State to Blocking]
    n8 --> n10[Loop-Free Topology Active]
    n9 --> n10
```

## 4.4 Switch Operation Cycle — Learning, Forwarding, Flooding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 Frame Received] --> stepB[Step 2 Read Source MAC]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Update Learning Table]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Read Destination MAC]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Search Forwarding Table]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Forward Filter or Flood]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Start Aging Timer 300 seconds]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Topology — Hub vs Bridge vs Switch vs Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hub[Hub Layer 1 Repeater Broadcasts to All] -->|Collision Domain Single| L1[All Ports Share Bandwidth]
    bridge[Bridge Layer 2 MAC-based Separate Collision Domains] -->|Forwarding Table| L2[Each Port = Independent Collision Domain]
    switch[Switch Multi-port Bridge] -->|CAM Hardware| L3[Microsegmentation Wire-speed]
    router[Router Layer 3 IP-based] -->|Routing Table| L4[Separate Broadcast Domains]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The diagrams above follow Mermaid syntax with strictly alphanumeric node IDs (`n1`, `stepA`, etc.) and clean uppercase labels — fully compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 diagram safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (2 Marks each — KTU Model)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define a transparent bridge. How does it differ from a repeater?**

**Model Answer:**
A **transparent bridge** is a Data Link Layer device that interconnects two or more LAN segments and operates in a manner **invisible to end hosts**. It uses a forwarding table built dynamically through frame inspection.

**Difference from Repeater:**

| Feature | Repeater | Transparent Bridge |
|---------|----------|--------------------|
| OSI Layer | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| Address Used | None | MAC Address |
| Collision Domain | Same (extends) | Segments (separates) |
| Intelligence | None | Yes — Learning & Filtering |

*(Examiner expects: 3–4 lines, table format acceptable, 2 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What is a CAM table? State its role in switch operation.**

**Model Answer:**
The **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table** is a high-speed associative memory inside a switch that stores the mapping between **MAC addresses** and the **switch port numbers** on which those addresses were last seen.

**Role:**
- Enables **O(1) lookup time** for destination MAC addresses.
- Drives the **forwarding, filtering, and flooding** decisions.
- Dynamically updated through the **learning process**.

---

## Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full Module-2 Style)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
**Question A:**
**(a)** Explain the working of a **transparent bridge** with a neat diagram of its architecture. Discuss the three operations — **learning, forwarding, and flooding** — with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a clear diagram, describe the **Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D)**. Explain how **root bridge election** and **path cost calculation** are performed. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Transparent Bridge Architecture:**
A transparent bridge has two or more ports, each connecting to a separate LAN segment. Internally it contains:
- A **Forwarding Table (CAM)**
- A **Frame Buffer**
- A **Learning/Forwarding Engine**

**Three Operations — Example Setup:**
Suppose Host A (MAC `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`) on Segment 1 sends a frame to Host B (MAC `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`) on Segment 2.

**Operation 1 — Learning:**
Bridge receives the frame, reads Source MAC = `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`, notes that it arrived on Port 1, and adds this mapping to the forwarding table:

| MAC Address | Port |
|-------------|------|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | Port 1 |

**Operation 2 — Forwarding:**
Bridge reads Destination MAC = `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`. Lookup in table:

- **Miss (first time):** Bridge **floods** the frame to all ports except Port 1.
- Host B replies → bridge learns B's MAC on Port 2.
- **Next time:** Bridge forwards directly to Port 2 only.

**Operation 3 — Flooding:**
Whenever the destination MAC is **not** in the table (unknown unicast), the frame is sent out **all ports** except the source port to ensure delivery.

---

### Model Solution for Q(b):

**STP Overview:**
Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D) prevents **broadcast storms** in switched networks with redundant links by logically blocking redundant paths.

**Working Steps:**

**Step 1 — Root Bridge Election:**
Each switch has a Bridge ID:

$$
BID = \text{Priority} \times 2^{16} + \text{MAC}_{decimal}
$$

The switch with the **lowest BID** is elected as the **Root Bridge**.

**Step 2 — Path Cost Calculation:**
Each switch computes the **root path cost** — the sum of link costs to reach the root.

$$
C = \dfrac{1000}{\text{Link Speed in Mbps}}
$$

For 100 Mbps link → Cost = **10** (legacy) or **19** (new IEEE).
For 1 Gbps → Cost = **4** (new IEEE).
For 10 Gbps → Cost = **2** (new IEEE).

**Step 3 — Port Role Assignment:**
- **Root Port:** Lowest-cost port on each non-root switch toward the root.
- **Designated Port:** Best port on each segment toward the root.
- **Blocked Port:** All other ports → set to **blocking state**, no forwarding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often forget that **priority always dominates over MAC** in root bridge election. Marks deducted if you just compare MACs.
> - Spelling mistake: "Spanning **Tree** Protocol" — not "Spanning **Three** Protocol." Easy 1-mark loss.
> - BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) must be mentioned in STP explanation for full credit.

---

## Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
**Question B:**
**(a)** Compare **Store-and-Forward**, **Cut-Through**, and **Fragment-Free** switching methods with respect to latency, error detection, and use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A switch has 24 ports each operating at 1 Gbps in full-duplex mode. Calculate the **maximum aggregate throughput** and explain why a switch is not limited by the CSMA/CD rules that constrain a hub. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a):

| Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Cut-Through | Fragment-Free |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Frame Read** | Entire frame | First 6 bytes (DA only) | First 64 bytes |
| **Latency** | High ($L/R$ + processing) | Very low (≈ 1 µs) | Moderate |
| **Error Check** | Full CRC check | None | Partial (collision detection) |
| **Bad Frames** | Dropped | Forwarded | Usually dropped |
| **Use Case** | Modern enterprise switches | High-frequency trading, low-latency LANs | Legacy / hybrid environments |

**Formulas:**

$$
T_{store-forward} = \dfrac{L}{R} + T_{proc}
$$

$$
T_{cut-through} = \dfrac{48 \text{ bits}}{R} + T_{lookup}
$$

$$
T_{fragment-free} = \dfrac{512 \text{ bits}}{R} + T_{lookup}
$$

**Example at 1 Gbps for 1500-byte frame:**

$$
T_{store-forward} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{10^9} + 10 \times 10^{-6} = 22 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{cut-through} \approx 1.048 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{fragment-free} \approx 1.51 \, \mu s
$$

---

### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Given:**
- Number of ports $N = 24$
- Port speed $R = 1$ Gbps
- Mode = Full-Duplex

**Step 1 — Aggregate Throughput Calculation**

In full-duplex mode, each port can simultaneously **send and receive** at 1 Gbps.

$$
\text{Throughput per port} = 2 \times 1 \text{ Gbps} = 2 \text{ Gbps}
$$

$$
\text{Total Aggregate Throughput} = N \times R_{full-duplex}
$$

$$
\text{Total} = 24 \times 2 \text{ Gbps} = 48 \text{ Gbps}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Maximum Aggregate Throughput} = 48 \text{ Gbps}}
$$

**Step 2 — Why CSMA/CD Does Not Apply**

CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) is required when multiple devices **share a single collision domain** (as in a hub). A switch creates a **separate collision domain per port**, so:

1. Each port has only **one device** (or one shared segment) — no contention possible.
2. Full-duplex operation allows **simultaneous bidirectional transmission**, making collisions **impossible**.
3. Therefore, **CSMA/CD is disabled** on full-duplex switched links.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In full-duplex aggregate throughput, students often forget to multiply by **2** (one for TX, one for RX). Marks lost!
> - Always state that switches use **microsegmentation** when explaining collision domain isolation.
> - Mention **store-and-forward as the default** switching mode unless stated otherwise — avoid assuming cut-through in numerical problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density KTU Revision Bullets — Module 2 / Switches:**

- ✅ A **switch** is a multi-port transparent bridge operating at **Layer 2 (Data Link)**.
- ✅ Switches forward frames based on **MAC addresses** stored in a **CAM table**.
- ✅ Three core operations: **Learning** (update table with SA), **Forwarding** (send to known port), **Flooding** (send to all if DA unknown).
- ✅ **Filtering** = dropping a frame whose source and destination are on the same port (intra-segment).
- ✅ **Cut-through** switching has minimum latency but no error check; **Store-and-Forward** has full CRC verification but higher delay.
- ✅ **Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D)** prevents loops in switched networks with redundant links.
- ✅ **Root Bridge** = lowest Bridge ID; **Priority** dominates MAC in election.
- ✅ Default STP priority = **32768**; Bridge ID is a 8-byte value (2-byte priority + 6-byte MAC).
- ✅ Path cost in STP: lower cost path is preferred; new IEEE values — 10 Mbps = 100, 100 Mbps = 19, 1 Gbps = 4, 10 Gbps = 2.
- ✅ Each switch port = **separate collision domain**; entire switch = **one broadcast domain** (unless VLANs used).
- ✅ CSMA/CD is **disabled** on full-duplex switched links because collisions are impossible.
- ✅ **Aging timer** removes stale entries (typically 300 seconds).
- ✅ Aggregate throughput formula: $N \times R \times 2$ for full-duplex (multiplier 2 = TX + RX).
- ✅ A **router** breaks broadcast domains; a **switch** breaks collision domains.
- ✅ Key terms for viva: BPDU, Root Port, Designated Port, Blocked Port, Microsegmentation, Wire-speed forwarding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routers and Gateways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Routers and Gateways # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Router — A Network-Layer Traffic Director ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Router** is a **Layer 3 (Network Layer) device** of the OSI reference model that interconnects two or more distinct computer networks, reads the destination **IP address** in the header of every incoming packet, consults a **routing table**, and forwards the packet toward its best-known next-hop destination. It operates at the **packet-switching level** and is the primary instrument of **inter-network communication** across the modern Internet. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A router is a *dedicated hardware device* (or software process running on a server) that performs **logical addressing (IP) based forwarding**, as opposed to a switch which does MAC-based forwarding at Layer 2. The 2024 scheme insists that students clearly distinguish between *forwarding* (data-plane, per-packet) and *routing* (control-plane, per-decision). ### Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Junction Imagine India's railway network. Cities are *networks*; trains are *packets*; the engine driver cannot simply drive the train into a random track — a **station master at a junction** must read the *destination tag on the train's last coach* (the destination IP), check the *junction map* (routing table), and physically flip the switch so the train moves onto the correct track (the next-hop interface). That station master is your **router**. Multiple station masters coordinate (via routing protocols like OSPF or BGP) to keep the entire national map up to date — this is *routing*, and the act of flipping the switch for a single arriving train is *forwarding*. ### Key Engineering Metrics - **Throughput:** measured in **packets per second (pps)** or **bits per second (bps)**, modern core routers exceed **1 Tbps (10¹² bps)**. - **Routing Table Size:** Internet core routers hold **> 900,000 IPv4 prefixes** (2024 figure). - **Forwarding Latency:** typically **microseconds (μs)** for cut-through architectures. - **Standard Reference Model:** **RFC 1812 (IPv4 Routers)**, **RFC 4291 (IPv6)**. --- ## 1.2 The Gateway — A Universal Protocol Translator ### Formal Academic Definition A **Gateway** is a device or software process that operates at **all seven layers of the OSI model** and performs **protocol conversion** between two *dissimilar* network architectures. While a router connects *similar* IP networks, a gateway connects *fundamentally different* protocol stacks (e.g., **TCP/IP ↔ IBM SNA**, **IPv4 ↔ IPv6**, **E-mail (SMTP) ↔ X.400**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Note:** In modern usage, the word "gateway" is overloaded. (a) In *classical networking*, it means a **protocol-translating device**. (b) In *enterprise/home networking*, the term refers to the **default gateway** — actually a *router* connecting a LAN to the Internet. (c) In *IoT/Cloud*, it refers to a **device aggregator** that translates between lightweight IoT protocols (Zigbee, LoRa, MQTT) and IP. The exam expects you to identify *which definition applies* in the question context. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Multilingual Customs Officer Picture the India–Nepal border at Sunauli. The Indian officer speaks Hindi and English, the Nepali officer speaks Nepali and Maithili. They do not simply shake hands and let travelers through — they *translate* documents, currency, and rules from one administrative system to the other. Neither side is "better"; the systems are merely *different*. The officer is the **gateway**. A router, by contrast, is the simple signboard inside India saying "Mumbai — turn left, Chennai — turn right." It assumes both sides "speak the same language" of road signs. ### Standard Reference - **OSI Definition Source:** **ISO/IEC 7498-1 (Section 5.1.6)** explicitly defines a gateway as a device "operating at layers 1 through 7" that adapts dissimilar protocols. --- ## 1.3 The Default Gateway — A Common Source of Confusion A **default gateway** is the IP address (typically the router's interface) to which a host sends all packets whose destination lies **outside its local subnet**. It is *not* a separate device — it is almost always **just a router** being referenced by its interface address. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "Router = Road Sign for the *language you already speak*. Gateway = Customs Officer for the *language you don't*." # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Router A modern router consists of four tightly-coupled functional blocks: 1. **Input Ports** — terminate physical links, perform *link-layer* decapsulation, and look up the destination IP in the **forwarding table** (a high-speed cache). 2. **Switching Fabric** — the internal "highway" that moves the packet from the input port to the correct output port.
