Three dominant designs exist: - **Memory-based** (traditional, slower, used in software routers) - **Bus-based** (single shared backplane; bottleneck) - **Crossbar** (modern, non-blocking, used in high-end core routers) 3. **Output Ports** — buffer the packet, perform *link-layer* encapsulation, and transmit onto the outgoing physical medium. 4. **Routing Processor** — runs the **routing protocols** (OSPF, BGP, RIP), maintains the *Routing Information Base (RIB)*, and installs entries into the *Forwarding Information Base (FIB)* for the data plane. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Distinction (very high-yield):** The **RIB** is *logical* (computed by routing algorithms). The **FIB** is the *hardware-optimized* lookup table pushed into TCAM on the line cards. Conflating the two costs marks. ## 2.2 How a Router Forwards a Packet — The 5-Phase Logic 1. **Decapsulation:** Strip the incoming datagram from the Layer-2 frame (Ethernet, PPP, etc.). 2. **Header Validation:** Verify the IP header checksum. If invalid → **discard** and increment an error counter (ICMP also generated to source for some failures). 3. **Destination Lookup:** Search the **FIB** (longest-prefix-match, LPM). 4. **TTL Decrement:** $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$. If $TTL_{new} = 0$ → **drop** and send **ICMP Time Exceeded** to source. 5. **Re-encapsulation & Forwarding:** Hand the packet to the selected output port, which re-frames it for the next-hop link. ## 2.3 Routing Algorithms — The Control Plane | Algorithm Type | Examples | Metric Used | Convergence Speed | Scalability | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Distance Vector** | RIP (RIPv2), IGRP | Hop count (or composite) | **Slow** (count-to-infinity) | Small networks | | **Link State** | OSPF, IS-IS | Cost (usually inverse bandwidth) | **Fast** (Dijkstra) | Large enterprise | | **Path Vector** | BGP (eBGP / iBGP) | Policy + AS-PATH length | Medium (policy-driven) | **Internet scale** | | **Hybrid** | EIGRP | Composite (BW, delay, load, reliability) | Fast (DUAL algorithm) | Enterprise | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** *Distance Vector* = "rumor-routing" (each router believes what its neighbor says). *Link State* = "map-routing" (each router builds the *complete* topology via **LSP flooding** and runs **Dijkstra's shortest-path first (SPF)** algorithm). BGP is the *only* inter-autonomous-system protocol on the public Internet. ## 2.4 Gateway Architecture & Protocol Conversion Model A gateway must perform **seven-layer translation** when the stacks differ: - **Application Layer:** Convert message semantics (e.g., SMTP RFC 822 ↔ X.400). - **Transport Layer:** Map port numbers and reliability semantics (TCP vs. SPX). - **Network Layer:** Re-map addresses and packet structure (IPv4 ↔ IPv6 via NAT64, DNS64). - **Data Link & Physical:** Convert framing (Ethernet ↔ Token Ring ↔ ATM). A canonical example is an **Email Gateway** that accepts SMTP on one side, converts message format and character encoding, and dispatches via X.400 on the legacy side. Another is a **VoIP Gateway** that bridges **PSTN (TDM circuits)** to **SIP/RTP** packet networks. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains *all* quantitative relationships you will need for a Router/Gateway problem in the KTU 2024 exam. | # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Boundary Conditions / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** | Match rule: choose entry with the **longest** matching prefix (e.g., /24 wins over /16). | Binary length $\in [0, 32]$ for IPv4. | | 2 | **TTL Decrement (Router-Hop)** | $TTL_{out} = TTL_{in} - 1$ | $TTL_{out} = 0 \Rightarrow$ drop, send ICMP Type 11. | | 3 | **IP Header Checksum** | $\text{Checksum} = \sum_{16\text{-bit words}} (\text{One's complement sum})$ | Computed at every router; **header-only**, not payload. | | 4 | **RIP Hop Count Limit** | $H_{max} = 15$ hops; $H = 16 \Rightarrow$ **unreachable** | Metric: integer hops. | | 5 | **OSPF Cost** | $C_{link} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{BW_{ref}}{BW_{link}} \right\rfloor$ | $BW_{ref} = 100$ Mbps (Cisco default). | | 6 | **Router Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{t}$ where $P$=packets, $L$=bits/packet, $t$=seconds | Units: **bps** (bits per second). | | 7 | **End-to-End Hop Count Estimate** | $N_{hops} = \text{TTL}_{init} - \text{TTL}_{arrival}$ | Use `traceroute`; ICMP reply TTL gives hop number. | | 8 | **Maximum End-to-End Delay through $N$ Routers** | $D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i} + d_{trans,i} + d_{prop,i} \right)$ | $d_{proc}$: processing, $d_{queue}$: queuing, $d_{trans}$: transmission, $d_{prop}$: propagation. | | 9 | **Bottleneck Bandwidth (End-to-End)** | $BW_{bottleneck} = \min(BW_{i})$ across all $i$ links | Path bandwidth is the **minimum** link. | | 10 | **Default Gateway Decision (Host)** | If $D \notin$ local subnet $\Rightarrow$ forward to **Default Gateway IP** | Computed as $D \oplus \text{SubnetMask}$. | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Networks:** Routers enforce *segmentation* (VLs broadcast domains, routers segment collision + broadcast domains at Layer 3). - **Internet Service Providers (ISPs):** Core routers form the **Internet backbone**, executing **BGP** to exchange *reachability* between autonomous systems. - **Industrial IoT:** Protocol-conversion gateways bridge **Modbus (RS-485)** field devices to **MQTT** cloud brokers. - **Telecom:** Media gateways bridge **PSTN TDM** voice circuits to **IP/SIP** for VoLTE. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: A Packet Traversing Three Routers **Problem Statement (Model KTU Type):** A source host $H_S$ with IP $\mathbf{10.0.0.5/24}$ sends a packet of size $\mathbf{L = 1500 \text{ bytes}}$ to destination $H_D$ at IP $\mathbf{203.0.113.42}$. The path traverses **3 routers** ($R_1, R_2, R_3$). Initial TTL is **64**. Link bandwidths are: $L_1 = 100$ Mbps, $L_2 = 10$ Mbps, $L_3 = 50$ Mbps. Compute: (i) TTL at each hop, (ii) bottleneck bandwidth, (iii) end-to-end transmission delay *excluding* propagation and queuing, (iv) explain what happens at $R_2$ if its FIB has **no matching entry**. ### Step (i) — TTL Evolution at Each Router At every router, the TTL is decremented **by exactly 1** before forwarding. Per **RFC 791**: $$ \begin{aligned} TTL_{R_1, \text{out}} &= TTL_{H_S, \text{out}} - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63 \\ TTL_{R_2, \text{out}} &= TTL_{R_1, \text{out}} - 1 = 63 - 1 = 62 \\ TTL_{R_3, \text{out}} &= TTL_{R_2, \text{out}} - 1 = 62 - 1 = 61 \\ TTL_{H_D, \text{received}} &= TTL_{R_3, \text{out}} = 61 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** TTL acts as a *forwarding budget*. Starting at 64, the packet survives 3 routers and arrives with 61. If the source had set TTL = 2, the packet would die at $R_2$ (TTL becomes 0), which would then send an **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11, Code 0)** to $H_S$ — the foundation of the `traceroute` tool. ### Step (ii) — Bottleneck Bandwidth The path bandwidth is the **minimum** of all link bandwidths (the *weakest link* principle): $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{path} &= \min(BW_{L_1},\, BW_{L_2},\, BW_{L_3}) \\ &= \min(100,\, 10,\, 50)\ \text{Mbps} \\ &= 10\ \text{Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ The middle link $L_2$ dominates the throughput. ### Step (iii) — End-to-End Transmission Delay (Packet-Serialised) Transmission delay on a link is the time to *push every bit* of the packet onto the wire: $$ d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{\text{Packet size (bits)}}{\text{Link rate (bps)}} $$ Convert $L$ to bits: $1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 12{,}000 \text{ bits}$. $$ \begin{aligned} d_{trans,1} &= \frac{12{,}000}{100 \times 10^6} = 1.20 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 120\ \mu s \\ d_{trans,2} &= \frac{12{,}000}{10 \times 10^6} = 1.20 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1200\ \mu s \\ d_{trans,3} &= \frac{12{,}000}{50 \times 10^6} = 2.40 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 240\ \mu s \\ \end{aligned} $$ Sum (ignoring propagation, queuing, processing, and store-and-forward overlap on parallel links): $$ D_{total} = 120 + 1200 + 240 = 1560\ \mu s = 1.56\ \text{ms} $$ > **Insight:** $L_2$ contributes $\approx 77\%$ of the total transmission delay, confirming it as the bottleneck. Real routers add **store-and-forward** waiting — the entire packet must be received before it can be re-sent on the next link, so this sum is the *floor* on delay. ### Step (iv) — Behaviour on FIB Miss at $R_2$ When a router receives a packet and **no entry in the FIB matches** the destination prefix, the router follows this decision tree: 1. **Check for a default route (0.0.0.0/0):** If present, forward via the default route. 2. **If no default route exists:** Drop the packet and send an **ICMP Destination Unreachable (Type 3, Code 0: "Network Unreachable")** back to the source. 3. The ICMP message contains the original packet's IP header + first 8 bytes of payload so the source can identify which datagram failed. 4. The router increments its `ipInDiscards` and `ipOutNoRoutes` MIB counters for monitoring. > **Engineering Implication:** This is *why* every enterprise router must have a default route — otherwise, the failure mode of any unknown destination is a silent drop, which is operationally catastrophic. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Router vs. Gateway vs. Switch vs. Bridge | Feature | **Hub** | **Bridge** | **Switch (L2)** | **Router (L3)** | **Gateway (L7/All)** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **OSI Layer** | 1 (Physical) | 2 (Data Link) | 2 (Data Link) | 3 (Network) | 1–7 (All) | | **Forwarding Basis** | None (repeats electrical signal) | MAC address | MAC address | IP address | **Protocol semantics** | | **Collision Domain Separation** | No | Yes | Yes (per port) | Yes (per interface) | Yes | | **Broadcast Domain Separation** | No | No | No | **Yes** | Yes | | **Connects** | Hosts only | 2 LAN segments | Many LAN segments | **Different IP networks** | **Dissimilar architectures** | | **Typical Use** | Obsolete | Legacy segmentation | LAN aggregation | **Inter-networking** | **Protocol translation** | | **Routing Table?** | No | No | No (CAM table only) | **Yes (FIB + RIB)** | No (translation maps) | | **Example Device** | Old 10Base-T hub | Software bridge | Cisco Catalyst | Cisco ISR 4000, Juniper MX | Email gateway, VoIP GW | | **KTU Expectation** | Know it exists | Mention in evolution | Compare with router | **Primary focus** | **Distinguish from router** | --- ## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: OSPF Cost Computation **Problem:** A router has three candidate outgoing links to reach network **N**. - Link A: Bandwidth = 100 Mbps, delay = 5 ms - Link B: Bandwidth = 1 Gbps, delay = 10 ms - Link C: Bandwidth = 10 Mbps, delay = 2 ms OSPF **uses cost only** (default: inversely proportional to bandwidth, $C = \lfloor BW_{ref} / BW \rfloor$, with Cisco $BW_{ref} = 100$ Mbps). $$ \begin{aligned} C_A &= \left\lfloor \frac{100 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^6} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 1 \rfloor = 1 \\ C_B &= \left\lfloor \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^9} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 0.1 \rfloor = 0 \\ C_C &= \left\lfloor \frac{100 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 10 \rfloor = 10 \end{aligned} $$ > **Decision:** Router selects **Link B** (cost = 0 is the minimum), even though it has the worst *delay*. This illustrates a **classic OSPF pitfall** — it ignores delay by default. Engineers often tune OSPF weights to consider both metrics. This is a *favourite KTU 14-marker* if phrased as "Critically evaluate OSPF's cost function." --- ## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: Default Gateway Selection by a Host **Problem:** A host has IP $\mathbf{192.168.10.25}$ with mask $\mathbf{255.255.255.0}$ (/24). The host wishes to send a packet to $\mathbf{8.8.8.8}$. Will it use the default gateway, and which IP does it ARP for? **Step 1 — Subnet check (bitwise AND):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Host network} &= 192.168.10.25 \ \vert\ \&\ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.10.0 \\ \text{Dest network} &= 8.8.8.8 \ \vert\ \&\ 255.255.255.0 = 8.8.8.0 \end{aligned} $$ Networks differ $\Rightarrow$ the destination is *off-subnet* $\Rightarrow$ forward to **default gateway**. **Step 2 — ARP for the gateway:** The host sends an **ARP request** asking: "Who has $\mathbf{192.168.10.1}$? Tell $192.168.10.25$." The router's interface replies with its **MAC address**. The packet is then framed with the **router's MAC as Layer-2 destination** but with the **original $\mathbf{8.8.8.8}$ as the Layer-3 destination** — a crucial distinction: **MAC changes at every hop, IP does not (in unicast routing without NAT).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{L2 (Ethernet)} &: \ \text{Dest MAC} = \text{Router MAC}, \quad \text{Src MAC} = \text{Host MAC} \\ \text{L3 (IP)} &: \ \text{Dest IP} = 8.8.8.8, \quad \text{Src IP} = 192.168.10.25 \end{aligned} $$ > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**MAC is local, IP is global.**" # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Router Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) > The following Mermaid diagram shows the **data-path architecture** of a router — input ports, switching fabric, output ports, and the routing processor that maintains the FIB. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph NetA[External Network A] NA1[Link from Network A] end subgraph Router1[Router Internals] IP1[Input Port 1
Decap and L2 termination] IP2[Input Port 2
Decap and L2 termination] SF1[Switching Fabric
Crossbar or Bus] OP1[Output Port 1
Buffer and Encapsulate] OP2[Output Port 2
Buffer and Encapsulate] RP1[Routing Processor
Runs OSPF and BGP] FIB1[(Forwarding Table FIB
TCAM lookup)] RIB1[(Routing Table RIB
Software computed)] end subgraph NetB[External Network B] NB1[Link to Network B] end NA1 --> IP1 IP2 --> SF1 IP1 --> SF1 SF1 --> OP1 SF1 --> OP2 OP1 --> NB1 RP1 -.installs routes.-> FIB1 FIB1 -.lookup at line rate.-> IP1 RIB1 -.computed via Dijkstra or BGP.-> RP1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** *Solid arrows* = data-plane (per-packet) traffic. *Dotted arrows* = control-plane (per-decision) flow. The FIB sits on the *fast path* (TCAM in the input port ASIC), while the RIB sits in the *slow path* (main CPU memory). ## 4.2 Gateway Protocol-Translation Flow (Layered View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SideA[Network A - TCP IP Stack] A7[App Layer SMTP] A4[Transport TCP] A3[Network IPv4] A2[Data Link Ethernet] end subgraph GW1[Gateway - Protocol Translator] T7[App Converter
SMTP to X.400] T4[Transport Mapper
TCP to TP4] T3[Network Translator
IPv4 to CLNP] T2[Link Adapter
Ethernet to X.25] end subgraph SideB[Network B - OSI Stack] B7[App Layer X.400] B4[Transport TP4] B3[Network CLNP] B2[Data Link X.25] end A7 --> T7 --> B7 A4 --> T4 --> B4 A3 --> T3 --> B3 A2 --> T2 --> B2 ``` > **Key Takeaway:** A gateway performs translation *at every layer where the stacks differ*. Unlike a router that only rewrites L2/L3 headers, a gateway may have to **interpret application-layer semantics** (e.g., translating MIME to X.400 body parts). ## 4.3 Router vs. Gateway Decision Flow (How to Classify a Device in KTU Exam) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Device connects which networks?} Q1 -->|Same protocol family
e.g. IP to IP| Q2{Forwarding basis?} Q1 -->|Different protocol stacks
e.g. IP to SNA| GW1[It is a GATEWAY] Q2 -->|MAC address
Layer 2| SW1[It is a SWITCH] Q2 -->|IP address
Layer 3| RT1[It is a ROUTER] Q2 -->|Application semantics| GW2[It is an APPLICATION GATEWAY] ``` ## 4.4 Packet Header Transformation Across Three Routers ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant HS as Host S 10.0.0.5 participant R1 as Router R1 participant R2 as Router R2 participant R3 as Router R3 participant HD as Host D 203.0.113.42 HS->>R1: Frame MAC_A_to_R1 IP 10.0.0.5 to 203.0.113.42 TTL 64 R1->>R2: Frame MAC_R1_to_R2 IP 10.0.0.5 to 203.0.113.42 TTL 63 R2->>R3: Frame MAC_R2_to_R3 IP 10.0.0.5 to 203.0.113.42 TTL 62 R3->>HD: Frame MAC_R3_to_HD IP 10.0.0.5 to 203.0.113.42 TTL 61 Note over HS,HD: Source and Destination IP remain unchanged end-to-end Note over R1,R3: MAC addresses are rewritten at every hop Note over R1,R3: TTL decremented by 1 at every hop ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Drawing Tip:** If asked to "draw the path of a packet through a router network", the examiner expects you to show *both* layers: the **MAC changes at every hop, the IP does not**, and the **TTL decreases by 1 at every router**. Forgetting any of these three facts is the single most common mark-loss. ## 4.5 Default Gateway Path (Host to Remote Internet) ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[Host 192.168.10.25/24] -->|ARP for 192.168.10.1| RG1[Default Gateway Router] RG1 -->|Forwards toward ISP| ISP1[ISP Edge Router] ISP1 -->|BGP to core| CORE1[Internet Backbone] CORE1 -->|BGP to destination AS| DST1[Destination Network Router] DST1 --> HD1[Host 8.8.8.8] ``` > **Note:** The "Default Gateway" in this figure is *literally a router* — its interface IP (192.168.10.1) is what the host has configured as its gateway. The host knows the gateway's IP but must **ARP** to discover its MAC before sending. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a Router and a Gateway. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Router | Gateway | |---|---|---| | **OSI Layer** | Operates primarily at **Layer 3 (Network)**. | Operates at **all 7 layers**, especially **Application Layer** for protocol conversion. | | **Function** | Forwards packets between **similar** IP networks using a routing table. | Translates packets between **dissimilar** network architectures/protocols. | | **Forwarding basis** | **IP address** + Longest Prefix Match. | **Protocol semantics** (e.g., SMTP ↔ X.400). | | **Speed** | Very fast, hardware-optimized (TCAM). | Slower, due to deep packet inspection and translation. | | **Example** | Cisco ISR 4000 series connecting two Ethernet LANs. | VoIP gateway between PSTN and SIP network. | > A "default gateway" in TCP/IP is colloquially a **router**, not a true OSI gateway. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is the function of a Routing Information Base (RIB) and how is it different from the Forwarding Information Base (FIB)? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - The **RIB** is the *logical* routing table computed by routing protocols (OSPF, BGP). It contains *all routes learned*, with metrics, next-hops, and administrative distances. It is maintained in **software (main CPU memory)**. - The **FIB** is a *hardware-optimized* subset of the RIB, programmed into **TCAM** on the line cards for **line-rate lookup** (nanosecond LPM). - The RIB is the *control plane*; the FIB is the *data plane*. The routing processor *installs* FIB entries from the RIB. **(3 marks)** --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Answer **one** of the two questions. Each has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Marks are awarded for: definition (2), diagram (2), explanation (2), example (1). --- ### **Question A** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) Explain the internal architecture of a router with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of input ports, switching fabric, and output ports.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A modern router is composed of four primary functional blocks: 1. **Input Ports (1.5 marks):** - Terminate the incoming physical link. - Perform **link-layer decapsulation** (strip Ethernet header, for example). - Look up the destination IP in the **FIB** (a TCAM-resident copy of the routing table). - Decide the appropriate **output port** and pass the packet to the switching fabric. - Modern input ports also perform **prefix aggregation**, **QoS classification**, and **ACL filtering**. 2. **Switching Fabric (2 marks):** - The internal "highway" connecting input and output ports. - **Three dominant designs:** - **Memory-based:** Packet copied to shared memory by CPU. *Slowest* (used in legacy/software routers). - **Bus-based:** A single shared backplane carries one packet at a time. *Bottlenecked.* - **Crossbar:** A matrix of switches that can establish **multiple simultaneous paths**. *Non-blocking*, used in core routers. 3. **Output Ports (1.5 marks):** - Receive packets from the switching fabric. - Store them in a **queue** (buffer) awaiting transmission. - Perform **link-layer encapsulation** (add Ethernet header for next hop). - Handle **congestion management** (e.g., **Weighted Random Early Detection — WRED**). 4. **Routing Processor (2 marks for diagram + explanation):** - Runs the **routing protocols** (OSPF, BGP). - Maintains the **Routing Information Base (RIB)** in software. - Installs the **FIB** onto each input port's TCAM. - Executes management functions: SNMP, CLI, logging. **Block Diagram (text representation — 2 marks if drawn neatly in exam):** > `Incoming Link → Input Port → Switching Fabric → Output Port → Outgoing Link` > with the **Routing Processor** connected to all three via a control bus, and the **FIB/RIB** as separate memory units. --- **(b) With a suitable example, explain the working of a Gateway. How is it different from a Router? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** A gateway is a network device that operates at **all seven layers of the OSI model** and performs **protocol conversion** between two *dissimilar* networks that use different communication protocols, data formats, or architectures. **Working with Example (3 marks):** *Example: Email Gateway between Internet (TCP/IP) and Legacy X.400 network.* - A user on the Internet composes an email in **MIME format over SMTP (port 25)**. - The gateway receives the SMTP message. - It **parses the application-layer content** (headers, body, attachments). - It **converts** the MIME structures to **X.400 body parts** (IPM, IPN, etc.). - It **re-packages** the message using the **X.400 P1/P2 protocols** over an **X.25** link. - A reverse translation happens for the reply. Other examples: **VoIP Gateway** (PSTN ↔ SIP), **IoT Gateway** (Modbus ↔ MQTT), **IBM Mainframe Gateway** (SNA ↔ TCP/IP). **Difference from Router (2 marks):** - A **router** connects networks running the **same protocol family** (e.g., two IP networks) and forwards packets using **IP addressing** at Layer 3. - A **gateway** connects **architecturally different** networks and performs **deep protocol translation** at multiple layers, including application semantics. - A router is *fast* (hardware lookup); a gateway is *slower* due to processing complexity. - A router is *transparent* to applications; a gateway is *visible* (e.g., it appears as an MX mail server). --- ### **Question B** — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]** **(a) Explain the different routing algorithms: Distance Vector and Link State. Compare their merits and demerits.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Distance Vector Routing (3 marks):** - **Principle:** Each router maintains a table of *distances* to all known destinations from the perspective of its *direct neighbors*. Periodically (e.g., every 30s in RIP), the entire table is shared with neighbors — *"routing by rumor."* - **Algorithm:** **Bellman-Ford**. - **Example:** **RIP** (Routing Information Protocol). Maximum hops = 15; metric = hop count. - **Merits:** Simple, low memory, easy to configure. - **Demerits:** **Slow convergence** (count-to-infinity problem), routing loops possible, scalability limited. **Link State Routing (3 marks):** - **Principle:** Every router discovers its *directly connected* neighbors, builds a **Link State Packet (LSP)** containing the state and cost of each link, **floods** the LSP to *all* routers in the area, and then each router runs **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF)** algorithm on the resulting complete topology map to compute the best path to every destination. - **Example:** **OSPF**, **IS-IS**. - **Merits:** Fast convergence, no routing loops, hierarchical (areas). - **Demerits:** Higher CPU/memory requirement, more complex configuration, requires careful area design. **Comparison (1 mark):** | Property | Distance Vector | Link State | |---|---|---| | View of network | Neighbor's view (partial) | Complete topology map | | Algorithm | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra (SPF) | | Update type | Periodic full table | Event-driven LSPs (flooded) | | Convergence | Slow | Fast | | Scalability | Small (RIP: 15 hops) | Large (OSPF: hierarchical) | --- **(b) A host with IP address 192.168.10.25/24 sends a packet to 8.8.8.8. Explain step-by-step how the host decides whether to use the default gateway, and what happens to the packet at Layer 2 and Layer 3 as it traverses the first router.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Subnet Determination (2 marks):** The host computes its own network and the destination's network by **bitwise AND** with the subnet mask: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Host network} &= 192.168.10.25 \ \vert\ \&\ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.10.0 \\ \text{Dest network} &= 8.8.8.8 \ \vert\ \&\ 255.255.255.0 = 8.8.8.0 \end{aligned} $$ Since the two network IDs differ ($\mathbf{192.168.10.0 \neq 8.8.8.0}$), the destination is **off-subnet** and the packet must be sent to the **default gateway**. **Step 2 — ARP for the Default Gateway (2 marks):** - The host knows the default gateway IP from its configuration (e.g., **192.168.10.1**). - It does *not* know the gateway's MAC address, so it broadcasts an **ARP request**: *"Who has 192.168.10.1? Tell 192.168.10.25."* - The router's interface replies with its **MAC address** (e.g., `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:01`). - The host now has both the destination IP (**8.8.8.8**) and the **next-hop MAC** (router's MAC). **Step 3 — Frame Construction and Transmission (1.5 marks):** - **Layer 2 (Ethernet Frame):** Source MAC = Host MAC; Destination MAC = **Router MAC**. - **Layer 3 (IP Packet):** Source IP = **192.168.10.25**; Destination IP = **8.8.8.8**; TTL = 64 (initial). **Step 4 — Action at the First Router (1.5 marks):** 1. The router receives the frame, **strips** the Ethernet header. 2. It **decrements TTL**: $64 - 1 = 63$. 3. It looks up **8.8.8.8** in its **FIB** using Longest Prefix Match. 4. It finds the best next-hop (say, interface $G_0/1$). 5. It re-encapsulates the packet in a **new Ethernet frame** with a *new* destination MAC (next-hop router) but the **same destination IP (8.8.8.8)**. 6. The packet is forwarded on $G_0/1$. > **Conclusion:** The MAC address changes at every hop; the IP address remains unchanged end-to-end (in a routing scenario without NAT). **(7 marks)** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Router/Gateway Questions:** > 1. **Confusing Router and Gateway:** Many students write "Gateway is used to connect different networks" and lose 2 marks. The correct phrasing is: *Gateway is used to connect networks running **different protocols**.* Two IP networks are connected by a **router**, not a gateway. > 2. **Forgetting the "Default Gateway is a Router" caveat:** In modern TCP/IP, the term "default gateway" is *not* an OSI gateway — it is the *router's interface IP*. Failing to note this in a 14-marker is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction. > 3. **Missing the L2 vs. L3 distinction in packet forwarding:** Students often write "the IP address changes at the router" — this is **wrong** (unless NAT is involved). IP is end-to-end; MAC is hop-by-hop. > 4. **Drawing the router as a "box" without internal blocks:** A block diagram with input port → switching fabric → output port, plus the routing processor, is mandatory for full marks on the 7-mark architecture question. > 5. **Omitting TTL in forwarding steps:** Every router decrements TTL by 1. Skipping this in a numerical problem costs at least 1 mark. > 6. **Confusing RIB with FIB:** These are *not* the same. RIB is the software routing table; FIB is the hardware forwarding table derived from it. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Routers & Gateways (Module 2)** - ✅ A **Router** is a **Layer 3 device** that forwards IP packets between networks using a **routing table (FIB)**. - ✅ A **Gateway** operates at **all 7 OSI layers** and performs **protocol conversion** between **dissimilar** networks. - ✅ The **Default Gateway** of a host is *typically a router's interface IP*, not an OSI gateway. - ✅ Router **internal architecture**: Input Ports → Switching Fabric (Memory / Bus / Crossbar) → Output Ports, with a **Routing Processor** maintaining the **RIB** and installing the **FIB**. - ✅ **Forwarding basis at a router:** Longest Prefix Match on the destination IP, with **TTL decremented by 1** at every hop. - ✅ **MAC changes at every hop; IP is end-to-end** (in unicast routing without NAT). - ✅ **Routing algorithms:** Distance Vector (Bellman-Ford, RIP) vs. Link State (Dijkstra, OSPF). BGP is path-vector, used between Autonomous Systems. - ✅ **OSPF cost** = $\lfloor BW_{ref} / BW_{link} \rfloor$ (Cisco default $BW_{ref} = 100$ Mbps). - ✅ **RIP** has a **15-hop maximum**; hop count 16 = unreachable. - ✅ **End-to-end delay** through $N$ routers = $\sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i} + d_{trans,i} + d_{prop,i})$. - ✅ **Bottleneck bandwidth** along a path = $\min(BW_i)$ across all links. - ✅ **ICMP messages** generated by routers: **Type 11** (Time Exceeded, TTL = 0), **Type 3** (Destination Unreachable, FIB miss). - ✅ **Gateway examples:** Email gateway (SMTP ↔ X.400), VoIP gateway (PSTN ↔ SIP), IoT gateway (Modbus ↔ MQTT). - ✅ **Standard references:** **ISO/IEC 7498-1** (gateway definition), **RFC 1812** (IPv4 routers), **RFC 4291** (IPv6 addressing), **RFC 791** (IP, TTL). - ✅ **Key contrast:** Router = *language you already speak*; Gateway = *customs officer for the language you don't*.

topics.title: Network Layer Design Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Network Layer Design Issues # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) The **Network Layer** is the third layer of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)** reference model and is responsible for the end-to-end delivery of data packets from a source host to a destination host across multiple interconnected networks. The **Network Layer Design Issues** refer to the set of architectural and operational decisions that govern how packets are routed, forwarded, addressed, and controlled within an internetwork. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The design issues encompass five pivotal concerns: (1) Store-and-Forward Packet Switching, (2) Services provided to the Transport Layer, (3) Implementation of Connectionless Service, (4) Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service, and (5) Comparison between Virtual Circuit (VC) and Datagram Subnets. The network layer must answer several critical design questions before a packet leaves its source: | Design Question | Engineering Concern | |---|---| | Who is responsible for routing? | Router logic vs. source routing | | What addressing scheme is used? | Hierarchical vs. flat addressing | | Is the service connection-oriented or connectionless? | Virtual circuits vs. datagrams | | How are congestion and errors handled? | Hop-by-hop vs. end-to-end control | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The National Postal Service > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Engine:** Imagine the **Network Layer** as the **Indian National Postal System**. > - **Post Office (Router):** Each post office receives mail (packets), inspects the destination pincode (IP address), and decides the next dispatch point. It does not deliver the mail itself to the final house; it only keeps forwarding. > - **Store-and-Forward:** A post office does not open a truck door and start distributing letter-by-letter mid-transit. It waits for the *entire* consignment to arrive, then forwards it. This is exactly how routers handle packets. > - **Datagram Service (Postcard):** Every postcard is independently routed. Two postcards mailed at the same time to the same address may travel via completely different cities, in different orders, and arrive at different times. > - **Virtual Circuit Service (Phone Call):** A path is established (dialing), data flows in order, and the path is torn down (hang up). The network guarantees ordered delivery along the established route. ## 1.3 Fundamental Operational Metrics > [!TIP] > The following are the **standard KTU-referenced metrics** for any network layer design analysis: > - **Packet Size:** Variable (typically 64 bytes to 1500 bytes in Ethernet). > - **Header Overhead:** Generally **20 bytes** (IPv4) or **40 bytes** (IPv6). > - **Throughput Unit:** Measured in **bits per second (bps)** or **packets per second (pps)**. > - **Hop Count:** Integer value $\geq 1$ representing the number of routers traversed. ## 1.4 The Five Core Design Issues at a Glance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Issue 1 — Store-and-Forward Packet Switching:** A packet must be received in its entirety by a router before it can be transmitted on the outgoing link. > **Issue 2 — Services to the Transport Layer:** Defines whether the network offers a connection-oriented or connectionless interface. > **Issue 3 — Connectionless Implementation:** Each packet (datagram) is routed independently. > **Issue 4 — Connection-Oriented Implementation:** A virtual circuit is set up before data transfer. > **Issue 5 — VC vs. Datagram Comparison:** Trade-offs in reliability, state, and overhead. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Issue 1 — Store-and-Forward Packet Switching In **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching**, every router along the path receives the complete packet, stores it in its buffer (memory), performs a routing table lookup, and then forwards it to the next hop. **Operational Logic Steps:** - **Step 1:** The source host transmits the packet onto the first link. - **Step 2:** The intermediate router receives *all bits* of the packet into its local buffer. - **Step 3:** Only after the last bit arrives does the router begin transmitting the packet on the outgoing link. - **Step 4:** This process repeats at every hop until the packet reaches the destination. > [!NOTE] > **Why Store-and-Forward?** It allows the router to perform error checking (CRC verification), check the packet's validity, and make intelligent routing decisions. Without it, a router would be a "repeater" with no intelligence. ### 2.1.1 End-to-End Delay Calculation For a packet traversing **N links** (N-1 routers), the total end-to-end delay is computed as: $$T_{total} = N \times \left( T_{transmission} + T_{propagation} + T_{processing} \right)$$ Where: - $T_{transmission} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ (packet length / link bandwidth) - $T_{propagation} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ (link length / signal propagation speed) - $T_{processing}$ = router lookup, error check, and queuing time ## 2.2 Issue 2 — Services Provided to the Transport Layer The network layer offers two fundamentally different service models to the upper **Transport Layer (Layer 4)**: | Service Model | Guarantee | Analogy | |---|---|---| | **Connectionless (Datagram)** | Best-effort, no guarantees | Postal system | | **Connection-Oriented (Virtual Circuit)** | Guaranteed, ordered, reliable path | Telephone call | > [!TIP] > **KTU Critical Distinction:** The Internet (IP) uses the **connectionless** model, while traditional telecom networks (ATM, Frame Relay) and X.25 use the **connection-oriented** model. This is a classic 2-mark or 3-mark question. ## 2.3 Issue 3 — Implementation of Connectionless Service In a **Datagram Subnet**, each packet is treated as an independent entity. The router uses the destination address in the packet header to make forwarding decisions, but no prior path is established. **Key Characteristics:** - **No setup phase** is required. - Each packet is called a **datagram**. - Routers maintain **no per-flow state** (stateless forwarding). - Different packets between the same source-destination pair may take **different routes**. - Packets may arrive **out of order** at the receiver. ## 2.4 Issue 4 — Implementation of Connection-Oriented Service In a **Virtual Circuit (VC) Subnet**, a logical path (the virtual circuit) is established between source and destination before any data is transmitted. This is implemented using **virtual circuit identifiers (VCIs)** at each hop. **Phases of a Virtual Circuit:** - **Phase 1 — VC Setup:** A signaling message travels from source to destination, and each router on the path allocates a VCI and updates its routing table. - **Phase 2 — Data Transfer:** Each packet carries a VCI. Routers forward the packet based on (incoming interface, VCI) lookups — no need to read the full destination address. - **Phase 3 — VC Teardown:** A teardown signal is sent, and each router releases the VCI and removes its table entry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** A Virtual Circuit is "virtual" because it is only a logical connection — the underlying physical path may be shared with other VCs, and may even change hop-by-hop. ## 2.5 Issue 5 — Comparison: Virtual Circuit vs. Datagram Subnet > [!NOTE] > This is a **highly recurring KTU question**, typically worth 7 or 14 marks. Memorize the table below. | Parameter | Virtual Circuit | Datagram | |---|---|---| | **Setup Phase** | Required | Not required | | **State Information** | Routers maintain per-VC state | Routers are stateless | | **Addressing** | Each packet has a short VCI | Each packet has full source & destination address | | **Routing Decision** | Made once at setup | Made independently per packet | | **Path** | Fixed for entire connection | May vary per packet | | **Failure Handling** | All VCs through a failed router are terminated | Packets can be rerouted around failures | | **Congestion Control** | Easier (admission control at setup) | Harder (no prior reservation) | | **Overhead** | Setup + teardown overhead | Per-packet addressing overhead | | **QoS Support** | Strong (suitable for voice/video) | Weak (best-effort) | | **Example Technologies** | X.25, Frame Relay, ATM, MPLS | IPv4, IPv6, UDP | ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | End-to-End Delay (Store-and-Forward) | $T_{total} = N(T_t + T_p + T_{proc})$ | seconds | | 2 | Transmission Delay | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds; $L$ in bits, $R$ in bps | | 3 | Propagation Delay | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | seconds; $d$ in meters, $s \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber | | 4 | Queueing Delay (M/M/1) | $T_q = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = utilization | | 5 | Number of Hops | $h = N - 1$ | dimensionless integer $\geq 0$ | | 6 | Total Bits Transmitted per Packet | $B_{total} = L_{header} + L_{data}$ | bits | | 7 | Throughput | $\theta = \dfrac{L_{data}}{T_{total}}$ | bits per second (bps) | | 8 | VC Identifier Length | $VCI \in [0, 2^{n} - 1]$ | integer, n = bits in VCI field | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > The **Virtual Circuit** model is the foundation of modern **MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching)** networks used by ISPs for traffic engineering. The **Datagram** model powers the public Internet (IPv4/IPv6). Understanding these design issues is essential for engineers building **SDN (Software Defined Networking)** controllers, designing **5G core networks**, and architecting **cloud data center fabrics**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total End-to-End Delay for a Multi-Hop Path **Given:** A packet of length $L = 1500$ bytes travels across a path with $N = 5$ links. Each link has a bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps. Propagation speed $s = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Each link has length $d = 1000$ km. Router processing delay $T_{proc} = 1$ ms. **Find:** Total end-to-end delay $T_{total}$. ### Step 1: Convert Units Packet length: $$L = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 1500 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 12000 \text{ bits}$$ Link distance: $$d = 1000 \text{ km} = 1000 \times 10^{3} \text{ m} = 10^{6} \text{ m}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Transmission Delay per Hop Using $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$: $$T_t = \frac{12000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Propagation Delay per Hop Using $T_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$: $$T_p = \frac{10^{6} \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 4: Sum Per-Hop Delay Each link contributes the sum of transmission, propagation, and processing delays. For one hop: $$T_{hop} = T_t + T_p + T_{proc} = 12 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} + 1 \text{ ms} = 18 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 5: Multiply by Number of Links For $N = 5$ links: $$T_{total} = N \times T_{hop} = 5 \times 18 \text{ ms} = 90 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 6: Interpretation > [!NOTE] > A 1500-byte packet sent over 5 links of 1 Mbps bandwidth with 1000 km each takes **90 milliseconds** to deliver end-to-end. This is why bandwidth, distance, and hop count are the three primary design variables in network layer engineering. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Comparing VC Overhead vs. Datagram Overhead **Given:** A session transmits $P = 100$ packets. Each data packet has payload $L_d = 1000$ bits. Each VC setup/teardown packet has size $L_{vc} = 200$ bits. Each datagram header $H_d = 40$ bits; each VC identifier $H_{vc} = 8$ bits. **Find:** Total bits transmitted on the network (header + data) for each model. ### Step 1: Datagram Network — Total Bits Each of the 100 packets must carry a full 40-bit datagram header: $$B_{datagram} = P \times (L_d + H_d) + 0 \text{ (setup)} = 100 \times (1000 + 40) = 100 \times 1040$$ $$B_{datagram} = 104000 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 2: Virtual Circuit Network — Total Bits Each data packet carries a short 8-bit VCI, but two extra control packets (setup + teardown) are sent: $$B_{VC} = 2 \times L_{vc} + P \times (L_d + H_{vc})$$ $$B_{VC} = 2 \times 200 + 100 \times (1000 + 8) = 400 + 100 \times 1008$$ $$B_{VC} = 400 + 100800 = 101200 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 3: Comparison $$B_{datagram} - B_{VC} = 104000 - 101200 = 2800 \text{ bits}$$ > [!TIP] > For this scenario, **VC is more efficient by 2800 bits**. However, the breakeven point depends on session length. For very short sessions, the VC setup overhead dominates; for very long sessions, the per-packet header savings dominate. ### Step 4: Breakeven Point Derivation Setting $B_{VC} = B_{datagram}$: $$2L_{vc} + P(L_d + H_{vc}) = P(L_d + H_d)$$ $$2L_{vc} = P(H_d - H_{vc})$$ $$P_{breakeven} = \frac{2 L_{vc}}{H_d - H_{vc}}$$ Substituting values: $$P_{breakeven} = \frac{2 \times 200}{40 - 8} = \frac{400}{32} = 12.5 \text{ packets}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For $P > 12.5$ packets, **Virtual Circuit** has lower total overhead. For $P < 12.5$ packets, **Datagram** is more efficient. This is a key engineering trade-off! --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing a Service Model | Application Requirement | Recommended Service | Justification | |---|---|---| | Real-time VoIP / Video | Virtual Circuit | Low jitter, QoS guarantees | | Email / File Transfer | Datagram | Bursty traffic, no latency sensitivity | | Short DNS Query | Datagram | Setup overhead dominates | | Long Database Session | Virtual Circuit | Setup amortized over many packets | | Mobile / Wireless | Datagram | Topology changes, frequent handoffs | | Mission-Critical Banking | Virtual Circuit | Predictable, auditable path | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Datagram Subnet Architecture > [!NOTE] > The diagram below illustrates the architecture of a **datagram subnet**. Each packet is independently routed at every router based on the destination address. ```mermaid flowchart LR srcH[Source Host A] --> rtr1{Router 1} rtr1 -->|Packet 1| rtr2{Router 2} rtr1 -->|Packet 2| rtr3{Router 3} rtr2 --> rtr4{Router 4} rtr3 --> rtr4 rtr4 --> dstH[Destination Host B] rtr2 -.Alternative Path.-> rtr3 classDef router fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef host fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class rtr1,rtr2,rtr3,rtr4 router class srcH,dstH host ``` **Key Visual Takeaway:** Notice how **Packet 1** and **Packet 2** from the same source take **different paths** through the network. This is the defining feature of a datagram subnet. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Virtual Circuit Subnet Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > In a VC subnet, all packets from the same source-destination pair follow the **same established path**. The VCI is a short label (not a full address) used for fast lookup. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Host A] -->|Setup: VCI 5| B{Router 1} B -->|VCI 5 -> VCI 12| C{Router 2} C -->|VCI 12 -> VCI 7| D{Router 3} D -->|VCI 7| E[Destination Host B] A -.Data Packet with VCI 5.-> B B -.Data Packet with VCI 12.-> C C -.Data Packet with VCI 7.-> D D -.Data Packet with VCI 7.-> E A -.Teardown Signal.-> B classDef router fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef host fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class B,C,D router class A,E host ``` **Key Visual Takeaway:** The VCI is **re-mapped** at every router. Router 1 uses VCI 5 on its incoming interface; Router 2 uses VCI 12. This is called **VC translation** and allows efficient label-swapping forwarding. --- ## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — Choosing Between VC and Datagram ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Design Decision] --> q1{Is real-time
QoS required?} q1 -->|Yes| vc1[Use Virtual Circuit] q1 -->|No| q2{Is traffic bursty
and short-lived?} q2 -->|Yes| dg1[Use Datagram] q2 -->|No| q3{Is network topology
highly dynamic?} q3 -->|Yes| dg2[Use Datagram] q3 -->|No| vc2[Use Virtual Circuit] classDef choice fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class start choice class q1,q2,q3 decision class vc1,vc2,dg1,dg2 choice ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Store-and-Forward Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Packet Arrives at Router] --> B[Store in Input Buffer] B --> C[Receive All Bits] C --> D[Compute CRC Check] D --> E{CRC Valid?} E -->|No| F[Discard Packet] E -->|Yes| G[Look Up Destination in Routing Table] G --> H[Determine Outgoing Interface] H --> I[Add to Output Queue] I --> J[Transmit on Outgoing Link] F --> K[Send ICMP Error to Source] J --> L[Forward to Next Hop] classDef process fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef action fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C,G,H,I,J,L process class D,E decision class F,K action ``` **Explanation of the Functional Flow:** - **A → C:** The packet is buffered bit-by-bit until the entire packet is received. This is the essence of *store-and-forward*. - **D → E:** A cyclic redundancy check is performed. Corrupted packets are dropped (no retransmission at the network layer). - **G → H:** The router uses its routing table (e.g., built by OSPF, BGP) to find the next hop. - **I → L:** The packet is queued and forwarded, repeating the cycle at the next router. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Store-and-Forward Packet Switching. **`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`** **Model Answer:** > Store-and-Forward Packet Switching is a technique used in packet-switched networks where a router must receive the **entire packet** in its buffer before it begins transmitting the packet on the outgoing link. This allows the router to perform **error detection (CRC check)** and **routing decisions** based on the destination address in the packet header. Each packet experiences transmission delay $T_t = L/R$ at every hop, and the total end-to-end delay is the sum of delays across all hops. **(3 marks: Definition 1m, Mechanics 1m, Formula 1m)** --- ### Question 2: List any three differences between Virtual Circuit and Datagram Subnets. **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`** **Model Answer:** | # | Virtual Circuit | Datagram | |---|---|---| | 1 | Requires a setup phase before data transfer | No setup phase is needed | | 2 | All packets follow the same path | Packets may take different paths | | 3 | Routers maintain per-connection state | Routers are stateless | **(3 marks: 1 mark per valid difference)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** In VC vs. Datagram comparison questions, students often confuse "path" with "physical route." Remember: a Virtual Circuit is a **logical** path, and the underlying physical route may change hop-by-hop via VCI translation. Also, do not claim that "datagrams are unreliable" — they are *best-effort*; the network layer in the Internet does not guarantee delivery, but this does not mean the network is broken. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the **Store-and-Forward Packet Switching** mechanism with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the end-to-end delay of a packet traversing $N$ links. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **Virtual Circuit** and **Datagram** subnets across any **seven** key parameters. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Definition (2 marks):** Store-and-Forward Packet Switching is the fundamental operational model of packet-switched networks in which each intermediate router must receive and store the complete packet in its buffer before beginning to forward it on the next link. **Mechanism with Diagram (2 marks):** Consider a path: Source → R1 → R2 → R3 → Destination (4 links, 3 routers). At each router: 1. All bits of the packet are received and stored. 2. The router checks for errors (CRC verification). 3. The router consults its routing table. 4. The packet is queued for transmission on the outgoing link. **Derivation of End-to-End Delay (3 marks):** Let $L$ = packet length (bits), $R_i$ = bandwidth of link $i$ (bps), $d_i$ = length of link $i$, $s$ = propagation speed, $T_{proc,i}$ = processing delay at router $i$. For a single link $i$, the delay incurred is: $$T_i = T_{t,i} + T_{p,i} + T_{proc,i} = \frac{L}{R_i} + \frac{d_i}{s} + T_{proc,i}$$ For $N$ links in series, the total end-to-end delay is: $$T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{L}{R_i} + \frac{d_i}{s} + T_{proc,i} \right)$$ If all links are identical ($R_i = R$, $d_i = d$, $T_{proc,i} = T_{proc}$): $$T_{total} = N \left( \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{s} + T_{proc} \right)$$ --- #### Model Solution to (b): **Virtual Circuit vs. Datagram Comparison (7 marks — 1 mark per parameter):** | # | Parameter | Virtual Circuit | Datagram | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Connection Setup** | Required (signaling phase) | Not required | | 2 | **Addressing in Packet** | Short VCI label | Full source & destination address | | 3 | **Routing Decision** | Made once at setup | Made per packet | | 4 | **Router State** | Stateful (per-VC table) | Stateless | | 5 | **Effect of Router Failure** | All VCs through it terminate | Packets can be rerouted | | 6 | **Congestion Control** | Easier (admission control) | Harder (no reservation) | | 7 | **Quality of Service (QoS)** | Strong support | Weak (best-effort) | | 8 | **Suitability** | Real-time (voice, video) | Bursty data (web, email) | | 9 | **Example** | X.25, ATM, MPLS | IP (IPv4, IPv6) | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **(a)** What are the **services provided by the Network Layer to the Transport Layer**? Explain the two major service models with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **implementation of a Connection-Oriented Service** using Virtual Circuits. List the three phases involved. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Introduction (1 mark):** The Network Layer (Layer 3) provides end-to-end packet delivery services to the Transport Layer (Layer 4). The two fundamental service models are: **1. Connectionless Service (2 marks):** - **Definition:** Each packet is treated independently. No prior path is established. - **Packets:** Called **datagrams**. - **Guarantees:** None — best-effort delivery. - **Example:** The Internet Protocol (IP). - **Analogy:** Postal system — letters may arrive out of order or be lost. **2. Connection-Oriented Service (2 marks):** - **Definition:** A logical path (virtual circuit) is established between source and destination before data transfer. - **Phases:** Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown. - **Guarantees:** Ordered, reliable packet delivery along the established path. - **Example:** X.25, ATM, Frame Relay. - **Analogy:** Telephone call — a dedicated logical channel is set up. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Feature | Connectionless | Connection-Oriented | |---|---|---| | Path Setup | No | Yes | | Packet Order | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed | | Router State | Stateless | Stateful | | Overhead | Low | Higher | | Example | IP | ATM | --- #### Model Solution to (b): **Diagram (3 marks):** ``` [Host A] --VC5--> [R1] --VC12--> [R2] --VC9--> [R3] --VC9--> [Host B] ``` (VCI translations occur at every router.) **Three Phases Explained (4 marks):** **Phase 1: Virtual Circuit Setup (1.5 marks)** - A **CALL REQUEST** packet travels from source to destination. - Each router on the path allocates a **VCI** for the new connection and creates an entry in its VC table mapping `(incoming_interface, incoming_VCI) → (outgoing_interface, outgoing_VCI)`. - The destination replies with a **CALL ACCEPTED** packet. **Phase 2: Data Transfer (1.5 marks)** - All data packets carry only the short **VCI label** (not a full destination address). - Routers perform fast table lookups using the VCI to determine the outgoing interface. - The original full addressing is used only at endpoints, making forwarding extremely efficient. **Phase 3: Virtual Circuit Teardown (1 mark)** - A **CLEAR REQUEST** signal is sent. - Each router deletes the corresponding VCI entry from its table. - The virtual circuit is dissolved, and resources are released. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** In modern networks, **MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching)** uses this exact model. Each MPLS label acts as a VCI, allowing ISPs to build fast, QoS-aware tunnels over IP networks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Network Layer Design Issues** - **Store-and-Forward:** Router must receive the **entire packet** before forwarding. Total delay = $N \times (T_t + T_p + T_{proc})$. - **Service Models:** Connectionless (datagrams, no setup) vs. Connection-Oriented (VCs, three phases). - **Connectionless Implementation:** Each packet routed independently. No router state. Internet IP is the canonical example. - **Connection-Oriented Implementation:** Three phases — **Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown**. VCI is used for fast forwarding. - **VC vs. Datagram Trade-off:** VCs offer QoS, ordered delivery, and easy congestion control but require setup and are vulnerable to router failures. Datagrams are robust, simple, and ideal for bursty traffic. - **Header Sizes:** Datagram header is large (full address); VC label is short (8–20 bits typically). - **Breakeven Point:** For sessions longer than $P_{breakeven} = \frac{2L_{vc}}{H_d - H_{vc}}$ packets, VC overhead is lower than datagram overhead. - **Examples to Remember:** **IP** = datagram; **ATM, X.25, Frame Relay, MPLS** = virtual circuit. - **Key Formula:** $T_t = L/R$ (transmission), $T_p = d/s$ (propagation), $s \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in optical fiber. - **OSI Layer:** Network Layer is **Layer 3**; Transport Layer is **Layer 4**. - **Stateless vs. Stateful:** Datagram networks are stateless; VC networks are stateful. - **Exam Trick:** When asked "compare," always use a **table with at least 5–7 rows**. KTU examiners reward structured answers.

topics.title: Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Routing Algorithms - The Optimality Principle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Optimality Principle (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)** > *If router J lies on the optimal (shortest) path from router I to router K, then the optimal path from J to K is also the *same* path segment — i.e., it is a subset of the optimal path from I to K.* Formally, let $d(i, k)$ denote the cost of the optimal (shortest) path from router $i$ to router $k$. The Optimality Principle states: $$ \text{If } j \in \text{OptimalPath}(i \rightarrow k), \text{ then } \text{OptimalPath}(j \rightarrow k) \subseteq \text{OptimalPath}(i \rightarrow k) $$ This means the optimal routes from all sources to a given destination form a **tree rooted at the destination** — called a **Sink Tree** (or **Forwarding Tree**). The sink tree contains no loops, and every router appears exactly once along the unique path to the destination. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Google Maps Shortcut"** > Imagine you are driving from Kochi (I) to Delhi (K), and the app shows the fastest highway route passes through Bengaluru (J). Now, if you *already start* from Bengaluru and want to go to Delhi, the app will *never* suggest a different exit at Bengaluru — it simply continues along the same highway. Why? Because **the fastest route from a mid-point of the optimal route must also be optimal** for the rest of the journey. The Optimality Principle is this exact logic applied to router tables: the best next-hop from any node on the optimal path is itself optimal. ## 1.3 The Sink Tree — Visualization of Optimal Routes A **sink tree** is the union of all optimal paths from *every* source node to a specific destination (the **sink** or **root**). Key properties: - **Root** = destination router. - **Branches** = optimal next hops. - **Loop-free** (acyclic). - Each node has exactly **one** path to the root. > [!TIP] > **KTU Key Highlight:** In the **2024 Scheme syllabus**, the Optimality Principle is the theoretical foundation that justifies **shortest-path routing algorithms** (Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford). It guarantees that a tree (and *not* a general graph) is sufficient to represent optimal routes — which is why router forwarding tables can be implemented using only the *next-hop* field. ## 1.4 Why Does This Principle Matter? | Reason | Engineering Significance | |--------|--------------------------| | **Loop Prevention** | A sink tree is inherently loop-free → no count-to-infinity problems. | | **Table Simplicity** | Routers only need to store the next-hop, not the entire path. | | **Convergence Proof** | Algorithms like Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford converge *because* an optimal tree exists. | | **Distributed Computing** | Each router can independently compute optimal routes if it knows the network topology. | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Optimality Principle The principle can be decomposed into three logical assertions: 1. **Existence of an Optimal Path:** For every source-destination pair in a connected graph with non-negative edge weights, a finite optimal path exists (because $N$ nodes ⇒ at most $N-1$ hops). 2. **Sub-path Optimality:** Every sub-segment of an optimal path is itself optimal. 3. **Tree Formation:** All optimal paths to a destination form a directed tree rooted at that destination — this is the **sink tree**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical Re-statement (KTU Exam Style):** > Let $d(i,k)$ = shortest distance from node $i$ to node $k$. > Then $\forall$ nodes $j$ on the shortest path from $i$ to $k$: > $$d(i, k) = d(i, j) + d(j, k)$$ ## 2.2 Consequences in Real-World Routing Protocols | Protocol | Family | Algorithm Basis | Uses Optimality Principle? | |----------|--------|-----------------|----------------------------| | **OSPF** | Link-State | Dijkstra | ✅ Yes (sink tree per area) | | **IS-IS** | Link-State | Dijkstra | ✅ Yes | | **RIP** | Distance-Vector | Bellman-Ford | ✅ Yes (but partially) | | **BGP** | Path-Vector | Policy-based | ❌ Not strictly (uses policy) | | **EIGRP** | Hybrid | DUAL | ✅ Yes (DUAL = Diffusing Update ALgorithm) | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Note on table syntax:** All absolute values / norms use `\vert` instead of `|` to protect table integrity. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Description | Boundary Condition / Unit | |---|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------| | 1 | **Shortest path definition** | $d(i,k) = \min_{\forall \text{paths}} \sum e$ | $e \geq 0$ for all edges | | 2 | **Optimality (triangle) condition** | $d(i,k) \leq d(i,j) + d(j,k)$ | Holds for *all* $j$ | | 3 | **Sub-path optimality** | If $j \in P(i,k)^{*}$ then $d(j,k) = \text{cost of sub-path}$ | $P^{*}$ = optimal path | | 4 | **Bellman-Ford relaxation** | $d(v) = \min\{\, d(u) + w(u,v)\,\} \quad \forall u \in N(v)$ | $N(v)$ = neighbours of $v$ | | 5 | **Dijkstra relaxation** | $d(v) = d(u) + w(u,v)$ if $d(u) + w < d(v)$ | Requires non-negative weights | | 6 | **Number of iterations (B-F)** | At most $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations | $\vert V \vert$ = # vertices | | 7 | **Sink tree node count** | Exactly $\vert V \vert$ nodes (including root) | Acyclic, directed | | 8 | **Loop-free condition** | $\nexists$ directed cycle in sink tree | Necessary & sufficient | | 9 | **Hop count (RIP max)** | $\leq 15$ hops | $16 = \infty$ in RIP | | 10 | **OSPF link cost** | $c = \frac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Link BW}}$ | Default ref = 100 Mbps | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Internet Backbone (ISPs):** OSPF routers in the same area *independently* compute the same sink tree to every destination, guaranteeing consistent forwarding decisions. - **Data Center Networks (Spine-Leaf):** ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) is a direct application of the Optimality Principle when *multiple* equal-cost shortest paths exist. - **SDN Controllers:** Modern OpenFlow controllers pre-compute sink trees for every switch and install flow rules that mirror the tree structure. ## 2.5 Limitations / Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > - The Optimality Principle assumes **static topology** and **non-negative link costs**. It fails when costs change rapidly (e.g., mobile ad-hoc networks without a stable link-state). > - In **policy-based routing (BGP)**, optimality is *not* based on path length but on administrative preferences — the principle does **not** strictly apply. > - With **negative edge weights**, Dijkstra fails; Bellman-Ford must be used (and the principle still holds but only for graphs without negative cycles). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Proof of the Optimality Principle (Triangle Inequality Argument) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem:** *If $j$ is on the shortest path from $i$ to $k$, then the shortest path from $j$ to $k$ lies entirely on the same path.* > > **Proof:** Let $P^* = (i \rightarrow j \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow k)$ be the optimal path with cost $d(i,k)$. **Step 1 — Decompose the optimal cost** $$ d(i,k) = d(i,j) + d(j,k) $$ where $d(i,j)$ = cost of sub-path from $i$ to $j$, and $d(j,k)$ = cost from $j$ to $k$. **Step 2 — Assume a contradiction** Suppose there exists a *strictly shorter* path $Q$ from $j$ to $k$ such that: $$ \text{cost}(Q) < d(j,k) $$ **Step 3 — Construct a cheaper path from $i$ to $k$** Combine the original sub-path $i \rightarrow j$ with $Q$: $$ \text{new cost from } i \text{ to } k = d(i,j) + \text{cost}(Q) $$ **Step 4 — Derive the contradiction** $$ d(i,j) + \text{cost}(Q) < d(i,j) + d(j,k) = d(i,k) $$ This contradicts the assumption that $P^*$ is the *shortest* path from $i$ to $k$. Therefore, no such $Q$ exists. **Step 5 — Conclude** The shortest path from $j$ to $k$ is a sub-path of $P^*$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Sink Tree Size Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected graph with $\vert V \vert = n$ nodes. **Claim:** A sink tree rooted at node $r$ contains exactly $n$ nodes. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Number of nodes in tree} &= \vert V \vert = n \\ \text{Number of edges in tree} &= n - 1 \quad \text{(property of any spanning tree)} \\ \text{Cycles} &= 0 \quad \text{(tree property)} \end{aligned} $$ **Why is it loop-free?** Any loop in the routing structure would mean a packet circulates forever — but since every edge along a path strictly decreases the distance to the sink (by optimality), traversal cannot revisit a node. Therefore, the structure is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** = a tree. ## 3.3 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Full Derivation (Link-State, OSPF Basis) > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Compute shortest paths from a source $s$ to every other node. > **Input:** Graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative weights $w(u,v)$. > **Output:** Array $D[v]$ = shortest distance from $s$ to $v$. **Initialization Step:** $$ \begin{aligned} D[s] &\leftarrow 0 \\ D[v] &\leftarrow \infty \quad \forall v \neq s \\ \text{Prev}[v] &\leftarrow \text{NIL} \quad \forall v \\ \text{Unvisited} &\leftarrow V \end{aligned} $$ **Main Loop (iterates $\vert V \vert$ times):** **Iteration k, Step 1 — Select the unvisited node with minimum tentative distance:** $$ u \leftarrow \arg\min_{x \in \text{Unvisited}} D[x] $$ **Step 2 — Remove $u$ from unvisited set:** $$ \text{Unvisited} \leftarrow \text{Unvisited} \setminus \{u\} $$ **Step 3 — Relax every outgoing edge $(u, v)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } D[u] + w(u,v) < D[v] \text{ then:} \\ \quad D[v] &\leftarrow D[u] + w(u,v) \\ \quad \text{Prev}[v] &\leftarrow u \end{aligned} $$ **Termination:** When Unvisited = $\emptyset$ (or all reachable distances finalized). **Complexity Analysis:** $$ T(n) = O(\vert V \vert^2) \text{ (naive array)}, \quad O(( \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert) \text{ (binary heap)} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Example — Optimality Principle in Action > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Style Numerical:** The following worked example mirrors typical 14-mark problems. **Graph:** - Nodes: $A, B, C, D, E$ - Edges (cost): $A-B:4$, $A-C:2$, $B-D:5$, $C-B:1$, $C-D:8$, $C-E:10$, $D-E:2$, $B-E:6$ **Find:** Sink tree rooted at $E$ using the Optimality Principle. ### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Direct neighbors of $E$:** - From $C$: cost 10 - From $D$: cost 2 - From $B$: cost 6 Closest to $E$ is $D$ (cost 2). Mark $D$ as **finalized**. **Step 2 — Update distances via $D$:** - $D[E] = 2$ **Step 3 — Look at $D$'s neighbors:** - $B$ via $D$: $2 + 5 = 7$ - $C$ via $D$: $2 + 8 = 10$ (tied with direct) **Step 4 — Compare alternatives for $C$:** - Direct $C \rightarrow E$: $10$ - Via $D$: $C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$ = $8 + 2 = 10$ - Both equal → Optimality Principle allows either. Choose via $D$ for symmetry. **Step 5 — Finalize $B$ next:** - Direct $B \rightarrow E$: $6$ - Via $D$: $5 + 2 = 7$ ✗ (worse) - So $B$ goes directly to $E$ with cost **6**. **Step 6 — $A$:** - Via $B$: $4 + 6 = 10$ - Via $C$: $2 + 10 = 12$ ✗ - So $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow E$ with cost **10**. ### 3.4.2 Final Sink Tree (rooted at $E$) | Node | Shortest Distance to $E$ | Next-Hop | |------|--------------------------|----------| | $A$ | 10 | $B$ | | $B$ | 6 | $E$ | | $C$ | 10 | $D$ (or $E$ direct) | | $D$ | 2 | $E$ | | $E$ | 0 | — (root) | **Verification of Optimality:** - $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow E$: $4 + 6 = 10$ ✅ - $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$: $2 + 8 + 2 = 12$ ✗ (longer, correctly excluded) ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Routing Algorithms | Property | Dijkstra (LS) | Bellman-Ford (DV) | Flooding | |----------|---------------|--------------------|----------| | **Type** | Link-State | Distance-Vector | Static / Reactive | | **Knowledge** | Global topology | Only neighbours | Neighbours | | **Negative weights** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert E \vert)$ per hop | | **Count-to-Infinity** | ✅ Immune | ❌ Vulnerable | ✅ Immune | | **Convergence speed** | Fast (after LS flood) | Slow (iterative) | Instant (per packet) | | **Optimality Principle used** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (any path is optimal) | | **Examples** | OSPF, IS-IS | RIP, IGRP | Ad-hoc / military nets | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — How a Sink Tree Emerges from the Optimality Principle ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Network Topology Graph G]) startA --> apply[Apply Optimality Principle] apply --> check{For every node j on optimal path i to k?} check -- Yes --> subset[Sub-path j to k is also optimal] check -- No --> skip[Node excluded from sink tree] subset --> aggregate[Aggregate all optimal paths to destination] skip --> aggregate aggregate --> tree[Construct Sink Tree rooted at destination] tree --> validate{Loop-free?} validate -- Yes --> deploy[Deploy forwarding table: next-hop only] validate -- No --> error[Algorithm error: weights or topology invalid] deploy --> done([Converged Routing State]) error --> apply classDef startStyle fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef processStyle fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef decisionStyle fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef outputStyle fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class startA,done startStyle class apply,aggregate,tree,deploy processStyle class check,validate decisionStyle class subset,skip,error outputStyle ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Optimal Path Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT: Network Graph with Edge Weights] --> B[Topology Database
OSPF Link-State DB] B --> C[Dijkstra Algorithm Engine] C --> D[Tentative Distance Array D] D --> E{All nodes finalized?} E -- No --> F[Pick min-D unvisited node u] F --> G[Relax all outgoing edges of u] G --> D E -- Yes --> H[Build Sink Tree from Prev array] H --> I[Generate Forwarding Table
Destination -> Next-Hop] I --> J[OUTPUT: Optimal Routes Table] classDef inStyle fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef engineStyle fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef outStyle fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#000 class A,B inStyle class C,D,F,G,H engineStyle class I,J outStyle ``` ## 4.3 Tree Structure — Visualizing the Sink Tree for Worked Example (Section 3.4) ```mermaid flowchart TD E((E - root)) --> D((D cost 2)) E --> B((B cost 6)) D --> C((C cost 10)) B --> A((A cost 10)) classDef rootStyle fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px classDef layer1Style fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef layer2Style fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff class E rootStyle class D,B layer1Style class C,A layer2Style ``` **Reading the diagram:** The arrow direction indicates *next-hop* flow towards the root $E$. Each child node stores only its parent (next-hop), embodying the Optimality Principle's consequence that routers need not know the entire path. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State and explain the Optimality Principle in routing algorithms. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Optimality Principle states that if router $J$ lies on the optimal (shortest) path from router $I$ to router $K$, then the optimal path from $J$ to $K$ also lies along the same route. In other words, the sub-path of any optimal path is itself optimal. **(1 mark)** > > A direct consequence is that the set of all optimal routes from every source to a given destination forms a tree — called the **sink tree** — rooted at that destination. The tree is loop-free, directed, and contains exactly $\vert V \vert$ nodes. **(1 mark)** > > This principle forms the theoretical basis for shortest-path routing algorithms like Dijkstra (used in OSPF) and Bellman-Ford (used in RIP). **(1 mark)** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is a sink tree? Why is it loop-free? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **sink tree** is a directed tree rooted at the destination router, formed by combining the optimal paths from all other routers to that destination. **(1 mark)** > > It is loop-free because the Optimality Principle guarantees that every step on a shortest path strictly reduces (or maintains, in case of equal-cost paths) the distance to the destination. Since the cost strictly decreases along each hop, a packet cannot revisit a previously traversed node — hence no cycles can form. **(2 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** **(a)** With the help of a suitable network graph, explain the **Optimality Principle** and show how a **sink tree** is constructed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Apply **Dijkstra's algorithm** to find the shortest path from node $A$ to all other nodes in the graph given below. Show each iteration in a table. **(7 marks)** | Edge | Cost | |------|------| | $A-B$ | 4 | | $A-C$ | 2 | | $B-C$ | 1 | | $B-D$ | 5 | | $C-D$ | 8 | | $C-E$ | 10 | | $D-E$ | 2 | | $B-E$ | 6 | --- #### Model Solution — Question A(a) **(i) The Optimality Principle — Definition (2 marks):** If router $J$ is on the optimal path from $I$ to $K$, then the optimal path from $J$ to $K$ is a sub-path of the optimal path from $I$ to $K$. Mathematically: $$ \text{If } j \in P^*(i,k) \Rightarrow P^*(j,k) \subseteq P^*(i,k) $$ **(ii) Sink Tree Construction (3 marks):** 1. Identify destination (sink) — node $E$. 2. For every other node, find the *shortest* path to $E$. 3. Aggregate all these shortest paths into a single directed tree rooted at $E$. 4. Verify: no cycles, exactly $\vert V \vert$ nodes, $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges. 5. Each non-root node stores only its **parent** as the *next-hop*. **(iii) Why the tree is loop-free (2 marks):** - Each edge represents a decrease in distance to the sink. - A strictly decreasing sequence of positive integers cannot cycle. - Hence the structure is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) = tree. --- #### Model Solution — Question A(b) **Dijkstra's Iterations Table:** | Step | Visited Set | $D(A)$ | $D(B)$ | $D(C)$ | $D(D)$ | $D(E)$ | Prev (parent) | |------|-------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|---------------| | Init | $\{A\}$ | 0 | 4 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | — | | 1 | $\{A, C\}$ | 0 | **3** (via $C$) | 2 | **10** (via $C$) | **12** (via $C$) | $B \to C$, $D \to C$, $E \to C$ | | 2 | $\{A, C, B\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | **8** (via $B$) | **9** (via $B$) | $D \to B$, $E \to B$ | | 3 | $\{A, C, B, D\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 8 | **8** (via $D$, ties with $B$) | $E \to D$ or $E \to B$ | | 4 | $\{A, C, B, D, E\}$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 8 | 8 | All finalized | **Final Shortest Distances from $A$:** - $A \rightarrow B$: cost **3** (path $A-C-B$) - $A \rightarrow C$: cost **2** (path $A-C$) - $A \rightarrow D$: cost **8** (path $A-C-B-D$) - $A \rightarrow E$: cost **8** (path $A-C-B-D-E$) **Sink Tree from $A$'s perspective (rooted at $A$):** - $A \to C$ (cost 2) - $C \to B$ (cost 1) - $B \to D$ (cost 5) - $D \to E$ (cost 2) This tree is loop-free and contains 5 nodes with 4 edges, consistent with $\vert V \vert - 1$. --- ### Question B **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain the **Optimality Principle** with a suitable example. State its **limitations**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the network below. Using **Bellman-Ford algorithm**, compute the shortest paths from node $A$ to all other nodes. Show all iterations clearly. **(7 marks)** | Edge | Cost | |------|------| | $A-B$ | 6 | | $A-D$ | 1 | | $B-C$ | 5 | | $B-D$ | 2 | | $B-E$ | 2 | | $C-E$ | 5 | | $D-E$ | 1 | | $D-F$ | 4 | | $E-F$ | 3 | --- #### Model Solution — Question B(a) **Optimality Principle with Example (4 marks):** Consider a 4-node linear topology $A-B-C-D$ with edge costs 2, 3, 1. - Optimal path from $A$ to $D$: $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$, total cost 6. - Since $B$ is on this optimal path, by the principle, the optimal path from $B$ to $D$ must be $B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$ (cost 4), *not* some other detour. The sink tree rooted at $D$: - $D$ (root) - $C \to D$ - $B \to C \to D$ - $A \to B \to C \to D$ **Limitations (3 marks):** 1. **Static Topology Assumption:** The principle assumes link costs do not change rapidly; in highly dynamic networks (e.g., MANETs), optimal trees must be recomputed continuously. 2. **Policy Routing:** In BGP, "optimal" is policy-driven, not metric-driven — the principle does not strictly apply. 3. **Equal-Cost Ambiguity:** When multiple equal-cost shortest paths exist, the principle does not specify which one is chosen (ECMP is needed). 4. **Negative Weights:** With negative edge weights, optimal sub-paths exist only if there are no negative cycles — but Dijkstra-based routing fails. --- #### Model Solution — Question B(b) **Bellman-Ford Iterations Table** (showing $D$ array after each pass): **Initialization:** $D[A] = 0$; all others = $\infty$. **Iteration 1** — Relax all edges: | Edge | New Distance Considered | Updated Value | |------|-------------------------|---------------| | $A-B$ | $0+6 = 6$ | $D[B] = 6$ | | $A-D$ | $0+1 = 1$ | $D[D] = 1$ | | $B-C$ | $6+5 = 11$ | $D[C] = 11$ | | $B-D$ | $6+2 = 8$ | $D[D] = 1$ (no change) | | $B-E$ | $6+2 = 8$ | $D[E] = 8$ | | $D-E$ | $1+1 = 2$ | $D[E] = 2$ | | $D-F$ | $1+4 = 5$ | $D[F] = 5$ | | $E-F$ | $2+3 = 5$ | $D[F] = 5$ (no change) | | $C-E$ | $11+5 = 16$ | $D[E] = 2$ (no change) | After Iter 1: $D = \{0, 6, 11, 1, 2, 5\}$ **Iteration 2:** | Edge | Update | |------|--------| | $B-D$: $6+2 = 8$ | no change | | $B-E$: $6+2 = 8$ | no change (2 < 8) | | $D-E$: $1+1 = 2$ | no change | | $D-F$: $1+4 = 5$ | no change | | $C-E$: $11+5 = 16$ | no change | After Iter 2: $D = \{0, 6, 11, 1, 2, 5\}$ — **converged**. **Final Shortest Distances from $A$:** - $A \to B$: **6** (direct) - $A \to C$: **11** (via $B$) - $A \to D$: **1** (direct) - $A \to E$: **2** (via $D$) - $A \to F$: **5** (via $D$, via $D \to E \to F$ also gives $1+1+3 = 5$) **Routing Table from $A$:** | Destination | Next-Hop | Cost | |-------------|----------|------| | $B$ | $B$ | 6 | | $C$ | $B$ | 11 | | $D$ | $D$ | 1 | | $E$ | $D$ | 2 | | $F$ | $D$ | 5 | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Optimality Principle Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention "sub-path" property** — many students write "shortest path is optimal" but lose 1 mark by missing the *subset / sub-path* wording, which is the heart of the principle. > 2. **Confusing sink tree with spanning tree** — a sink tree is a *directed, rooted* structure with distances; an undirected spanning tree has no notion of "root" or direction. > 3. **Skipping iteration tables in Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford** — full marks require *every* iteration to be tabulated; showing only the final answer loses 3-4 marks. > 4. **Not verifying loop-free property** — explicitly stating "since distances strictly decrease along each hop, cycles are impossible" earns 1-2 marks extra. > 5. **Misapplying to BGP** — BGP violates the pure metric-based Optimality Principle;
