> 4. **Stating "Split Horizon" without explaining it** — Naming the technique without describing the rule fetches 0 marks. The rule itself is worth full credit.
> 5. **Confusing DVR with Link State Routing** — DVR shares vectors with neighbors; LSR floods LSAs to all. Mixing these up in a comparison question yields zero.
> 6. **Forgetting the infinity = 16 convention in RIP** — The cap of 15 hops is a direct KTU syllabus point. Omitting it loses 1 mark in problems involving the count-to-infinity limit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DVR** stands for **Distance Vector Routing** — a Bellman-Ford-based dynamic algorithm.
- [x] The core equation is $D_x(y) = \min_v \{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ — **memorize this verbatim**.
- [x] Each router shares its distance vector with **directly connected neighbors only** (this is the key distinction from LSR).
- [x] The metric in RIP is **hop count**; maximum allowed is **15**; **16 = infinity**.
- [x] Update timer in RIP = **30 seconds**; hold-down timer = **180 seconds**.
- [x] Convergence in DVR is **slow** because information propagates only **one hop per iteration**.
- [x] Worst-case convergence = $O(N)$ iterations for an $N$-node network.
- [x] **Count-to-Infinity** is DVR's most serious drawback — slow propagation of link failures.
- [x] **Split Horizon**: do not advertise a route back to the neighbor you learned it from.
- [x] **Poison Reverse**: advertise the route back as **infinity (16)** — stronger than split horizon.
- [x] **Triggered Updates** speed up convergence by sending updates immediately on change.
- [x] **Hold-Down Timers** suppress flapping routes for ~180 seconds.
- [x] DVR is suitable for **small networks only** (< 50 routers); for larger networks use **OSPF/IS-IS (Link State)** or **BGP (Path Vector)**.
- [x] The two main implementation protocols of DVR are **RIP** and **IGRP** (Cisco proprietary).
- [x] Always present routing table evolution in **iteration-by-iteration tables** in exam answers.
- [x] The **next-hop field** is critical — do not confuse it with the destination field.
- [x] DVR's main advantage: **low memory and computational overhead** per router.
- [x] DVR's main disadvantage: **slow convergence and transient routing loops**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link State Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Link State Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link State Routing (LSR)** is a dynamic, intra-domain (intra-AS) routing paradigm in which every router maintains an **identical, complete Link-State Database (LSDB)** describing the topology and cost of the entire network. Each router independently computes its own shortest-path tree to all destinations by running **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** on this database.

The two canonical implementations of LSR are:

| Protocol | Standard | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **OSPF** (Open Shortest Path First) | RFC 2328 / RFC 5340 | IP networks (most common) |
| **IS-IS** (Intermediate System to Intermediate System) | ISO 10589 / RFC 1195 | ISP backbones, large service provider networks |

The opposite paradigm is **Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** (e.g., RIP, IGRP), where each router only knows its **directly-attached neighbors and their advertised distances**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** A *Link State Advertisement (LSA)* is a small packet containing the router's ID, the ID and cost of each directly connected link, and a sequence number. Flooding is the reliable, controlled distribution of LSAs across the entire area.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Complete City Map"

Imagine you are a tourist in an unfamiliar city. You have two choices to find the best route to every landmark:

- **Distance Vector (RIP-style):** You stop at every intersection and ask a local, *"How far is the museum from here, and which way?"* You trust their answer, and they, in turn, trusted the answers they got. Errors **accumulate and propagate** (count-to-infinity). You never see the whole map.
- **Link State (OSPF-style):** A **satellite image of the entire city** is dropped on your lap. You can see **every road, every distance, and every one-way street**. You sit down, pull out a ruler, and **draw the shortest path yourself** to any landmark. No trust required, no rumor chains, and no cumulative errors.

That satellite image is the **LSDB**, and drawing the shortest path is **Dijkstra's algorithm**.

## 1.3 Core LSR Principles (The Five Pillars)

1. **Discover Neighbors** — Each router sends a small **HELLO** packet over every point-to-point link and discovers the neighbor's identity and the link's cost.
2. **Measure Link Cost** — Cost is typically inverse bandwidth (Cisco-style: `Cost = 10^8 / Bandwidth in bps`) or administratively assigned.
3. **Build Link State Packets (LSPs / LSAs)** — A router's LSP lists **its own ID + cost of every attached link**.
4. **Reliable Flooding** — LSPs are sent to **all other routers in the area** using a controlled flood (acknowledged, with sequence numbers and aging timers).
5. **Compute Shortest Paths** — Each router runs **Dijkstra's SPF** on its LSDB to build the shortest-path tree and populate the routing table.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Hello — Measure — Pack — Flood — Solve"* — the 5 stages of LSR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of LSR

### Stage 1 — Neighbor Discovery (HELLO Protocol)
- Routers exchange **HELLO** packets on each interface every **HelloInterval** (default OSPF: **10 seconds** on broadcast, **30 seconds** on NBMA/point-to-point).
- A neighbor is declared **DOWN** if no HELLO is heard within the **DeadInterval** (typically **4 × HelloInterval = 40 s**).
- HELLO packets also elect the **Designated Router (DR)** and **Backup DR (BDR)** on broadcast networks (e.g., Ethernet) to reduce LSA flooding from **O(n²) to O(n)**.

### Stage 2 — Building the Link-State Packet (LSP)
An LSP contains four key fields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSP} = \big\{ &\text{Advertising Router ID}, \\
                     &\text{Link ID (neighbor ID)}, \\
                     &\text{Link Cost } c(u,v), \\
                     &\text{Sequence Number, Age} \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Stage 3 — Reliable Flooding
- When a router receives a **newer** LSP (higher sequence number), it:
  1. Acknowledges the LSP.
  2. Stores it in its LSDB.
  3. Forwards a copy out of **every interface except the one it arrived on**.
- Sequence numbers use **32-bit signed linear space** (old ones wrap using "lollipop" sequencing) to prevent loops and ensure **most-recent wins**.

### Stage 4 — Dijkstra's SPF Algorithm
A router `S` (source) maintains two sets:
- **N′** — the set of nodes whose shortest path from `S` is **definitively known**.
- **D(v)** — the current best-known distance from `S` to every node `v`.

The algorithm iterates: **pick the unvisited node with the smallest tentative D(v), lock it in, then "relax" every outgoing edge.**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas for Link State Routing — memorize for ESE.**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\text{Cost}(u,v)$ | Administrative cost of link between $u$ and $v$ | dimensionless (Cisco: $10^8 / \text{BW in bps}$) |
| $D(v) = D(u) + c(u,v)$ | **Relaxation equation** of Dijkstra | update only if RHS $< D(v)$ |
| $N' \leftarrow N' \cup \{w\}$ | Node $w$ is locked into the shortest-path tree | iteration step |
| $d_{\text{total}}(S,D) = \sum c(e_i)$ | Total cost of the shortest path from $S$ to $D$ | sum of edge costs on SPF tree |
| $L_{\text{DB}} = \sum_{i} L_i$ | LSDB size = sum of every router's LSPs | grows as $O(\mid V \mid + \mid E \mid)$ |
| $\text{OSPF Cost} = \dfrac{10^8}{\text{BW (bps)}}$ | Reference bandwidth = **100 Mbps** | 1 Gbps link → cost **1**; 10 Mbps → **10** |
| $T_{\text{convergence}} \propto \mid V \mid \log\mid V \mid$ | SPF complexity using binary heap | Dijkstra with min-priority queue |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In handwritten exams, never confuse the absolute-value bars with the KTU markdown pipe `|`. The exam cell formula $\vert D(v) \vert$ must be written with vertical bars but rendered here as $\lvert D(v) \rvert$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISP Backbones (IS-IS):** Carries ~80% of global internet routes. Chosen because it runs directly over Layer 2 and is protocol-agnostic (originally built for OSI/CLNP).
- **Enterprise Campus (OSPF):** Hierarchical design with **Area 0 (Backbone)** and non-backbone areas connected via **Area Border Routers (ABRs)**. Each area runs SPF independently → scalability.
- **Data Center Fabrics:** OSPF (and increasingly BGP) underpins VXLAN-EVPN overlays; understanding LSR remains essential.
- **SDN/OpenFlow:** Controllers run centralized SPF (e.g., ONOS, OpenDaylight) — the same Dijkstra mathematics, just centralized.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Formal Pseudocode (Mathematical Form)

Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights $c: E \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ and source node $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Initialize: } N' = \{S\}, \quad D(S) = 0, \quad D(v) = \infty \;\; \forall v \neq S \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Repeat until } N' = V: \\[2pt]
&\quad \text{1. Pick } w \notin N' \text{ such that } D(w) = \min_{v \notin N'} D(v) \\[2pt]
&\quad \text{2. Add } w \text{ to } N' \;\; (\text{lock in shortest path to } w) \\[2pt]
&\quad \text{3. For every neighbor } v \text{ of } w: \\[2pt]
&\qquad \text{If } D(v) > D(w) + c(w,v), \text{ then } D(v) \leftarrow D(w) + c(w,v) \;\; \text{(RELAX)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Termination:** After $|V|$ iterations, $N' = V$ and $D(v)$ holds the minimum cost from $S$ to every $v$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Standard Question

> **Network Topology (5 nodes, undirected, link costs shown):**
>
> - A — B : 2
> - A — C : 5
> - B — C : 1
> - B — D : 3
> - C — D : 2
> - C — E : 4
> - D — E : 1

**Task:** Run Dijkstra from source **S = A** and construct the **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)**.

### Iteration Table (Analytical Matrix)

> Each row is one iteration. $N'$ is the permanently locked set. $p(v)$ stores the predecessor on the shortest path.

| Iter | Node added | $N'$ | D(A) | D(B), p(B) | D(C), p(C) | D(D), p(D) | D(E), p(E) | Reasoning |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **0** | — | $\{A\}$ | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Initialize: only $A$ is known with distance 0. |
| **1** | **A** | $\{A\}$ | 0 | **2**, A | **5**, A | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Relax A's edges: A→B (2), A→C (5). |
| **2** | **B** | $\{A,B\}$ | 0 | 2 | **3**, B | **5**, B | $\infty$ | B chosen (D(B)=2). Relax B: B→C (2+1=3 < 5 ✓), B→D (2+3=5). |
| **3** | **C** | $\{A,B,C\}$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | **5**, B | **7**, C | C chosen (D(C)=3). Relax C: C→D (3+2=5, tie — keep B), C→E (3+4=7). |
| **4** | **D** | $\{A,B,C,D\}$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | 5 | **6**, D | D chosen (D(D)=5). Relax D: D→E (5+1=6 < 7 ✓). |
| **5** | **E** | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 6 | E chosen. All nodes locked. **STOP.** |

### Derived Shortest Path Tree from A

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\to A \quad \text{cost} = 0 \\
A &\to B \quad \text{cost} = 2 \quad \text{path} = A \to B \\
A &\to C \quad \text{cost} = 3 \quad \text{path} = A \to B \to C \\
A &\to D \quad \text{cost} = 5 \quad \text{path} = A \to B \to D \;\;(\text{also } A\to B\to C\to D \text{ at tie}) \\
A &\to E \quad \text{cost} = 6 \quad \text{path} = A \to B \to D \to E
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Routing Table at Router A

| Destination | Next Hop | Total Cost |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| B | B | 2 |
| C | B | 3 |
| D | B | 5 |
| E | B | 6 |

## 3.3 Verification by Enumeration

To validate, list candidate paths from A to E:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \to B \to D \to E &= 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \\
A \to B \to C \to D \to E &= 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 6 \quad \text{(tie)} \\
A \to B \to C \to E &= 2 + 1 + 4 = 7 \\
A \to C \to D \to E &= 5 + 2 + 1 = 8 \\
A \to C \to E &= 5 + 4 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum is **6**, achieved by $A \to B \to D \to E$ ✓. Dijkstra's output is verified.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the 14-mark problems, always show the **iteration table**, then explicitly state the **SPT**, then **the routing table**. Examiners reward each stage separately. Skipping the table = losing 4–5 marks.

## 3.4 LSR vs. DVR — Mathematical Comparison

| Property | Link State (OSPF) | Distance Vector (RIP) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Information held | **Full topology** (LSDB) | Only neighbor's distance table |
| Algorithm | **Dijkstra (link-state)** | Bellman–Ford |
| Update trigger | **Event-driven** (LSA change) | Periodic (RIP every **30 s**) |
| Convergence | **Fast** (seconds) | **Slow** (minutes, count-to-infinity) |
| Metric | Cost (bandwidth-aware) | Hop count (max **15 hops**) |
| CPU/Memory | **Higher** (runs SPF) | Lower |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LSR

> Below is the end-to-end data-flow of a Link State Router — from interface input to populated routing table.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INTF1[PHYSICAL INTERFACE LAYER 1 2] --> HELLO
    INTF2[PHYSICAL INTERFACE LAYER 1 2] --> HELLO
    HELLO[HELLO PROTOCOL MODULE NEIGHBOR DISCOVERY] --> NB[NEIGHBOR TABLE R1 R2 R3]
    NB --> METRIC[LINK COST MEASUREMENT BANDWIDTH BASED]
    METRIC --> LSPGEN[LSP GENERATOR ROUTER ID LINKS COSTS]
    LSPGEN --> FLOOD[RELIABLE FLOODING ENGINE ACK SEQ NUM]
    FLOOD --> LSDB[(LINK STATE DATABASE LSDB SHARED BY ALL)]
    LSDB --> DIJK[Dijkstra SPF ENGINE SHORTEST PATH TREE]
    NB --> DIJK
    METRIC --> DIJK
    DIJK --> RT[(ROUTING TABLE DEST NEXT HOP COST)]
    RT --> FWD[FORWARDING ENGINE L2 L3 SWITCHING]
```

## 4.2 LSDB Convergence and SPF Recomputation

> Illustrates how a single topology change (Link X to Y failure) propagates as an LSA, refloods, and triggers a fresh SPF calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AREA0[OSPF AREA 0 LOGICAL VIEW]
        RA[ROUTER A] --- RB[ROUTER B]
        RB --- RC[ROUTER C]
        RA --- RC
    end
    EVT[LINK A TO B FAILS EVENT] --> LSP_NEW[RA GENERATES NEW LSP SEQ NUM PLUS 1]
    LSP_NEW --> FLOOD1[FLOOD TO B AND C ACK]
    FLOOD1 --> LSDB_UP[LSDB UPDATED ON B AND C]
    LSDB_UP --> SPF_RUN[B AND C RUN Dijkstra RE COMPUTE]
    SPF_RUN --> RT_NEW[ROUTING TABLE UPDATED CONVERGED]
    RT_NEW --> STATE_STABLE[NETWORK STABLE NEW TREE]
```

## 4.3 OSPF Hierarchical Area Design (Scalability View)

> Real-world deployments use a **two-level hierarchy** to bound LSDB growth and SPF cost.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BACKBONE[AREA 0 BACKBONE]
        ABR1[AREA BORDER ROUTER ABR1]
        ABR2[AREA BORDER ROUTER ABR2]
        ABR3[AREA BORDER ROUTER ABR3]
    end
    subgraph AREA1[AREA 1]
        R11[INTERNAL ROUTER]
        R12[INTERNAL ROUTER]
    end
    subgraph AREA2[AREA 2]
        R21[INTERNAL ROUTER]
        R22[INTERNAL ROUTER]
    end
    ABR1 --- R11
    ABR1 --- R12
    ABR2 --- R21
    ABR2 --- R22
    ABR1 --- ABR2
    ABR2 --- ABR3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Distance Vector Routing and Link State Routing. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Distance Vector | Link State |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Each router knows only its **directly-connected neighbors** and their distances (Bellman–Ford). | Each router has a **complete map (LSDB)** of the entire network topology. |
| 2 | Suffers from **count-to-infinity**; slow convergence (minutes). | **Fast convergence** (seconds); no count-to-infinity because every router sees the truth. |
| 3 | **Periodic** updates (e.g., RIP every 30 s). | **Event-driven** updates (LSA flooded only on topology change). |
| 4 | Example: **RIP, IGRP**. | Example: **OSPF, IS-IS**. |
| 5 | Lower CPU/memory cost. | Higher CPU (runs Dijkstra) and memory cost. |

*(Any three points × 1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Link State Advertisement (LSA)? List its four essential fields.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **LSA (Link State Advertisement)** is a small data packet generated by a router to advertise its locally-attached links and their costs to every other router in the OSPF area. It is the **unit of flooding** in LSR.

**Four essential fields:**
1. **Advertising Router ID** — uniquely identifies the originating router.
2. **Link ID** — identifies the link (neighbor router ID or subnet).
3. **Link Cost** — the metric (e.g., inverse bandwidth).
4. **Sequence Number** + **Age** — used to detect newer/older LSAs and to age out stale ones.

*(Definition = 1 mark; 4 fields = 2 marks = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> As per KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question has an **internal choice**. Both options below are full 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five operational steps of Link State Routing**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Apply **Dijkstra's algorithm** on the network shown in Fig. 1 to find the **shortest path tree and routing table at node A**. All link costs are given. (7 marks)

```
         A
        / \
       2   5
      /     \
     B --1-- C
     |     / |
     3   2   4
     |  /    |
     D --1-- E
```

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five Steps of LSR (7 marks):**

1. **Neighbor Discovery (HELLO):** Each router sends HELLO packets on every interface to identify directly-connected neighbors and the cost of the link. (1.5 marks)
2. **Link Cost Measurement:** Cost is computed from link bandwidth (e.g., $10^8 / \text{BW}$) or administratively configured. (1 mark)
3. **LSP Construction:** Each router builds an **LSP** containing its ID, the ID of each neighbor, and the cost of each connecting link. (1.5 marks)
4. **Reliable Flooding:** LSPs are flooded to **every router in the area** using acknowledged, sequence-numbered packets. Older LSAs are discarded; newer ones replace and re-flood. (2 marks)
5. **Dijkstra SPF Computation:** Each router runs Dijkstra on its LSDB to build the **shortest-path tree** and populates its routing table. (1 mark)

**(b) Dijkstra Computation (7 marks):**

Iteration table (compact form):

| Iter | $N'$ | D(A) | D(B) | D(C) | D(D) | D(E) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | $\{A\}$ | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | $\{A\}$ | 0 | **2** $A$ | **5** $A$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | $\{A,B\}$ | 0 | 2 | **3** $B$ | **5** $B$ | $\infty$ |
| 3 | $\{A,B,C\}$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | **5** $B$ | **7** $C$ |
| 4 | $\{A,B,C,D\}$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | 5 | **6** $D$ |
| 5 | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 6 |

(Iteration table = 3 marks)

**Shortest Path Tree (2 marks):**

- A → B : cost 2
- A → C : cost 3 (A–B–C)
- A → D : cost 5 (A–B–D)
- A → E : cost 6 (A–B–D–E)

**Routing Table at A (2 marks):**

| Destination | Next Hop | Cost |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| B | B | 2 |
| C | B | 3 |
| D | B | 5 |
| E | B | 6 |

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Dijkstra shortest path algorithm** with its **pseudo-code / mathematical steps** and discuss its **time complexity**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare **OSPF and RIP** in terms of algorithm, metric, update mechanism, convergence, and scalability. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Dijkstra Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Inputs:** Graph $G=(V,E)$ with non-negative weights, source $S$.
**Outputs:** Shortest distance $D(v)$ and predecessor $p(v)$ for every $v \in V$.

**Steps:** (as already derived in Section 3.1)

1. Initialize $N' = \{S\}$, $D(S)=0$, $D(v) = \infty$ for $v \neq S$.
2. Find $w \notin N'$ minimizing $D(w)$.
3. Add $w$ to $N'$.
4. For every neighbor $v$ of $w$: if $D(w) + c(w,v) < D(v)$, then $D(v) \leftarrow D(w) + c(w,v)$ and $p(v) \leftarrow w$.
5. Repeat until $N' = V$.

(Algorithm = 4 marks; time complexity = 3 marks)

**Time Complexity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n, m) &= O\!\left(\vert V \vert^2 + \vert E \vert \right) \quad \text{(naive array)} \\
        &= O\!\left((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert \right) \quad \text{(binary heap / Fibonacci)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **dense graph** ($m \approx n^2$): $O(n^2)$; for a **sparse graph** ($m \approx n$): $O(n \log n)$.

**(b) OSPF vs. RIP Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | OSPF (Link State) | RIP (Distance Vector) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Algorithm | **Dijkstra (SPF)** | Bellman–Ford |
| Metric | **Cost** (bandwidth-based) | **Hop count** (max 15) |
| Updates | **Event-driven** (on change) | **Periodic** every 30 s |
| Convergence | **Fast** (seconds) | **Slow** (minutes) |
| Hierarchy | **Areas** (Area 0 backbone) | **Flat** — single domain |
| Loop prevention | Sequence numbers + LSDB | Split horizon, poison reverse, hold-down |
| Scalability | **High** (used in ISP cores) | **Low** (max 15 hops, LAN scale only) |
| VLSM / CIDR | **Yes**, fully supported | RIPv2 yes, RIPv1 no |
| Auth | MD5 / SHA-HMAC | RIPv2 plain/MD5 |

(Any 7 well-explained points × 1 mark each = 7 marks)

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Avoid These Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to show the iteration table** for Dijkstra → lose 3–4 marks. The table **is** the proof; don't just write the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing relaxation** — writing $D(v) = D(v) + c(w,v)$ (WRONG) instead of $D(v) = D(w) + c(w,v)$ (CORRECT). This is a **classic 2-mark deduction** on KTU boards.
> 3. **Not handling ties** — when two nodes have equal $D(v)$, pick deterministically (alphabetically) and **state the tie-breaking rule**. Examiners love this.
> 4. **Writing DVR Bellman–Ford as Dijkstra** — they look similar. In DVR: $D_x(y) = \min\{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$ over neighbors. In LSR: $D(v) = D(w) + c(w,v)$ where $w$ is **already in $N'$**.
> 5. **Skipping the LSDB flooding step** when explaining LSR — many students jump straight to Dijkstra and lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong sign on cost formula** — Cisco cost is $10^8 / \text{BW}$, not $\text{BW} / 10^8$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Twice Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ✅ LSR uses **Dijkstra's algorithm**; DVR uses **Bellman–Ford**.
- ✅ LSR has **5 stages**: Hello → Measure → Pack → Flood → Solve.
- ✅ OSPF = **Open Shortest Path First**; IS-IS = ISO/ISP routing. Both are LSR.
- ✅ LSDB = **identical, synchronized** database of LSAs in every router in an area.
- ✅ Sequence numbers prevent **old LSPs from overwriting new ones**; LSA aging (default 30 min) removes stale entries.
- ✅ DR/BDR election on broadcast links reduces LSA flooding from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$.
- ✅ **Hierarchical areas** (Area 0 + non-backbone) bound LSDB size and SPF cost.
- ✅ Relaxation equation (Dijkstra): $D(v) \leftarrow D(w) + c(w,v)$ **only if RHS $< D(v)$**.
- ✅ LSR converges **fast**; DVR suffers from **count-to-infinity** and slow convergence.
- ✅ OSPF cost metric: $\text{Cost} = 10^8 / \text{BW(bps)}$ (reference bandwidth 100 Mbps).
- ✅ RIP metric: **hop count**, max **15**; 16 = unreachable.
- ✅ For a 14-mark Dijkstra problem: **diagram → iteration table → SPT → routing table** (each stage earns marks).
- ✅ Final routing table from SPT: **next hop = first node on the shortest path from source**, not the destination.
- ✅ LSR has **higher CPU and memory** requirements than DVR — but better scalability and faster convergence.
- ✅ Tie-breaking in Dijkstra: pick the node with the **smallest ID** (or whichever order your textbook specifies).
- ✅ Bandwidth to cost reference values: 100 Mbps → 1; 10 Mbps → 10; 1 Mbps → 100; 1 Gbps → 1 (use reference-bandwidth command to scale).

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Link State Routing**
*Good luck with your KTU ESE preparation!* 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Congestion Control Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Congestion Control Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Network Congestion?

**Congestion** in a computer network is formally defined as a state in which the demand for network resources (link bandwidth, buffer space in routers, and processor cycles) exceeds the available capacity of the network at a given point in time. When too many packets compete for the same shared transmission medium or router queue simultaneously, queues overflow, packets are dropped, retransmissions occur, and end-to-end performance collapses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"Congestion is a condition of severe delay caused by an overload of datagrams at any intermediate router on the path from source to destination. Congestion Control refers to the set of techniques and algorithms used by the network layer and transport layer to prevent collapse, maintain stability, and ensure fairness when such overload conditions occur."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Highway Traffic Jam

Imagine a **4-lane highway** suddenly converging into a **single-lane bottleneck** (e.g., a toll booth). Cars pile up because the outflow rate is much slower than the inflow rate. This is precisely what happens in a router:

| Highway Element | Network Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cars | Packets |
| Highway lanes | Link bandwidth |
| Toll booth | Router output link |
| Traffic police | Congestion control algorithm |
| Horn honking / brake lights | Explicit feedback (choke packets, ECN) |

When the toll booth (router) cannot service cars (packets) fast enough, the queue (buffer) fills up and eventually overflows → packets are dropped → sender retransmits → which **worsens the congestion further**. This vicious cycle is known as **Congestion Collapse**.

## 1.3 Congestion Control vs. Flow Control

Students often confuse these two related-but-distinct concepts. The difference is critical for KTU exams:

- **Flow Control** is an **end-to-end** mechanism between a single **sender** and a single **receiver** to prevent the sender from overwhelming the receiver's buffer (e.g., TCP sliding window with `rwnd`).
- **Congestion Control** is a **network-wide** mechanism that prevents too many senders from collectively overwhelming the **network infrastructure** (routers, links) in between.

> [!NOTE]
> Flow control protects the **destination**, while congestion control protects the **network itself**.

## 1.4 Causes of Congestion (Root Causes)

1. **Bursty Traffic:** Input rate > Output link capacity (bandwidth mismatch).
2. **Insufficient Router Buffers:** When buffer overflows, packets must be dropped.
3. **Slow Routers:** Processing delay causes queue build-up.
4. **Unreliable Multiplexing:** Multiple input flows multiplexed onto a single output link.

## 1.5 Effects of Congestion (Symptoms)

- Excessive queuing delay (latency spikes).
- High packet loss ratio.
- Degraded throughput (goodput collapses despite high offered load).
- Retransmissions → cascading effect → **congestion collapse**.
- Increased jitter and unfair bandwidth allocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kleinrock's Critical Observation (1978):** *"As offered load increases, throughput first increases linearly, then saturates, and finally COLLAPSES to near zero if congestion control is absent."* This is the foundation of all congestion control theory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Two Broad Approaches to Congestion Control

### A. Open-Loop Solutions (Prevention)
Algorithms that **prevent congestion before it happens** by good design. They do **not** use feedback from the network. Examples: Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Scheduling (FIFO, WFQ), Traffic Shaping/Policing.

### B. Closed-Loop Solutions (Reactive)
Algorithms that **react to congestion after it is detected** using feedback signals. They have three phases:
1. **Congestion Detection** – by observing packet loss, delay, or explicit bits.
2. **Congestion Notification** – via choke packets, ECN bits, or implicit inference (timeout / duplicate ACK).
3. **Rate Adjustment** – sender reduces window size or rate accordingly.

## 2.2 General Principles of Congestion Control (Tanenbaum's Principles)

Jacobson's foundational principles guiding modern congestion control:
1. **Watch the packets** (passive observation).
2. **Pass the information to where action can be taken.**
3. **Adjust the rate based on feedback** (AIMD paradigm).
4. **Apply the principle of conservation of packets** (no packet created/destroyed mid-network).

## 2.3 Algorithm 1: Leaky Bucket Algorithm

The **Leaky Bucket** is an analog of a bucket with a constant outflow rate, regardless of the input burst rate. It enforces a **constant output rate** at the host or network edge.

### How It Works
- A bucket holds up to **B** packets.
- It drains at a constant rate **ρ** (packets/second) into the network.
- Input bursts are absorbed if bucket not full; otherwise packets are discarded.

### Mathematical Model (Continuous Version)
For a bucket of capacity $B$ with constant output rate $\rho$ and input rate $\lambda(t)$:

$$
\text{Net fill rate} = \lambda(t) - \rho
$$

The bucket content $x(t)$ evolves as:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} =
\begin{cases}
\lambda(t) - \rho, & \text{if } x(t) < B \\
0, & \text{if } x(t) = B \text{ (overflow)}
\end{cases}
$$

The **burst tolerance time** is the time it takes to drain a full bucket:

$$
T_{\text{drain}} = \frac{B}{\rho} \text{ seconds}
$$

### KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Constant output rate | packets/sec |
| $B$ | Bucket capacity | packets |
| $T_{\text{drain}} = B / \rho$ | Time to empty a full bucket | seconds |
| $\lambda(t)$ | Instantaneous input rate | packets/sec |
| Output is **always** $\rho$ | Independent of $\lambda$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Leaky Bucket is used in **ATM networks** (Traffic Contract Policing), **ISDN**, and **network shapers** to enforce Constant Bit Rate (CBR) for voice/video traffic.

## 2.4 Algorithm 2: Token Bucket Algorithm

The **Token Bucket** is more flexible than Leaky Bucket. Tokens accumulate at rate $\rho$ in a bucket of capacity $C$. A packet can be transmitted only if a token is available, then the token is consumed.

### How It Works
- Tokens generated at rate $\rho$ tokens/sec.
- Maximum bucket size = $C$ (any extra tokens discarded).
- An arriving packet consumes 1 token → if none available → packet waits (or is sent unshaped).
- Output rate can **burst up to $C$ packets instantly** if bucket is full.

### Mathematical Model
The number of tokens available at time $t$:

$$
T(t) = \min\left(C,\; T(t_0) + \rho(t - t_0)\right)
$$

The **maximum burst size** that can be sent at the line rate $R$ is:

$$
B_{\max} = C + \rho \cdot \tau
$$

where $\tau$ is the time to transmit one packet at line rate $R$, i.e., $\tau = 1/R$.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Comparison Table)

| Property | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
|---|---|---|
| Output rate | Constant ($\rho$) | Can burst up to $C$ |
| Burst tolerance | No bursts allowed | Yes, up to $C$ packets |
| Token generation | Not applicable | Rate $\rho$ |
| Equation for content | $B$ (constant capacity) | $T(t) = \min(C, T(t_0) + \rho \Delta t)$ |
| Packet drop | On bucket full | On token shortage |
| Discards bursts | Yes (enforces smoothness) | No (permits bursts) |
| Implementation | Output interface shaping | Traffic shaping at input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"compare leaky bucket vs. token bucket"*, always emphasize that **Token Bucket allows bursts up to $C$**, while Leaky Bucket strictly enforces a constant rate.

## 2.5 Algorithm 3: Choke Packet Technique (ICMP Source Quench)

When a router detects congestion (e.g., queue threshold exceeded), it:
1. **Discards** the incoming packet.
2. **Sends a choke packet (Source Quench)** back to the source.
3. Source reduces its sending rate by some fraction (e.g., 50%).

**Drawback:** The discarded packet was already transmitted, wasting upstream bandwidth — and the choke packet itself contributes to congestion. Hence, this is now obsolete and replaced by **ECN**.

## 2.6 Algorithm 4: Random Early Detection (RED)

RED is a proactive congestion avoidance mechanism operating at the **router level** (proposed by Floyd & Jacobson, 1993).

### Operating Principle
Instead of waiting for buffer to be 100% full, the router computes an **average queue length** $\bar{q}$ and probabilistically drops packets even before overflow.

$$
\bar{q} = (1 - w_q) \cdot \bar{q}_{\text{prev}} + w_q \cdot q
$$

where $w_q \in (0, 1)$ is the queue weight (typically $0.002$).

### Drop Probability (Three Regions)

$$
p =
\begin{cases}
0, & \bar{q} < \text{Min}_{th} \\
P_b, & \text{Min}_{th} \leq \bar{q} < \text{Max}_{th} \\
1, & \bar{q} \geq \text{Max}_{th}
\end{cases}
$$

The actual drop probability is:

$$
P_b = \frac{P_{\max} \cdot (\bar{q} - \text{Min}_{th})}{\text{Max}_{th} - \text{Min}_{th}}
$$

Final drop probability (with early randomization):

$$
p = \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b}
$$

## 2.7 Algorithm 5: TCP Congestion Control (The Most Important)

TCP uses a **congestion window** (`cwnd`) in addition to the receiver window (`rwnd`). The effective window is:

$$
W_{\text{eff}} = \min(\text{cwnd},\; \text{rwnd})
$$

### Four Phases of TCP Congestion Control

| Phase | Trigger | cwnd Evolution | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow Start** | Connection start or timeout | cwnd doubles per RTT (exponential) | Until cwnd $\geq$ ssthresh |
| **Congestion Avoidance** | cwnd $\geq$ ssthresh | cwnd += MSS/cwnd per ACK (linear) | Until timeout / 3 dupACK |
| **Fast Retransmit** | 3 duplicate ACKs | ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = ssthresh + 3 | Immediate |
| **Fast Recovery** | After fast retransmit | cwnd = ssthresh; enter Cong. Avoidance | Until ACK received |

### Slow Start Equation (Exponential Growth)

$$
\text{cwnd}_{n+1} = 2 \cdot \text{cwnd}_n \quad \text{(per RTT)}
$$

Or equivalently per ACK:

$$
\text{cwnd} = \text{cwnd} + \text{MSS} \cdot \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{cwnd}} \quad \text{(per ACK)}
$$

### Congestion Avoidance Equation (Linear Growth / AIMD Additive Increase)

$$
\text{cwnd}_{n+1} = \text{cwnd}_n + \frac{\text{MSS}^2}{\text{cwnd}_n} \quad \text{(per RTT, equivalent to } \text{cwnd} + \text{MSS)}
$$

### Multiplicative Decrease (On Timeout)

$$
\text{ssthresh} = \frac{\text{cwnd}}{2}, \qquad \text{cwnd} = 1 \cdot \text{MSS}
$$

### On Triple Duplicate ACK (Fast Retransmit / Fast Recovery)

$$
\text{ssthresh} = \frac{\text{cwnd}}{2}, \qquad \text{cwnd} = \text{ssthresh}
$$

### AIMD Equation (Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease)

$$
W_{n+1} =
\begin{cases}
W_n + \alpha, & \text{(additive increase, per RTT)} \\
W_n / 2, & \text{(multiplicative decrease, on loss)}
\end{cases}
$$

For TCP Reno, $\alpha = 1$ MSS per RTT, division factor = 2.

## 2.8 Long-Run Throughput of TCP (Mathis Equation)

The steady-state average throughput of a TCP connection in the presence of packet loss probability $p$ (where $p \ll 1$) is:

$$
B \approx \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}}
$$

Or more precisely (PFTK / Padhye model):

$$
B(p) \approx \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{2p}{3}} + 12 \sqrt{\frac{3p}{8}} \cdot p \left(1 + 32p^2\right)}
$$

## 2.9 Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN)

ECN uses two bits in the IP header (ECT, CE) to allow **end-to-end notification without dropping packets**:

| ECT (00) | Not ECN-capable |
|---|---|
| ECT(0) = 10 | ECN-capable transport, no congestion |
| ECT(1) = 01 | ECN-capable transport, no congestion |
| CE = 11 | Congestion Experienced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** ECN is used in **data centers (DCTCP)**, **modern broadband networks (Comcast, BT)**, and is supported in **Windows 10/11 and Linux kernels** by default since ~2018.

## 2.10 TCP Variants Summary (Highly Asked in KTU)

| Variant | Year | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **TCP Tahoe** | 1988 | Slow Start + Congestion Avoidance + Fast Retransmit (cwnd → 1 on loss) |
| **TCP Reno** | 1990 | Adds Fast Recovery (cwnd → ssthresh on 3 dupACK) |
| **TCP Vegas** | 1995 | Delay-based, not loss-based |
| **TCP BIC / CUBIC** | 2004 | Default in Linux; cubic function window growth |
| **TCP BBR** | 2016 | Model-based (Google), targets bottleneck bandwidth × RTT |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Burst Tolerance in Token Bucket

> **Problem Setup:** A Token Bucket has capacity $C = 10$ MB and token generation rate $\rho = 2$ MB/s. The output link rate is $R = 10$ MB/s. Find the maximum burst size that can be sent instantaneously when the bucket is full.

### Step 1: Time to drain bucket at output rate

$$
\tau = \frac{C}{R}
$$

### Step 2: Tokens generated during drain time

$$
T_{\text{generated}} = \rho \cdot \tau = \rho \cdot \frac{C}{R}
$$

### Step 3: Tokens still available when bucket fully drained

$$
T_{\text{available}} = C + T_{\text{generated}} = C + \frac{\rho C}{R} = C\left(1 + \frac{\rho}{R}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Substitute values

$$
T_{\text{available}} = 10 \left(1 + \frac{2}{10}\right) = 10 \times 1.2 = 12 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 5: Burst duration

$$
T_{\text{burst}} = \frac{C + \rho \tau}{R} = \frac{12 \text{ MB}}{10 \text{ MB/s}} = 1.2 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Final Answer:** Maximum sustained burst = **12 MB** lasting **1.2 seconds**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State TCP Throughput (Mathis Equation)

> **Problem Setup:** A TCP Reno connection has MSS = 1500 bytes, RTT = 100 ms, and packet loss probability $p = 10^{-2}$. Estimate the long-run throughput.

### Step 1: Recall the simplified Mathis Equation

$$
B \approx \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute MSS, RTT, p

$$
B \approx \frac{1500 \text{ bytes}}{0.100 \text{ s}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.01}}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the divisor

$$
\sqrt{0.01} = 0.1
$$

### Step 4: Compute the rate term

$$
\frac{1500}{0.1} = 15000 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the $1/\sqrt{p}$ factor

$$
B \approx 15000 \times \frac{1}{0.1} = 150000 \text{ bytes/s} = 150 \text{ KB/s} = 1.2 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Interpretation:** At 1% loss, a TCP connection using 1500-byte segments over a 100 ms RTT link achieves only ~1.2 Mbps — illustrating the **fragility of loss-based congestion control**.

## 3.3 Derivation: AIMD Convergence to Fairness

> **Goal:** Prove that two TCP flows sharing a bottleneck converge to equal bandwidth using AIMD ($\alpha$ additive, $\beta = 1/2$ multiplicative).

### Step 1: Define window vectors at time $t$
Let flows 1 and 2 have windows $W_1(t)$ and $W_2(t)$.

### Step 2: Each RTT they increase by $\alpha$

$$
W_1 \leftarrow W_1 + \alpha, \quad W_2 \leftarrow W_2 + \alpha
$$

### Step 3: On congestion event (total $W_1 + W_2 > B$), both decrease by factor $\beta = 1/2$

$$
W_i^{\text{new}} = \beta \cdot W_i
$$

### Step 4: Compute change in difference

$$
\Delta (W_1 - W_2) = (\beta - 1)(W_1 - W_2) \text{ on decrease}
$$

### Step 5: Since $|\beta - 1| < 1$, the difference contracts by factor $1/2$ each loss event

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \vert W_1^{(n)} - W_2^{(n)} \vert = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** AIMD is **provably convergent and fair** among competing flows. This is the cornerstone of TCP's success.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Algorithm

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Voice over IP (smooth CBR required) | **Leaky Bucket** | Strict constant rate needed |
| Web browsing (bursty HTTP) | **Token Bucket** | Allows idle-then-burst behavior |
| Backbone router with mixed traffic | **RED** | Proactive drop avoids global synchronization |
| Modern data center | **DCTCP + ECN** | Fine-grained feedback, low queue occupancy |
| Wireless lossy link | **TCP Westwood / BBR** | Loss-based misinterprets wireless loss as congestion |

## 3.5 Derivation: Slow Start Time to Reach ssthresh

> **Problem Setup:** Initial cwnd = 1 MSS, ssthresh = 64 MSS. Find the number of RTTs to reach ssthresh.

### Step 1: Slow start doubles cwnd each RTT
After $k$ RTTs: $\text{cwnd} = 2^k \cdot \text{MSS}$

### Step 2: Set equal to ssthresh and solve

$$
2^k = 64 \implies k = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ RTTs}
$$

### Step 3: General formula

$$
k = \lceil \log_2(\text{ssthresh}/\text{cwnd}_{\text{init}}) \rceil
$$

**Final Answer:** It takes **6 RTTs** for slow start to grow cwnd from 1 MSS to 64 MSS.

## 3.6 Derivation: RED Average Queue Length

> **Problem Setup:** $w_q = 0.002$, $\text{Min}_{th} = 10$ packets, $\text{Max}_{th} = 30$ packets, $P_{\max} = 0.1$. If instantaneous queue $q = 25$ and prior average $\bar{q} = 15$, compute drop probability.

### Step 1: Update average queue length

$$
\bar{q}_{\text{new}} = (1 - 0.002) \cdot 15 + 0.002 \cdot 25
$$

$$
\bar{q}_{\text{new}} = 0.998 \cdot 15 + 0.05 = 14.97 + 0.05 = 15.02
$$

### Step 2: Verify region — $10 \leq 15.02 < 30$ ✓ (middle region)

### Step 3: Compute $P_b$

$$
P_b = 0.1 \cdot \frac{15.02 - 10}{30 - 10} = 0.1 \cdot \frac{5.02}{20} = 0.0251
$$

### Step 4: Apply randomization correction (count = 1)

$$
p = \frac{0.0251}{1 - 1 \cdot 0.0251} = \frac{0.0251}{0.9749} \approx 0.0258
$$

**Final Answer:** Drop probability ≈ **2.58%**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Congestion Control Feedback Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Sender Host] -->|cwnd packets| nodeR1[Router R1]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2[Router R2]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3[Router R3 with RED]
    nodeR3 -->|Forwarded Packets| nodeD[Receiver Host]
    nodeR3 -->|Detects queue build up| nodeM{Monitor Queue Length}
    nodeM -->|If q exceeds Max threshold| nodeD1[Drop / Mark ECN CE]
    nodeD -->|ACK feedback| nodeS
    nodeD1 -->|Trigger ECN Echo| nodeS
    nodeS -->|Reduce cwnd via AIMD| nodeS
    style nodeS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeD fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeM fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeD1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.2 TCP Congestion Control State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SlowStart
    SlowStart --> CongestionAvoidance : cwnd GE ssthresh
    CongestionAvoidance --> FastRetransmit : 3 Duplicate ACKs
    SlowStart --> Timeout : Packet timeout
    CongestionAvoidance --> Timeout : Packet timeout
    FastRetransmit --> FastRecovery : ssthresh set to cwnd div 2
    FastRecovery --> CongestionAvoidance : New ACK received
    Timeout --> SlowStart : cwnd reset to 1 MSS
    note right of SlowStart : Exponential growth
    note right of CongestionAvoidance : Linear growth by 1 MSS per RTT
    note right of FastRecovery : AIMD multiplicative decrease applied
```

## 4.3 Leaky Bucket Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Bursty Input Traffic] -->|Variable rate| nodeB[Bucket Capacity B packets]
    nodeB -->|Drains at constant rate rho| nodeC[Constant Output to Network]
    nodeB -->|Overflow| nodeD[Discarded Packets]

    style nodeB fill:#ccccff,stroke:#3333cc

```

    nodeBk -->|If token available consume 1| nodeQ[Packet Queue]
    nodeBk -->|Extra tokens discarded| nodeX[Overflow Tokens]
    nodeQ -->|Variable burst rate up to R| nodeN[Network Interface]
    nodeA[Arriving Packets] --> nodeQ

    nodeB -->|Below| nodeC[Accept all packets]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define network congestion. List any four causes of congestion.**

**Model Answer:**
Congestion is a condition in a packet-switched network where the demand for transmission resources exceeds the available capacity, causing excessive queuing delay and packet loss.

Four causes of congestion:
1. **Bursty input traffic** exceeding link capacity.
2. **Insufficient router buffer memory** for transient overloads.
3. **Slow processor in routers** causing queuing delays.
4. **Multiplexing multiple high-rate flows** onto a single low-rate link.

*(Bloom Level: Remember / Understand | CO Mapping: CO3)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop congestion control. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Prevention-based | Reaction-based |
| Feedback used | No | Yes |
| Timing | Before congestion | After congestion detected |
| Example | Leaky Bucket / Token Bucket | TCP Congestion Control / RED |

*(Bloom Level: Understand | CO Mapping: CO3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Leaky Bucket algorithm with a neat diagram. Derive the equation for the time to drain a full bucket.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The Leaky Bucket algorithm enforces a constant output rate $\rho$ regardless of input burstiness, analogous to a water bucket with a hole.

**Diagram:**
- A bucket of capacity $B$ packets.
- Input arrives at variable rate $\lambda(t)$.
- Output drains at constant rate $\rho$ into the network.
- Overflow when bucket full.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1: Let the bucket hold $x(t)$ packets at time $t$. The input fills it at rate $\lambda(t)$ and it empties at constant rate $\rho$.

Step 2: Differential equation:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \lambda(t) - \rho \quad \text{whenever } 0 \le x(t) < B
$$

Step 3: At full capacity, $x = B$ and any further input is **discarded** (overflow).

Step 4: When bucket is completely full at $x = B$ and input stops, drain time $T_d$ satisfies:

$$
\int_0^{T_d} \rho \, dt = B
$$

Step 5: Therefore:

$$
T_d = \frac{B}{\rho} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Numerical example:** If $B = 100$ packets and $\rho = 20$ packets/sec, then $T_d = 5$ seconds.

**Advantages:** Simple, enforces strict rate, predictable latency.
**Disadvantages:** Discards bursts, inflexible, no allowance for idle-then-burst behavior.

*(Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze | CO Mapping: CO3 | 7 Marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Token Bucket algorithm. Compute the maximum burst size given $C = 5$ MB, $\rho = 1$ MB/s, $R = 50$ MB/s.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Token Bucket allows a maximum burst by accumulating tokens at rate $\rho$, each transmitted packet consumes one token. Bursts up to $C$ are permitted.

**Operating Principle:**
1. Tokens generated at rate $\rho$ tokens/sec, max stored = $C$.
2. Idle period fills the bucket (up to capacity $C$).
3. On arrival, packet waits if no token; consumes one if available.
4. Allows **bursts up to $C$ packets instantly** — unlike Leaky Bucket.

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

Step 1: Time to drain one full bucket at line rate:

$$
\tau = \frac{C}{R} = \frac{5 \text{ MB}}{50 \text{ MB/s}} = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

Step 2: Tokens generated during drain time $\tau$:

$$
\Delta T = \rho \cdot \tau = 1 \text{ MB/s} \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 0.1 \text{ MB}
$$

Step 3: Total tokens available = initial bucket + new tokens generated:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = C + \Delta T = 5 + 0.1 = 5.1 \text{ MB}
$$

Step 4: This equals the maximum burst sent at line rate $R$:

$$
B_{\max} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{R} = \frac{5.1}{50} = 0.102 \text{ seconds of full-speed transmission}
$$

**Final Answer:** Maximum sustainable burst ≈ **5.1 MB**.

*(Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze | CO Mapping: CO3 | 7 Marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe TCP congestion control with the help of a state diagram. Explain Slow Start and Congestion Avoidance phases in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Overview:** TCP uses a congestion window `cwnd` and a slow-start threshold `ssthresh`. The effective send rate is bounded by:

$$
W_{\text{eff}} = \min(\text{cwnd},\, \text{rwnd})
$$

**State Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
\text{SS} \xrightarrow{\text{cwnd} \ge \text{ssthresh}} \text{CA} \xrightarrow{3 \text{ dupACK}} \text{FR} \xrightarrow{\text{New ACK}} \text{CA}
$$
$$
\text{SS or CA} \xrightarrow{\text{Timeout}} \text{SS (reset cwnd = 1)}
$$

**Slow Start Phase:**
- Initial cwnd = 1 MSS (or 10 MSS in modern Linux).
- For every ACK received: `cwnd += MSS` (or per RTT, `cwnd = 2 × cwnd`).
- This produces **exponential growth**: 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 → 16 → ...
- Terminates when cwnd ≥ ssthresh.

**Congestion Avoidance Phase:**
- cwnd grows **linearly**: per RTT, `cwnd += MSS` (one packet per RTT).
- Equivalent per-ACK formula:

$$
\text{cwnd}_{i+1} = \text{cwnd}_i + \frac{\text{MSS}^2}{\text{cwnd}_i}
$$

- Terminates on packet loss (timeout or 3 duplicate ACKs).

**Reaction to Loss:**
- On timeout: `ssthresh = cwnd/2`, `cwnd = 1 MSS`, return to Slow Start.
- On 3 dupACK: `ssthresh = cwnd/2`, `cwnd = ssthresh`, enter Congestion Avoidance (Fast Recovery).

**Why AIMD Works:**
- Additive Increase maintains utilization.
- Multiplicative Decrease ensures rapid reaction and fair convergence.

*(Bloom Level: Analyze | CO Mapping: CO3 | 7 Marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the RED (Random Early Detection) algorithm. How does it differ from Tail Drop queue management?**

**Model Solution:**

**RED Overview:** RED is a proactive queue management algorithm (Floyd & Jacobson, 1993) that drops packets probabilistically *before* the buffer is full, providing early congestion signals to TCP senders.

**Operation Steps:**

Step 1: Compute **Exponentially Weighted Moving Average** of queue length:

$$
\bar{q}_n = (1 - w_q) \cdot \bar{q}_{n-1} + w_q \cdot q_n
$$

Step 2: Compare $\bar{q}$ against two thresholds:

- If $\bar{q} < \text{Min}_{th}$: **accept all** packets.
- If $\text{Min}_{th} \le \bar{q} < \text{Max}_{th}$: drop with probability $p_b$.
- If $\bar{q} \ge \text{Max}_{th}$: **drop all** (forced drop).

Step 3: Compute drop probability linearly:

$$
P_b = P_{\max} \cdot \frac{\bar{q} - \text{Min}_{th}}{\text{Max}_{th} - \text{Min}_{th}}
$$

Step 4: Apply randomization (prevents synchronization):

$$
p_{\text{final}} = \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b}
$$

**RED vs Tail Drop — Comparison:**

| Aspect | Tail Drop | RED |
|---|---|---|
| Drop criterion | Buffer 100% full | Average queue > Min threshold |
| Proactive? | No (reactive) | Yes |
| Avoids global sync? | No (synchronized drops) | Yes (randomization) |
| Penalizes bursty flows | Sometimes unfairly | More fairly |
| Latency | High (buffer fills) | Lower (early drop) |
| Complexity | Trivial | Moderate |

**Advantages of RED:**
- Prevents buffer overflow before it occurs.
- Avoids **TCP global synchronization** phenomenon.
- Maintains shorter average queues → lower queuing delay.

**Disadvantages:**
- Parameter tuning ($\text{Min}_{th}, \text{Max}_{th}, w_q, P_{\max}$) is sensitive.
- Without proper tuning, can drop too aggressively or too lazily.

*(Bloom Level: Analyze / Evaluate | CO Mapping: CO3 | 7 Marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Flow Control with Congestion Control** — flow control is end-to-end (rwnd), congestion control is network-wide (cwnd). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Wrong formula for TCP throughput** — many students write $B \approx \text{MSS}/\text{RTT}$ forgetting the $1/\sqrt{p}$ factor. Always mention the **Mathis Equation** explicitly.
>
> 3. **Leaky Bucket vs Token Bucket confusion** — remember: Leaky Bucket enforces constant rate; Token Bucket permits bursts. Forgetting the formula $B_{\max} = C + \rho \cdot C/R$ is a 2-mark loss.
>
> 4. **RED parameters left unmentioned** — always mention $\text{Min}_{th}$, $\text{Max}_{th}$, $P_{\max}$, and $w_q$ when describing RED.
>
> 5. **TCP state transitions incomplete** — examiners expect all 4 states: Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, Fast Recovery. Missing Fast Recovery costs marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — throughput should always be quoted in **Mbps** or **packets/sec**, not raw numbers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Congestion Control Algorithms":**

**Core Concepts**
- Congestion ≠ Flow Control (end-to-end vs. network-wide).
- Kleinrock's curve: throughput collapses after saturation if uncontrolled.
- Open-loop (prevention) vs Closed-loop (reactive with feedback).

**Leaky Bucket**
- Constant output rate $\rho$ regardless of input burst.
- Drains full bucket in time $T_d = B/\rho$.
- No burst tolerance; strict rate enforcement.

**Token Bucket**
- Bursts allowed up to capacity $C$.
- Burst size: $B_{\max} = C + \rho \cdot C/R = C(1 + \rho/R)$.
- More flexible than Leaky Bucket for bursty traffic.

**Choke Packet**
- Router sends explicit feedback to source.
- Now obsolete; replaced by ECN.

**RED**
- Three regions: no-drop, probabilistic drop, forced drop.
- Uses EWMA: $\bar{q} = (1 - w_q)\bar{q}_{prev} + w_q q$.
- Drop probability grows linearly between thresholds.
- Prevents global synchronization among TCP flows.

**TCP Congestion Control**
- **Slow Start:** cwnd doubles per RTT (exponential).
- **Congestion Avoidance:** cwnd += MSS per RTT (linear).
- **Fast Retransmit:** triggered by 3 duplicate ACKs.
- **Fast Recovery:** ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = ssthresh.
- **Timeout:** ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = 1 MSS.
- **AIMD equation:** $W \to W+1$ per RTT, $W \to W/2$ on loss.
- **Mathis throughput:** $B \approx \frac{\text{MSS}}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}}$.

**ECN**
- IP header bits (ECT, CE) signal congestion without dropping.
- Modern networks prefer ECN over explicit drops.

**TCP Variants**
- **Tahoe:** No Fast Recovery.
- **Reno:** Adds Fast Recovery.
- **Vegas:** Delay-based (not loss-based).
- **CUBIC:** Linux default; uses cubic function for window growth.
- **BBR:** Google; model-based on bottleneck bandwidth and RTT.

**Key Numerical Constants to Remember**
- TCP Reno decrease factor = **1/2**.
- TCP Reno increase slope = **1 MSS per RTT**.
- RED $w_q$ typical = **0.002**.
- RED $P_{\max}$ typical range = **0.02 to 0.10**.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Congestion Control Algorithms** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Internet Protocol (IP)** is the principal network-layer (Layer 3) communication protocol of the Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP). It is a **connectionless**, **best-effort**, and **unreliable** datagram service responsible for **logical addressing**, **routing**, and **fragmentation/reassembly** of data packets across heterogeneous networks. The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on two primary versions: **IPv4** (32-bit addresses, ~4.3 billion nodes) and **IPv6** (128-bit addresses, ~3.4 × 10³⁸ nodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IP is a "best-effort" protocol – it provides **no guarantees** on delivery, ordering, or duplication. Reliability is pushed to the upper transport layer (TCP). The unit of data at this layer is called a **datagram** (or **packet**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **Indian Postal System**:
- The **IP address** is analogous to the **postal address** written on an envelope (e.g., "House No. 45, MG Road, Kochi, Kerala, India").
- The **IP header** is like the **cover letter** of the envelope — it contains routing instructions, sender/receiver details, and handling instructions.
- **Routing** is the job of the **postal sorting office** — it decides the best path from sender to receiver.
- **Fragmentation** is like the postal service splitting a very large parcel into smaller boxes for shipping, with each box tagged ("Box 1 of 4", "Box 2 of 4"...) so the recipient can reassemble the original.

If a parcel is lost, the postal service does **not** call to inform you — that is the *best-effort* nature. If you need confirmation, you pay for **registered/speed post** (analogous to **TCP** layered on top of IP).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| IPv4 Address Size | **32 bits** (4 bytes) | Written in dotted-decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.1) |
| IPv6 Address Size | **128 bits** (16 bytes) | Written in hexadecimal colon-separated |
| Default IPv4 Header | **20 bytes** (160 bits) | Without options |
| Max IPv4 Header | **60 bytes** (480 bits) | With 40 bytes of options |
| Max IPv4 Datagram Size | **65,535 bytes** | 16-bit Total Length field limit |
| Min IPv4 Header | **20 bytes** | RFC 791 standard |
| IPv6 Fixed Header | **40 bytes** | RFC 8200 |
| Standard MTU (Ethernet) | **1500 bytes** | Maximum Transmission Unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why best-effort?** IP was designed for the military's ARPANET, which needed to survive partial network failures. Sacrificing guaranteed delivery kept routers simple, fast, and resilient — a brilliant engineering trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of IP: Addressing and Forwarding

IP performs three logical functions:
1. **Addressing** — uniquely identify every host/router interface.
2. **Routing / Forwarding** — determine the next hop toward the destination.
3. **Encapsulation/Fragmentation** — break large datagrams to fit the underlying network's MTU.

## 2.2 IPv4 Header — Field-by-Field Deep Dive

The IPv4 header (20 bytes minimum) is the most important topic in Module 3. The KTU exam frequently asks students to **draw and explain** this structure.

| # | Field | Size (bits) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **Version (VER)** | 4 | Identifies IP version (4 for IPv4, 6 for IPv6). |
| 1 | **IHL (Internet Header Length)** | 4 | Length of header in **32-bit words**. Min = 5 (20 bytes), Max = 15 (60 bytes). |
| 2 | **Type of Service (ToS/DSCP)** | 8 | QoS priority, delay, throughput, reliability hints. |
| 3 | **Total Length** | 16 | Entire datagram size (header + data), max = **65,535 bytes**. |
| 4 | **Identification** | 16 | Used to group fragments of a single original datagram. |
| 5 | **Flags** | 3 | Bit 0 = Reserved, Bit 1 = **DF** (Don't Fragment), Bit 2 = **MF** (More Fragments). |
| 6 | **Fragment Offset** | 13 | Position of fragment's data in the original datagram, measured in **8-byte units**. |
| 7 | **Time-to-Live (TTL)** | 8 | Hop counter; decremented by 1 at each router; packet discarded when TTL = 0. |
| 8 | **Protocol** | 8 | Identifies next-layer protocol (6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 1 = ICMP). |
| 9 | **Header Checksum** | 16 | 1's complement sum of header only; recomputed at each router. |
| 10 | **Source IP Address** | 32 | Original sender's IP. |
| 11 | **Destination IP Address** | 32 | Final recipient's IP. |
| 12 | **Options (optional)** | 0–320 | Rare; padded to 32-bit boundary. |

## 2.3 The Three Stepwise Operations of an IP Router

**Step 1 — Header Validation**
- Router checks the **version**, **IHL**, and **header checksum**. If invalid, the packet is silently dropped.

**Step 2 — Destination Lookup**
- Router extracts the **Destination IP Address** and performs a longest-prefix-match lookup in its routing table to determine the next-hop interface.

**Step 3 — Decrement TTL, Recompute Checksum, Forward**
- **TTL** is decremented by 1. If it becomes 0, an **ICMP "Time Exceeded"** message is sent back to the source. Otherwise the header checksum is recomputed and the packet is forwarded (possibly fragmented if it exceeds the outgoing MTU).

## 2.4 IPv4 Fragmentation Logic

When a datagram of size $D$ must traverse a link with MTU $M$ where $D > M$, the router splits the datagram into fragments. Each fragment is its own IP datagram with the same **Identification** number.

- **Fragment data size** must be a **multiple of 8 bytes** (except the last one).
- **Fragment Offset** = position of fragment's first data byte ÷ 8.

**Reassembly** happens **only at the destination host**, never at intermediate routers. The host waits up to 60 seconds for all fragments; missing fragments cause the whole datagram to be discarded.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Header Length} = \text{IHL} \times 4$ | IHL is in 32-bit words | Compute header size |
| 2 | $\text{Data Length} = \text{Total Length} - \text{Header Length}$ | Find payload size | Question solving |
| 3 | $\text{Number of Fragments} = \left\lceil \frac{D-M}{M-IHL} \right\rceil + 1$ | Split datagram | Fragmentation problems |
| 4 | $\text{Fragment Offset} = \frac{\text{Bytes already sent}}{8}$ | Position in 8-byte units | Fragmentation problems |
| 5 | $\text{Last Fragment Offset} = \left\lfloor \frac{D-\text{header}}{8} \right\rfloor$ | Offset of final piece | Validation |
| 6 | $\text{Number of Usable Hosts in Subnet} = 2^{h} - 2$ | Subtract network & broadcast | Subnetting |
| 7 | $\text{Number of Subnets} = 2^{s}$ | $s$ = borrowed bits | Subnetting |
| 8 | $\text{CIDR Block Size} = 2^{(32-n)}$ | $n$ = prefix length | Classless addressing |
| 9 | $\text{Subnet Mask} = 2^{n} - 1$ in binary, then dotted | Convert /n to decimal | Subnetting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the "8-byte rule":** The Fragment Offset field is **always a multiple of 8**. This is the most common pitfall in KTU exam fragmentation problems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IP Fragmentation** is the basis for how **video streaming, VoIP, and large file transfers** traverse networks with varying link capacities.
- **TTL** prevents packets from looping forever in misconfigured networks (e.g., routing loops), saving bandwidth.
- **Header Checksum** ensures header integrity; it does NOT cover payload (TCP/UDP have their own checksums).
- **IP addressing & subnetting** form the foundation of **enterprise network design, cloud VPCs (AWS/Azure), and cybersecurity firewalls**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — IPv4 Header Decoding

> **Problem:** A captured IPv4 packet has the following first 20 bytes in hexadecimal:
> `45 00 00 3C 1C 46 40 00 40 06 B1 E6 C0 A8 01 64 C0 A8 01 C7`
> Decode every field.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Extract **Version** and **IHL** from byte 0 = `0x45`:
- Binary: `0100 0101`
- High nibble `0100` = **4** → IPv4
- Low nibble `0101` = **5** → IHL = 5 × 4 = **20 bytes** (no options)

Step 2 — **Total Length** from bytes 2–3 = `0x003C` = **60 bytes**

Step 3 — **Identification** from bytes 4–5 = `0x1C46` = **7238**

Step 4 — **Flags + Fragment Offset** from bytes 6–7 = `0x4000`:
- Binary: `0100 0000 0000 0000`
- Flags bits: `010` → **Reserved=0, DF=1, MF=0** → Do Not Fragment set.
- Fragment Offset = `0 0000 0000 0000` = **0**

Step 5 — **TTL** = byte 8 = `0x40` = **64**

Step 6 — **Protocol** = byte 9 = `0x06` = **6 = TCP**

Step 7 — **Header Checksum** = bytes 10–11 = `0xB1E6`

Step 8 — **Source IP** = bytes 12–15 = `C0 A8 01 64`:
- Decimal: **192.168.1.100**

Step 9 — **Destination IP** = bytes 16–19 = `C0 A8 01 C7`:
- Decimal: **192.168.1.199**

Step 10 — **Payload length** = Total Length − Header Length = $60 - 20 = \mathbf{40}$ **bytes** of TCP data.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — IP Fragmentation Calculation

> **Problem:** A 4,200-byte datagram (20-byte header) must pass through a network with MTU = 1,500 bytes. Find the number of fragments, their data sizes, and fragment offsets.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute data portion of original datagram:
$$D_{data} = 4200 - 20 = 4180 \text{ bytes}$$

Step 2 — Maximum data per fragment (must be multiple of 8 and leave room for 20-byte header):
$$M_{data} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}$$

Step 3 — Since 1480 is already a multiple of 8, we can use 1480 for all non-final fragments.

Step 4 — Number of full fragments:
$$n_1 = \left\lfloor \frac{4180}{1480} \right\rfloor = 2$$

Step 5 — Remaining bytes for final fragment:
$$R = 4180 - (2 \times 1480) = 1220 \text{ bytes}$$

Step 6 — Total fragments = $2 + 1 = \mathbf{3}$ fragments.

| Fragment | Data Bytes | Total Length (Header+Data) | Fragment Offset (bytes/8) | MF Flag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1480 | 1500 | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 1480 | 1500 | 185 (1480/8) | 1 |
| 3 | 1220 | 1240 | 370 (2960/8) | 0 |

Verification: $1480 + 1480 + 1220 = 4180$ ✓ (matches original data length)

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Subnetting with CIDR

> **Problem:** Given the network **192.168.10.0/24**, design **4 equal subnets**. Provide network address, broadcast address, usable range, and subnet mask for each.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Original network: 192.168.10.0/24 → 24 network bits, 8 host bits.

Step 2 — To make 4 subnets, we need to borrow bits $s$ such that $2^s \ge 4$ → $s = 2$.

Step 3 — New prefix = /24 + 2 = **/26** → subnet mask = 255.255.255.192.

Step 4 — Block size = $2^{(32-26)} = 2^6 = 64$ addresses per subnet.

Step 5 — Usable hosts per subnet = $2^{(32-26)} - 2 = 62$.

| Subnet | Network Address | First Usable | Last Usable | Broadcast | Mask |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 192.168.10.0 | 192.168.10.1 | 192.168.10.62 | 192.168.10.63 | /26 |
| B | 192.168.10.64 | 192.168.10.65 | 192.168.10.126 | 192.168.10.127 | /26 |
| C | 192.168.10.128 | 192.168.10.129 | 192.168.10.190 | 192.168.10.191 | /26 |
| D | 192.168.10.192 | 192.168.10.193 | 192.168.10.254 | 192.168.10.255 | /26 |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IPv4 vs IPv6

| Feature | IPv4 | IPv6 |
|---|---|---|
| Address size | 32 bits | 128 bits |
| Header size | 20–60 bytes (variable) | 40 bytes (fixed) |
| Fragmentation | Routers & hosts | **Hosts only** (no router fragmentation) |
| Header Checksum | Yes | **Removed** (faster routing) |
| Options | In header | **Extension headers** (separate) |
| Address types | Unicast, Multicast, Broadcast | Unicast, Multicast, **Anycast** (no broadcast) |
| Configuration | Manual / DHCP | **SLAAC** (auto) / DHCPv6 |
| Security | Optional (IPSec) | **Built-in IPSec** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IPv4 Datagram Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    bit0["Bit 0"]:::edge
    bit31["Bit 31"]:::edge

    subgraph hdr1["Row 1: First 32 Bits"]
        ver["VER (4 bits)"]:::mand
        ihl["IHL (4 bits)"]:::mand
        tos["TOS / DSCP (8 bits)"]:::mand
        totsz["Total Length (16 bits)"]:::mand
    end

    subgraph hdr2["Row 2: Identification, Flags, Offset"]
        ident["Identification (16 bits)"]:::mand
        flags["Flags (3 bits) DF MF Res"]:::mand
        foff["Fragment Offset (13 bits)"]:::mand
    end

    subgraph hdr3["Row 3: TTL, Protocol, Checksum"]
        ttl["TTL (8 bits)"]:::mand
        proto["Protocol (8 bits)"]:::mand
        csum["Header Checksum (16 bits)"]:::mand
    end

    subgraph hdr4["Row 4: Source IP"]
        sip["Source IP Address (32 bits)"]:::addr
    end

    subgraph hdr5["Row 5: Destination IP"]
        dip["Destination IP Address (32 bits)"]:::addr
    end

    subgraph hdr6["Row 6: Options + Padding (0 to 320 bits)"]
        opt["Options / Padding (variable)"]:::opt
    end

    subgraph hdr7["Row 7 Onwards: Payload"]
        data["DATA (0 to 65535 minus header bytes)"]:::data
    end

    bit0 --> hdr1 --> hdr2 --> hdr3 --> hdr4 --> hdr5 --> hdr6 --> hdr7 --> bit31

    classDef mand fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef addr fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#070,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef edge fill:#eee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 IPv4 Fragmentation & Reassembly Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Original Datagram 4180 bytes data"]):::start
    chk{"Data size > MTU minus header?"}:::dec
    frag1["Fragment 1: 1480 bytes data, Offset 0, MF=1"]:::frag
    frag2["Fragment 2: 1480 bytes data, Offset 185, MF=1"]:::frag
    frag3["Fragment 3: 1220 bytes data, Offset 370, MF=0"]:::frag
    rx["Destination Reassembly Buffer"]:::rx
    wait{"All fragments with same ID received?"}:::dec
    timer["Start 60 sec timer"]:::timer
    disc["Discard incomplete datagram"]:::discard
    done["Deliver full datagram to upper layer"]:::done

    start --> chk
    chk -- Yes --> frag1
    chk -- Yes --> frag2
    chk -- Yes --> frag3
    frag1 --> rx
    frag2 --> rx
    frag3 --> rx
    rx --> timer
    rx --> wait
    wait -- Yes --> done
    wait -- No, timer expired --> disc

    classDef start fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#036,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef frag fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#e1d6ff,stroke:#505,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef timer fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#c60,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef discard fill:#d6d6d6,stroke:#555,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#070,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 IP Protocol Operation Stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app["Application Layer: HTTP, FTP, DNS"]:::app
    trans["Transport Layer: TCP or UDP segment"]:::trans
    net["Network Layer: IP datagram header + payload"]:::net
    data["Data Link Layer: Frame with MAC addresses"]:::dl
    phys["Physical Layer: Electrical or optical bits"]:::phys

    app --> trans --> net --> data --> phys
    phys -->|bits travel| data -->|recv| net -->|hand to| trans -->|sockets| app

    classDef app fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#a60,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#ffd6f0,stroke:#a06,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dl fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef phys fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#070,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Router Forwarding Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pkg["Incoming IP packet arrives at Router"]:::in
    chkver{"Version valid?"}:::dec
    chkcsum{"Header checksum valid?"}:::dec
    decTTL["TTL = TTL minus 1"]:::op
    ttlchk{"TTL = 0?"}:::dec
    icmpttl["Send ICMP Time Exceeded, drop packet"]:::drop
    lookup["Perform Longest Prefix Match on Destination IP"]:::op
    mtuChk{"Packet size > Outgoing MTU?"}:::dec
    frag["Fragment the packet"]:::op
    dfChk{"DF flag set?"}:::dec
    icmpfrag["Send ICMP Fragmentation Needed, drop"]:::drop
    fwd["Forward to next hop"]:::done

    pkg --> chkver
    chkver -- No --> icmpttl
    chkver -- Yes --> chkcsum
    chkcsum -- No --> icmpttl
    chkcsum -- Yes --> decTTL
    decTTL --> ttlchk
    ttlchk -- Yes --> icmpttl
    ttlchk -- No --> lookup
    lookup --> mtuChk
    mtuChk -- No --> fwd
    mtuChk -- Yes --> dfChk
    dfChk -- Yes --> icmpfrag
    dfChk -- No --> frag --> fwd

    classDef in fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#036,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#e1d6ff,stroke:#505,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef drop fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#070,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the purpose of the **Time-to-Live (TTL)** field in the IPv4 header?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The TTL field is an **8-bit** counter present in the IPv4 header. Its purpose is to **limit the lifetime of a datagram** in the network. Every time a router forwards the packet, it **decrements TTL by 1**. When TTL reaches 0, the packet is **discarded** and the router sends an **ICMP "Time Exceeded"** message back to the source. This mechanism prevents datagrams from looping indefinitely in case of routing errors, conserving network bandwidth.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Connection-oriented** and **Connectionless** service. Which category does IP belong to?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|---|---|---|
| Setup | Requires handshake | No setup |
| Path | Fixed virtual path | Independent datagrams |
| Reliability | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed |
| Example | TCP, ATM | **IP, UDP** |

IP belongs to the **connectionless** category. It is a **best-effort delivery** protocol — each datagram is routed independently and the network does not establish any prior connection. There is no acknowledgement, no sequencing, and no retransmission at the IP layer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks): IPv4 Header Analysis & Fragmentation

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

> An IPv4 datagram carries **4,000 bytes** of total length (including a 20-byte header) and must pass through a network with **MTU = 1,500 bytes**.
>
> **(a)** Draw the IPv4 header format and explain the function of **any six** important fields. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Perform the fragmentation of the datagram. Show the **length, fragment offset, and MF flag** for each fragment. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**IPv4 Header Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["VER 4b | IHL 4b | TOS 8b | Total Length 16b"]:::h
    H2["Identification 16b | Flags 3b | Fragment Offset 13b"]:::h
    H3["TTL 8b | Protocol 8b | Header Checksum 16b"]:::h
    H4["Source IP Address 32b"]:::a
    H5["Destination IP Address 32b"]:::a
    H6["Options 0-320b + Padding"]:::o
    H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6

    classDef h fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef a fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#003,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef o fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Six Key Field Explanations:**

1. **Version (4 bits):** Indicates IP version. Value = 4 for IPv4. Routers inspect this first to know how to interpret the rest of the header.

2. **IHL — Internet Header Length (4 bits):** Length of header in **32-bit words**. Minimum = 5 (20 bytes), Maximum = 15 (60 bytes). Needed because options make header variable length.

3. **Total Length (16 bits):** Length of the **entire datagram** (header + data) in bytes. Maximum = **65,535 bytes**.

4. **Time-to-Live (8 bits):** Hop-count limit to prevent infinite looping. Decremented by 1 at each router; packet dropped at TTL = 0.

5. **Protocol (8 bits):** Demultiplexing key. Tells the destination host which transport-layer protocol to hand the payload to (6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 1 = ICMP).

6. **Fragment Offset (13 bits):** Position of this fragment's data relative to the start of the original datagram, measured in **8-byte units**. Allows correct reassembly.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Total Length = 4,000 bytes → Header = 20 bytes → **Data = 3,980 bytes**
- MTU = 1,500 bytes → Max data per fragment = 1,500 − 20 = **1,480 bytes**
- 1,480 is a multiple of 8 ✓

**Number of full fragments:**
$$n_1 = \left\lfloor \frac{3980}{1480} \right\rfloor = 2$$

**Remaining bytes:**
$$R = 3980 - (2 \times 1480) = 1020 \text{ bytes}$$

**Total fragments = 3.**

| Fragment | Data Bytes | Total Length | Fragment Offset (bytes/8) | MF Flag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | 1,480 | 1,500 | 0 | 1 |
| F2 | 1,480 | 1,500 | 185 (1480÷8) | 1 |
| F3 | 1,020 | 1,040 | 370 (2960÷8) | 0 |

**Verification:** 1480 + 1480 + 1020 = 3,980 bytes ✓

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks): IPv4 Addressing & Subnetting

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

> An organization is allotted the IP block **200.10.20.0/24**. The organization has **4 departments**: Marketing (60 hosts), Sales (60 hosts), R&D (30 hosts), and HR (30 hosts).
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)** and **Variable Length Subnet Masking (VLSM)**. Why is VLSM preferred over equal-sized subnetting? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Using **VLSM**, design subnets for all four departments. Provide the **subnet address, subnet mask, first usable IP, last usable IP, and broadcast address** for each. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing):**
- Introduced in **1993 (RFC 1518/1519)** to replace the inefficient classful addressing (A/B/C/D/E).
- Uses a **prefix length** (e.g., /24) instead of fixed class boundaries.
- Allows flexible block sizes — e.g., a /27 gives 30 usable hosts, /28 gives 14, etc.
- Reduces **routing table size** in the Internet backbone via **route aggregation/supernetting**.

**VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking):**
- A subnetting technique where **different subnets within the same network can have different prefix lengths**.
- Subnets are sized according to the **actual host requirement**, minimizing wastage.
- Requires a **classless routing protocol** like OSPF, EIGRP, RIPv2, or BGP (classful protocols like RIPv1 do not support VLSM).

**Why VLSM > Equal Subnetting?**
Consider /24 divided into 4 **equal /26** subnets: each has 62 usable hosts. Total waste = 4 × 62 = 248 IPs, but we only need 60+60+30+30 = 180. With VLSM, we allocate exact sizes, drastically reducing address wastage and conserving the scarce IPv4 address space.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sort departments by size (descending):**
1. Marketing: 60 hosts → needs /26 (62 usable)
2. Sales: 60 hosts → needs /26 (62 usable)
3. R&D: 30 hosts → needs /27 (30 usable)
4. HR: 30 hosts → needs /27 (30 usable)

**Step 2 — Allocate sequentially from base 200.10.20.0/24:**

| Department | Subnet Address | Mask (Prefix) | First Usable | Last Usable | Broadcast | Usable Hosts |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Marketing | 200.10.20.0 | 255.255.255.192 (/26) | 200.10.20.1 | 200.10.20.62 | 200.10.20.63 | 62 |
| Sales | 200.10.20.64 | 255.255.255.192 (/26) | 200.10.20.65 | 200.10.20.126 | 200.10.20.127 | 62 |
| R&D | 200.10.20.128 | 255.255.255.224 (/27) | 200.10.20.129 | 200.10.20.158 | 200.10.20.159 | 30 |
| HR | 200.10.20.160 | 255.255.255.224 (/27) | 200.10.20.161 | 200.10.20.190 | 200.10.20.191 | 30 |

**Step 3 — Verification:** 62 + 62 + 30 + 30 = 184 usable hosts allocated from 200.10.20.0–200.10.20.191. Remaining 200.10.20.192–200.10.20.255 (64 addresses) is reserved for future use.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Fragment Offset MUST be a multiple of 8.** Offsets like 23 or 41 are invalid and cost 1 mark.
> 2. The **last fragment** must have its **MF flag = 0**; forgetting this loses a mark.
> 3. In subnetting, students often forget to **exclude network and broadcast addresses** from the usable range.
> 4. The **Total Length** field counts **header + data**, not just data. Many students lose 1 mark for this.
