It functions as the *phonebook of the Internet*, decoupling resource locators from resource identifiers through a global, decentralized namespace governed by **IANA** and operated via the **client–server model** over **UDP port 53** (with TCP fallback on port 53).

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology Aligned with KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Term | Exact Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Domain Name** | A case-insensitive, hierarchical ASCII label, max **253 characters**, composed of labels (≤ **63 chars** each) separated by dots. |
| **Namespace** | The complete set of valid, unique names managed by DNS. |
| **Zone** | A contiguous portion of the DNS namespace administered by a single authoritative server. |
| **Resolver** | The client-side library/software (e.g., `glibc`, `systemd-resolved`) that initiates DNS queries on behalf of applications. |
| **Name Server** | A server program that holds DNS records and answers queries (authoritative or recursive). |
| **Resource Record (RR)** | The fundamental unit of data in DNS, e.g., `A`, `AAAA`, `MX`, `CNAME`, `NS`, `SOA`, `PTR`. |
| **TTL** | **Time-To-Live**, the duration (in seconds) a record may be cached, range **0 – 2,147,483,647** (≈ 68 years). |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Global Library Catalogue:**
> Imagine the Internet as an enormous library holding billions of books (web servers). Each book has a cryptic shelf number (IP address) like `142.250.195.68`. Nobody wants to memorize such numbers. So the library installs a **giant, distributed catalogue** (DNS). When you ask for *"Forouzan, Data Communications"*, the catalogue:
> 1. Looks at the **section** (`.com`, `.in` → **TLD server**).
> 2. Asks the **floor librarian** (Authoritative server) for the exact shelf.
> 3. Returns the answer back to you (the resolver).
> DNS works identically — you type `www.google.com`, and a chain of specialized servers collaborates to return `142.250.195.68`. The genius is that no single server holds the entire catalogue; the data is **distributed**, **cached**, and **hierarchical**, making it both **fast** and **fault-tolerant**.

### 1.3 Why DNS Exists — The Problem It Solves

- Humans recall words far better than dotted-decimal IPv4 numbers (32-bit) or hexadecimal IPv6 (128-bit) addresses.
- IP addresses can change (due to renumbering, cloud migration, or load-balancing); names can stay constant.
- A single namespace allows **scalable delegation** — no central database can hold all records for **~5+ billion** daily users.
- DNS is **application-layer** (uses UDP/TCP at the transport layer), so it is decoupled from any specific physical topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (must memorize for KTU):**
> - Default DNS port: **53** (UDP) / **53** (TCP for large responses or zone transfers)
> - Maximum domain name length: **253 characters**
> - Maximum label length: **63 characters**
> - Root servers globally: **13 logical root servers** (A–M), physically replicated via **anycast** to **1,500+ instances** worldwide.
> - Header size: **12 bytes (fixed)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of DNS

DNS uses a **3-tier logical hierarchy** plus a local caching layer:

1. **Root Level** — 13 logical root servers (`a.root-servers.net` … `m.root-servers.net`). They do **not** resolve names; they only redirect to the appropriate **TLD** server.
2. **Top-Level Domain (TLD) Servers** — responsible for `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, country-codes like `.in`, `.uk`, and new gTLDs like `.app`, `.blog`.
3. **Authoritative (Second-Level) Servers** — maintained by organizations, ISPs, or DNS providers; hold the **actual A/MX/NS records** for a domain.
4. **Local DNS Resolver** — operated by the user's ISP, organization, or public providers (Google `8.8.8.8`, Cloudflare `1.1.1.1`).

## 2.2 Query Types — The Heart of DNS Resolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask the difference between these — you MUST know both the diagram and the precise definitions.

### 2.2.1 Recursive Query
> *"Find this name and tell me the final answer — don't come back until you have it."*

The queried server takes **full responsibility** to track down the answer, contacting other servers as needed, and returns the final IP.

### 2.2.2 Iterative Query
> *"Give me the best answer you have, or tell me who to ask next."*

The queried server returns either the answer or a **referral** (NS record) pointing to the next server to ask.

> [!NOTE]
> **In practice:** The *stub resolver* in the user's OS sends a **recursive** query to the *local recursive resolver*, which then issues a series of **iterative** queries to root, TLD, and authoritative servers.

## 2.3 DNS Resolution Process — Step-by-Step

When you type `http://www.ktu.ac.in` in a browser:

1. Browser checks its **internal cache**.
2. OS sends query to the **local recursive resolver** (configured via DHCP or manually).
3. Resolver checks its **cache**; if miss:
   - Queries a **root server** → returns `.in` TLD server NS records.
   - Queries the **`.in` TLD server** → returns `ac.in` authoritative NS records.
   - Queries the **`ac.in` authoritative server** → returns NS records for `ktu.ac.in`.
   - Queries the **`ktu.ac.in` authoritative server** → returns the **A record** for `www.ktu.ac.in`.
4. Resolver caches the answer per its TTL and returns it to the OS/browser.
5. Browser establishes a **TCP connection** (port 80/443) to the returned IP.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> DNS is conceptually light on equations, but heavy on **header-field arithmetic**. The following are the KTU high-yield items.

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Domain Name Length** | $L_{\text{domain}} \le 253$ | characters |
| **Label Length** | $L_{\text{label}} \le 63$ | characters per label |
| **DNS Header Size** | $S_{\text{header}} = 12$ | bytes (fixed) |
| **Total Message Size** | $S_{\text{msg}} = 12 + L_{Q} + L_{A} + L_{\text{Auth}} + L_{\text{Add}}$ | bytes (UDP max 512 unless EDNS0 used) |
| **Compression Pointer** | $\text{Offset} = 0xC0 \mid \text{position}$ | 14-bit value (max offset 16383) |
| **UDP Default Cap** | $S_{\text{UDP}} \le 512$ | bytes — larger responses use TCP |
| **EDNS0 UDP Cap** | $S_{\text{EDNS0}} \le 4096$ | bytes (typical default), extendable to 65535 |
| **TTL Range** | $0 \le \text{TTL} \le 2^{31} - 1$ | seconds |
| **CNAME Chain Bound** | $N_{\text{CNAME}} \le 16$ | per resolution (RFC 1035 limit) |
| **DNS over UDP Reliability** | $P_{\text{loss}} \le 1\%$ | for low-latency DNS to remain functional |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of DNS

| Domain | DNS Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Load Balancing** | Round-robin DNS returns different IPs for the same name. |
| **Geo-Redundancy** | Authoritative servers return IPs based on client location (anycast). |
| **Email Routing** | `MX` records direct mail to specific mail servers. |
| **Service Discovery** | `SRV` records locate SIP, XMPP, LDAP, Minecraft servers. |
| **Security** | `DNSSEC` adds cryptographic signatures; `CAA` records control CA issuance. |
| **Content Filtering** | `RPZ`, `RPZ` zones (Response Policy Zones) block malicious domains. |

## 2.6 Common Resource Records (RRs)

| Type | Code (RFC) | Purpose | Example |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| A | 1 | IPv4 address | `www A 142.250.195.68` |
| AAAA | 28 | IPv6 address | `www AAAA 2607:f8b0:4004:c1b::68` |
| CNAME | 5 | Canonical alias | `blog CNAME www` |
| MX | 15 | Mail exchanger | `@ MX 10 mail.ktu.ac.in` |
| NS | 2 | Name server | `@ NS ns1.ktu.ac.in` |
| SOA | 6 | Start of authority | `@ SOA ns1 admin 2024010101 10800 3600 604800 86400` |
| PTR | 12 | Reverse lookup | `1.2.0.192.in-addr.arpa PTR host.example.com` |
| TXT | 16 | Arbitrary text (SPF, DKIM) | `@ TXT "v=spf1 -all"` |
| SRV | 33 | Service locator | `_sip._tcp SRV 10 5 5060 sip.example.com` |
| CAA | 257 | Certification authority authorization | `@ CAA 0 issue "letsencrypt.org"` |

## 2.7 DNS Message Format — Header Structure

The DNS header is exactly **12 bytes**, divided into 6 fields of 16 bits each:

| Offset | Field | Bits | Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | `ID` | 16 | Query identifier (matched in response) |
| 2 | `FLAGS` | 16 | QR (1), Opcode (4), AA (1), TC (1), RD (1), RA (1), Z (3), RCODE (4) |
| 4 | `QDCOUNT` | 16 | Number of questions |
| 6 | `ANCOUNT` | 16 | Number of answer RRs |
| 8 | `NSCOUNT` | 16 | Number of authority RRs |
| 10 | `ARCOUNT` | 16 | Number of additional RRs |

The header is followed by the **Question Section** (QNAME, QTYPE, QCLASS) and then the Answer, Authority, and Additional sections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Domain-Name Wire Format Length

DNS names are encoded on the wire as a sequence of **length-prefixed labels**, each label terminated by a **zero-length octet** (`0x00`).

### 3.1.1 Wire-Format Length of `www.ktu.ac.in`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Wire length} &= L(\text{label}_1) + L(\text{label}_2) + L(\text{label}_3) + L(\text{label}_4) + 1 \\
\text{where } L(\text{label}_i) &= 1 + \text{len}(\text{label}_i) \\
\text{For } \texttt{www.ktu.ac.in} &: \text{labels} = \{\text{"www", "ktu", "ac", "in"} \} \\
\text{len}(\text{"www"}) = 3, \;\; \text{len}(\text{``ktu"}) = 3, \;\; \text{len}(\text{``ac"}) &= 2, \;\; \text{len}(\text{``in"}) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Wire bytes for labels} &= (1+3) + (1+3) + (1+2) + (1+2) \\
&= 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 = 14 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Terminator byte} &= 1 \text{ byte} \\
\text{Total wire length} &= 14 + 1 = 15 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The same name when stored using a **compression pointer** (offset = 14) would consume only **2 bytes**, demonstrating the importance of compression.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Number of Name Compressions

The compression pointer is a 14-bit field, allowing offsets **0 to 16383** within the message.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{offsets}} &= 2^{14} = 16384 \text{ distinct positions} \\
\text{Therefore } \text{maximum number of unique compressed labels} &\le 16384 \\
\text{but in practice } &< 100 \text{ due to message-size limits}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Iterative Query Message Count

For a fully cold cache resolving a single domain `a.b.c.example.com`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{queries}} &= N_{\text{levels}} + N_{\text{retries}} \\
N_{\text{levels}} &= \text{Root} + \text{TLD} + \text{Authoritative chain} + \text{Final answer} \\
&= 1 + 1 + (\text{depth of delegation}) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For `www.ktu.ac.in`, the delegation chain has depth = 2 (`.in` → `ac.in` → `ktu.ac.in`), so:

$$
N_{\text{queries}} = 1_{\text{root}} + 1_{\text{.in}} + 1_{\text{ac.in}} + 1_{\text{ktu.ac.in}} = 4 \text{ iterative queries}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A typical 14-mark question asks "Trace the queries when a host resolves `www.example.com` for the first time" — you **must** count **every** query and **name every server** visited.

## 3.4 Derivation: DNS Cache Hit Probability (Birthday Analysis)

If a recursive resolver serves $N$ distinct users from a population of $M$ domains, the cache hit probability follows an **LRU approximation**:

$$
P_{\text{hit}}(t) \approx 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{M}\right)^{N \cdot f \cdot t}
$$

where $f$ = average query rate per user, $t$ = time window. With $M = 10^9$ domains and $N \cdot f \cdot t = 10^6$ queries, we get:

$$
P_{\text{hit}} \approx 1 - e^{-10^{-3}} \approx 0.001 \; (0.1\%)
$$

showing that **the cache hits are dominated by a small set of "hot" domains** (Pareto distribution).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Recursive vs. Iterative Resolution

| Parameter | Recursive Query | Iterative Query |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Responsibility** | Server does all the work | Server returns best answer or referral |
| **Server Load** | High (must traverse all levels) | Low (one referral per query) |
| **Client Complexity** | Low (single ask) | High (must re-issue queries) |
| **Bandwidth at Root** | Reduced (root server answers only once per name) | Increased (each client re-traverses) |
| **Caching Granularity** | Per-resolver, persistent | Per-resolver, persistent |
| **Failure Visibility** | Server reports final error | Client sees each step fail |
| **Used by** | Stub resolvers ↔ Local resolver | Local resolver ↔ Root/TLD/Authoritative |
| **KDU board favourite** | **Often confused — remember this:** Recursive = "Do the whole job"; Iterative = "Best effort". |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: DNS over UDP vs TCP

| Aspect | UDP (Port 53) | TCP (Port 53) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Connection** | Connectionless | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) |
| **Latency** | **Low** (1 RTT) | Higher (3-way handshake adds latency) |
| **Max Payload** | 512 bytes (RFC 1035); up to **4096 bytes with EDNS0** | Unlimited (theoretically 64 KB) |
| **Used When** | Standard A/AAAA queries, small responses | Zone transfers (`AXFR`), large `DNSSEC`/ `TXT` responses, any response > UDP cap |
| **Reliability** | Application must handle loss/retry | TCP handles retransmission |
| **DNS Cookies / DoT** | Typically DoT uses TCP 853; DoQ uses UDP 8853 | — |
| **Resource Cost** | Minimal (stateless) | Connection-state, buffers, TIME_WAIT |

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Common Attack Surfaces on DNS

| Attack | Mechanism | Defence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cache Poisoning** | Forging a UDP response with the correct ID/port | Randomize transaction ID + source port; **DNSSEC** |
| **DDoS on Authoritative** | Flood UDP/53 to take down a zone | **Anycast**, scrubbing centers, BCP 38 ingress filtering |
| **NXDOMAIN Flood** | Queries for non-existent names saturate resolvers | Negative caching, rate limiting |
| **DNS Amplification** | Small query → large response via spoofed IP source | BCP 38, RRL (Response Rate Limiting) |
| **Domain Hijacking** | Changing NS records at registrar | Registry lock, registrar 2FA |
| **Tunneling** | Encoding data in TXT/NULL records | DLP, anomaly detection |

## 3.8 Worked Example: Full DNS Resolution Trace

**Question:** Trace the resolution of `mail.google.com` from a clean cache. State every query, the server contacted, and the response type.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} &\;\; \text{Host} \rightarrow \text{Local Resolver} : \text{Recursive query for ``mail.google.com"} \\
\text{Step 2:} &\;\; \text{Local Resolver} \rightarrow \text{Root Server (a.root-servers.net)} : \text{Iterative query} \\
&\;\; \text{Response: Referral to .com TLD servers (NS records)} \\
\text{Step 3:} &\;\; \text{Local Resolver} \rightarrow \text{.com TLD Server (e.g., a.gtld-servers.net)} : \text{Iterative query} \\
&\;\; \text{Response: Referral to google.com authoritative servers (NS records)} \\
\text{Step 4:} &\;\; \text{Local Resolver} \rightarrow \text{google.com Authoritative Server (ns1.google.com)} : \text{Iterative query} \\
&\;\; \text{Response: A record (e.g., 142.250.195.69)} \\
\text{Step 5:} &\;\; \text{Local Resolver caches with TTL, then returns 142.250.195.69 to host.} \\
\text{Total messages on the wire} &= 6 \;(\text{3 queries} + 3 \text{ responses}) \\
\text{Host-to-Resolver messages} &= 2 \;(\text{recursive query + final answer}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchical DNS Namespace (Tree Structure)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1[Root Namespace - 13 Logical Root Servers]
    tldA[.com TLD]
    tldB[.org TLD]
    tldC[.net TLD]
    tldD[.in Country TLD]
    sec1[example.com - Authoritative]
    sec2[google.com - Authoritative]
    sec3[ac.in - Authoritative]
    ktuSec[ktu.ac.in - Authoritative]
    rec1[www.example.com A 93.184.216.34]
    rec2[mail.example.com MX 10 mail]
    rec3[www.google.com A 142.250.195.68]
    rec4[www.ktu.ac.in A 103.21.244.10]

    root1 --> tldA
    root1 --> tldB
    root1 --> tldC
    root1 --> tldD
    tldA --> sec1
    tldA --> sec2
    tldD --> sec3
    sec3 --> ktuSec
    sec1 --> rec1
    sec1 --> rec2
    sec2 --> rec3
    ktuSec --> rec4
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Iterative vs. Recursive Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[Host - Stub Resolver]
    localR[Local Recursive Resolver - ISP]
    rootS[Root Server]
    tldS[TLD Server .com]
    authS[Authoritative Server example.com]

    userA -->|1. Recursive Query mail.example.com| localR
    localR -->|2. Iterative Query| rootS
    rootS -->|3. Referral to .com TLD NS| localR
    localR -->|4. Iterative Query| tldS
    tldS -->|5. Referral to example.com NS| localR
    localR -->|6. Iterative Query| authS
    authS -->|7. Final A Record 93.184.216.34| localR
    localR -->|8. Final Answer with TTL 3600| userA
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: DNS Message Format Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    msgH[DNS Message - Total Variable Length]
    hdr[Header - 12 Bytes Fixed]
    qsec[Question Section]
    ans[Answer Section]
    auth[Authority Section]
    add[Additional Section]

    hdr --> h1[ID - 16 bits]
    hdr --> h2[FLAGS - 16 bits]
    hdr --> h3[QDCOUNT - 16 bits]
    hdr --> h4[ANCOUNT - 16 bits]
    hdr --> h5[NSCOUNT - 16 bits]
    hdr --> h6[ARCOUNT - 16 bits]

    qsec --> q1[QNAME - Variable Length Labels]
    qsec --> q2[QTYPE - 16 bits]
    qsec --> q3[QCLASS - 16 bits]

    ans --> r1[NAME - Variable with Compression]
    ans --> r2[TYPE - 16 bits]
    ans --> r3[CLASS - 16 bits]
    ans --> r4[TTL - 32 bits]
    ans --> r5[RDLENGTH - 16 bits]
    ans --> r6[RDATA - Variable]

    msgH --> hdr
    msgH --> qsec
    msgH --> ans
    msgH --> auth
    msgH --> add
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of DNS Query Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blkA[Application Layer - Browser Mail Client]
    blkB[OS Stub Resolver - getaddrinfo]
    blkC[Local Recursive Resolver - Bind Unbound]
    blkD[Root Server Cluster]
    blkE[TLD Server Cluster]
    blkF[Authoritative Server]
    blkG[Cache Storage - LRU TTL Tree]

    blkA -->|SOCKADDR Lookup| blkB
    blkB -->|UDP 53 Recursive Query| blkC
    blkC -->|Cache Lookup| blkG
    blkG -->|Cache Miss Trigger| blkC
    blkC -->|UDP 53 Iterative Query| blkD
    blkD -->|NS Referral| blkC
    blkC -->|UDP 53 Iterative Query| blkE
    blkE -->|NS Referral| blkC
    blkC -->|UDP 53 Iterative Query| blkF
    blkF -->|A Record Answer| blkC
    blkC -->|Write with TTL| blkG
    blkC -->|Final Answer| blkB
    blkB -->|IP Return| blkA
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Caching Flow with TTL Expiry

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> QueryReceived
    QueryReceived --> CacheLookup
    CacheLookup --> CacheHit: Entry Exists and TTL Greater Than 0
    CacheLookup --> CacheMiss: No Entry or TTL Expired
    CacheHit --> ReturnCached: Return Cached Answer to Client
    ReturnCached --> [*]
    CacheMiss --> SendIterativeQueries: Query Root TLD Authoritative
    SendIterativeQueries --> ReceiveAnswer: Receive Authoritative Response
    ReceiveAnswer --> StoreCache: Store Record with TTL Value
    StoreCache --> ReturnAnswer: Return Answer to Client
    ReturnAnswer --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the role of the Domain Name System (DNS) in the Internet. Why is it described as a *distributed* and *hierarchical* database?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Keywords to use for full marks:** *application layer, distributed, hierarchical, namespace, name-to-address, UDP port 53, redundancy, scalability*.

DNS is an **application-layer protocol** that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses (and vice-versa). It is called *distributed* because no single server holds the entire mapping table; instead, the namespace is **delegated** across millions of independent servers worldwide. It is *hierarchical* because it follows a strict tree structure — **Root → TLD → Second-Level → Sub-domain**, where each lower level is delegated by its parent. This design provides **scalability** (billions of names), **fault-tolerance** (anycast root servers), and **administrative autonomy** (each zone owner controls their records).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between an **Authoritative DNS Server** and a **Recursive Resolver**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Authoritative DNS Server | Recursive Resolver |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Role** | Holds the *original* DNS records for a zone | Performs lookups on behalf of clients |
| **Response** | Returns *authoritative* answers (AA flag set) | Returns cached or fetched answers |
| **Query type received** | Only **iterative** | Receives **recursive** queries, issues iterative queries downstream |
| **Example** | `ns1.google.com` | `8.8.8.8`, ISP resolver |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **iterative DNS query** process when a host resolves `www.example.com` for the first time. Assume no caching. List every message exchanged and identify each server visited. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Browser/Application Layer (Host H):** Host H sends a **recursive** DNS query to its configured local resolver (e.g., ISP at `10.0.0.1`). Query: *What is the A record of `www.example.com`?*

**Step 2 — Local Resolver → Root Server:** Local resolver does not know, so it queries one of the 13 root servers (e.g., `a.root-servers.net`) with an **iterative** query. The root server does **not** resolve the name; it returns a **referral** containing the NS records of the `.com` TLD servers.

**Step 3 — Local Resolver → `.com` TLD Server:** The resolver contacts a `.com` TLD server (e.g., `a.gtld-servers.net`). It also returns a **referral** with NS records of `example.com`'s authoritative servers.

**Step 4 — Local Resolver → Authoritative Server:** The resolver contacts `ns1.example.com` (authoritative). This server returns the **A record** for `www.example.com` (e.g., `93.184.216.34`).

**Step 5 — Cache and Reply:** The resolver caches the record with its TTL (e.g., 86400 s), then sends the answer back to Host H.

**Diagram (must draw):**

```
H --(Recursive)--> LocalR --(Iter)--> Root --(Referral)--> LocalR
                                                |
                                                v
                LocalR <--(Iter)-- TLD (.com) --(Referral)
                                                |
                                                v
                LocalR <--(Iter)-- Auth (ns1.example.com) --(A Record)
                                                |
                                                v
                                          H receives IP
```

**Total messages = 6** (3 queries + 3 responses). **Total server visits = 3** (Root, TLD, Authoritative).

---

**(b)** Describe the **DNS message header** format in detail. Explain the function of each field and the role of the **QR, AA, TC, RD, RA, and RCODE** flags. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The DNS header is a **fixed 12-byte** structure preceding the Question and Answer sections.

| Field | Size | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| `ID` | 16 bits | Random transaction identifier; the response must echo this ID. |
| `QR` | 1 bit | **0 = Query, 1 = Response**. |
| `Opcode` | 4 bits | Defines query kind: 0 = standard query, 1 = inverse, 2 = server status. |
| `AA` | 1 bit | **Authoritative Answer** — set if the responding server owns the zone. |
| `TC` | 1 bit | **Truncation** — set when the message exceeds the UDP limit (512 bytes). |
| `RD` | 1 bit | **Recursion Desired** — set by client to request recursive resolution. |
| `RA` | 1 bit | **Recursion Available** — set by server if it supports recursion. |
| `Z` | 3 bits | Reserved (must be 0); used by DNSSEC for extended flags. |
| `RCODE` | 4 bits | **Response Code** — 0 = No error, 1 = Format error, 2 = Server failure, 3 = NXDOMAIN, 4 = Not implemented, 5 = Refused. |
| `QDCOUNT` | 16 bits | Number of entries in the Question section. |
| `ANCOUNT` | 16 bits | Number of resource records in the Answer section. |
| `NSCOUNT` | 16 bits | Number of NS records in the Authority section. |
| `ARCOUNT` | 16 bits | Number of records in the Additional section. |

**Example header byte sequence for a query:** `12 34 01 00 00 01 00 00 00 00 00 00`
- ID = `0x1234`, QR=0, Opcode=0, AA=0, TC=0, RD=1, RA=0, Z=0, RCODE=0, QDCOUNT=1.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

**(a)** Explain the **DNS namespace hierarchy** with a suitable diagram. How are **zones** administered, and what is **zone delegation**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The DNS namespace is an **inverted tree** with the unnamed root `.` at the top. Below it are:

- **Root Zone** — managed by IANA; contains TLD NS records.
- **TLD Zone** — `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, country codes `.in`, `.uk`.
- **Second-Level Domains** — `example.com`, `google.com`, `ktu.ac.in`.
- **Sub-Domains** — `mail.example.com`, `research.ktu.ac.in`.

**Zone Delegation** is the process by which a parent zone passes administrative authority for a sub-domain to a separate set of name servers. For example, the `.com` zone delegates `example.com` by publishing `NS` records pointing to `ns1.example.com`. The parent zone retains only the glue records (A records for the NS hostnames to break circular dependency). This delegation allows **distributed administration** — each organization controls its own records without needing centralized coordination.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Diagram 4.1 in Section 4)*.

---

**(b)** Discuss **DNS caching and TTL**. How does caching improve performance? What happens during TTL expiry? Also explain **negative caching** with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TTL (Time-To-Live)** is a 32-bit field (in seconds) attached to every DNS resource record. It defines the maximum duration a record may be retained in any resolver's cache.

**Caching improves performance by:**

1. **Reducing latency** — subsequent queries are answered from RAM, avoiding network round trips.
2. **Lowering authoritative server load** — popular domains like `google.com` may be queried millions of times per second; without caching, the authoritative server would collapse.
3. **Reducing cross-Internet traffic** — local answers eliminate upstream queries.

**TTL Expiry Behaviour:**

- When TTL reaches 0, the record is marked **stale**.
- Many resolvers (e.g., BIND, Unbound) serve the stale record for a short grace period while a fresh query is initiated in the background.
- After the grace period, the record is **purged** and the next query triggers a full upstream lookup.

**Negative Caching:**
When a query returns `NXDOMAIN` (name does not exist) or `NODATA` (name exists but record type missing), resolvers cache this **negative** answer with a special **SOA MINIMUM** field (typically 300–3600 s). This prevents repeated futile queries for non-existent names.

**Example:** Querying `nosuchhost.invalid` returns NXDOMAIN. The resolver caches *"nosuchhost.invalid does not exist"* for 3600 s. During that hour, all subsequent queries for that name are answered locally with NXDOMAIN, saving upstream bandwidth.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marking deductions in DNS questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cache layer** — Many students draw only Root → TLD → Authoritative and skip the **Local Resolver**, losing 2 marks. The local resolver is **always** the first hop from the host.
> 2. **Confusing recursive and iterative** — A common mistake is showing the host sending iterative queries directly to the root. The host sends **only recursive** queries; only the resolver uses iterative.
> 3. **Skipping the `.` (root label)** in wire-format questions — DNS names on the wire **always** end with a zero-length label representing the root. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Port number** — DNS uses **port 53** for both UDP and TCP. Writing 80 or 25 is an instant loss.
> 5. **Header size** — Students often state "DNS header is 16 bytes" — it is exactly **12 bytes**.
> 6. **Not mentioning UDP vs TCP choice** — When asked about transport, always justify: *"DNS primarily uses UDP for low latency, but switches to TCP for responses > 512 bytes or for zone transfers."*
> 7. **Confusing authoritative vs recursive resolver** — These are **different roles**, possibly co-located on the same server but conceptually distinct. Always use the correct terminology.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DNS (Module 4)**

- ✅ DNS = **Application-layer**, **distributed**, **hierarchical** name-resolution system.
- ✅ Primary function: **Domain Name ↔ IP Address** translation.
- ✅ Uses **UDP port 53** (default), falls back to **TCP port 53** for large responses.
- ✅ Header is **fixed 12 bytes**: ID, FLAGS, QDCOUNT, ANCOUNT, NSCOUNT, ARCOUNT.
- ✅ **Recursive query**: server does all the work (used by stub resolvers).
- ✅ **Iterative query**: server returns best answer or referral (used between resolvers and authoritative servers).
- ✅ Hierarchy: **Root (13 logical) → TLD → Authoritative → Local Resolver → Stub Resolver → Host**.
- ✅ Common RRs: **A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, SOA, PTR, TXT, SRV, CAA**.
- ✅ **TTL** in seconds (0 to 2³¹−1); governs cache lifetime.
- ✅ **Negative caching** stores NXDOMAIN/NODATA with SOA MINIMUM TTL.
- ✅ Standard UDP payload = **512 bytes**; **EDNS0** extends to 4096 (or up to 65535).
- ✅ Domain name length ≤ **253** characters; label ≤ **63** characters.
- ✅ Wire format uses **length-prefixed labels** terminated by `0x00`; **compression pointers** (top 2 bits = `11`) reuse previous labels.
- ✅ **DNSSEC** adds cryptographic signatures (chain of trust from root) to prevent cache poisoning.
- ✅ **Anycast** root servers (1,500+ instances globally) provide DDoS resilience.
- ✅ DNS supports **load balancing** (round-robin A records), **geo-routing**, and **service discovery** (SRV records).
- ✅ Common attacks: **cache poisoning**, **DDoS amplification**, **NXDOMAIN flooding**, **DNS tunneling**.
- ✅ **CNAME chain limit**: 16 hops (RFC 1035) to prevent infinite loops.
- ✅ **Reverse DNS** uses the special domain `in-addr.arpa` (IPv4) / `ip6.arpa` (IPv6) with PTR records.
- ✅ **SOA record** contains primary NS, admin email, serial, refresh, retry, expire, and minimum TTL.
- ✅ **Zone transfer** (`AXFR` over TCP) replicates zones between primary and secondary servers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Mail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electronic Mail ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Electronic Mail > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electronic Mail (E-mail)** is an asynchronous, store-and-forward communication service defined by a suite of Internet protocols that allow a user to compose, transmit, receive, and store textual and multimedia messages across a TCP/IP network using standardized message formats and addressing schemes. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, Electronic Mail encompasses **four foundational pillars**: | Pillar | Protocol | Default Port | Transport | |--------|----------|--------------|-----------| | **Sending** | SMTP | **25**, 587 (Submission) | TCP | | **Retrieval** | POP3 | **110** | TCP | | **Remote Mgmt** | IMAP4 | **143** | TCP | | **Multimedia Extension** | MIME | — | Application Layer Wrapper | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Model > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Think of e-mail exactly like the traditional **India Post / Royal Mail system**: > - **Sender (You)** writes a letter and drops it in a **Mailbox** → Your **User Agent (UA)** like Outlook/Gmail. > - The letter travels through **Sorting Offices** → **Message Transfer Agents (MTAs)** running SMTP. > - The postman delivers it to the **Recipient's P.O. Box** → **Mail Server** mailbox file. > - The recipient uses a **key** to open the P.O. Box → **POP3/IMAP** authentication. > - **POP3** = Collect all letters and take them home (download-and-delete). > - **IMAP** = Read letters directly at the P.O. Box (server-side sync). ### 1.3 Core Architecture Components > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Logical Building Blocks of the E-mail System (RFC 5321 / Forouzen Ch. 18):** > > 1. **User Agent (UA)** — The mail client (e.g., Mozilla Thunderbird, MS Outlook). It is the *mail composer + mail reader*. > 2. **Message Transfer Agent (MTA)** — The mail server (e.g., Postfix, Sendmail, Exim). It is the *postman* that relays mail using **SMTP**. > 3. **Message Access Agent (MAA)** — The retrieval server (e.g., Dovecot for POP3/IMAP). It is the *mailbox keeper*. **Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold for Emphasis):** - SMTP operates on **TCP port 25** (MTA-to-MTA) and **port 587** (Submission by UA). - POP3 default port is **110**, IMAP4 default port is **143**. - Maximum practical RFC 5322 line length: **998 characters**. - MIME multipart boundaries are preceded by **two hyphens (`--`)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 SMTP — Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (RFC 5321) SMTP is a **command-response, text-based, push protocol** that uses **TCP on port 25** to transfer mail reliably between MTAs. It follows a strict request-reply dialogue using **ASCII commands** and **3-digit numeric reply codes**. > [!NOTE] > **SMTP Operational Phases (Forouzen / Kurose-Ross Model):** > > 1. **Connection Establishment** — Sender MTA opens TCP connection to Receiver MTA on port 25. > 2. **Handshaking (HELO / EHLO)** — Sender identifies itself; receiver announces capabilities via reply code **250**. > 3. **Mail Transaction** — `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA` commands are exchanged. > 4. **Termination** — `QUIT` command terminates the session gracefully (reply **221**). ### 2.2 POP3 — Post Office Protocol Version 3 (RFC 1939) POP3 is a **simple, stateless, download-and-delete (or download-and-keep)** retrieval protocol. It operates in **three states**: | State | Command Mode | Purpose | |-------|--------------|---------| | **Authorization** | After TCP connect | User sends `USER` and `PASS` | | **Transaction** | Authenticated | `LIST`, `RETR`, `DELE`, `QUIT` | | **Update** | After `QUIT` | Server deletes marked messages | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key POP3 Limitation:** It does **not** support folder management on the server. All mail is typically moved to a single local inbox folder — this is why **IMAP** was developed for modern multi-device synchronization. ### 2.3 IMAP4 — Internet Message Access Protocol (RFC 3501) IMAP is a **stateful, online, server-side manipulation protocol**. Unlike POP3, it allows: - **Selective downloading** of message parts (e.g., headers first). - **Server-side folder creation** (e.g., `INBOX.Work`, `INBOX.Personal`). - **Synchronization across multiple devices** (laptop ↔ phone ↔ webmail). - **Partial fetch** of large attachments using `FETCH BODY[HEADER]` or partial byte-range. ### 2.4 MIME — Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions (RFC 2045–2049) The original RFC 822 mail format supports only **7-bit ASCII text**. MIME extends this to support: - Non-text bodies (images, audio, video, PDFs). - Multiple body parts within a single message. - Non-ASCII character sets. **Five New Header Fields introduced by MIME:** | Header Field | Function | Example | |--------------|----------|---------| | `MIME-Version` | Identifies MIME version | `1.0` | | `Content-Type` | Nature of body | `text/plain`, `image/jpeg`, `multipart/mixed` | | `Content-Transfer-Encoding` | Encoding method | `7bit`, `8bit`, `base64`, `quoted-printable` | | `Content-ID` | Unique identifier | `` | | `Content-Description` | Human-readable info | `Diagram of SMTP flow` | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET — KTU Board Exam Perspective** | Concept | Standard / Value | Engineering Significance | |---------|------------------|--------------------------| | SMTP default port | **TCP 25** | MTA-to-MTA push transfer | | SMTP Submission port | **TCP 587** | UA-to-MSA submission | | POP3 default port | **TCP 110** | Mailbox retrieval | | IMAP4 default port | **TCP 143** | Remote mailbox manipulation | | HTTP default port | **TCP 80** | Comparison (Kurose exercises) | | Base64 output size | $\lceil \frac{n}{3} \rceil \times 4$ chars | Encodes $n$ input bytes | | Base64 expansion ratio | **4 : 3** (≈ **33.33%** overhead) | Binary → ASCII transmission | | Base64 alphabet size | $2^6 = \mathbf{64}$ symbols | 6 bits per printable char | | Quoted-printable | Encodes 1 byte → 3 bytes `=XX` | Used for mostly-text content | | RFC 822 max line length | **998 characters** | Line-folding rule | | SMTP reply code structure | **XYZ** (X: class, YZ: detail) | `2xx` Success, `3xx` Info, `4xx` Transient, `5xx` Permanent | > [!NOTE] > **MIME Base64 Size Formula (Critical for Sub-questions):** > $$\text{Encoded Length} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{input}}}{3} \right\rceil \times 4 \text{ characters}$$ > where $N_{\text{input}}$ is the number of *input bytes*. Each 3 input bytes map to 4 output characters. The output is always padded with `=` so that its length is a multiple of 4. ### 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real-World Systems - **SMTP is the backbone of every enterprise mail server** (e.g., Microsoft Exchange, Zimbra, Postfix). - **MIME enables rich HTML emails** with embedded images and attachments used universally in Gmail/Outlook. - **IMAP vs POP3 choice** impacts cloud-mailbox design: Gmail chose IMAP-like sync; legacy Outlook Express defaulted to POP3. - **Base64 encoding** in MIME is also reused outside mail — in **OAuth tokens, JSON Web Tokens (JWT), and cryptographic certificates**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Base64 Encoding Size for MIME Attachments > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A student receives an email with a PDF attachment of **1500 bytes**. When transmitted via MIME with **Base64 Content-Transfer-Encoding**, how many characters will the encoded body contain? **Step 1 — Identify the encoding rule.** Base64 processes input in groups of **3 bytes (24 bits)**. Each group produces **4 output characters** (since $4 \times 6 = 24$ bits). $$N_{\text{groups}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{input}}}{3} \right\rceil$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the input size $N_{\text{input}} = 1500$ bytes.** $$\begin{aligned} N_{\text{groups}} &= \left\lceil \frac{1500}{3} \right\rceil \\ &= \left\lceil 500.000 \right\rceil \\ &= 500 \text{ groups} \end{aligned}$$ This is an **exact multiple** of 3, so no padding is needed and no `=` characters will appear at the end of the encoded stream. **Step 3 — Compute the encoded character count.** Each group yields 4 characters: $$\begin{aligned} L_{\text{encoded}} &= N_{\text{groups}} \times 4 \\ &= 500 \times 4 \\ &= 2000 \text{ characters} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Express the overhead (expansion ratio).** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Overhead \%} &= \frac{L_{\text{encoded}} - N_{\text{input}}}{N_{\text{input}}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{2000 - 1500}{1500} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{500}{1500} \times 100 \\ &= 33.33\% \text{ expansion} \end{aligned}$$ This matches the universal **4 : 3 expansion ratio** of Base64. **Step 5 — Alternate verification via the master formula.** $$L_{\text{encoded}} = \left\lceil \frac{1500}{3} \right\rceil \times 4 = 500 \times 4 = \mathbf{2000 \text{ characters}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** If $N_{\text{input}} \mod 3 = 1$, then **2** `=` padding chars are appended. If $N_{\text{input}} \mod 3 = 2$, then **1** `=` padding char is appended. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Quoted-Printable Encoding for Non-ASCII Characters Suppose the subject contains `"café = ₹100"` which contains non-ASCII bytes. **Step 1 — Identify characters that need encoding.** Characters with ASCII code > 127 (or `=`, or line endings) are replaced by `=XX` where `XX` is the hex value. **Step 2 — Encode each problematic byte.** | Character | ASCII | Hex | Encoded Form | |-----------|-------|-----|--------------| | `é` | 233 | `E9` | `=E9` | | `₹` | 8377 (UTF-8: `E2 82 B9`) | — | `=E2=82=B9` | **Step 3 — Final encoded subject line.** ``` caf=C3=A9 =3D =E2=82=B9100 ``` > [!NOTE] > Quoted-printable is **human-readable for debugging** but adds **300% overhead** for non-ASCII bytes, while Base64 adds a flat **33.33%** overhead regardless of content. This is why Base64 is preferred for binary attachments. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SMTP vs POP3 vs IMAP | Parameter | SMTP | POP3 | IMAP4 | |-----------|------|------|-------| | **Direction** | Push (Send) | Pull (Retrieve) | Pull + Sync | | **TCP Port** | **25 / 587** | **110** | **143** | | **Statefulness** | Stateful session | Stateless per session | **Highly stateful** | | **Server Folder Support** | No (relay only) | **No** | **Yes** | | **Partial Download** | N/A | No (full msg only) | **Yes (RFC 3501 FETCH)** | | **Online/Offline** | Online | Offline-after-download | Online / Offline sync | | **RFC** | 5321 | 1939 | 3501 | | **Use Case** | Server relay | Single-device legacy | Multi-device modern | | **Connection Persistence** | One per session | One per session | **Idle (push) support** | --- ### 3.4 SMTP Mail Flow — Exhaustive Step Trace > [!IMPORTANT] > **Scenario:** `alice@ktu.edu` sends mail to `bob@cet.ac.in` ``` S: 220 cet.ac.in SMTP ready C: EHLO ktu.edu S: 250-cet.ac.in Hello ktu.edu S: 250-SIZE 10240000 S: 250 AUTH LOGIN PLAIN C: MAIL FROM: S: 250 OK C: RCPT TO: S: 250 OK C: DATA S: 354 Start mail input; end with . C: From: Alice C: To: Bob C: Subject: KTU Exam C: C: Hello Bob, see you at 10 AM. C: . S: 250 OK Message accepted C: QUIT S: 221 Bye ``` > [!NOTE] > Every server response starts with a **3-digit reply code** followed by descriptive text. The leading digit indicates the response class. --- ### 3.5 SMTP Reply Code Reference (KTU Exam Favorite) | Code | Class | Meaning | |------|-------|---------| | **211** | 2xx | System status | | **214** | 2xx | Help message | | **220** | 2xx | Service ready | | **221** | 2xx | Service closing | | **250** | 2xx | Requested mail action OK | | **354** | 3xx | Start mail input | | **421** | 4xx | Service not available | | **450** | 4xx | Mailbox unavailable | | **500** | 5xx | Syntax error | | **550** | 5xx | Mailbox not found / Access denied | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End E-mail Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Agent Alice
Outlook / Gmail Web] --> B[SMTP Submission
Port 587] B --> C[Outgoing MTA
ktu.edu mail server] C --> D[DNS MX Lookup
cet.ac.in] D --> E[Receiving MTA
cet.ac.in mail server] E --> F[Mailbox Storage
bob user account] F --> G[IMAP4 Service
Port 143] G --> H[User Agent Bob
Thunderbird Mobile Web] I[User Agent Bob
legacy POP3 client] --> J[POP3 Service
Port 110] J --> F K[Binary Attachment
PDF or Image] --> L[MIME Encoding
Base64 or QP] L --> A ``` ### 4.2 SMTP Command-Response Dialogue — Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client UA participant S as Server MTA C->>S: TCP SYN port 25 S-->>C: 220 cet.ac.in SMTP service ready C->>S: EHLO ktu.edu S-->>C: 250 OK capabilities listed C->>S: MAIL FROM: S-->>C: 250 Sender OK C->>S: RCPT TO: S-->>C: 250 Recipient OK C->>S: DATA S-->>C: 354 Send message end with CRLF dot CRLF C->>S: Headers and body CRLF dot CRLF S-->>C: 250 Message accepted for delivery C->>S: QUIT S-->>C: 221 Service closing transmission channel ``` ### 4.3 MIME Multipart Message Structure — Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB M[Root MIME Entity
Content-Type multipart mixed] --> P1[Part 1
Content-Type text plain
Encoding 7bit] M --> P2[Part 2
Content-Type image png
Encoding base64] M --> P3[Part 3
Content-Type application pdf
Encoding base64] B1[Boundary String xyz123] --- M B2[--xyz123 precedes each part] --- P1 B2 --- P2 B2 --- P3 B3[--xyz123-- closes multipart] --- M ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Push vs Pull Paradigm ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SEND_PIPELINE[SMTP Push Pipeline] SA[Sender UA] --> SB[Sender MSA Port 587] SB --> SC[Sender MTA] SC --> SD[Receiver MTA Port 25] end SD --> RE[Receiver Mailbox] subgraph RETRIEVE_PIPELINE[Pull Pipeline] RA[Recipient UA] --> RB[POP3 Port 110 OR IMAP Port 143] RB --> RE end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > Differentiate between POP3 and IMAP protocols. Which one is more suitable for users accessing mail from multiple devices and why? **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | POP3 | IMAP4 | |---------|------|-------| | Default Port | 110 | 143 | | Folder Support | No (single INBOX) | Yes (server-side folders) | | Partial Download | No | Yes | | State | Stateless | Stateful | | Best For | Single device legacy | Multi-device sync | **IMAP is more suitable for multi-device users** because it maintains mail and folder state on the server, allowing the same mailbox view across laptop, phone, and webmail simultaneously. POP3 downloads and typically deletes mail from the server, breaking multi-device consistency. **(3 marks)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** > What is MIME? List any **three** Content-Type values used in MIME headers. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)** is an extension to RFC 822 that allows email messages to contain non-text bodies, multi-part bodies, and non-ASCII character sets. Three common Content-Type values are: 1. `text/plain` 2. `image/jpeg` 3. `application/pdf` Each part of a MIME message carries its own Content-Type header identifying the nature of its body. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — SMTP Deep Dive** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** > **(a)** Explain the architecture of the electronic mail system with a neat diagram. List and explain the functions of User Agent, MTA, and MAA. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a suitable example, describe the SMTP protocol transaction showing the client–server command-response dialogue between two MTAs. State any **five** SMTP commands with their functions. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture of E-mail System (7 marks):** The e-mail system consists of three logical components: 1. **User Agent (UA)** — A software application used by the user to compose, read, send, and receive e-mail. Examples: Outlook, Gmail web, Thunderbird. It provides an MUA interface with Inbox, Outbox, Drafts, etc. *(1 mark)* 2. **Message Transfer Agent (MTA)** — A background daemon program that routes and relays e-mail between hosts using SMTP. Example: Postfix, Sendmail. MTAs communicate over **TCP port 25**. *(2 marks)* 3. **Message Access Agent (MAA)** — A server that allows users to retrieve mail from the server mailbox using POP3 (port 110) or IMAP (port 143). Example: Dovecot. *(2 marks)* **Flow:** `Sender UA → Sender MTA (SMTP) → Receiver MTA → Mailbox → Receiver MAA (POP3/IMAP) → Receiver UA` *(2 marks)* **(b) SMTP Transaction (7 marks):** SMTP is a text-based protocol. After TCP connection on port 25, the following exchange occurs (showing commands and reply codes): ``` S: 220 mail.cet.ac.in ESMTP ready C: EHLO mail.ktu.edu S: 250 mail.cet.ac.in Hello mail.ktu.edu C: MAIL FROM: S: 250 OK C: RCPT TO: S: 250 OK C: DATA S: 354 Enter mail, end with CRLF.CRLF C: [Headers + Body] C: . S: 250 OK Message queued C: QUIT S: 221 Bye ``` *(4 marks for dialogue trace)* **Five SMTP Commands:** | Command | Function | |---------|----------| | `HELO` / `EHLO` | Identify sender to receiver *(0.6 marks)* | | `MAIL FROM` | Start a mail transaction and specify sender *(0.6 marks)* | | `RCPT TO` | Specify one recipient *(0.6 marks)* | | `DATA` | Begin message body transmission *(0.6 marks)* | | `QUIT` | Terminate the SMTP session *(0.6 marks)* | **(Total: 14 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — MIME & Base64** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]** > **(a)** Explain the MIME header fields. Describe the structure of a `multipart/mixed` MIME message with an example showing one text part and one image attachment. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A user sends an e-mail with a **3000-byte** binary attachment using **Base64** Content-Transfer-Encoding. > (i) Calculate the size of the encoded body in characters. > (ii) If the same attachment were encoded using Quoted-Printable for purely binary bytes, what would be the encoded length assuming every byte is non-ASCII? Compare both encodings. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) MIME Headers (7 marks):** MIME introduces five new header fields. *(1 mark for list)* - **MIME-Version**: Declares MIME version (e.g., `1.0`). - **Content-Type**: Indicates media type — `text/plain`, `image/jpeg`, `multipart/mixed`, etc. - **Content-Transfer-Encoding**: Indicates encoding — `7bit`, `8bit`, `binary`, `quoted-printable`, `base64`. - **Content-ID**: Unique reference identifier for the part. - **Content-Description**: Plain-text description of the body. *(3 marks for the above)* **Example multipart/mixed structure:** ``` From: alice@ktu.edu To: bob@cet.ac.in MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary="Boundary42" --Boundary42 Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8" Hello Bob, find the photo attached. --Boundary42 Content-Type: image/jpeg Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64 /9j/4AAQSkZJRgABAQEASABIAAD... (base64 blob) --Boundary42-- ``` *(3 marks for structure + example)* **(b) Encoding Calculations (7 marks):** **(i) Base64 encoding for 3000 bytes:** $$\begin{aligned} N_{\text{groups}} &= \left\lceil \frac{3000}{3} \right\rceil = 1000 \text{ groups} \\ L_{\text{base64}} &= 1000 \times 4 = \mathbf{4000 \text{ characters}} \end{aligned}$$ Since $3000 \mod 3 = 0$, no `=` padding is appended. *(2 marks)* **(ii) Quoted-Printable encoding (worst case — every byte is non-ASCII):** In Quoted-Printable, each non-ASCII byte is represented as `=XX` (3 ASCII characters). $$L_{\text{QP}} = N_{\text{input}} \times 3 = 3000 \times 3 = \mathbf{9000 \text{ characters}}$$ *(2 marks)* **Comparison:** | Encoding | Output Length | Overhead | Best For | |----------|---------------|----------|----------| | Base64 | **4000 chars** | 33.33% | Binary attachments | | Quoted-Printable | **9000 chars** | 200% | Mostly-text content | *(3 marks)* **Conclusion:** Base64 is significantly more efficient for binary attachments and is therefore the de-facto standard for transferring images, PDFs, and executables via e-mail. Quoted-Printable is preferred only when the body is mostly readable text with a few non-ASCII characters. **(Total: 14 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing port numbers** — Students frequently write SMTP as port 110 (that's POP3). Memorize: **S=25, P=110, I=143**. Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** per wrong port. > > 2. **Writing "SMTP is used to receive mail"** — SMTP is a **push** protocol for *sending*. POP3/IMAP are pull protocols for *retrieving*. > > 3. **Forgetting the `.` terminator** in SMTP `DATA` command — the message body must end with **`.`**. Missing this loses the trace question marks. > > 4. **Wrong Base64 formula** — Students often multiply by 8 or 6 instead of 4. The correct multiplier is **always 4 characters per 3 input bytes**. > > 5. **Not labelling the diagram axes / arrows** — KTU examiners explicitly award **2 marks** for a *correctly labelled* diagram in 14-mark questions. A plain box-and-arrow without labels = **0/2**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Electronic Mail** - ✅ **SMTP** = Send, **Port 25**, TCP-based, push protocol, command-response ASCII. - ✅ **POP3** = Retrieve, **Port 110**, stateless, download-and-delete, single device. - ✅ **IMAP4** = Retrieve + Manage, **Port 143**, stateful, server-side folders, multi-device. - ✅ **MIME** extends RFC 822 to support binary and non-ASCII content via **5 new headers**. - ✅ **Base64 expansion ratio is always 4 : 3 = 33.33%** overhead, regardless of input. - ✅ **Quoted-Printable** encodes each non-ASCII byte as `=XX` → **3× overhead**, ideal for mostly-text bodies. - ✅ **SMTP dialogue terminates with `QUIT` and reply code `221`**. - ✅ **Three-component architecture**: User Agent + Message Transfer Agent + Message Access Agent. - ✅ **SMTP commands**: `HELO/EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `QUIT`, `RSET`, `NOOP`, `VRFY`. - ✅ **Reply code prefixes**: `2xx` success, `3xx` intermediate, `4xx` transient failure, `5xx` permanent failure. - ✅ **Default port for SMTP Submission (UA → MSA)** is **587**, often required for authenticated submission. - ✅ **DNS MX records** are queried by MTAs to locate the receiving mail server for a domain. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Ports — "Simple Postman Internet" → S=25, P=110, I=143**

topics.title: Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension (MIME)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension (MIME)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension (MIME)** is an Internet standard (originally defined in **RFC 2045, RFC 2046, and RFC 2049**, later refined in **RFC 2046** and **RFC 4288/4289**) that extends the limited format of email messages defined by **RFC 822 / RFC 5322** to support text in character sets other than **US-ASCII**, non-text attachments (audio, video, images, application programs), and multiple body parts within a single message.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MIME is classified as an **Application Layer protocol enhancement** that operates on top of the **Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP)** and **POP3/IMAP** infrastructure to enable multimedia and internationalized electronic mail exchange over the **TCP/IP** suite.

## 1.2 The Problem MIME Solves (RFC 822 Limitations)

The original RFC 822 email standard was designed in an era when email contained only **English text encoded in 7-bit US-ASCII**. This created three major problems:

| Limitation | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| **Character Set Restriction** | Could only send 7-bit ASCII (0–127). Could not represent accented characters (é, ñ, ü), non-Latin scripts (Devanagari, Chinese, Arabic), or symbols. |
| **No Multimedia Support** | Could not carry binary data such as images (JPEG, PNG), audio (MP3), video (MP4), PDFs, or executables. |
| **Single-Body Restriction** | Could not send multiple distinct items (e.g., text + attachment) within a single message entity. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Postal Envelope Upgrade:**
>
> Imagine the **original RFC 822 email** as a **plain paper letter** that only accepts black ink and can only carry one single sheet of paper inside a standard envelope. Now imagine you want to send a **birthday greeting** that includes a *handwritten note*, a *photograph*, and an *audio recording*.
>
> Without MIME, this is impossible — the envelope literally does not have a defined format for photographs or audio.
>
> **MIME** is like an **upgraded, multi-compartment envelope system** that:
> - Includes a **label on the outside** (MIME headers) telling the receiver what is inside and how to handle it.
> - Uses an **encoding translator** (Content-Transfer-Encoding) that converts any binary content into a format the legacy postal system (SMTP) can carry.
> - Allows **compartments inside the envelope** (multipart structure) to bundle text, images, audio, and applications into one logical package.
>
> When the receiver opens the envelope, the labels tell them exactly how to decode each compartment back into its original form.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **RFC Number:** Originally **RFC 2045–2049 (1996)**; current revision **RFC 2046, RFC 4288, RFC 4289**.
- **Character Set Origin:** ASCII (**American Standard Code for Information Interchange**), **7-bit** per character.
- **Base64 Expansion Ratio:** Encoded size ≈ **1.33 × original** binary size (precisely **4 output bytes per 3 input bytes**).
- **Quoted-Printable Expansion:** Encoded size ≤ **3 × original** in worst case (every byte encoded as `=XX`).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five MIME Header Fields

MIME extends the existing RFC 822 header set by adding five new header fields. These are **mandatory for any MIME-compliant message**:

1. **MIME-Version**
2. **Content-Type**
3. **Content-Transfer-Encoding**
4. **Content-ID** *(optional)*
5. **Content-Description** *(optional)*

### 2.1.1 MIME-Version Header

```text
MIME-Version: 1.0
```

- Declares that the message conforms to **MIME version 1.0**.
- Currently the only defined version, but the header is mandatory to allow forward compatibility.

### 2.1.2 Content-Type Header

This is the **most important** MIME header. It specifies the **nature of the data** in the body and is structured as:

$$\text{Content-Type: } \underbrace{\text{type}}_{\text{discrete or composite}} \, / \, \underbrace{\text{subtype}}_{\text{specific format}}$$

**The seven standard discrete content types:**

| Type | Meaning | Example Subtypes |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **text** | Human-readable text | plain, html, richtext, xml |
| **image** | Still images | jpeg, png, gif, bmp |
| **audio** | Sound data | basic, mpeg, wav, ogg |
| **video** | Moving images / video | mpeg, mp4, quicktime |
| **application** | Other binary data / programs | octet-stream, pdf, zip, msword, json |
| **message** | Encapsulated message | rfc822, partial |
| **multipart** | Multiple body parts | mixed, alternative, parallel, digest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU University Exams, students are frequently asked to list the **7 standard content types**. Memorize them in the order above — this is a recurring 3-mark question.

### 2.1.3 Content-Transfer-Encoding Header

Specifies how the body has been encoded for safe transport over **7-bit SMTP**:

| Encoding | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| **7bit** | Raw 7-bit ASCII data (no encoding needed) |
| **8bit** | Allows 8-bit characters (used when SMTP extension supports it) |
| **binary** | Raw binary, but **NOT recommended** for SMTP transport |
| **quoted-printable** | Encodes 8-bit characters into 7-bit ASCII using `=XX` hex notation |
| **base64** | Encodes arbitrary binary into a 6-bit-to-character alphanumeric scheme |

### 2.1.4 Content-ID Header

A globally unique identifier for the MIME entity, used for **referencing parts** within other parts (e.g., embedding images inline within HTML emails).

### 2.1.5 Content-Description Header

A free-form human-readable description of the body part (e.g., `"Photo of class picnic 2024"`).

## 2.2 The Multipart Structure (Composite Type)

The `multipart` content type is what makes MIME truly powerful. A multipart message is a **single message containing multiple independent body parts**, each with its own set of headers.

**Structure declaration:**
```
Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary="--unique-boundary-1234"
```

Each part is separated by the **boundary delimiter** on its own line:
```text
--unique-boundary-1234
Content-Type: text/plain

This is the message body.

--unique-boundary-1234
Content-Type: image/jpeg
Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64

<base64-encoded image data>

--unique-boundary-1234--
```

**The four standard multipart subtypes:**

| Subtype | Use Case |
|---------|----------|
| **mixed** | Independent parts (e.g., text + attachment) |
| **alternative** | Same content in different formats (e.g., plain text + HTML) |
| **parallel** | Parts meant to be viewed simultaneously (e.g., audio + slideshow) |
| **digest** | Each part is itself a complete RFC 822 message |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept / Symbol | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|------------------|----------------|--------------|
| Base64 encoding expansion | $L_{\text{encoded}} = \left\lceil \frac{4 \cdot n}{3} \right\rceil$ | $n$ = input bytes; output is in 6-bit groups mapped to A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, / |
| Base64 output padding | $\text{PadChars} = (3 - (n \bmod 3)) \bmod 3$ | Number of `=` signs appended |
| Quoted-printable encoding | Each byte $\rightarrow$ `=XX` if $> 127$ | XX is uppercase hex of byte value |
| Quoted-printable line length | Max **76** characters per line | Soft line break uses `=\r\n` |
| ASCII range used by SMTP | **0 to 127** (7-bit) | CR=13, LF=10 |
| MIME body parts | $N_{\text{parts}} = N_{\text{boundaries}} - 1$ | Boundary count includes closing delimiter |
| Total encoded size (worst case QP) | $L_{\max}^{\text{QP}} \leq 3 \cdot n$ | Every byte becomes 3 chars |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> MIME's role extends **far beyond email**. The same encoding standards (`Content-Type` and `base64`) form the foundation of:
> - **HTTP** message bodies (`Content-Type: application/json` is MIME).
> - **REST API** request/response payloads.
> - **WebSocket** and **SOAP** message envelopes.
> - **MIME in browsers** for `data:` URIs and inline media.
>
> This is why every Computer Networks engineer must understand MIME — it is the **lingua franca** of Internet content negotiation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Base64 Encoding — Exhaustive Derivation

### 3.1.1 The Underlying Mathematical Principle

Base64 converts a stream of **8-bit bytes** into a stream of **6-bit values**, each of which is mapped to one of **64 printable ASCII characters**. The alphabet is:

$$\text{Alphabet} = \{A..Z\} \cup \{a..z\} \cup \{0..9\} \cup \{+, /\} \cup \{=\}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mid \text{Alphabet} \mid = 26 + 26 + 10 + 2 = 64$$

The output uses exactly **6 bits of information per character**, which justifies the name **Base64**.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Expansion Ratio

Let $n$ = number of input bytes (each 8 bits).

$$\text{Total input bits} = 8n$$

Since each output character carries **6 bits**:

$$\text{Total output characters} = \frac{8n}{6} = \frac{4n}{3}$$

Because output must be a whole number of characters, and because base64 processes input in **3-byte blocks**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Input bits per block} &= 3 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 24 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Output chars per block} &= \frac{24 \text{ bits}}{6 \text{ bits/char}} = 4 \text{ characters}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the expansion ratio is:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{4 \text{ output chars}}{3 \text{ input bytes}} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.3333$$

The general formula for total encoded length is:

$$L_{\text{out}} = \left\lceil \frac{4n}{3} \right\rceil$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Encode the ASCII string "Hi!" (3 bytes: H=0x48, i=0x69, !=0x21)**

**Step 1 — Convert each byte to binary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 0\text{x}48 = 01001000 \\
i &= 0\text{x}69 = 01101001 \\
! &= 0\text{x}21 = 00100001
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate into one 24-bit stream:**

$$01001000 \, 01101001 \, 00100001$$

**Step 3 — Split into four 6-bit groups:**

$$\underbrace{010010}_{18} \quad \underbrace{000110}_{6} \quad \underbrace{100100}_{36} \quad \underbrace{010001}_{17}$$

**Step 4 — Convert each 6-bit group to decimal:**

$$18, \quad 6, \quad 36, \quad 17$$

**Step 5 — Map each decimal to the Base64 alphabet using index:**

| Index | 0–25 | 26–51 | 52–61 | 62 | 63 |
|-------|------|-------|-------|----|----|
| Maps to | A–Z | a–z | 0–9 | + | / |

$$\begin{aligned}
18 &= S \\
6  &= G \\
36 &= k \\
17 &= R
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Result:**

$$\text{"Hi!"} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{"SGk!"}$$

### 3.1.4 Padding Rule Derivation

If input length $n$ is **not divisible by 3**, the last block has fewer than 24 bits, and we append padding `=` characters so the output length remains a multiple of 4:

$$\text{PadChars} = (3 - (n \bmod 3)) \bmod 3$$

| Input bytes $n$ | Mod 3 | Padding `=` |
|-----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 |

### 3.1.5 Verification Example — Encode "Hi" (2 bytes)

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 01001000 \\
i &= 01101001
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Concatenated: } 0100100001101001 \;(16 \text{ bits})$$

$$\text{Pad to 18 bits: } 0100100001101001\underbrace{00}_{\text{pad}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{6-bit groups} &= 010010 \mid 000110 \mid 100100 \\
\text{Decimal} &= 18, \, 6, \, 36 \\
\text{Base64 chars} &= S, \, G, \, k
\end{aligned}$$

Plus **one `=` padding** (since $n \bmod 3 = 2$):

$$\boxed{\text{"Hi"} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{"SGk="}}$$

## 3.2 Quoted-Printable Encoding — Derivation

### 3.2.1 Rule Set

A byte $b$ is encoded as follows:

$$\text{Encoded}(b) = \begin{cases} b & \text{if } 33 \leq b \leq 126 \text{ and } b \neq 61 \\ \text{"=XX"} & \text{if } b \leq 31 \text{ or } b \geq 127 \text{ or } b = 61 \end{cases}$$

where `XX` is the **two-digit uppercase hexadecimal** representation of $b$.

### 3.2.2 Verification Example — Encode "Café"

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 0\text{x}43 \rightarrow C \text{ (printable, kept as-is)} \\
a &= 0\text{x}61 \rightarrow a \text{ (printable, kept as-is)} \\
f &= 0\text{x}66 \rightarrow f \text{ (printable, kept as-is)} \\
\text{é} &= 0\text{x}C3 \, 0\text{x}A9 \;(\text{UTF-8}) 
\end{aligned}$$

Each byte $> 127$ is encoded:

$$\begin{aligned}
0\text{x}C3 &\rightarrow \text{"=C3"} \\
0\text{x}A9 &\rightarrow \text{"=A9"}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{"Café"} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{"Caf=C3=A9"}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Encoding Comparison

| Property | Base64 | Quoted-Printable |
|----------|--------|-------------------|
| Bits per output char | 6 | 8 (mostly), 24 (when encoded) |
| Worst-case expansion | $4/3 \approx 1.33\times$ | $3\times$ |
| Best-case expansion | $4/3 \approx 1.33\times$ | $1\times$ (for ASCII-only text) |
| Human readability | Low (alphanumeric soup) | High (ASCII text remains readable) |
| Preferred for | Binary attachments, images, audio | Mostly-textual data with occasional 8-bit chars |
| Typical use case | PDF, JPEG, ZIP, EXE files | Email body with occasional accented characters |

## 3.4 Multipart Boundary Counting

For a multipart message with $b$ boundary delimiter lines in the body (including the closing one), the number of body parts is:

$$N_{\text{parts}} = b - 1$$

**Example:** A multipart message contains the boundary string `--BOUND` three times in the body (twice as separators, once as the closing delimiter `--BOUND--`):

$$N_{\text{parts}} = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ body parts}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MIME Message — Overall Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer Email Client] --> B[MIME Entity Construction]
    B --> C{Content Type?}
    C -->|Text only| D1[Content-Type: text/plain]
    C -->|Text + Attachment| D2[Content-Type: multipart/mixed]
    C -->|Multiple Formats| D3[Content-Type: multipart/alternative]
    D1 --> E[Add MIME Headers]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Content-Transfer-Encoding Applied]
    F --> F1[7bit or quoted-printable for text]
    F --> F2[base64 for binary attachments]
    F1 --> G[Hand off to SMTP]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Transport over TCP Port 25]
    H --> I[Receiving Mail Server]
    I --> J[MIME Decoder]
    J --> K[Reconstruct Original Content]
    K --> L[Deliver to Recipient Inbox]
```

## 4.2 Multipart Message — Internal Block Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H[Message Header RFC 822] --> M1[MIME Headers]
    M1 --> M2[Content-Type multipart/mixed boundary=B1]
    M2 --> P1[Part 1 Headers Content-Type text/plain]
    P1 --> P1B[Part 1 Body Hello World]
    P1B --> B1[Boundary B1 Separator]
    B1 --> P2[Part 2 Headers Content-Type image/jpeg Content-Transfer-Encoding base64]
    P2 --> P2B[Part 2 Body Base64 Encoded Image]
    P2B --> B2[Closing Boundary B1 followed by double hyphen]
```

## 4.3 Base64 Encoding — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Binary Stream 8 bit per byte] --> G1[Read 3 bytes at a time]
    G1 --> G2[Concatenate into 24 bit block]
    G2 --> G3[Split into four 6 bit groups]
    G3 --> G4[Map each 6 bit value to Base64 Alphabet Index 0 to 63]
    G4 --> G5[Output 4 Base64 characters]
    G5 --> D1{Remaining input?}
    D1 -->|Yes| G1
    D1 -->|No, but last block short| P1[Pad with zero bits]
    P1 --> P2[Append equal signs to make length multiple of 4]
    P2 --> OUT[Final Encoded String]
    D1 -->|No, exact multiple| OUT
```

## 4.4 MIME in the TCP/IP Stack — Context Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        MUA[Mail User Agent MUA e.g. Outlook Gmail]
        MIME[MIME Encoding Decoding Engine]
        SMTP[SMTP Protocol RFC 5321]
    end
    subgraph TRANS[Transport Layer]
        TCP[TCP Reliable Stream Port 25]
    end
    subgraph NET[Network Layer]
        IP[IP Internet Protocol]
    end
    MUA --> MIME
    MIME --> SMTP
    SMTP --> TCP
    TCP --> IP
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Encoding Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Input Data] --> Q1{Is data 7 bit ASCII only?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, all printable| R1[Use 7bit encoding no transformation]
    Q1 -->|No, has occasional 8 bit chars| R2[Use quoted printable encoding]
    Q1 -->|No, fully binary| R3[Use base64 encoding]
    R1 --> END[Ready for SMTP transport]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: List and briefly explain any five MIME content types.**

**Model Answer:**

MIME defines the following content types:

1. **text** — Human-readable textual content. Subtypes include `plain`, `html`, `xml`, `richtext`.
2. **image** — Still graphical images. Subtypes include `jpeg`, `png`, `gif`, `bmp`.
3. **audio** — Sound or music data. Subtypes include `basic`, `mpeg`, `wav`.
4. **video** — Motion video data. Subtypes include `mpeg`, `mp4`, `quicktime`.
5. **application** — Other binary data such as executables, PDFs, ZIP files. Subtypes include `octet-stream`, `pdf`, `zip`, `json`.
6. **message** — An encapsulated mail message (e.g., `rfc822`, `partial`).
7. **multipart** — A composite body containing multiple parts (e.g., `mixed`, `alternative`, `parallel`, `digest`).

*(Any five with brief explanation — full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q: Differentiate between base64 and quoted-printable encoding in MIME.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Base64 | Quoted-Printable |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| **Encoding principle** | Converts every 3 bytes (24 bits) into 4 characters (6 bits each) | Replaces 8-bit or non-printable bytes with `=XX` hex |
| **Expansion ratio** | Always **4/3 ≈ 1.33×** | Up to **3×** in worst case, **1×** for pure ASCII |
| **Output characters** | A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, /, = | Mostly printable ASCII with `=XX` escapes |
| **Best suited for** | Binary data — images, PDFs, executables | Mostly text with occasional 8-bit characters |
| **Human readability** | Poor | High |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer either Question A OR Question B. Both carry equal marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the limitations of RFC 822 that led to the development of MIME. Describe the five MIME header fields with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the `multipart/mixed` content type. Show how a text message and a JPEG image can be combined in one MIME message. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Limitations of RFC 822:**

1. **ASCII-only restriction** — RFC 822 only supports 7-bit US-ASCII characters (0–127). It cannot transmit accented Latin characters, non-Latin scripts (Chinese, Arabic, Malayalam), or any symbol outside this range.
2. **No binary content** — Attachments such as images, audio, video, PDFs, and ZIP archives cannot be transmitted because they contain 8-bit data which would be corrupted by SMTP's 7-bit-clean transport.
3. **Single body restriction** — RFC 822 allows only one body per message. There is no mechanism to combine a text message with an attachment into one logical entity.
4. **No internationalization** — Email headers themselves could not contain non-ASCII text (subjects, names).

These limitations motivated **MIME (RFC 2045–2049)** to extend RFC 822 while remaining backward compatible.

**The Five MIME Header Fields:**

| Header | Example | Purpose |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **MIME-Version** | `MIME-Version: 1.0` | Declares MIME conformance version |
| **Content-Type** | `Content-Type: image/jpeg` | Identifies the type and subtype of body data |
| **Content-Transfer-Encoding** | `Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64` | Specifies how body has been encoded for transport |
| **Content-ID** | `Content-ID: <image1@company.com>` | Unique identifier for cross-referencing parts |
| **Content-Description** | `Content-Description: Photo of event` | Human-readable description of the body part |

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

The `multipart/mixed` subtype is used when a message contains **multiple independent body parts**, typically text plus one or more file attachments.

**Key structural requirements:**
- Top-level `Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary="<unique-string>"`
- Each part separated by `--<boundary>` on its own line
- Final boundary ends with `--<boundary>--`
- Each part has its **own headers** (Content-Type, optional Content-Transfer-Encoding)

**Example MIME message combining text and a JPEG image:**

```text
From: sender@ktu.edu
To: receiver@ktu.edu
Subject: Vacation Photos
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary="KTU_BOUND_2024"

--KTU_BOUND_2024
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="UTF-8"

Hi, please find the attached photo from our trip.

--KTU_BOUND_2024
Content-Type: image/jpeg
Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64
Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="beach.jpg"

/9j/4AAQSkZJRgABAQEASABIAAD/2wBDAAYEBQYFBAYGBQYHBwYIChAKCgkJChQODwwQF
xQYGBcUFhYaHSUfGhsjHBYWICwgIyYnKSopGR8tMC0oMCUoKSj/2wBDAQcHBwoIChMK
ChMoGhYaKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgoKCgo
KCgoKCj/wAARCAABAAEDASIAAhEBAxEB/8QAHwAAAQUBAQEBAQEAAAAAAAAAAAECAwQF
BgcICQoL/8QAtRAAAgEDAwIEAwUFBAQAAAF9AQIDAAQRBRIhMUEGE1FhByJxFDKBka
EII0KxwRVS0fAkM2JyggkKFhcYGRolJicoKSo0NTY3ODk6Q0RFRkdISUpTVFVWV1hZ
WmNkZWZnaGlqc3R1dnd4eXqDhIWGh4iJipKTlJWWl5iZmqKjpKWmp6ipqrKztLW2t
7i5usLDxMXGx8jJytLT1NXW19jZ2uHi4+Tl5ufo6erx8vP09fb3+Pn6/8QAHwEAA
wEBAQEBAQEBAQAAAAAAAAECAwQFBgcICQoL/8QAtREAAgECBAQDBAcFBAQAAQJ3
AAECAxEEBSExBhJBUQdhcRMiMoEIFEKRobHBCSMzUvAVYnLRChYkNOEl8RcYGRom
JygpKjU2Nzg5OkNERUZHSElKU1RVVldYWVpjGR0xJikrK0tNTd5ufo6erx8vP09fb3
+Pn6/9oADAMBAAIRAxEAPwD3+iiigD//2Q==

--KTU_BOUND_2024--
```

**Explanation of each block:**
- The top-level `Content-Type: multipart/mixed` tells the receiver this message contains multiple parts.
- `boundary="KTU_BOUND_2024"` defines the delimiter string.
- The first part has `Content-Type: text/plain` and contains the readable text body.
- The second part has `Content-Type: image/jpeg` and `Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64` because JPEG is binary data that must be encoded for 7-bit SMTP transport.
- The final `--KTU_BOUND_2024--` (with two hyphens appended) marks the end of the multipart message.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the base64 encoding technique used in MIME. Encode the string `"Net"` into its base64 equivalent, showing all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the `multipart/alternative` subtype. How does it differ from `multipart/mixed`? Give an example where it is useful. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Concept:** Base64 encodes binary data into a 64-character ASCII alphabet by converting **3 input bytes (24 bits) into 4 output characters (6 bits each)**. This makes binary data safe for 7-bit SMTP transport.

**Encoding `"Net"` (3 bytes — N=0x4E, e=0x65, t=0x74):**

**Step 1 — Convert each character to 8-bit binary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 0\text{x}4E = 01001110 \\
e &= 0\text{x}65 = 01100101 \\
t &= 0\text{x}74 = 01110100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate the three bytes into a 24-bit stream:**

$$01001110 \, 01100101 \, 01110100$$

**Step 3 — Split into four 6-bit groups:**

$$\underbrace{010011}_{19} \quad \underbrace{100110}_{38} \quad \underbrace{010101}_{21} \quad \underbrace{110100}_{52}$$

**Step 4 — Convert each 6-bit value to its decimal index:**

$$19, \; 38, \; 21, \; 52$$

**Step 5 — Map each index to the Base64 alphabet:**

| Index range | Mapping |
|-------------|---------|
| 0–25 | A–Z |
| 26–51 | a–z |
| 52–61 | 0–9 |
| 62 | + |
| 63 | / |

$$\begin{aligned}
19 \rightarrow T \; (\text{5th from A: A=0, T=19}) \\
38 \rightarrow m \; (\text{12th from a: a=26, m=38}) \\
21 \rightarrow V \; (\text{22nd from A: V=21}) \\
52 \rightarrow 0 \; (\text{first digit: 0=52})
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{"Net"} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{"TmV0"}}$$

Since the input length is exactly **3 bytes** (multiple of 3), **no padding** is required.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**`multipart/alternative` Subtype:**

The `multipart/alternative` subtype is used when the **same content is provided in multiple different formats**. The parts are **semantically equivalent** — the receiver should display only **one** of them, choosing the most appropriate one it can handle.

**Real-world example:** A marketing email that contains the **same message** in three formats:
1. `text/plain` — for plain-text email clients.
2. `text/html` — for HTML-capable clients.
3. `text/markdown` — for specialized clients.

The receiver's mail agent picks the **last format it understands** (MIME spec recommends ordering from least faithful to most faithful).

**Structural example:**

```text
From: marketing@company.com
To: user@gmail.com
Subject: New Product Launch
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: multipart/alternative; boundary="ALT_BOUND"

--ALT_BOUND
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="UTF-8"

Check out our new product!

--ALT_BOUND
Content-Type: text/html; charset="UTF-8"

<html><body><h1>Check out our new product!</h1></body></html>

--ALT_BOUND--
```

**Difference from `multipart/mixed`:**

| Parameter | multipart/mixed | multipart/alternative |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Purpose** | Carries **different / independent** parts | Carries **same content** in **different formats** |
| **Number of parts shown** | **All** parts are presented | **Only one** (the best supported) is shown |
