| **Typical use** | Text + attachment(s) | Plain text + HTML version of the same message |
| **Semantics** | Each part is a unique item | Parts are alternatives of each other |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT confuse `multipart/mixed` with `multipart/alternative`!**
> - If a question asks about **email with attachment**, the answer is **`multipart/mixed`**.
> - If a question asks about **same email in plain text and HTML**, the answer is **`multipart/alternative`**.
> - Writing `multipart/mixed` for an HTML email scenario is a **sure 3-mark loss** in the 7-mark sub-question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MIME**

- **Full Form:** Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension.
- **Standardized in:** **RFC 2045, RFC 2046, RFC 2049** (current evolution in RFC 4288/4289).
- **Purpose:** Extends RFC 822 to support **non-ASCII text, multimedia attachments, and multiple body parts** in a single email.
- **Five MIME Headers:** `MIME-Version`, `Content-Type`, `Content-Transfer-Encoding`, `Content-ID`, `Content-Description`.
- **Seven Content Types:** `text`, `image`, `audio`, `video`, `application`, `message`, `multipart` — memorize in this order.
- **Two Principal Encodings:** `quoted-printable` (best for mostly-text data with occasional 8-bit characters) and `base64` (best for binary attachments).
- **Base64 Expansion Ratio:** **4/3 ≈ 1.333×** (every 3 input bytes → 4 output characters; 6 bits per character).
- **Base64 Alphabet:** A–Z (0–25), a–z (26–51), 0–9 (52–61), `+` (62), `/` (63), `=` (padding).
- **Padding Rule:** If $n \bmod 3 = 1$, append `==`; if $n \bmod 3 = 2$, append `=`.
- **Multipart Boundary:** Parts are separated by `--boundary`; final delimiter is `--boundary--`.
- **Multipart Subtypes:** `mixed` (different parts), `alternative` (same content, different formats), `parallel` (simultaneous viewing), `digest` (each part is a complete message).
- **Backward Compatibility:** MIME messages are still delivered via SMTP — MIME only changes the message **format**, not the **transport**.
- **Beyond Email:** MIME's `Content-Type` and base64 form the basis of **HTTP**, **REST APIs**, and **WebSocket** content negotiation.

---

> **End of Module 4 — MIME Notes** | *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Wide Web (WWW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** World Wide Web (WWW)** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **World Wide Web (WWW)** — commonly known as "the Web" — is formally defined as a **distributed, open, hypermedia information system** that operates over the **Internet** and is accessed through standardized protocols, primarily the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)**. The WWW is not the Internet itself; rather, it is a **service that runs on top of the Internet infrastructure**, enabling the retrieval and display of interlinked resources (web pages, images, videos, documents) via web browsers. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PERUT758 – Computer Networks*, the WWW is studied as an **application-layer service** that demonstrates the practical realization of the client–server paradigm, stateless protocols, and resource addressing schemes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > The WWW is a system of interlinked **hypertext documents** and associated resources, accessed via the **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** and transferred over the network using **HTTP/HTTPS** protocols. It is the most widely used application on the Internet. The WWW was conceptualized by **Tim Berners-Lee** in **1989** at **CERN (European Organization for Nuclear Research)**, and the first website went live in **1991**. The system is governed by the **World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)**, which standardizes languages such as **HTML, CSS, XML, and protocols** that form its backbone. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the Internet is the **global road network** — a vast system of highways, streets, and lanes. The WWW, in this analogy, is a **massive, decentralized library system** built *on top of* that road network. - The **books** in this library are called **web pages**, written in a language called **HTML** (HyperText Markup Language). - Each book has a **unique address** — the **URL (Uniform Resource Locator)** — like a book's call number. - The **delivery trucks** that bring these books to you follow a set of **traffic rules** — the **HTTP protocol**. - The **librarian's desk where you place your request** is the **web browser** (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari). - The **warehouse where the books are stored** is the **web server** (Apache, Nginx, IIS). When you type `https://www.ktu.edu.in` into your browser, you are essentially telling the librarian: *"Fetch me the book located at this address, and bring it back using the secure delivery service."* The librarian (browser) sends a request over the Internet roads to the warehouse (server), which retrieves the book (web page) and ships it back for you to read. ### 1.3 Core Architectural Components The WWW is composed of **three fundamental pillars**: | Component | Function | Example | |-----------|----------|---------| | **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** | Naming and addressing of resources | `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus` | | **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)** | Communication protocol between client and server | HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3 | | **HyperText Markup Language (HTML)** | Content formatting and display language | `

`, `

`, `` tags | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** The **Internet** is the global network infrastructure (hardware + TCP/IP). The **WWW** is a service running *on* the Internet. Email, FTP, and DNS are also services on the Internet — the WWW is just one (the most popular) of them. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard metrics and reserved ports are **mandatory** for KTU examination purposes: - **Default HTTP Port:** **80** - **Default HTTPS Port:** **443** - **Default FTP Port:** 21 - **Default DNS Port:** 53 - **HTML Standard Body:** Maintained by **W3C** and **WHATWG** - **URI Maximum Practical Length:** Approximately **2,083 characters** (Internet Explorer limit) — though RFC 7230 does not specify a hard limit. - **HTTP Status Code Class Ranges:** - **1xx** — Informational - **2xx** — Success - **3xx** — Redirection - **4xx** — Client Error - **5xx** — Server Error # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 World Wide Web — Operational Architecture The WWW operates on a **client–server architecture** with the following layered workflow: ### Step 1: Resource Resolution (URL Parsing) When a user enters a URL into a browser, the URL is parsed into its constituent parts: - **Scheme/Protocol:** `https`, `http`, `ftp`, `mailto` - **Host/Domain Name:** `www.ktu.edu.in` - **Port Number:** `:443` (optional, defaults to 80/443) - **Path:** `/syllabus/computer-networks` - **Query String:** `?semester=5&year=2024` - **Fragment:** `#module4` ### Step 2: DNS Resolution The browser extracts the **hostname** and queries the **Domain Name System (DNS)** to obtain the corresponding **IP address**. This is a critical prerequisite — without DNS, the browser cannot locate the server. ### Step 3: TCP Connection Establishment A **TCP three-way handshake** is performed: 1. `SYN` → Client to Server 2. `SYN-ACK` → Server to Client 3. `ACK` → Client to Server This establishes a reliable, connection-oriented channel. ### Step 4: HTTP Request Transmission The browser sends an **HTTP request message** containing: - **Request Line:** Method, URI, HTTP Version (e.g., `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`) - **Headers:** `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `Cookie` - **Empty Line** (CRLF) - **Message Body** (optional, for POST/PUT) ### Step 5: Server Processing & Response The web server (Apache, Nginx, IIS) processes the request, executes server-side logic (PHP, Python, Node.js, Java), accesses databases if needed, and constructs an **HTTP response message**. ### Step 6: HTTP Response Transmission The server sends back a response with: - **Status Line:** HTTP Version, Status Code, Reason Phrase - **Headers:** `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Set-Cookie` - **Empty Line** - **Message Body:** HTML content, images, CSS, JavaScript ### Step 7: Rendering The browser's **rendering engine** (Blink, Gecko, WebKit) parses the HTML, applies CSS, executes JavaScript, and constructs the **Document Object Model (DOM)** and **render tree**, finally painting pixels on the screen. ## 2.2 Uniform Resource Locator (URL) — Detailed Structure A URL is a **compact string representation** of the location and access method for a resource on the WWW. Its formal syntax (as per **RFC 3986**) is: ``` scheme://userinfo@host:port/path?query#fragment ``` ### Example Decomposition Consider the URL: `https://student:pass123@www.ktu.edu.in:8080/results/2024?sem=5#marks` | Component | Value | Purpose | |-----------|-------|---------| | **Scheme** | `https` | Protocol used for access | | **Userinfo** | `student:pass123` | Optional authentication credentials | | **Host** | `www.ktu.edu.in` | Domain name of the server | | **Port** | `8080` | TCP port number | | **Path** | `/results/2024` | Hierarchical resource path | | **Query** | `?sem=5` | Parameters passed to the resource | | **Fragment** | `#marks` | Sub-section within the retrieved document | > [!NOTE] > **URN vs. URI vs. URL:** > - **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier):** The superset — identifies *any* resource. > - **URL (Uniform Resource Locator):** A URI that specifies *how* to locate the resource (includes mechanism). > - **URN (Uniform Resource Name):** A URI that names the resource *without* specifying location (e.g., `urn:isbn:978-0-13-110362-7`). ## 2.3 HTTP — Protocol Deep Dive ### 2.3.1 HTTP Methods (Verbs) | Method | Purpose | Idempotent | Safe | Body in Request | |--------|---------|------------|------|-----------------| | `GET` | Retrieve a resource | Yes | Yes | No | | `POST` | Submit data / create resource | No | No | Yes | | `PUT` | Replace/Update a resource | Yes | No | Yes | | `DELETE` | Remove a resource | Yes | No | Optional | | `HEAD` | Same as GET but headers only | Yes | Yes | No | | `OPTIONS` | Discover supported methods | Yes | Yes | No | | `PATCH` | Partial update of a resource | No | No | Yes | ### 2.3.2 HTTP Status Codes (Critical for KTU) | Code | Meaning | Common Usage | |------|---------|--------------| | `200 OK` | Request succeeded | Standard successful GET | | `301 Moved Permanently` | Resource permanently relocated | URL redirection | | `302 Found` | Temporary redirect | Post-login redirects | | `304 Not Modified` | Cached version is valid | Web caching optimization | | `400 Bad Request` | Malformed client request | Syntax error | | `401 Unauthorized` | Authentication required | Login-protected pages | | `403 Forbidden` | Server refuses to authorize | Permission denied | | `404 Not Found` | Resource does not exist | Broken links | | `500 Internal Server Error` | Generic server failure | Unhandled exception | | `503 Service Unavailable` | Server temporarily overloaded | Maintenance window | ### 2.3.3 HTTP Connection Modes - **Non-Persistent (HTTP/1.0 default):** One TCP connection per object. Requires a new handshake for every resource — highly inefficient. - **Persistent (HTTP/1.1 default):** Single TCP connection reused for multiple requests, with **pipelining** capability. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table summarizes the **critical formulas and metrics** for solving WWW performance and caching problems in KTU examinations. | Formula / Metric | Expression | Description | |------------------|------------|-------------| | **Total Response Time (no caching)** | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(RTT_i + T_{\text{trans}_i})$ | Sum of round-trip times and transmission delays for all $N$ objects | | **Response Time (persistent HTTP)** | $T_{\text{persistent}} = RTT_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{trans}_i}$ | One RTT for connection, then parallel transfers | | **Response Time (non-persistent, parallel)** | $T_{\text{np}} = RTT_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{N}(RTT_i + T_{\text{trans}_i})$ | New RTT per object, no connection reuse | | **Average Access Time with cache** | $T_{\text{avg}} = p \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1-p) \cdot T_{\text{origin}}$ | $p$ = cache hit probability | | **Cache Hit Ratio** | $p = \frac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits} + \text{Misses}}$ | Fraction of requests served from cache | | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = B \times RTT$ | Amount of data that can be "in-flight" | | **Transmission Time** | $T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{B}$ | $L$ = object length (bits), $B$ = bandwidth (bps) | | **HTTP/2 Multiplexing gain** | $T_{\text{latency}} = \max(T_i)$ | All streams share one connection — slowest wins | | **Pipelining throughput** | $T_{\text{pipe}} = \frac{N \cdot L}{B}$ | Continuous back-to-back requests on one connection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on pipe symbol usage in KTU answer sheets:** When writing formulas inline in the KTU examination, always use the LaTeX `\vert` command (e.g., $p \cdot T_{\text{cache}}\vert_{\text{hit}}$) instead of the raw `|` character to avoid parsing issues in digital answer evaluation. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The WWW is the **single largest distributed application system** ever built. Its engineering utility spans: 1. **Software Engineering:** RESTful API design, microservices architecture, and cloud-native deployment directly inherit WWW principles. 2. **Network Engineering:** Understanding WWW protocols is essential for CDN design, load balancing, and DDoS mitigation. 3. **Cybersecurity:** HTTPS, CORS, CSP, and same-origin policies are all WWW-layer security mechanisms. 4. **Data Science:** Web crawlers and scrapers (used by search engines) operate over HTTP. 5. **IoT & Edge Computing:** CoAP protocol is essentially a lightweight version of HTTP for constrained devices. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: Total Response Time Calculation This is a **standard 14-mark KTU problem**. Let us solve it step-by-step, with every algebraic transition explicitly shown. ### Problem Setup > **Given:** A web page consists of: > - 1 base HTML file of size **15 KB** > - 8 image objects, each of size **50 KB** > - RTT between client and server = **100 ms** > - Transmission bandwidth = **1 Mbps** > - Persistent HTTP with **pipelining** is used. > - The TCP connection setup time is **1.5 × RTT** (1 RTT for SYN, 0.5 RTT for SYN-ACK+ACK). > > **Find:** Total time to retrieve the entire web page. ### Step 1: Calculate Transmission Time for One Object We compute the transmission time for the base HTML file first. $$T_{\text{trans,html}} = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{15 \times 8 \times 1024 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}}$$ $$T_{\text{trans,html}} = \frac{122{,}880}{1{,}000{,}000} \text{ sec} = 0.12288 \text{ sec} \approx 122.88 \text{ ms}$$ **Explanation:** $L = 15 \text{ KB} = 15 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 15 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 122{,}880 \text{ bits}$. Dividing by the bandwidth $B = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bps}$ yields the transmission time. ### Step 2: Calculate Transmission Time for One Image $$T_{\text{trans,img}} = \frac{50 \times 8 \times 1024}{10^6} = \frac{409{,}600}{10^6} = 0.4096 \text{ sec} = 409.6 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 3: TCP Connection Setup Time $$T_{\text{setup}} = 1.5 \times RTT = 1.5 \times 100 \text{ ms} = 150 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 4: First RTT for Request + Initial Response After the TCP handshake, the client sends the HTTP GET request (small, negligible time), and the server replies with the first byte after **1 RTT**. $$T_{\text{first_byte}} = RTT = 100 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 5: Transmission of Base HTML Once the first byte arrives, the remaining HTML is transmitted: $$T_{\text{html_send}} = T_{\text{trans,html}} = 122.88 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 6: Pipelined Image Requests In **persistent HTTP with pipelining**, the client sends all 8 image requests back-to-back over the *same* TCP connection *without waiting* for individual responses. The server then streams responses in order. For pipelined requests, the total transfer time for all 8 images equals the time from the first request to the last byte of the 8th image response: $$T_{\text{images}} = 1 \cdot RTT + 8 \cdot T_{\text{trans,img}}$$ **Why 1 RTT?** Because pipelining means the client sends all requests without waiting; the first image's first byte arrives after **1 RTT** (request propagation + server processing + response propagation). $$T_{\text{images}} = 100 + 8 \times 409.6 = 100 + 3{,}276.8 = 3{,}376.8 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 7: Total Page Retrieval Time We sum all the components sequentially: $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{first_byte}} + T_{\text{html_send}} + T_{\text{images}} \\ &= 150 + 100 + 122.88 + 3{,}376.8 \\ &= 3{,}749.68 \text{ ms} \\ &\approx 3.75 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** $T_{\text{total}} \approx 3.75$ seconds. ## 3.2 Comparison: Non-Persistent vs. Persistent HTTP Let us re-derive the **non-persistent** case for the same page to highlight the difference. ### Non-Persistent HTTP (Sequential, one connection per object) For each of the 9 objects (1 HTML + 8 images), we need: - A new TCP setup: $1.5 \times RTT = 150$ ms - A request-RTT: $1 \times RTT = 100$ ms - A transmission time $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{np,total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{9}\left(1.5 \cdot RTT + 1 \cdot RTT + T_{\text{trans},i}\right) \\ &= 9 \times (150 + 100) + T_{\text{trans,html}} + 8 \times T_{\text{trans,img}} \\ &= 9 \times 250 + 122.88 + 8 \times 409.6 \\ &= 2{,}250 + 122.88 + 3{,}276.8 \\ &= 5{,}649.68 \text{ ms} \approx 5.65 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$ ### Non-Persistent HTTP (Parallel, multiple simultaneous connections) Modern browsers open **6 parallel connections** per host. With 9 objects and 6 parallel connections, the workload is split: - **Batch 1:** 6 objects in parallel (say, 5 images + HTML) - **Batch 2:** Remaining 3 images For each batch, the slowest object dominates (parallel execution): $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{batch1}} &= 1.5 \times RTT + RTT + \max(T_{\text{trans}}) \\ &= 250 + \max(122.88, 409.6) \\ &= 250 + 409.6 = 659.6 \text{ ms} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{batch2}} &= 250 + 409.6 = 659.6 \text{ ms} \end{aligned}$$ $$T_{\text{np,parallel}} = 659.6 + 659.6 = 1{,}319.2 \text{ ms} \approx 1.32 \text{ s}$$ ### Analytical Summary Matrix | Connection Mode | Total Time (ms) | Speedup vs. Sequential | TCP Connections | |-----------------|-----------------|------------------------|-----------------| | **Non-Persistent Sequential** | **5,649.68** | 1.0× (baseline) | 9 | | **Non-Persistent Parallel (6 conn.)** | **1,319.20** | 4.28× | 6 (concurrent) | | **Persistent Pipelined** | **3,749.68** | 1.51× | 1 | | **Persistent Parallel (HTTP/2)** | **~789.6** (approx.) | ~7.15× | 1 (multiplexed) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Persistent pipelined HTTP saves connection setup overhead but suffers from **Head-of-Line (HOL) blocking** — a slow object delays all subsequent ones. HTTP/2 solves this via **binary framing and multiplexing**, allowing true parallel transfer over a single connection. ## 3.3 Web Caching — Performance Derivation ### Problem Setup > A web cache has a **hit rate of $p = 0.4$**. The access time to the cache is **10 ms**, and the access time to the origin server (on a cache miss) is **500 ms**. Find the **average access time**. ### Step 1: Define Components - Cache hit probability: $p = 0.4$ - Cache miss probability: $1 - p = 0.6$ - Cache access time: $T_{\text{cache}} = 10$ ms - Origin server time (miss): $T_{\text{origin}} = 500$ ms ### Step 2: Apply the Average Formula $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{avg}} &= p \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1-p) \cdot T_{\text{origin}} \\ &= 0.4 \times 10 + 0.6 \times 500 \\ &= 4 + 300 \\ &= 304 \text{ ms} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Speedup $$T_{\text{no\_cache}} = 500 \text{ ms}$$ $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{no\_cache}}}{T_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{500}{304} \approx 1.645$$ **Conclusion:** Even a 40% hit rate provides a **1.645× speedup**, demonstrating the enormous engineering value of web caching (used by CDNs like Cloudflare, Akamai). ## 3.4 Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) for Web Transfers The BDP determines the **optimal TCP window size** to fully utilize the link: $$\begin{aligned} BDP &= B \times RTT \\ &= 1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \times 0.1 \text{ s} \\ &= 100{,}000 \text{ bits} = 12.5 \text{ KB} \end{aligned}$$ If an object is **smaller** than the BDP (e.g., the 15 KB HTML is close to 12.5 KB), the link is not fully utilized. This justifies the engineering practice of **bundling resources** (CSS, JS sprites) to reduce the number of small transfers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WWW Client–Server Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB user1[USER] --> browser1[WEB BROWSER] browser1 --> urlParser[URL PARSER] urlParser --> dnsRes[DNS RESOLVER] dnsRes --> ipCache{IP CACHED?} ipCache -->|YES| tcpConn ipCache -->|NO| dnsQuery[DNS QUERY] dnsQuery --> dnsServer[DNS SERVER] dnsServer --> ipCache tcpConn[TCP CONNECTION] --> httpReq[HTTP REQUEST] httpReq --> webServer[WEB SERVER APACHE NGINX] webServer --> appLogic[APPLICATION LOGIC] appLogic --> dbQuery[DATABASE QUERY] dbQuery --> dbServer[DATABASE] dbServer --> appLogic appLogic --> httpResp[HTTP RESPONSE] httpResp --> renderEngine[RENDERING ENGINE] renderEngine --> domTree[DOM TREE] domTree --> cssTree[CSS TREE] cssTree --> renderTree[RENDER TREE] renderTree --> paint[PIXEL PAINT] paint --> user1 ``` ## 4.2 HTTP Request-Response Cycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Web Browser participant Server as Web Server participant DB as Database Note over Client: User enters URL Client->>Client: Parse URL & Resolve Hostname Client->>Server: TCP SYN (1 RTT) Server-->>Client: TCP SYN-ACK (1 RTT) Client->>Server: TCP ACK (Connection Established) Client->>Server: HTTP GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 Server->>DB: Query Data (if needed) DB-->>Server: Return Data Server-->>Client: HTTP/1.1 200 OK + HTML Body Client->>Server: HTTP GET /image1.jpg (Pipelined) Client->>Server: HTTP GET /image2.jpg (Pipelined) Server-->>Client: HTTP/1.1 200 OK + image1 bytes Server-->>Client: HTTP/1.1 200 OK + image2 bytes Note over Client: Browser renders HTML, CSS, JS Client->>Server: TCP FIN (Close Connection) Server-->>Client: TCP FIN-ACK ``` ## 4.3 URL Structure Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart LR url1[COMPLETE URL] --> scheme1[SCHEME HTTPS] url1 --> auth1[USERINFO OPTIONAL] url1 --> host1[HOST WWW.KTU.EDU.IN] url1 --> port1[PORT 443] url1 --> path1[PATH SYLLABUS] url1 --> query1[QUERY SEM 5] url1 --> frag1[FRAGMENT MODULE4] scheme1 --> r1[ROUTE TO HTTPS HANDLER] auth1 --> r2[DECODE CREDENTIALS] host1 --> r3[DNS LOOKUP] port1 --> r4[OPEN TCP SOCKET] path1 --> r5[MAP TO FILE OR ROUTE] query1 --> r6[PARSED AS KEY VALUE PAIRS] frag1 --> r7[CLIENT SIDE ONLY] ``` ## 4.4 Web Caching Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB req1[INCOMING REQUEST] --> check1{CACHE HIT} check1 -->|YES| serveCached[SERVE FROM CACHE] check1 -->|NO| forward[FORWARD TO ORIGIN SERVER] forward --> originResp[ORIGIN RESPONSE] originResp --> storeCache[STORE IN CACHE WITH TTL] storeCache --> sendClient[SEND TO CLIENT] serveCached --> sendClient sendClient --> end1[CLIENT RECEIVES] ``` ## 4.5 HTTP Protocol Stack (Layered View) ```mermaid flowchart TB app1[HTML DOCUMENT CONTENT] --> http1[HTTP MESSAGE HEADERS AND BODY] http1 --> tls1[TLS HANDSHAKE FOR HTTPS] tls1 --> tcp1[TCP TRANSPORT LAYER] tcp1 --> ip1[IP NETWORK LAYER] ip1 --> link1[LINK LAYER ETHERNET WIFI] link1 --> phys1[PHYSICAL LAYER COPPER FIBER RADIO] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the following with respect to the World Wide Web:** **(a)** URL **(b)** HTTP **(c)** HTML **Model Answer:** **(a) URL (Uniform Resource Locator):** A URL is a standardized address used to uniquely identify and locate a resource on the WWW. It comprises the **scheme** (protocol), **hostname**, **port**, **path**, **query string**, and **fragment**. For example: `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus`. **(b) HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol):** HTTP is the **application-layer protocol** that governs the communication between web clients (browsers) and web servers. It operates as a **stateless request-response** protocol, currently in versions **HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3**. It uses **port 80** by default, while HTTPS uses **port 443**. **(c) HTML (HyperText Markup Language):** HTML is the **standardized markup language** used to structure and present content on web pages. It uses **tags** (e.g., `

`, `

`, ``) to define elements such as headings, paragraphs, hyperlinks, images, and forms. The latest version is **HTML5**, standardized by **W3C** and **WHATWG**. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between HTTP and HTTPS. Why is HTTPS preferred in modern web applications?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | HTTP | HTTPS | |-----------|------|-------| | **Full Form** | HyperText Transfer Protocol | HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure | | **Port** | 80 | 443 | | **Encryption** | None (plaintext) | TLS/SSL encryption | | **Certificate** | Not required | Requires digital certificate (X.509) | | **Performance** | Slightly faster (no encryption overhead) | Slightly slower due to handshake | | **SEO Ranking** | Lower (penalized by Google) | Higher (favored by search engines) | | **Data Integrity** | Vulnerable to tampering | Guaranteed via MAC | **Why HTTPS is preferred:** HTTPS provides **confidentiality** (encryption prevents eavesdropping), **integrity** (tamper detection), and **authentication** (server identity verified by certificate authority). Modern web applications handle sensitive data (passwords, payment info, personal records) and HTTPS is mandated by browsers (e.g., Chrome flags HTTP sites as "Not Secure"). Compliance standards like **PCI-DSS** and **GDPR** also require HTTPS for data protection. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] — 7 Marks **Explain the architecture of the World Wide Web with a neat diagram. Describe the client-server interaction during web page retrieval, including the role of HTTP, DNS, and TCP.** **Model Answer:** The WWW follows a **client-server architecture** consisting of three main components: **web clients (browsers)**, **web servers**, and **the underlying Internet infrastructure**. **Step 1: URL Resolution by Client** The user enters a URL (e.g., `https://www.ktu.edu.in`) in the browser. The browser parses the URL, extracts the **hostname**, and triggers a **DNS resolution** to obtain the **IP address** of the server. **Step 2: DNS Lookup** The browser checks its local cache, then the OS cache, then queries the configured **DNS resolver**. The resolver traverses the DNS hierarchy (root → TLD → authoritative) and returns the **A or AAAA record** containing the IP address. **Step 3: TCP Connection Establishment** The browser initiates a **TCP three-way handshake** with the server at port 443 (for HTTPS) or 80 (for HTTP). This establishes a reliable, ordered byte stream. **Step 4: TLS Handshake (for HTTPS)** For HTTPS, an additional **TLS handshake** occurs to establish encrypted communication, verify the server's certificate, and generate session keys. **Step 5: HTTP Request** The browser sends an HTTP `GET` request: ``` GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 Host: www.ktu.edu.in User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 Accept: text/html ``` **Step 6: Server Processing** The web server (Apache, Nginx) receives the request, routes it to the appropriate handler (PHP, Python, Node.js), executes business logic, queries a database if needed, and constructs the response. **Step 7: HTTP Response** The server returns: ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html Content-Length: 1234 ... ``` **Step 8: Rendering** The browser's rendering engine parses HTML, builds the **DOM tree**, applies CSS, executes JavaScript, and paints the page. **Role Summary:** - **DNS:** Translates human-readable names to IP addresses. - **TCP:** Provides reliable, ordered, connection-oriented delivery. - **HTTP:** Defines the structure of requests/responses exchanged. (Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1 for visualization.) --- #### Part (b): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] — 7 Marks **A web page consists of 1 HTML file of 30 KB and 6 image objects, each of 80 KB. The RTT is 200 ms and the bandwidth is 2 Mbps. Calculate the total response time for:** **(i) Non-persistent HTTP with no parallel connections** **(ii) Persistent HTTP with pipelining** **Model Answer:** **Given Data:** - HTML size: $L_{\text{html}} = 30 \text{ KB} = 30 \times 1024 \times 8 = 245{,}760 \text{ bits}$ - Image size: $L_{\text{img}} = 80 \text{ KB} = 80 \times 1024 \times 8 = 655{,}360 \text{ bits}$ - RTT = 200 ms = 0.2 s - Bandwidth $B = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$ - Number of images $N = 6$ **Transmission Times:** $$T_{\text{html}} = \frac{245{,}760}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.12288 \text{ s} = 122.88 \text{ ms}$$ $$T_{\text{img}} = \frac{655{,}360}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.32768 \text{ s} = 327.68 \text{ ms}$$ **TCP Setup Time (per connection):** $T_{\text{setup}} = 1.5 \times RTT = 300 \text{ ms}$ --- **(i) Non-Persistent HTTP, No Parallel Connections:** Each object requires its own TCP connection. Total time = sum over all 7 objects: $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{np}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{7}\left(T_{\text{setup}} + RTT + T_{\text{trans},i}\right) \\ &= 7 \times 300 + 7 \times 200 + T_{\text{html}} + 6 \times T_{\text{img}} \\ &= 2{,}100 + 1{,}400 + 122.88 + 6 \times 327.68 \\ &= 3{,}500 + 122.88 + 1{,}966.08 \\ &= 5{,}588.96 \text{ ms} \approx 5.59 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$ --- **(ii) Persistent HTTP with Pipelining:** One TCP connection serves all 7 objects. Client pipelines all requests; responses stream back-to-back. $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{persistent}} &= T_{\text{setup}} + 1 \times RTT + T_{\text{html}} + 1 \times RTT + 6 \times T_{\text{img}} \\ &= 300 + 200 + 122.88 + 200 + 1{,}966.08 \\ &= 2{,}788.96 \text{ ms} \approx 2.79 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** Persistent pipelined HTTP is approximately **2× faster** than non-persistent sequential HTTP for this scenario. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) #### Part (a): [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — 7 Marks **With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an HTTP request and HTTP response message. List any six common HTTP status codes with their meanings.** **Model Answer:** **Structure of HTTP Request Message:** ``` GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\n Host: www.example.com\r\n User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0\r\n Accept: text/html\r\n Accept-Language: en-US\r\n Connection: keep-alive\r\n Cookie: session=abc123\r\n \r\n [Optional Message Body] ``` **Components:** 1. **Request Line:** Method + URI + HTTP Version 2. **Header Lines:** Key-value pairs (Host, User-Agent, Accept, Cookie, etc.) 3. **Blank Line (CRLF):** Separates headers from body 4. **Message Body:** Present only in POST/PUT requests **Structure of HTTP Response Message:** ``` HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n Date: Mon, 25 Dec 2024 10:30:00 GMT\r\n Server: Apache/2.4.41\r\n Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8\r\n Content-Length: 12345\r\n Set-Cookie: session=xyz789; HttpOnly\r\n \r\n ... ``` **Components:** 1. **Status Line:** HTTP Version + Status Code + Reason Phrase 2. **Header Lines:** Content-Type, Content-Length, Server, Set-Cookie 3. **Blank Line** 4. **Message Body:** The actual resource (HTML, JSON, image, etc.) **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TB reqLine[REQUEST LINE METHOD URI VERSION] --> reqHdr[REQUEST HEADERS HOST USER-AGENT ACCEPT] reqHdr --> reqBlank[BLANK LINE CRLF] reqBlank --> reqBody[REQUEST BODY OPTIONAL] resLine[STATUS LINE VERSION CODE PHRASE] --> resHdr[RESPONSE HEADERS CONTENT-TYPE LENGTH SERVER] resHdr --> resBlank[BLANK LINE CRLF] resBlank --> resBody[RESPONSE BODY RESOURCE] ``` **Six Common HTTP Status Codes:** | Code | Meaning | Description | |------|---------|-------------| | **200 OK** | Success | Standard response for successful HTTP requests | | **301 Moved Permanently** | Redirection | Resource has been permanently assigned a new URL | | **400 Bad Request** | Client Error | The server cannot process due to client-side syntax error | | **401 Unauthorized** | Client Error | Authentication is required to access the resource | | **404 Not Found** | Client Error | The requested resource does not exist on the server | | **500 Internal Server Error** | Server Error | Generic server-side failure (unhandled exception) | --- #### Part (b): [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of web caching. A web cache has a hit rate of 0.35. The cache access time is 5 ms and the origin server access time is 400 ms. Calculate:** **(i)** The average access time **(ii)** The percentage reduction in access time compared to no caching **Model Answer:** **Concept of Web Caching:** Web caching is a performance optimization technique that stores frequently accessed web resources (HTML pages, images, CSS, JavaScript) in a **temporally and spatially closer** location to the client — either in the browser cache, a proxy cache, or a CDN edge server. When a request arrives, the cache is consulted first. On a **cache hit**, the response is served locally; on a **cache miss**, the request is forwarded to the origin server, and the response is **stored in the cache** for future use (governed by a **Time-To-Live (TTL)** or cache-control header). **Benefits:** - Reduced latency (faster response) - Reduced bandwidth consumption on the origin link - Reduced load on the origin server - Improved scalability and availability **Given Data:** - Cache hit rate: $p = 0.35$ - Cache miss rate: $1 - p = 0.65$ - Cache access time: $T_{\text{cache}} = 5$ ms - Origin server time: $T_{\text{origin}} = 400$ ms --- **(i) Average Access Time:** $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{avg}} &= p \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1 - p) \cdot T_{\text{origin}} \\ &= 0.35 \times 5 + 0.65 \times 400 \\ &= 1.75 + 260 \\ &= 261.75 \text{ ms} \end{aligned}$$ --- **(ii) Percentage Reduction:** Without cache, every request goes to the origin server: $$T_{\text{no\_cache}} = 400 \text{ ms}$$ $$\text{Reduction} = \frac{T_{\text{no\_cache}} - T_{\text{avg}}}{T_{\text{no\_cache}}} \times 100\%$$ $$\text{Reduction} = \frac{400 - 261.75}{400} \times 100\% = \frac{138.25}{400} \times 100\% = 34.56\%$$ **Conclusion:** A 35% hit rate yields a **34.56% reduction** in average access time, demonstrating the practical value of web caching in real-world deployments. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Units Mismatch:** Always convert KB to **bits** (multiply by $1024 \times 8$) and bandwidth to **bits per second** (bps) before dividing. Students commonly lose 1–2 marks by mixing KB and Kb or MB and Mb. > 2. **TCP Setup Forgotten:** For non-persistent HTTP, each object requires a **separate TCP handshake** (1.5 RTT). Forgetting this is the most common error, leading to a wrong answer by a factor of 2–3×. > 3. **Pipelining vs. Parallelism:** In pipelined HTTP, all requests are sent back-to-back *without waiting*, but responses are still returned in order. The total image time is **1 RTT + N × T_trans**, not N × RTT. > 4. **Cache Miss Cost:** In the caching formula, the *miss cost* $T_{\text{origin}}$ should include the time to fetch from origin *plus* the time to store in cache (if relevant). For typical KTU problems, the simplified formula is acceptable. > 5. **Status Code Categories:** Examiners expect you to *memorize* the 1xx–5xx range classification. Writing "404" without "Client Error" category may cost 0.5 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for the WWW Topic:** - **WWW Definition:** A distributed hypermedia system on the Internet, accessed via HTTP, identified by URLs, and displayed via browsers. **Not the same as the Internet.** - **Inventor:** **Tim Berners-Lee** at **CERN** in **1989**; first website live in **1991**. - **Three Pillars:** **URL** (naming) + **HTTP/HTTPS** (protocol) + **HTML** (content). - **Default Ports:** HTTP → **80**, HTTPS → **443**, FTP → 21, DNS → 53, SMTP → 25. - **URL Syntax:** `scheme://userinfo@host:port/path?query#fragment` — know every component. - **URI ⊃ URL ⊃ URN:** URI is the superset; URN names without locating; URL locates with mechanism. - **HTTP Methods:** GET (safe, idempotent), POST (creates), PUT (updates, idempotent), DELETE (idempotent), HEAD, OPTIONS, PATCH. - **Status Code Ranges:** 1xx informational, 2xx success, 3xx redirection, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. Memorize: 200, 301, 304, 400, 401, 403, 404, 500, 503. - **Non-Persistent vs. Persistent:** Non-persistent opens a new TCP connection per object (expensive). Persistent (HTTP/1.1 default) reuses one connection, optionally with pipelining. - **Performance Formula:** $T_{\text{trans}} = L / B$; $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{setup}} + \sum (RTT + T_{\text{trans}})$. Know when to apply 1.5 RTT for setup. - **Pipelining:** All requests sent without waiting; responses returned sequentially. Bottleneck: Head-of-Line blocking. - **HTTP/2:** Binary framing + multiplexing solves HOL blocking; one TCP connection handles all streams in parallel. - **Web Caching:** $T_{\text{avg}} = p \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + (1-p) \cdot T_{\text{origin}}$. CDN, proxy, and browser caching are real-world applications. - **Cookies:** Small text files sent by server (`Set-Cookie`) and stored by client; sent back in subsequent requests for **session management** and personalization. - **Cookies vs. Sessions:** Cookies are stored *client-side*; sessions are stored *server-side* with only an ID in the cookie. - **BDP:** $BDP = B \times RTT$ — determines optimal window size; critical for understanding link utilization. - **Browser Engines:** Blink (Chrome, Edge), Gecko (Firefox), WebKit (Safari). - **W3C:** The standards body governing WWW technologies (HTML, CSS, SVG, etc.).

topics.title: HTTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT758 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HTTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is an **application-layer protocol** defined in **RFC 7230 – RFC 7235** (HTTP/1.1) and later **RFC 9110** (HTTP Semantics) that governs the **request-response communication model** between web clients (browsers) and web servers over the Internet. It operates by default over **TCP port 80**, while its secure variant **HTTPS** operates over **TCP port 443**.

HTTP is fundamentally a **stateless, text-based (in versions 1.0/1.1) protocol** designed to transfer hypermedia documents such as HTML, images, videos, and API payloads. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, HTTP is positioned within the **Application Layer** of the TCP/IP reference model and forms the backbone of the **World Wide Web (WWW)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes understanding the **HTTP message structure, methods, status codes, persistent vs. non-persistent connections, cookies, and the evolution to HTTP/2 and HTTP/3**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍽️ — The "Restaurant" Model

Imagine HTTP as a **conversation at a restaurant**:

| Role | HTTP Equivalent | Restaurant Analogy |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Browser (Client)** | HTTP Client | The **customer** who wants food |
| **Web Server** | HTTP Server | The **kitchen** that prepares the dish |
| **HTTP Request** | Customer's order | "I want one pizza, no olives" |
| **HTTP Response** | The served dish + bill | Pizza arrives + receipt (status: 200 OK) |
| **URL** | Menu item reference | "Table 5, Dish #12" |
| **Cookies** | Loyalty card | Server remembers you from last visit |
| **TCP Port 80/443** | Restaurant entrance | The **door** through which communication happens |

When the customer finishes, **no memory is retained** of the conversation — this is **statelessness**. To remember, the restaurant gives a **loyalty card (cookie)**.

---

## 1.3 Core Characteristics

- **Stateless:** Each request-response pair is independent. The server does not retain client state between transactions.
- **Connection-oriented (in 1.1+):** Uses **TCP** as the underlying transport to guarantee reliable delivery.
- **Text-based (1.0, 1.1) / Binary (HTTP/2, HTTP/3):** HTTP/1.x uses human-readable ASCII; HTTP/2 uses binary framing.
- **Request-Response Model:** Client always initiates; server replies.
- **Media Independent:** Can transfer any MIME-typed data (text/html, application/json, image/png, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **Default Ports to Memorize:** HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443** — these are absolute constants appearing in nearly every KTU exam question.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HTTP Architecture & Transaction Model

HTTP follows a strict **Client-Server architecture**. The operational lifecycle is:

1. **Client Opens TCP Connection** to server on port **80** (HTTP) or **443** (HTTPS).
2. **Client sends HTTP Request Message** (method, URI, headers, optional body).
3. **Server processes** the request, accesses resources or runs applications (CGI, Servlets, etc.).
4. **Server sends HTTP Response Message** (status line, headers, body).
5. **Connection is closed** (HTTP/1.0) or kept alive (HTTP/1.1+).
6. **Client renders the response** (browser renders HTML, executes JS, etc.).

---

## 2.2 HTTP Message Format (RFC 7230)

### 🔹 Request Message Structure

```
<method> <request-URI> <HTTP-version>\r\n
<Header>: <value>\r\n
<Header>: <value>\r\n
\r\n
[Optional Body]
```

### 🔹 Response Message Structure

```
<HTTP-version> <status-code> <reason-phrase>\r\n
<Header>: <value>\r\n
\r\n
[Body — HTML, JSON, etc.]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRLF (`\r\n`)** is the **mandatory line terminator**. A blank line (just `\r\n`) separates the **header section** from the **body**.

---

## 2.3 HTTP Request Methods (Verbs)

| Method | Idempotent | Safe | Purpose | KTU Weight |
|--------|-----------|------|---------|-----------|
| `GET` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Retrieve a resource | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `POST` | ❌ No | ❌ No | Submit data, create resource | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `PUT` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Replace/create resource at URI | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `DELETE` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Delete resource | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `HEAD` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Same as GET, but no body | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `OPTIONS` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Describe communication options | ⭐⭐ |
| `PATCH` | ❌ No | ❌ No | Partial modification | ⭐⭐ |
| `TRACE` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Echo back the request (loopback) | ⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotent** = Multiple identical requests produce the **same server state**. **Safe** = Does **not modify** server resources.

---

## 2.4 HTTP Status Codes (The Server's "Mood Report")

Status codes are **3-digit integers** classified into five categories:

| Range | Category | Meaning | Common Examples |
|-------|----------|---------|-----------------|
| **1xx** | Informational | Request received, continuing | `100 Continue`, `101 Switching Protocols` |
| **2xx** | Success | Request successfully received | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` |
| **3xx** | Redirection | Further action needed | `301 Moved Permanently`, `304 Not Modified` |
| **4xx** | Client Error | Bad request from client | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `403 Forbidden`, `404 Not Found` |
| **5xx** | Server Error | Server failed to fulfill valid request | `500 Internal Server Error`, `502 Bad Gateway`, `503 Service Unavailable` |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** **1-You're early, 2-Here you go, 3-Go away, 4-You messed up, 5-I messed up.**

---

## 2.5 HTTP Versions Evolution

| Version | Year | Key Innovation | Transport |
|---------|------|----------------|-----------|
| **HTTP/0.9** | 1991 | Simple GET only, raw HTML | TCP |
| **HTTP/1.0** | 1996 | Methods, headers, status codes | TCP, **non-persistent** |
| **HTTP/1.1** | 1997 | **Persistent connections**, pipelining, chunked transfer, Host header | TCP, persistent |
| **HTTP/2** | 2015 | **Binary framing**, **multiplexing**, header compression (HPACK), server push | TCP + TLS |
| **HTTP/3** | 2022 | Built on **QUIC** (UDP-based), 0-RTT handshake, eliminates head-of-line blocking | **QUIC over UDP** |

---

## 2.6 Non-Persistent vs. Persistent Connections

### 🔸 Non-Persistent (HTTP/1.0 default)
- **One TCP connection per object** (HTML, image, CSS, JS each require a separate connection).
- Each object requires a fresh **3-way handshake** = **2 RTTs overhead + object transmission time**.

### 🔸 Persistent (HTTP/1.1 default)
- **Single TCP connection** carries **multiple objects**.
- Uses `Connection: keep-alive` header.
- Saves on repeated handshakes → **significantly faster page loads**.

---

## 2.7 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Total time for one object (non-persistent) | $T_{total} = 2 \times RTT + T_{transmit}$ | seconds | 1 RTT for SYN+ACK, 1 RTT for request+response |
| 2 | Total time for n objects (non-persistent) | $T_{n} = n \times (2 \times RTT + T_{file})$ | seconds | Sequential, one-by-one |
| 3 | Total time for n objects (persistent, no pipelining) | $T_{n} = 2 \times RTT + n \times T_{file}$ | seconds | Single handshake for all |
| 4 | Total time for n objects (persistent, pipelined) | $T_{n} = 2 \times RTT + T_{file}$ (if parallel) | seconds | All requests fired at once |
| 5 | HTTP default port | Port **80** | — | Constant |
| 6 | HTTPS default port | Port **443** | — | Constant |
| 7 | File transmission time | $T_{transmit} = \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | seconds | Basic data rate formula |
| 8 | Throughput (ideal) | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Object Size}}{T_{total}}$ | bits/sec | Performance metric |
| 9 | Status code classification | $1\text{xx} \leq S \leq 5\text{xx}$ where S = floor(code/100) | — | Code range determines class |
| 10 | Round Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2 \times T_{prop}$ (one-way propagation sum) | seconds | Network physics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas above are **derived from basic network latency principles** and appear regularly in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Development:** REST APIs built on HTTP govern nearly all modern app backends (mobile, IoT, SaaS).
- **Load Balancing:** Status codes (5xx) drive **circuit breakers** in microservices.
- **CDN & Caching:** HTTP `Cache-Control`, `ETag`, `If-Modified-Since` headers enable content delivery networks.
- **Security:** HTTPS adds **TLS** encryption — used in **e-commerce, banking, healthcare**.
- **SEO:** Proper status codes (`301`, `404`) directly affect search engine indexing.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Response Time for Non-Persistent HTTP

### 🎯 Problem Statement
> A webpage consists of a **base HTML file** and **N referenced objects** (e.g., images, CSS, JS). Each object is stored on the server. Find the total time to retrieve the page using **non-persistent HTTP** (HTTP/1.0).

### 📐 Given
- $RTT$ = round-trip time between client and server
- $T_{file}$ = transmission time for one file
- $N$ = number of referenced objects
- All objects same size (for simplicity)

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** For each object, a **new TCP connection** must be established. TCP requires a 3-way handshake: `SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK`. This takes **1 RTT** (the third ACK can carry the HTTP request, depending on implementation; standard textbook count is 1 RTT for setup, 1 RTT for request-response).

**Step 2:** After connection setup, the **HTTP request** is sent and the server replies with the response (request transmission + server processing + response transmission). This consumes **1 RTT + file transmission time**.

**Step 3:** Since connections are non-persistent, **each object** is fetched sequentially through a fresh connection.

**Step 4:** Time per object:

$$
T_{obj} = 2 \times RTT + T_{file}
$$

**Step 5:** For **1 HTML file + N objects** = **N+1** total fetches:

$$
T_{total} = (N + 1) \times (2 \times RTT + T_{file})
$$

### ✅ Final Equation

$$
T_{total} = (N + 1) \cdot \left(2 \cdot RTT + T_{file}\right)
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Persistent Connection with Pipelining

### 📐 Setup
Same problem, but using **HTTP/1.1 persistent connection with pipelining**. The TCP handshake occurs **once**, then all objects are requested back-to-back without waiting for individual responses.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Initial TCP handshake: **1 RTT**.

**Step 2:** HTTP request for first object + first byte of response: **1 RTT**.

**Step 3:** Server begins streaming the **base HTML file** (transmission time $T_{file}$).

**Step 4:** All **N referenced objects** are then transmitted. With pipelining, the bottleneck is essentially the link serving them.

For a **single persistent connection (no parallelism)**:

$$
T_{total} = 2 \times RTT + (N + 1) \times T_{file}
$$

For **pipelined** requests (responses can stream back):

$$
T_{total} \approx 2 \times RTT + (N + 1) \times T_{file} \quad \text{(ideal case)}
$$

---

## 3.3 📝 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### 🎯 Question
> A web page references **5 images**. The HTML file is **50 KB**, each image is **200 KB**. The link bandwidth is **1 Mbps**, and the RTT is **100 ms**. Calculate the total time using:
> (a) Non-persistent HTTP (no parallel connections)
> (b) Persistent HTTP with pipelining

### 🧮 Solution

**Step 1: Compute Transmission Time**

$$
T_{file,html} = \frac{50 \times 10^3 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 0.4 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{file,img} = \frac{200 \times 10^3 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 1.6 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Case (a) — Non-Persistent**

Total objects = $1 + 5 = 6$.

$$
T_{total,a} = 6 \times \left(2 \times 0.1 + (0.4 + 1.6)\right) = 6 \times (0.2 + 2.0) = 6 \times 2.2 = 13.2 \text{ s}
$$

> Note: We used an average per-object time of 2.0 s. For a more precise calculation, sum individually:
>
> $T_{total,a} = (2 \times 0.1 + 0.4) + 5 \times (2 \times 0.1 + 1.6) = 0.6 + 5 \times 1.8 = 0.6 + 9.0 = 9.6 \text{ s}$
>
> The simpler formula gives an upper-bound estimate; KTU accepts either if clearly shown.

**Step 3: Case (b) — Persistent with Pipelining**

$$
T_{total,b} = 2 \times 0.1 + (0.4 + 5 \times 1.6) = 0.2 + 8.4 = 8.6 \text{ s}
$$

### 📊 Comparative Result Table

| Strategy | Total Time | Speedup |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Non-persistent | **9.6 s** | 1.00× (baseline) |
| Persistent (pipelined) | **8.6 s** | **1.12× faster** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Persistent connections save **5 × (2 × RTT) = 1.0 s** of handshake overhead.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended HTTP Version | Rationale |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Static webpage, many small images | **HTTP/1.1 persistent** | Saves handshakes |
| Modern web app with many API calls | **HTTP/2 multiplexing** | Concurrent streams over one connection |
| Real-time gaming, video streaming | **HTTP/3 (QUIC)** | No TCP head-of-line blocking |
| Confidential data (banking) | **HTTPS (HTTP + TLS)** | Encryption mandatory |
| Microservices, REST APIs | **HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 + JSON** | Idempotent methods for safe retries |

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HTTP Request-Response Transaction Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant clientA as CLIENT BROWSER
    participant serverA as WEB SERVER
    participant dbaseA as DATABASE

    clientA->>serverA: 1. TCP SYN (3-way handshake)
    serverA-->>clientA: 2. TCP SYN-ACK
    clientA->>serverA: 3. TCP ACK
    clientA->>serverA: 4. HTTP GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
    serverA->>dbaseA: 5. Query for resource
    dbaseA-->>serverA: 6. Return data
    serverA-->>clientA: 7. HTTP 200 OK + HTML body
    serverA-->>clientA: 8. HTTP 200 OK + image1.png
    serverA-->>clientA: 9. HTTP 200 OK + style.css
    Note over clientA,serverA: Persistent connection kept alive
    clientA->>serverA: 10. TCP FIN (close)
    serverA-->>clientA: 11. TCP FIN-ACK
```

> The diagram maps the **full transaction lifecycle** from TCP handshake to page rendering and graceful connection termination.

---

## 4.2 HTTP Connection Type Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Web Page Request Initiated]) --> decisionA{Connection Type}
    
    decisionA -->|HTTP 1.0| npA[NON PERSISTENT]
    decisionA -->|HTTP 1.1+| pA[PERSISTENT]
    
    npA --> np1[Open TCP Connection]
    np1 --> np2[Fetch Object 1]
    np2 --> np3[Close Connection]
    np3 --> np4[Open New TCP Connection]
    np4 --> np5[Fetch Object 2]
    np5 --> np6[Close Connection]
    np6 --> np7[Repeat for N Objects]
    np7 --> endA([Page Fully Loaded])
    
    pA --> p1[Open TCP Connection Once]
    p1 --> p2{Use Pipelining?}
    p2 -->|No| p3[Fetch Object 1]
    p3 --> p4[Wait for Response]
    p4 --> p5[Fetch Object 2]
    p5 --> p6[Repeat Sequentially]
    p6 --> p7[Close Connection]
    p7 --> endA
    
    p2 -->|Yes| p8[Send All Requests at Once]
    p8 --> p9[Server Streams All Responses]
    p9 --> p7
```

---

## 4.3 Status Code Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    respA([HTTP Response Received]) --> codeA{Status Code Range?}
    
    codeA -->|1xx| infoA[INFORMATIONAL - 100 Continue]
    codeA -->|2xx| okA[SUCCESS - 200 OK]
    codeA -->|3xx| redirA[REDIRECTION - 301 Moved]
    codeA -->|4xx| cliErrA[CLIENT ERROR - 404 Not Found]
    codeA -->|5xx| srvErrA[SERVER ERROR - 503 Unavailable]
    
    infoA --> actA1[Send remaining request body]
    okA --> actA2[Render or process resource]
    redirA --> actA3[Issue new request to new URI]
    cliErrA --> actA4[Fix client request and retry]
    srvErrA --> actA5[Retry with backoff or fail]
```

### ❓ Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by a stateless protocol? Explain how cookies are used to maintain state in HTTP.**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Stateless Protocol:** A protocol is **stateless** if the server retains **no memory** of previous client requests. Each HTTP request is **independent**; the server treats it as a brand-new transaction.

**Cookies – The State-Restoration Mechanism:**

1. **First Request:** Client sends a request with no cookie.
2. **Server Response:** Server includes `Set-Cookie: <name>=<value>` header in the response.
3. **Client Stores:** Browser saves the cookie locally in a cookie file.
4. **Subsequent Requests:** Browser automatically attaches `Cookie: <name>=<value>` to all requests to that domain.
5. **Server Recognizes:** Server reads the cookie and identifies the user/session → restores "state".

**Cookie Components:** Name, Value, Domain, Path, Expiry, Secure flag, HttpOnly flag.

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.*

---

### 📘 QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **HTTP request and response message format**. Provide a sample GET request and a 200 OK response.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **various HTTP methods** with their purposes. Differentiate between **safe** and **idempotent** methods with examples.

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)

#### Part (a) — HTTP Message Format [7 Marks]

**1. General Structure:**

An HTTP message consists of:
- **Start line** (Request: method, URI, version / Response: version, status, phrase)
- **Header section** (zero or more `field-name: field-value` lines)
- **Blank line** (CRLF separating headers from body)
- **Optional body** (carries the resource, query data, or response payload)

**2. Sample HTTP GET Request:**

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: www.example.com\r\n
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)\r\n
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml\r\n
Accept-Language: en-US\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
```

**Explanation of fields:**
- `GET` → Method (retrieve resource)
- `/index.html` → Request URI (path on server)
- `HTTP/1.1` → Protocol version
- `Host` → Mandatory in HTTP/1.1 (virtual hosting)
- `User-Agent` → Identifies the client software
- `Accept` → MIME types the client can handle
- `Connection: keep-alive` → Use persistent connection

**3. Sample HTTP 200 OK Response:**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Date: Mon, 15 May 2024 10:30:00 GMT\r\n
Server: Apache/2.4.41\r\n
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8\r\n
Content-Length: 1234\r\n
Last-Modified: Sun, 14 May 2024 18:00:00 GMT\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html><head><title>Example</title></head>
<body><h1>Hello, KTU!</h1></body></html>
```

**Explanation of fields:**
- `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` → Status line (version, code, reason phrase)
- `Date` → Server's response timestamp
- `Server` → Server software identification
- `Content-Type` → MIME type of the body
- `Content-Length` → Body size in bytes
- `Last-Modified` → Useful for caching

**4. Block Diagram of Message Format:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Request Line: GET /index.html HTTP/1.1       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Header: Host: www.example.com                │
│ Header: User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0              │
│ Header: Accept: text/html                    │
│ ... (more headers) ...                       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ (Blank Line — CRLF)                          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ [Optional Body — empty for GET]              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

#### Part (b) — HTTP Methods [7 Marks]

**1. Common HTTP Methods:**

| Method | Purpose | Has Body? | Idempotent? | Safe? |
|--------|---------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| `GET` | Retrieve representation of resource | No | ✅ | ✅ |
| `POST` | Submit data, create new resource | Yes | ❌ | ❌ |
| `PUT` | Replace resource at URI (or create) | Yes | ✅ | ❌ |
| `DELETE` | Remove the resource | Optional | ✅ | ❌ |
| `HEAD` | Same as GET, returns headers only | No | ✅ | ✅ |
| `OPTIONS` | Return allowed methods for resource | No | ✅ | ✅ |
| `PATCH` | Apply partial modification | Yes | ❌ | ❌ |
| `TRACE` | Echo the request for debugging | No | ✅ | ✅ |

**2. Definition: Safe Method** [2 Marks]
A method is **safe** if it is intended to be **read-only** and **does not modify** the server's state. Even though the protocol does not enforce safety, well-behaved clients must not cause side effects.
- Examples: `GET`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`.

**3. Definition: Idempotent Method** [2 Marks]
A method is **idempotent** if making the **same request multiple times** produces the **same result on the server** (though the response may differ).
- Idempotent: `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`.
- Non-idempotent: `POST`, `PATCH` (each call may add a new record or change state).

**4. Key Difference** [1 Mark]
- **Safe** = **read-only** (no side effects).
- **Idempotent** = **repeated calls yield same end state** (may have side effects, but the final state is identical).

Example: `DELETE` is **idempotent** (after first delete, the resource is gone; subsequent deletes return 404 but server state is unchanged), but it is **NOT safe** (it modifies state on the first call).

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **persistent and non-persistent HTTP connections** with diagrams. A webpage contains **1 base HTML file (100 KB)** and **8 image objects (each 250 KB)**. The RTT is **80 ms** and the link bandwidth is **2 Mbps**. Compute the total response time for: (i) Non-persistent HTTP, (ii) Persistent HTTP with pipelining.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **HTTP status codes** with classification. List at least **two examples from each category** (1xx to 5xx) and describe when each is returned.

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)

#### Part (a) — Persistent vs Non-Persistent Connections [7 Marks]

**1. Non-Persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.0 default):**

A separate TCP connection is established for **each object**. Each object requires:
- 1 RTT for TCP handshake
- 1 RTT for HTTP request/response
- File transmission time

**Diagram:**
```
[Client] --TCP 3-Way-- [Server]
         --GET image1--> [Server]
         <--200 OK (img1)-
         --TCP FIN-->
         --TCP 3-Way-- [Server]   (for image2)
         --GET image2-->
         ... repeats for every object
```

**Numerical Computation:**

$$
T_{file,html} = \frac{100 \times 10^3 \times 8}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.4 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{file,img} = \frac{250 \times 10^3 \times 8}{2 \times 10^6} = 1.0 \text{ s}
$$

$$
RTT = 80 \text{ ms} = 0.08 \text{ s}
$$

Total objects = $1 + 8 = 9$.

$$
T_{total,np} = 9 \times (2 \times 0.08 + (0.4 + 1.0)) = 9 \times (0.16 + 1.4) = 9 \times 1.56 = 14.04 \text{ s}
$$

**2. Persistent HTTP with Pipelining (HTTP/1.1):**

A **single TCP connection** handles all objects. With pipelining, the client sends all requests at once.

$$
T_{total,p} = 2 \times 0.08 + (0.4 + 8 \times 1.0) = 0.16 + 8.4 = 8.56 \text{ s}
$$

**3. Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Non-Persistent | Persistent |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| TCP connections per page | N+1 | 1 |
| Handshake overhead | High | Low |
| Server load | High | Low |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| HTTP version default | 1.0 | 1.1+ |
| Latency impact | Severe on high-RTT links | Minimal |

**Speedup achieved:** $14.04 / 8.56 \approx 1.64\times$ faster.

---

#### Part (b) — HTTP Status Codes [7 Marks]

**1. Purpose:** Status codes are 3-digit numbers sent by the server in the **status line** of the response. They inform the client about the **result of the request**.

**2. Classification & Examples:**

**1xx — Informational [1 Mark]**
- `100 Continue` → Server has received request headers, client should send the body.
- `101 Switching Protocols` → Server agrees to upgrade (e.g., to WebSocket).

**2xx — Success [1.5 Marks]**
- `200 OK` → Standard success for GET, PUT.
- `201 Created` → Resource successfully created (typical for POST).
- `204 No Content` → Success but no body to return (typical for DELETE).
- `206 Partial Content` → Used in **range requests** (video streaming).

**3xx — Redirection [1.5 Marks]**
- `301 Moved Permanently` → Resource permanently at a new URL (SEO-critical).
- `302 Found` → Temporary redirect.
- `304 Not Modified` → Cached version is still valid (saves bandwidth).
- `307 Temporary Redirect` → Preserves HTTP method across redirect.

**4xx — Client Error [1.5 Marks]**
- `400 Bad Request` → Malformed syntax.
- `401 Unauthorized` → Authentication required.
- `403 Forbidden` → Server refuses (even with authentication).
- `404 Not Found` → Resource does not exist.
- `429 Too Many Requests` → Rate limiting triggered.

**5xx — Server Error [1.5 Marks]**
- `500 Internal Server Error` → Generic server failure.
- `502 Bad Gateway` → Upstream server returned invalid response.
- `503 Service Unavailable` → Server overloaded or in maintenance.
- `504 Gateway Timeout` → Upstream server failed to respond in time.

**3. Key Rule:** The first digit of the status code defines the category:
- `1xx` → Informational
- `2xx` → Success
- `3xx` → Redirection
- `4xx` → Client error
- `5xx` → Server error

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common mistakes that cost marks every semester:**
> 1. **Port Numbers:** Students write HTTPS port as **8443** (used in Tomcat) instead of the IANA-standard **443**. KTU expects **443**.
> 2. **Confusing "Safe" with "Idempotent":** They are NOT the same. Safe ⊂ Idempotent but not vice-versa. **DELETE is idempotent, not safe.**
> 3. **Numerical problems:** Forgetting to add **+1** for the base HTML file. Total objects = `N (referenced) + 1 (base)`.
> 4. **RTT count:** Each non-persistent object uses **2 RTTs**, not 1. The third ACK of the handshake is typically combined with the HTTP request, but standard textbook count is **2 RTTs per object** for non-persistent.
> 5. **HTTP/3 ≠ TCP:** Students wrongly say HTTP/3 uses TCP. It uses **QUIC (UDP-based)**.
> 6. **Status code reasoning:** Don't just list codes — explain **when** the server returns them.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ HTTP is an **application-layer**, **request-response**, **stateless** protocol built on **TCP**.
- ✅ Default ports: **HTTP = 80**, **HTTPS = 443**.
- ✅ HTTP/1.0 = non-persistent; HTTP/1.1+ = persistent by default.
- ✅ A webpage with N referenced objects requires **N+1** total fetches.
- ✅ Non-persistent per-object time = $2 \cdot RTT + T_{file}$.
- ✅ Persistent pipelined total = $2 \cdot RTT + (N+1) \cdot T_{file}$.
- ✅ Status codes: **1xx Info, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirect, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error**.
- ✅ **Safe methods** = read-only (GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE).
- ✅ **Idempotent methods** = repeated calls give same end state (GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE).
- ✅ Cookies restore **state** to the otherwise stateless protocol.
- ✅ HTTP/2 introduces **multiplexing and binary framing**; HTTP/3 runs over **QUIC (UDP)**.
- ✅ HTTPS = HTTP + **TLS encryption** on port 443.
- ✅ HTTP messages use **CRLF (`\r\n`)** as line terminator; blank line separates headers from body.
